source.title: Per-contract Buffered Commitment Allocation Sheet Q4 2029
source.content:
Purpose:
This working sheet captures per-contract reserved capacity allocations with a buffered commit model. The goal is to map contractual GPU-hour commitments into operational slots across regions and GPU classes, include a configurable buffer for burst capacity, and maintain utilization targets and rollover rules per customer.

Summary of approach:
- Convert monetary/commitment terms into a canonical unit: GPU-slot-hours (GSH) where 1 GSH = 1 GPU of the specified class for 1 hour. Different GPU classes (A4, V100, H100-80G) have conversion factors to normalize horsepower (see GPU_CLASS_FACTOR).
- Allocate base entitlement per contract by region according to geographic constraints and contract clauses.
- Reserve a buffer (default 18% of base GSH) in each region for short-term burst. Buffer is shared across contracts in fairness pool unless contract forbids pooling.
- Track soft-utilization target (target_util_pct) and hard-cap floor (min_guarantee_pct). If sustained usage > target for 30d, trigger capacity review and potential uplift.
- Rollover: unused base GSH up to 5% of committed value rolls to next month automatically; anything above requires CS approval.

Legend / fields in table below (CSV):
- contract_id: internal contract identifier
- customer: legal customer name
- gpu_class: A4 | V100 | H100-80G (normalized via GPU_CLASS_FACTOR column)
- region_pref: primary region(s) for allocation (comma-separated)
- committed_gsh_month: contracted GPU-slot-hours per month (canonical units)
- gpu_class_factor: normalization multiplier for class (e.g., A4=0.33, V100=1.0, H100-80G=2.8)
- base_allocation_gsh: committed_gsh_month * (1) -- base before regional split and buffer
- region_split: percent allocation per region (format: region:percent; multiple separated by | )
- region_allocation_gsh: computed per region from base_allocation_gsh and region_split (see rows below)
- buffer_pct: percent of base held as buffered burst in same region (default 18% unless contract specifies)
- buffer_gsh: region_allocation_gsh * buffer_pct
- effective_guarantee_gsh: region_allocation_gsh - buffer_gsh (this is hard-guaranteed capacity)
- target_util_pct: operational target utilization (soft) for billing and autoscaling decisions
- min_guarantee_pct: minimum guaranteed fraction to avoid SLA breach
- monthly_rollover_policy: free-rollover-percent|requires_cs_approval-after-percent
- notes: free text with references to clauses or special handling

CSV_TABLE_START
contract_id,customer,gpu_class,gpu_class_factor,committed_gsh_month,base_allocation_gsh,region_pref,region_split,region_allocation_gsh,buffer_pct,buffer_gsh,effective_guarantee_gsh,target_util_pct,min_guarantee_pct,monthly_rollover_policy,notes
INF-CT-00421,BlueJar Labs,H100-80G,2.8,19200,19200,us-west1|europe-west1,"us-west1:70|europe-west1:30","us-west1:13440|europe-west1:5760",0.18,2419.2,"us-west1:11020.8|europe-west1:4340.8",0.78,0.65,5%|10%,high-latency-tolerance; prefer us-west1 for model-serving warmed pools; legal clause: lat-req waived Q4
INF-CT-00422,Greenline Health,V100,1.0,7200,7200,us-east1,"us-east1:100","us-east1:7200",0.18,1296,5904,0.8,0.7,5%|10%,HIPAA addendum; buffer cannot be pooled (contract-level restriction)
INF-CT-00423,ArcadeForge,A4,0.33,54000,54000,asia-southeast1|us-west1,"asia-southeast1:50|us-west1:50","asia-southeast1:27000|us-west1:27000",0.2,5400,"asia-southeast1:21600|us-west1:21600",0.7,0.6,3%|8%,PLG customer; heavy diurnal spikes; recommend pre-warm windows 06:00-10:00 local
INF-CT-00424,Vertex Insurance,H100-80G,2.8,38400,38400,europe-west1|us-central1,"europe-west1:60|us-central1:40","europe-west1:23040|us-central1:15360",0.15,5760,"europe-west1:17280|us-central1:12960",0.82,0.75,5%|12%,regulated customer; dedicated hardware tenancy required in europe-west1; buffer retained in same tenancy
INF-CT-00425,Neptune Maps,V100,1.0,10800,10800,us-west2|us-east1|asia-east1,"us-west2:40|us-east1:40|asia-east1:20","us-west2:4320|us-east1:4320|asia-east1:2160",0.18,777.6|777.6|388.8,"us-west2:3542.4|us-east1:3542.4|asia-east1:1771.2",0.75,0.6,5%|10%,seasonal transport surge forecast Nov-Feb; ask sales for uplift schedule
CSV_TABLE_END

Operational notes and formulas (working):
- GPU_CLASS_FACTOR table: A4=0.33, V100=1.0, H100-80G=2.8. committed_gsh_month is recorded in canonical GSH already for consistency (sales converts when drafting contracts).
- region_allocation_gsh = round(base_allocation_gsh * (region_percent / 100), 1) for each region.
- buffer_gsh = round(region_allocation_gsh * buffer_pct, 1). If contract buffer_pct_override present, use that value.
- effective_guarantee_gsh = region_allocation_gsh - buffer_gsh. Rounded to 1 decimal.
- If customer prohibits pooling, buffer_gsh is reserved in isolated slab and cannot be borrowed by fairness pool. Tag these rows with notes: "buffer:isolated".
- Fairness pool algorithm (sketch): maintain per-region available_buffer_pool = sum(buffer_gsh where poolable) - sum(current_borrowed). Borrow order: 1) contracts in same legal region with soft-util > target_util_pct but within 12% of target; 2) emergency uplift requests via SRE runbook. Borrow duration limited to 72 hours unless auto-extended by CS.

Alerts and triggers (for on-call / capacity ops):
- Sustained utilization > 90% of (effective_guarantee_gsh + borrowed_buffer) for 48h in any region -> open JIRA-INFRA-ALERT and notify capacity-oncall.
- Borrowing > 50% of pool for > 24h -> email ops-leads and CS owners.
- Contract nearing 95% of monthly committed_gsh with > 7 days remaining -> auto-create capacity review ticket.

Runbook snippets (how to adjust allocations):
- To increase base_allocation_gsh: validate contract uplift (Sales signed amendment) -> update committed_gsh_month -> re-run regional split job -> apply changes to reserved-slab appliance (Infra CLI) with change window.
- To convert buffer to guaranteed (temporary uplift): create short-term commitment token (token TTL <= 30d) and debit fairness pool if pooled; if isolated, require customer approval + billing adjustment.

Known data gaps / action items (TODO):
- Sales to supply exact conversion multipliers for newer GPU SKUs (A100X, T4-v2) by 2029-11-15.
- CS to provide per-contract buffer pooling opt-in flags for contracts signed prior to 2028-01-01.
- Run periodic reconciliation with billing (monthly) to ensure recorded usage vs committed_gsh_month with rounding tolerances; propose auto-notifications for delta > 3%

Appendix: quick sample queries (for infra team)
- Find customers with buffer_isolated: SELECT contract_id FROM contracts WHERE buffer_poolable = false AND committed_gsh_month > 5000
- Region stress check: SELECT region, SUM(effective_guarantee_gsh + buffer_gsh_poolable) FROM allocations GROUP BY region HAVING SUM(...) > region_capacity_threshold

End of sheet (working).
source._note: This file is a working spreadsheet-like doc stored as JSON per agents.md rules. Field values are strings or arrays of strings.

source.title: Interim replay guidance and customer-facing post-incident note: persistent output inconsistencies
source.status_page_snippet: Interim: We are investigating reports of intermittent and persistent inconsistent outputs for a subset of requests. Some customers may see responses that differ from recent historical results. No customer data was lost. Engineers have throttled a cache code path as a temporary mitigation. Next update in 60–90 minutes or sooner if there is material change.
source.postmortem_summary: Summary: Between 2029-10-23 22:10 UTC and 2029-10-24 06:40 UTC some requests served from Redwood's prefix/KV caching layer returned stale or semantically divergent outputs compared to non-cached executions. The behavior affected approximately 0.8% of generation requests for affected models (primarily open-model variants with long-context caching enabled). The issue manifested as missing recent context, truncated continuations, or low-quality hallucinated content for particular prompt shapes. Impact severity: P2 for platform (customer-visible but not full outage).
source.detailed_timeline:
2029-10-23 21:55 UTC: Deploy that introduced a change to the cache warmup heuristic was rolled to canary.
2029-10-23 22:10 UTC: Anomalous error signature detected by runtime telemetry (cache-miss-to-hit ratio spike and increased divergence metric on eval traffic). Alert routed to on-call serving-runtime engineer.
2029-10-23 22:20 UTC: Initial mitigation — canary deployment automatically rolled back to previous binary by release system; telemetry partially recovered but divergence continued on some steady-state nodes.
2029-10-24 00:05 UTC: Investigation reveals an interaction between a new prefix aggregation optimization and background cache eviction that resulted in stale KV pointers being served for certain request shapes.
2029-10-24 01:30 UTC: Engineers apply a targeted throttle to the prefix aggregation path in prod to prevent warmup races while preserving overall caching capacity.
2029-10-24 03:15 UTC: Customer Success begins proactive outreach to top-affected tenants with examples and suggested temporary configuration changes.
2029-10-24 06:40 UTC: Full mitigations in place (throttle + patched eviction checker) and monitoring shows stable divergence metrics; tickets opened for hard fix and further validation.
source.root_cause: A race condition between the prefix aggregation optimization (intended to coalesce similar prefixes into shared cache entries) and an asynchronous cache eviction sweep produced valid but stale KV references on a subset of nodes. Under certain timing conditions, the aggregation path would reuse an entry that had been marked for eviction but not yet fully reclaimed; that entry retained older tokenizer/offset metadata resulting in misaligned continuations. This was not a model-level regression — model weights were unchanged — but a serving-layer correctness issue introduced in a recent performance optimization.
source.impact_details:
- Scope: ~0.8% of generation requests across a narrow set of model+config combinations (long context + prefix caching + aggregation enabled).
- Customer experience: Inaccurate continuations, missing recent context, and occasional hallucinations. No unauthorized data exposure.
- Data integrity: No token-level loss or truncation in persistent storage; only served outputs were inconsistent.
- Duration: Visible from detection (2029-10-23 22:10 UTC) until mainline mitigations (2029-10-24 06:40 UTC).
source.mitigations_taken:
Short term (done):
- Rolled back canary where applicable.
- Throttled prefix aggregation path to avoid warmup races.
- Deployed an eviction-sanity checker to prevent reuse of in-flight-to-evict entries.
- Proactively notified top impacted customers and provided sample request/response logs.
Medium term (in progress):
- Patch merged to add stronger lifetime checks and atomic swap semantics for cache entries (PR: https://github.com/redwood-inference/serving-runtime/pull/2987).
- Expanded canary traffic with synthetic prompt shapes to validate correctness.
Long term (planned):
- Replace legacy aggregation with a lock-free version with explicit epoching.
- Add regression tests to capture prefix-edge cases across tokenizer boundary conditions.
source.customer_actions_and_guidance:
If you observed inconsistent outputs during the window above we recommend:
1) Replay affected requests using non-cached routing by setting the request flag 'cache: bypass' — this will force a fresh model run and should produce expected outputs.
2) If you rely on deterministic behavior for a prompt set, temporarily pin to model variants that have caching disabled for long-context paths.
3) Share request IDs and timestamps with our support team (use the example format below) so we can validate and, if needed, re-run the same workload in a controlled environment.
Example: request-id=abc123-20291024T0015Z model=gpt-open-16k context-shape=system+user+2000tokens
We are offering support-assisted replays for customers who need validated re-runs of traffic between 2029-10-23 21:50 UTC and 2029-10-24 07:00 UTC — please open a ticket referencing JIRA-INC-5438 and we will prioritize.
source.customer_facing_q_and_a:
Q: Was customer data exposed or deleted?
A: No. There was no data exfiltration or deletion. The issue affected how cached state was read when serving; raw customer payloads and stored logs are intact.

Q: Do I need to rotate keys or change credentials?
A: No credential rotation is required as this was not an auth/credential issue.

Q: Will Redwood compensate for this incident?
A: We are reviewing impacted usage for eligible SLO credit. Customer Success will reach out with any credits and the evaluation criteria.

Q: How will you prevent a recurrence?
A: See the 'mitigations_taken' and 'long term' sections — we will add stronger unit/regression tests, finalize the lock-free aggregation rewrite, and improve canary coverage for border tokenizer shapes.
source.next_steps_and_timeline:
Immediate (24-72h):
- Finish expanded canary and monitor divergence metrics for 48 hours.
- Run a targeted audit of other caching heuristics for race potential.
- Customer Success to complete all outreach to impacted tenants.
Near term (2-4 weeks):
- Roll out the merged patch globally after canary validation.
- Begin development on the lock-free aggregation replacement.
Longer term (8-12 weeks):
- Ship aggregation replacement + new regression suite.
- Post-deployment review and external-facing postmortem (finalized) to be published on status.redwood.ai and direct emails to impacted customers.
source.communication_playbook_notes:
- Keep status page terse and focused: short headline, impact (who/what), what we did, and next update ETA.
- For customers who experienced user-visible regressions offer a replay and attach the re-run artifacts.
- Avoid technical jargon in initial status updates; include more detail in the post-incident note.
- Label messages clearly as 'interim' vs 'final' and date them.
source.contact_and_support:
Primary contact: customer-success@redwood.ai
Escalation: Priya Natarajan (priya@redwood.ai) / +1-415-555-0198
For replays or urgent validation, open a support ticket with subject: 'Replay request — JIRA-INC-5438' and include request IDs or a time window.
source.draft_notes_and_open_questions:
- Need to confirm exact list of tenants affected by the aggregation path (data team to provide query by 2029-10-25 EOD).
- Verify whether any third-party partner integrations observed degraded outputs (solutions-eng to confirm).
- Decide SLO credit formula for partially degraded but not fully failed requests (finance + legal meeting scheduled 2029-10-27).
source.closing_line: We sincerely apologize for the disruption. Our focus is on restoring deterministic behavior and reducing risk of recurrence. We will publish a final customer-facing postmortem after the remaining mitigations are validated.
source.body:
(This doc is an internal customer-facing draft. Use the 'status_page_snippet' for updates on status.redwood.ai and the 'postmortem_summary' + 'detailed_timeline' for the longer customer note. Keep language concise and action-oriented.)

Prepared by: Priya Natarajan (Customer Success)
Reviewers: Ethan Park (Serving Runtime), Maya Li (SRE), Samira Khan (Solutions Eng)

Example quick status update (for status.redwood.ai):
Title: Interim — inconsistent generation outputs for a subset of requests
Impact: Some customers may see responses that omit recent context or contain unexpected continuations.
What we are doing: Engineers identified and throttled a cache aggregation path; additional fixes are being validated in canary.
ETA for next update: 60–90 minutes.

Example customer email snippet (short):
Subject: Update from Redwood — intermittent response inconsistencies (Oct 24)
Hi —
We detected intermittent inconsistencies in generated outputs for a small subset of requests and have implemented mitigations. If you saw unexpected outputs between 2029-10-23 21:50 UTC and 2029-10-24 07:00 UTC, please reply with sample request IDs so we can re-run them for you. We apologize for the disruption; more details and a final postmortem will follow.

(End of draft)

Some customers may see responses that differ from recent historical results. No customer data was lost. Engineers have throttled a cache code path as a temporary mitigation. Next update in 60–90 minutes or sooner if there is material change.

source.title: Opportunistic fleet fill playbook (scratch)
source.content:
Opportunistic Fleet Fill — playbook scratch notes (week Sep 2–8, 2029)

Summary:
- Goal: safely increase usable capacity by filling a controlled fraction of the fleet with opportunistic (preemptible/spot) nodes during low-demand windows while preserving latency SLOs and minimizing evictions impact on customer traffic.
- This doc is a working scratchpad: experiment results, quick heuristics, proposed runbook steps, and unanswered questions. Not final.

What's changed this week:
- Ran two short A/B windows in us-west and ap-southeast (local nights): 02:00–05:30 local.
  - us-west: opportunistic fill = 8% of pool for 3.5h, median latency unchanged, P95 +18ms.
  - ap-southeast: opportunistic fill = 12% of pool for 3h, saw 2.4% eviction churn and transient retry spike on platform routes with no user-visible errors (retry layer absorbed most).
- Quick win: small scheduler tweak reduced KV-cache warm penalty at reattach by ~22% (scratch measurement).

Observations & metrics (last 48h experimental windows):
- Baseline usage: US-west peak utilization 78% (token throughput window), APAC baseline 64%.
- Opportunistic fill applied: US-west +8%, APAC +12%.
- Eviction rate (opportunistic nodes): APAC 2.4% over 3h window, US-west 0.6% (likely provider differences).
- Latency: median stayed ~90ms; P95 moved from 292ms -> 310ms in APAC during last 30m of window.
- Cost delta (preliminary): estimated -11.8% per-token infra cost on night-window traffic when opportunistic capacity held.
- Cold-attach failures: observed two incidents in APAC (attach timeout) tied to a specific provider image; filed INFRA-4792.

Experiment notes (configuration tested):
- Fill-policy v0.7 (controlled by region): {fill-cap: 12%, prewarm-ratio: 0.15, warm-standby: 4% of pool, eviction-hedge: keep N spare for fallback} (scratch math below).
- Warm-standby approach: keep a small fraction of dedicated nodes (4%) as hot fallback. On eviction event > 3%/30m, route traffic to fallback pool and throttle new opportunistic attach requests.
- Backoff: exponential attach backoff (initial 15s -> 60s max) when provider rate limits hit.

Scratch math (rough):
- Region pool size: 1250 GPUs. 12% opportunistic = 150 GPUs.
- Expected usable capacity after evictions (~2.5% observed) = 150 * (1 - 0.025) ≈ 146 GPUs.
- If opportunistic fill avoids provisioning 40 reserved GPUs, monthly saving ≈ $Xk (need finance calc).

Risks & failure modes:
- Provider-specific eviction spikes (observed in ap-southeast) causing transient retry storms upstream.
- Cold-attach/image issues causing attach failures and delayed KV-cache warm paths.
- Latency tail regression at scale if opportunistic attach frequency overlaps with customer traffic spike.
- Billing/chargeback complexity for customers on Dedicated if opportunistic nodes are misattributed.

Runbook excerpt (operational gating rules — very conservative):
1) Pre-checks before enabling fill in region:
   - Region P95 latency for last 24h < region-SLO * 0.9
   - Eviction rate for tested providers < 3% in last 7d
   - No ongoing platform deploys touching scheduler components
2) Start window (human-approved or scheduled): enable fill-cap = 4% for first 60m, monitor metrics (latency, evictions, attach failures)
3) If metrics stable after 60m -> step to configured fill-cap (8–12%)
4) If eviction rate > 5% in any 1h => immediate rollback: disable new opportunistic attaches, migrate traffic to warm-standby, page on-call (pager: #gpu-ops)
5) Post-window: run a 30m cool-down where opportunistic nodes drain gracefully (no preemptive reassign)

Open questions / decisions needed:
- Financial: precise unit economics per-region: need finance to run model for provider A vs B (assign to Leah).
- Customer-facing policy: do we expose opportunistic tagging to Dedicated customers? (legal/product question).
- Can SRE add a synthetic load test that exercises KV-cache reattach path under eviction to validate warm penalty? (assign to Marco).

Action items (owners + due):
- Marco: run synthetic eviction + reattach baseline test, capture KV-cache warm penalty (due 2029-09-10).
- Leah: push cost model for two provider choices and quantify NPV for next 3 months (due 2029-09-12).
- Owen: land scheduler backoff fix and add metrics to emit attach-failures by provider (PR referenced above) (due 2029-09-07).
- Priya: prepare short email for PMs on rollout plan and guardrails (due 2029-09-06).

Notes to self / loose thoughts:
- Consider time-shifted fill windows (local night) vs single global window; local windows reduce cross-region impact.
- Might be worth a small canary that only fills stateless routes (rerank/embedding) first, then stateful chat models.
- If attach timeouts persist, we should fall back to a warm image cache at the hypervisor level (cost vs complexity trade-off).

References and quick links:
- JIRA INFRA-4792 (image attach timeouts)
- GH PR: github.com/redwood-inference/fleet-autoscale/pull/312 (scheduler backoff)
- Slack thread: #gpu-ops t1234567890 (experiment logs)

End of scratch.

-- Priya
(working notes — do not treat as final playbook)

source.title: customer-brief-timeline-and-self-serve-actions-2029-08-30
source.content:
Purpose:
This note is a concise customer-facing briefing and post-incident summary we will publish on the status page and email to impacted tenants. It includes a short timeline, user-visible impact, high level root cause, immediate mitigations we implemented, suggested self-serve actions for customers, and next steps (permanent fixes + compensations).

Executive summary (for customers)
- Incident window: 2029-08-29 21:17 UTC to 2029-08-30 03:42 UTC (partial degradation; full mitigation completed).
- Impact: Intermittent truncated streaming responses and sporadic stale completions for ~0.6% of requests in affected regions (us-west and eu-west). A subset of streaming sessions to /v1/streams intermittently returned truncated JSON or `<STREAM-END>` markers early.
- User impact: Affected applications may have seen malformed structured outputs (function-calls, JSON blobs) and occasional outdated responses when prefix caching hit a stale entry. No customer data was exfiltrated.
- Current status: Service restored to normal for all customers; we are in follow-up mode and rolling out permanent fixes in the next 48 hours.

Scope and who was affected
- Affected service(s): hosted-generation streaming endpoint, streaming websocket proxy, and prefix/KV cache for small response footprints.
- Geography: Primary impact in us-west and eu-west clusters that were serving traffic through a recently changed autoscaling policy.
- Customer types: Both free-tier and paid tenants experienced transient issues; dedicated customers with pinned dedicated pools were largely unaffected.

Short timeline (UTC)
- 2029-08-29 21:17: First alert - elevated error rate on streaming sessions and increased JSON parse errors reported by monitoring and early customer reports.
- 21:22: Routing engineers initiated targeted throttles and routed new streaming requests to an unaffected fallback pool.
- 21:35: Initial mitigation deployed - disabled the new cache invalidation path (hotfix) and reverted the autoscaler change for the affected host pool. Error rate dropped by ~70%.
- 23:05: Partial rollback of a recent kernel selection optimization in the runtime to remove an off-by-one tokenization edge case observed in quantized attention kernels. This improved structured output integrity.
- 00:40: Additional cache sweep and safe eviction applied; stale-prefix rate dropped to near zero.
- 03:12: All streaming backpressure and truncation indicators returned to baseline.
- 03:42: Incident declared mitigated.

Root cause (high-level customer-friendly explanation)
- Two simultaneous factors combined to produce customer-visible failures: 1) a cache invalidation race introduced by a recent autoscaler/invalidation interaction that left a small subset of prefix entries stale; and 2) a tokenization off-by-one interaction with a new quantized attention kernel that in rare cases truncated the final tokens of streaming structured outputs (function-calls/JSON). When both conditions occurred on the same request path, customers saw malformed or stale outputs.

What we did to restore service
- Reverted the autoscaler policy change that caused the invalidation race and applied a controlled eviction procedure for the prefix cache to remove stale entries safely.
- Reverted the kernel selection optimization for quantized attention to avoid the off-by-one tokenization edge case.
- Temporarily routed new streaming requests to a fallback pool and engaged additional request-level validation that rejects truncated structured outputs server-side (fail-open to a safe non-structured response if validation fails).
- Added monitoring and alerts keyed to structured-output-validation failures and stale-prefix hits.

Customer-facing mitigations and recommended self-serve actions
- If you observed truncated JSON/function-call outputs: retry the request or re-run the affected job. The server-side validation and routing changes will prevent new truncated responses from reaching customers.
- If you have custom client-side parsers that fail on partial streams, add a short reassembly/retry loop to re-request the final chunk up to 3 times (recommended).
- For customers using prefix caching in their own clients: consider clearing any local long-lived caches or increasing your cache TTL temporarily until our permanent fix is live.
- Contact your assigned TAM or support@redwood.ai with logs (request IDs or timestamps) if you need us to retroactively re-run inference or re-evaluate a specific request.

Compensation and SLA information
- For paid accounts with measurable SLO impact during the incident window, our CS team will auto-evaluate SLO credit eligibility and reach out to account owners. You can also open a ticket with account details for expedited review.

Follow-up work (what we’re doing next)
- Code: deploy a robust invalidation protocol and a safer kernel-selection guard to eliminate the off-by-one edge case (ETA: 48 hours with staged rollout).
- Tests: add synthetic streaming test harnesses that validate structured-output integrity across quantization modes, and add chaos tests around autoscaler+cache interactions (ETA: 1 week).
- Monitoring: instrument per-tenant structured-output failure metrics and a short-lived audit log for cache eviction anomalies (ETA: 72 hours).
- Process: update release checklist to include autoscaler/cache interaction sign-off for future changes.

Points of contact
- Primary CS contact: Priya Natarajan (priya@redwood.ai).
- Engineering liaison for technical follow-up: Ethan Cole (ethan@redwood.ai).
- If you prefer enterprise/exec escalation: María González (maria@redwood.ai).

FAQ (anticipated customer questions)
Q: Was any customer data exposed?
A: No evidence of data exfiltration. The issue produced stale or truncated outputs only; we did not observe any cross-tenant data leakage.

Q: Are dedicated/reserved customers affected?
A: Most Dedicated pools were not impacted because they do not use the same autoscaler/cache invalidation path. If you saw an issue and are on a Dedicated plan, please contact your TAM for a tenant-specific review.

Q: Should I re-run failed jobs?
A: Yes — re-running will typically return correct results. If you need us to re-run on your behalf, provide request IDs and timestamps to support.

Draft status and next publication steps
- Current doc status: draft. Suggested next actions: 1) validate wording with legal for the compensation paragraph, 2) prepare a short status page entry (three bullet summary + link to full post-incident note), 3) schedule an outbound email to impacted account owners with tailored remediation offers.

Notes to internal stakeholders
- Tagging: add tag INC-2698 and alert the dedicated-accounts CS queue for follow-up.
- Slack: we will publish an update to #customer-notices with a short blurb and link to the full note once legal approves.

End of doc.

A: Most Dedicated pools were not impacted because they do not use the same autoscaler/cache invalidation path. If you saw an issue and are on a Dedicated plan, please contact your TAM for a tenant-specific review.

Q: Should I re-run failed jobs?
A: Yes — re-running will typically return correct results. If you need us to re-run on your behalf, provide request IDs and timestamps to support.

Draft status and next publication steps
- Current doc status: draft. Suggested next actions: 1) validate wording with legal for the compensation paragraph, 2) prepare a short status page entry (three bullet summary + link to full post-incident note), 3) schedule an outbound email to impacted account owners with tailored remediation offers.

Notes to internal stakeholders
- Tagging: add tag INC-2698 and alert the dedicated-accounts CS queue for follow-up.
- Slack: we will publish an update to #customer-notices with a short blurb and link to the full note once legal approves.

End of doc.
text:
Purpose:
This note is a concise customer-facing briefing and post-incident summary we will publish on the status page and email to impacted tenants. It includes a short timeline, user-visible impact, high level root cause, immediate mitigations we implemented, suggested self-serve actions for customers, and next steps (permanent fixes + compensations).

Executive summary (for customers)
- Incident window: 2029-08-29 21:17 UTC to 2029-08-30 03:42 UTC (partial degradation; full mitigation completed).
- Impact: Intermittent truncated streaming responses and sporadic stale completions for ~0.6% of requests in affected regions (us-west and eu-west). A subset of streaming sessions to /v1/streams intermittently returned truncated JSON or `<STREAM-END>` markers early.
- User impact: Affected applications may have seen malformed structured outputs (function-calls, JSON blobs) and occasional outdated responses when prefix caching hit a stale entry. No customer data was exfiltrated.
- Current status: Service restored to normal for all customers; we are in follow-up mode and rolling out permanent fixes in the next 48 hours.

Scope and who was affected
- Affected service(s): hosted-generation streaming endpoint, streaming websocket proxy, and prefix/KV cache for small response footprints.
- Geography: Primary impact in us-west and eu-west clusters that were serving traffic through a recently changed autoscaling policy.
- Customer types: Both free-tier and paid tenants experienced transient issues; dedicated customers with pinned dedicated pools were largely unaffected.

Short timeline (UTC)
- 2029-08-29 21:17: First alert - elevated error rate on streaming sessions and increased JSON parse errors reported by monitoring and early customer reports.
- 21:22: Routing engineers initiated targeted throttles and routed new streaming requests to an unaffected fallback pool.
- 21:35: Initial mitigation deployed - disabled the new cache invalidation path (hotfix) and reverted the autoscaler change for the affected host pool. Error rate dropped by ~70%.
- 23:05: Partial rollback of a recent kernel selection optimization in the runtime to remove an off-by-one tokenization edge case observed in quantized attention kernels. This improved structured output integrity.
- 00:40: Additional cache sweep and safe eviction applied; stale-prefix rate dropped to near zero.
- 03:12: All streaming backpressure and truncation indicators returned to baseline.
- 03:42: Incident declared mitigated.

Root cause (high-level customer-friendly explanation)
- Two simultaneous factors combined to produce customer-visible failures: 1) a cache invalidation race introduced by a recent autoscaler/invalidation interaction that left a small subset of prefix entries stale; and 2) a tokenization off-by-one interaction with a new quantized attention kernel that in rare cases truncated the final tokens of streaming structured outputs (function-calls/JSON). When both conditions occurred on the same request path, customers saw malformed or stale outputs.

What we did to restore service
- Reverted the autoscaler policy change that caused the invalidation race and applied a controlled eviction procedure for the prefix cache to remove stale entries safely.
- Reverted the kernel selection optimization for quantized attention to avoid the off-by-one tokenization edge case.
- Temporarily routed new streaming requests to a fallback pool and engaged additional request-level validation that rejects truncated structured outputs server-side (fail-open to a safe non-structured response if validation fails).
- Added monitoring and alerts keyed to structured-output-validation failures and stale-prefix hits.

Customer-facing mitigations and recommended self-serve actions
- If you observed truncated JSON/function-call outputs: retry the request or re-run the affected job. The server-side validation and routing changes will prevent new truncated responses from reaching customers.
- If you have custom client-side parsers that fail on partial streams, add a short reassembly/retry loop to re-request the final chunk up to 3 times (recommended).
- For customers using prefix caching in their own clients: consider clearing any local long-lived caches or increasing your cache TTL temporarily until our permanent fix is live.
- Contact your assigned TAM or support@redwood.ai with logs (request IDs or timestamps) if you need us to retroactively re-run inference or re-evaluate a specific request.

Compensation and SLA information
- For paid accounts with measurable SLO impact during the incident window, our CS team will auto-evaluate SLO credit eligibility and reach out to account owners. You can also open a ticket with account details for expedited review.

Follow-up work (what we’re doing next)
- Code: deploy a robust invalidation protocol and a safer kernel-selection guard to eliminate the off-by-one edge case (ETA: 48 hours with staged rollout).
- Tests: add synthetic streaming test harnesses that validate structured-output integrity across quantization modes, and add chaos tests around autoscaler+cache interactions (ETA: 1 week).
- Monitoring: instrument per-tenant structured-output failure metrics and a short-lived audit log for cache eviction anomalies (ETA: 72 hours).
- Process: update release checklist to include autoscaler/cache interaction sign-off for future changes.

Points of contact
- Primary CS contact: Priya Natarajan (priya@redwood.ai).
- Engineering liaison for technical follow-up: Ethan Cole (ethan@redwood.ai).
- If you prefer enterprise/exec escalation: María González (maria@redwood.ai).

FAQ (anticipated customer questions)
Q: Was any customer data exposed?
A: No evidence of data exfiltration. The issue produced stale or truncated outputs only; we did not observe any cross-tenant data leakage.

Q: Are dedicated/reserved customers affected?
A: Most Dedicated pools were not impacted because they do not use the same autoscaler/cache invalidation path. If you saw an issue and are on a Dedicated plan, please contact your TAM for a tenant-specific review.

Q: Should I re-run failed jobs?
A: Yes — re-running will typically return correct results. If you need us to re-run on your behalf, provide request IDs and timestamps to support.

Draft status and next publication steps
- Current doc status: draft. Suggested next actions: 1) validate wording with legal for the compensation paragraph, 2) prepare a short status page entry (three bullet summary + link to full post-incident note), 3) schedule an outbound email to impacted account owners with tailored remediation offers.

Notes to internal stakeholders
- Tagging: add tag INC-2698 and alert the dedicated-accounts CS queue for follow-up.
- Slack: we will publish an update to #customer-notices with a short blurb and link to the full note once legal approves.

End of doc.

source.title: Client wishboard: scrapbook & action ledger (Sep 2028)
source.content:
Purpose:
Quick, messy synthesis of field feedback from Aug–Sep 2028 cadence calls, customer interviews, and in-product reports. Goal: surface low-friction patches we can prototype in Q4 + a compact backlog of medium-impact asks to validate with product. Not an exhaustive list — this is a working ledger (notes to self).

Executive snapshot (2 lines):
- High-frequency asks cluster around three usable pain points: (1) unpredictable tail latency for interactive chat at >1k tokens, (2) inability to pin model variant fallbacks per route, and (3) embedding freshness / stale-updates for streaming pipelines.
- Several customers ask for small UX/observability changes that materially reduce ops load (token-level cost attribution on error traces; per-request trace links in alert emails). Those are quick wins.

Signal clusters (raw -> theme):
1) Tail latency & KV-cache miss behavior
   - Source notes: three customers (two enterprise fintech, one health SaaS) reported sessions where cold-key-value-cache requests spiked 3–5x latency. Tickets reference unpredictable spikes when sequence lengths jump during long-context chat.
   - Representative quote: "Works great 95% of time, but that one message that pulls 8k tokens blows the P99 and triggers our circuit breaker."
   - Customer impact: session timeouts, degraded UX, manual throttling by customer engineers -> revenue risk for high-SLA accounts.
   - Rough ask: per-customer cache priming hooks + better telemetry to indicate cache hit/miss rate per keyset.

2) Routing & controlled downgrade policies per-route
   - Source notes: multiple field calls—customers want to pin a primary hosted model but automatically fallback to a cheaper quantized variant for non-essential conversational turns. Right now rollouts are global or per-account.
   - Quote: "We need to say: 'for billing-reasonable queries, use quantized-xx; for priority queries use full-precision', at the route level."
   - Impact: cost savings + predictable behavior for high-volume non-critical endpoints.
   - Potential work: add a per-route policy object with tiered routing and explicit fallback mapping.

3) Embedding freshness + streaming index updates
   - Source notes: search / retrieval workloads where streaming ingestion outpaces the embedding refresh window. Customers see stale search results for 30–90 seconds. Some have built workarounds (temporary local caches) but prefer platform-level guarantees.
   - Ask: lightweight hook to force re-embed a doc or trigger incremental near-real-time embed flush.

4) Observability & incident triage ergonomics (low-hanging)
   - Requests: "include requestId link in alert emails", "show which quantization profile served a response", "token-level cost on the trace view", "explainable rerank scoring breakdown in rerank traces".
   - These are high-impact, low-effort product moves that reduce support tickets and mean-time-to-resolution.

Representative customer vignettes (short):
- Fintech A (large reserved capacity): experienced P99 blowouts during billing month-end reporting. They turned on manual rate-limits and flagged for potential churn if we don’t provide per-route fallback policies.
- Health startup B (hosted API): worried about embeddings not reflecting recent claims ingestion -> mitigations are brittle and ops-heavy.
- Platform marketplace C (mid-market): wants more transparent cost attribution to route-level teams to do internal chargebacks; asks for CSV export of per-route token spend and per-request traces.

Proposed quick probes (2–6 week spikes):
- Probe Q1: "Trace Link in Alerts" (owner: Lina). Ship email templates that include requestId and a one-click link to the trace. Surface the most recent 10 traces for a requestId in the console. Effort: small UI + template change + alert hook. Outcome: expected 20–30% drop in 'cannot repro' support threads.
- Probe Q2: "Per-route fallback policy: beta" (owner: Marco). MVP: add a per-route flag that maps fallbacks to named variants (e.g., gptx-2 -> gptx-2-quant). Backend checks compatibility. Effort: medium backend change + rollout UI. Success metric: 10 paying customers enabling policy within 8 weeks.
- Probe Q3: "Embedding flush API" (owner: Jordan). Expose /v1/embeds/flush?doc_id= and a soft SLA (best-effort <2s for in-queue small docs). Effort: small infra + docs. Expected: reduce customer side-caching hacks.

Longer runway items (Q4–Q1 planning):
- Cache priming hooks + server-side warmup strategies (needs infra design; may couple with Dedicated autoscale signals).
- Per-request quantization metadata in traces (requires instrumentation across serving runtime).
- Fine-grain cost attribution exports (work with billing infra; medium priority).

Open questions & risks (things to resolve before committing):
- Compatibility matrix: which models can safely map to which quantized fallbacks? Need research / model team input. (Linked: github:repo/model-onboarding/issues/213).
- SLA promises for embedding flush API — if we expose, we must be explicit. We can start best-effort.
- Security considerations for requestId trace links in emails (leak risk if email forwarded). Need short-lived tokens + audit trail.

Misc field notes (raw snippets):
- "If we had even a per-route token cap and routing hint we'd avoid 70% of our manual throttles" — CS lead (mid-market).
- "We tried to fudge with caching at our side but that adds operational complexity; prefer platform to surface 'freshness' metadata" — customer engineer.
- "Trace emails would save our on-call from being woken at 3am for 'can't repro'" — support manager.

Metrics and heuristics observed (from quick log scans):
- 8% of requests that exceed 1s latency are associated with sudden 3–7x increases in sequence length; often correlated with cold sessions after >24h inactivity.
- KV-cache hit rate varies wildly by tenant: median 72%, mean 63% with long tail.

Suggested next steps (short):
1) Sync with Product-Hosted-API on per-route policy feasibility (cc: product-hosted-api@redwood).
2) Quick mock for the alert-email trace link + security review (Lina owns).
3) Run two-week infra spike on lightweight embedding flush API (Jordan + infra).
4) Schedule a 1-hour field-review with the three named customers to validate priority (Priya to own outreach).

Notes to self: keep this doc intentionally rough. If a quick probe shows promise, we’ll copy an entry to the canonical CS proposal template and update Confluence ID above.

Appendix: raw links + references (working):
- Confluence sketch: CS-WISHLIST-4521 (internal draft)
- Jira: CSUP-9843 (customer reports - tail-latency)
- GitHub spike: repo/redwood-cs-probes/pulls/18 (email trace link POC)
- Slack thread: #cs-field-notes-thread-attach-872 (exchange with Fintech A)

End of ledger entry (updated 2028-11-20).

source.title: pingcurve-annotated-lab-notes-microprofile
source.content:
Context:
This is a working lab note for a small-form benchmark we started to characterize microprofile latency-cost behavior under 'pingcurve' shaped request patterns. The aim is to produce a short collateral-style snippet (text + ascii charts) we can paste into Console tooltips and the team deck. NOT a polished report — contains raw numbers, hypotheses, and TODOs.

Motivation:
- Many of our SLO conversations use steady-state throughput numbers; real clients have microbursts and interleaved low-rate background traffic.
- We want a compact artifact illustrating how p50/p95/p99 and amortized cost per token move across three dimensions: request cadence (steady vs pingcurve), model variant (fp16 vs int4-quant), and batching strategy (smallbatch vs multiplex).

Definition: 'pingcurve' workload
- A synthetic waveform: base idle rate 0.2 qps, then a 1s 'ping' spike to 25 qps, then decay back to idle over 4s (exponential-like). Repeat every 10s.
- Each request = chat turn, avg tokens generated = 40 (prompt+response), sequence length distribution skewed to short tail (80% <= 32 tokens).

Methodology:
- Node: Ubuntu 22.04, single-worker runtime container pinned to A100-80GB equivalent.
- Off-path network noise minimized (isolated lab subnet).
- Run variants: baseline_fp16_smallbatch, baseline_fp16_multiplex, quant_int4_smallbatch, quant_int4_multiplex, and a hybrid-prefetch mode (smallbatch + KV-prefetch).
- Duration: 60 minutes per run (warmup 5m discarded).
- Metrics collected: p50/p90/p95/p99 latency (end-to-end server observed), tokens/sec aggregated, tokens-per-dollar (simple infra unit cost USD/hour for the worker), KV cache hit rate, batch-size distribution.
- Note: cost numbers are approximations for internal sketching (not billing).

Raw aggregated numbers (median across 4 runs each):
- baseline_fp16_smallbatch: p50=34ms, p90=110ms, p95=160ms, p99=420ms, tokens/s=760, cost_per_1k_tokens=$0.012, avg_batch=3.9, kv_hitrate=0.06
- baseline_fp16_multiplex: p50=45ms, p90=135ms, p95=210ms, p99=580ms, tokens/s=1200, cost_per_1k_tokens=$0.0105, avg_batch=1.6, kv_hitrate=0.03
- quant_int4_smallbatch: p50=52ms, p90=145ms, p95=220ms, p99=600ms, tokens/s=880, cost_per_1k_tokens=$0.0078, avg_batch=3.5, kv_hitrate=0.05
- quant_int4_multiplex: p50=68ms, p90=190ms, p95=320ms, p99=980ms, tokens/s=1380, cost_per_1k_tokens=$0.0062, avg_batch=1.4, kv_hitrate=0.02
- hybrid_prefetch_smallbatch: p50=36ms, p90=105ms, p95=150ms, p99=380ms, tokens/s=820, cost_per_1k_tokens=$0.011, avg_batch=3.8, kv_hitrate=0.28

Quick interpretation notes (rough):
- Multiplex increases throughput at the expense of percentile latency — p99 increases substantially for multiplex modes during ping spikes because requests are interleaved and head-of-line blocking emerges for long-turn requests.
- Quantization (int4) gives clear cost savings per token but increases single-request latency baseline (kernel overhead & dequantization). Under smallbatch, int4 trades a small p50 increase for ~35% cost reduction.
- KV-prefetch shows huge wins on p99 for pingcurve because many spikes are repetition of previous context (sessiony). The kv_hitrate jump to 28% cut p99 by ~10-20% vs baseline smallbatch. Good candidate for Console tooltip: "Enable KV-prefetch: reduces tail latency on conversational microbursts where identity reuse is common."

title: pingcurve-annotated-lab-notes-microprofile
preview:
Context:
This is a working lab note for a small-form benchmark we started to characterize microprofile latency-cost behavior under 'pingcurve' shaped request patterns. The aim is to produce a short collateral-style snippet (text + ascii charts) we can paste into Console tooltips and the team deck. NOT a polished report — contains raw numbers, hypotheses, and TODOs.

Motivation:
- Many of our SLO conversations use steady-state throughput numbers; real clients have microbursts and interleaved low-rate background traffic.
- We want a compact artifact illustrating how p50/p95/p99 and amortized cost per token move across three dimensions: request cadence (steady vs pingcurve), model variant (fp16 vs int4-quant), and batching strategy (smallbatch vs multiplex).

Definition: 'pingcurve' workload
- A synthetic waveform: base idle rate 0.2 qps, then a 1s 'ping' spike to 25 qps, then decay back to idle over 4s (exponential-like). Repeat every 10s.
- Each request = chat turn, avg tokens generated = 40 (prompt+response), sequence length distribution skewed to short tail (80% <= 32 tokens).

Methodology:
- Node: Ubuntu 22.04, single-worker runtime container pinned to A100-80GB equivalent.
- Off-path network noise minimized (isolated lab subnet).
- Run variants: baseline_fp16_smallbatch, baseline_fp16_multiplex, quant_int4_smallbatch, quant_int4_multiplex, and a hybrid-prefetch mode (smallbatch + KV-prefetch).
- Duration: 60 minutes per run (warmup 5m discarded).
- Metrics collected: p50/p90/p95/p99 latency (end-to-end server observed), tokens/sec aggregated, tokens-per-dollar (simple infra unit cost USD/hour for the worker), KV cache hit rate, batch-size distribution.
- Note: cost numbers are approximations for internal sketching (not billing).

Raw aggregated numbers (median across 4 runs each):
- baseline_fp16_smallbatch: p50=34ms, p90=110ms, p95=160ms, p99=420ms, tokens/s=760, cost_per_1k_tokens=$0.012, avg_batch=3.9, kv_hitrate=0.06
- baseline_fp16_multiplex: p50=45ms, p90=135ms, p95=210ms, p99=580ms, tokens/s=1200, cost_per_1k_tokens=$0.0105, avg_batch=1.6, kv_hitrate=0.03
- quant_int4_smallbatch: p50=52ms, p90=145ms, p95=220ms, p99=600ms, tokens/s=880, cost_per_1k_tokens=$0.0078, avg_batch=3.5, kv_hitrate=0.05
- quant_int4_multiplex: p50=68ms, p90=190ms, p95=320ms, p99=980ms, tokens/s=1380, cost_per_1k_tokens=$0.0062, avg_batch=1.4, kv_hitrate=0.02
- hybrid_prefetch_smallbatch: p50=36ms, p90=105ms, p95=150ms, p99=380ms, tokens/s=820, cost_per_1k_tokens=$0.011, avg_batch=3.8, kv_hitrate=0.28

Quick interpretation notes (rough):
- Multiplex increases throughput at the expense of percentile latency — p99 increases substantially for multiplex modes during ping spikes because requests are interleaved and head-of-line blocking emerges for long-turn requests.
- Quantization (int4) gives clear cost savings per token but increases single-request latency baseline (kernel overhead & dequantization). Under smallbatch, int4 trades a small p50 increase for ~35% cost reduction.
- KV-prefetch shows huge wins on p99 for pingcurve because many spikes are repetition of previous context (sessiony). The kv_hitrate jump to 28% cut p99 by ~10-20% vs baseline smallbatch. Good candidate for Console tooltip: "Enable KV-prefetch: reduces tail latency on conversational microbursts where identity reuse is common."

ASCII microprofile charts (for slide or tooltip). Each line is p99 latency across a 10s cycle sampled every 0.5s. Values normalized to ms and simplified for readability.
- baseline_fp16_smallbatch p99 series (sample):
  30  32  35  40  75  120  240  380  420  410  200  80  45  38  36  34  33  32  32  30
  sparkline: ▂▂▂▃▅▇████▇▆▃▂▂▂▂▂▂
- baseline_fp16_multiplex p99 series:
  35  40  48  60  130  260  520  680  580  560  250  100  60  52  48  46  44  42  40  36
  sparkline: ▂▂▃▃▆▇█████▇▇▃▂▂▂
- quant_int4_multiplex p99 series:
  40  50  60  90  180  360  720  980  860  820  300  120  80  70  64  60  56  52  50  46
  sparkline: ▂▃▃▅▇█████████▇▃▂
- hybrid_prefetch_smallbatch p99 series:
  28  30  32  35  55  90  160  240  190  150  90  54  40  36  34  33  32  31  30  28
  sparkline: ▂▂▂▂▃▅▆██▆▅▃▂▂

Collateral-friendly callouts (one-liners we can drop into Console):
- "Multiplex = higher throughput, watch the p99 during microbursts; consider hybrid-prefetch for sessiony workloads."
- "Int4 quant cuts per-token cost ~35% but shifts p50 up ~15–25ms depending on batch strategy."
- "KV-prefetch yields the largest tail-latency improvement for conversational repeat patterns; consider as default for sessions with >15% repeat-token probability."

Open questions / caveats:
- We used a single worker profile. Multi-worker admission interactions (cross-host queuing) might amplify multiplex tails. Need small cluster runs (3 workers) to validate.
- Network tails outside the worker (edge/region) not considered — these add an additive tail that may mask kernel-level changes.
- Session lengths in real traffic show heavier long-tail than our synthetic model; long turns could increase effective p99 for all modes.
- Our cost model is simplistic; accounting for Dedicated reserved pricing and burst credits will change per-token economics.

Next steps / TODOs:
- [ ] Run 3x worker cluster with same pingcurve to observe cross-host p99 amplification (owner: Luca). Target date: 2028-05-30.
- [ ] Add realistic session trace replay from prod anonymized sample (owner: Janet). Target: 2028-06-06.
- [ ] Build a Console micro-visual: two-panel: tokens_per_dollar vs p99 for the 5 modes. Draft copy for tooltip. (owner: Priya).
- [ ] Confirm billing-aligned cost numbers with FinOps (link to ticket: JIRA-INF-4217).

Quick reproducibility notes:
- Command used: ./bench/run_pingcurve --profile a100-80g --waveform pingcurve.json --duration 3600 --collect-raw
- Bench repo PR: https://github.com/redwood-inference/runtime/pull/1142

Slack shorthand conclusions (for channel post):
- baseline smallbatch: best p50 & moderate p99
- multiplex: best throughput, worst tails during spike
- int4: cheapest tokens, higher latency baseline
- kv-prefetch + smallbatch: sweet spot for pingcurve-like conversational spikes

If anyone wants the raw CSVs, I pushed them to the bench s3 path (internal): s3://redwood-bench/pingcurve/2028-05-09/ — I can attach a short notebook that converts tokens/sec -> cost-per-1k using multiple pricing models.

Notes to self: turn the ascii sparklines into lightweight SVGs for Console (Janet to prototype). Keep this doc short and sticky.

END

source.title: Quant Ops Sprint: stitching notes and weekly research snapshot
source.content:
Sprint purpose and framing
------------------------
Quick capture from this week: stitching together three parallel threads — compacting KV-cache storage, onboarding the 4-bit variant for internal evals, and stabilizing microbatch behavior under burst load. Goal this sprint: get a reproducible eval pipeline that reports both MRR/EM-style quality metrics and token-level performance + p95 latency for v0 and v4 (4-bit) variants so we can make a go/no-go for a limited canary.

Attendees (sync Thu 2028-05-16, 10:00 PT)
- Priya Natarajan (lead applied ML)
- Ethan Morgan (perf infra)
- Sofia Alvarez (evals)
- Liu Wei (runtime / kv-cache)
- Ravi Patel (solutions eng)
- Optional FYI: Jenna K. (product) dropped a quick note about customer rollout constraints

High-level decisions made
- Proceed with a small canary (10% traffic slice) for the 4-bit model once the eval pipeline hits parity thresholds below.
- Use the new prefix-compact serialization for KV-cache (Liu's branch) in canary; fall back to default format on mismatch.
- Defer quant-aware beam search tuning to next sprint — too many moving pieces now.

Experiment summaries (quick takeaways)
1) KV-cache prefix-compact serialization (branch: liu/kv-compact)\- Aim: reduce peak GPU memory and network serialization overhead for long-context chat sessions.
\- Setup: synthetic replay of 1k conversations (avg length 1.2k tokens), compare baseline vs compact encoding.
\- Result (observed): 28% reduction in serialized payload size; end-to-end median latency improved ~6% under steady load; p95 improved 10–12% in microbench harness.
\- Notes: two failing replays traced to edge-case tokenization boundary when mergespan > 256 tokens — added a guard to split caches at 128.

2) 4-bit model onboarding evals (model id: redwood/gptx-mini-4bit-v1)\- Eval corpora: internal assistant prompts (5k examples), open QA (NQ subset), and internal user-safety probe set.
\- Metrics: MRR@5, exact-match on short QA, and human-specified quality score (0–3) on 400 sampled dialogs.
\- Result: MRR down 2.6% absolute vs fp16 baseline on assistant prompts; EM drop 1.2% on NQ; human quality mean drop 0.18 (on 3.0) but failure modes concentrated on numeric precision answers.
\- Tradeoffs: token cost reduction estimated 38% vs fp16 in serving cost; latency difference negligible (+4ms median).\- Action: require targeted small-scale prompt fixes and numeric-answer heuristics before canary.

3) Microbatch behavior under burst (perf infra mini-surge)\- Setup: sawtooth traffic with spikes to 4x normal QPS for 30s every 5m. Compare batching windows = {8ms, 16ms, 32ms} and max-batch-size caps.
\- Observed: 8ms window reduces p50 latency but suffers heavy p95 tail during spikes; 32ms smooths tail but worsens median. 16ms with max-batch-size=64 gave best p95 amortization while keeping median within SLO.
\- Note: TTL-based backlog trimming improved graceful degradation under memory pressure.

Quality gating thresholds (proposed for canary)
- MRR drop <= 3.5% absolute vs fp16 baseline on assistant prompts\- EM drop <= 2.0% on QA suite\- Human-quality mean delta <= 0.25\- p95 latency increase <= 20% for baseline workload

Outstanding risks / open questions
- Numeric precision failure class: need a reproducible unit test for arithmetic/numeric-answer generation. Sofia to instrument eval harness for deterministic replay of those cases.\- KV-cache split heuristic still heuristic-y; might interact poorly with very long prefix re-use patterns. Liu to add a small tracer that captures split frequency and locations.\- Fallback behavior in corner cases (cache mismatch, serialization error) needs a deterministic rollback path that doesn't double-bill customers. Ravi to coordinate with billing infra.

Action items (owners + rough ETA)
- Sofia: add numeric-answer focused tests to the eval harness; aim EOD 2028-05-18. (Jira: JIRA-QUANT-9140)
- Liu: land compact serialization guard for mergespan; add tracer for split frequency; PR by 2028-05-19. (PR: #284)
- Ethan: run 72h canary simulator with 10% traffic slice using 4-bit variant + compact kv-cache configuration; report perf and anomaly counts by 2028-05-24.
- Priya: draft canary rollout playbook (rollback, alerting thresholds, customer comms) and sync with product for constraints — draft by 2028-05-20.
- Ravi: confirm billing rollback semantics for cache serialization fallback and add spec to Ops runbook by 2028-05-22.

Notes to self / quick brainstorm fragments
- Consider a lightweight numeric-answer post-processor (regex + verifier) that triggers for short spans that look like numbers/dates — cheap guard, catches ~60% of current failures in sampled set.
- Could we opportunistically transcode very long KV segments to a compressed delta form when cold? Might buy more headroom for long-convo customers.
- Evaluate whether 4-bit quant + per-token fallback to fp16 for high-entropy token generation reduces visible errors while keeping cost savings.

Meeting follow-ups / references
- Slack thread: #quant-ops (see thread ts 1680001200 for early perf notes)\- Eval CI: https://ci.redwood.internal/quant-evals/12345\- PR: https://github.com/redwood-inference/quant-playground/pull/284

Quick status board (one-liners)
- Eval pipeline: green for baseline corpora, red for numeric-answer probe set.\- KV-cache compact: PR opened, needs guard fix.\- Canary readiness: blocked on numeric-answer test + billing fallback spec.

If you have time before EOD: skim the sample failures in the numeric probe (Sofia attached a csv in the PR), pick 3 quick heuristics and test locally. We'll surface best candidates in Monday sync.

-- end capture --

- Slack thread: #quant-ops (see thread ts 1680001200 for early perf notes)\- Eval CI: https://ci.redwood.internal/quant-evals/12345\- PR: https://github.com/redwood-inference/quant-playground/pull/284

Quick status board (one-liners)
- Eval pipeline: green for baseline corpora, red for numeric-answer probe set.\- KV-cache compact: PR opened, needs guard fix.\- Canary readiness: blocked on numeric-answer test + billing fallback spec.

If you have time before EOD: skim the sample failures in the numeric probe (Sofia attached a csv in the PR), pick 3 quick heuristics and test locally. We'll surface best candidates in Monday sync.

-- end capture --
text:
Sprint purpose and framing
------------------------
Quick capture from this week: stitching together three parallel threads — compacting KV-cache storage, onboarding the 4-bit variant for internal evals, and stabilizing microbatch behavior under burst load. Goal this sprint: get a reproducible eval pipeline that reports both MRR/EM-style quality metrics and token-level performance + p95 latency for v0 and v4 (4-bit) variants so we can make a go/no-go for a limited canary.

Attendees (sync Thu 2028-05-16, 10:00 PT)
- Priya Natarajan (lead applied ML)
- Ethan Morgan (perf infra)
- Sofia Alvarez (evals)
- Liu Wei (runtime / kv-cache)
- Ravi Patel (solutions eng)
- Optional FYI: Jenna K. (product) dropped a quick note about customer rollout constraints

High-level decisions made
- Proceed with a small canary (10% traffic slice) for the 4-bit model once the eval pipeline hits parity thresholds below.
- Use the new prefix-compact serialization for KV-cache (Liu's branch) in canary; fall back to default format on mismatch.
- Defer quant-aware beam search tuning to next sprint — too many moving pieces now.

Experiment summaries (quick takeaways)
1) KV-cache prefix-compact serialization (branch: liu/kv-compact)\- Aim: reduce peak GPU memory and network serialization overhead for long-context chat sessions.
\- Setup: synthetic replay of 1k conversations (avg length 1.2k tokens), compare baseline vs compact encoding.
\- Result (observed): 28% reduction in serialized payload size; end-to-end median latency improved ~6% under steady load; p95 improved 10–12% in microbench harness.
\- Notes: two failing replays traced to edge-case tokenization boundary when mergespan > 256 tokens — added a guard to split caches at 128.

2) 4-bit model onboarding evals (model id: redwood/gptx-mini-4bit-v1)\- Eval corpora: internal assistant prompts (5k examples), open QA (NQ subset), and internal user-safety probe set.
\- Metrics: MRR@5, exact-match on short QA, and human-specified quality score (0–3) on 400 sampled dialogs.
\- Result: MRR down 2.6% absolute vs fp16 baseline on assistant prompts; EM drop 1.2% on NQ; human quality mean drop 0.18 (on 3.0) but failure modes concentrated on numeric precision answers.
\- Tradeoffs: token cost reduction estimated 38% vs fp16 in serving cost; latency difference negligible (+4ms median).\- Action: require targeted small-scale prompt fixes and numeric-answer heuristics before canary.

3) Microbatch behavior under burst (perf infra mini-surge)\- Setup: sawtooth traffic with spikes to 4x normal QPS for 30s every 5m. Compare batching windows = {8ms, 16ms, 32ms} and max-batch-size caps.
\- Observed: 8ms window reduces p50 latency but suffers heavy p95 tail during spikes; 32ms smooths tail but worsens median. 16ms with max-batch-size=64 gave best p95 amortization while keeping median within SLO.
\- Note: TTL-based backlog trimming improved graceful degradation under memory pressure.

Quality gating thresholds (proposed for canary)
- MRR drop <= 3.5% absolute vs fp16 baseline on assistant prompts\- EM drop <= 2.0% on QA suite\- Human-quality mean delta <= 0.25\- p95 latency increase <= 20% for baseline workload

Outstanding risks / open questions
- Numeric precision failure class: need a reproducible unit test for arithmetic/numeric-answer generation. Sofia to instrument eval harness for deterministic replay of those cases.\- KV-cache split heuristic still heuristic-y; might interact poorly with very long prefix re-use patterns. Liu to add a small tracer that captures split frequency and locations.\- Fallback behavior in corner cases (cache mismatch, serialization error) needs a deterministic rollback path that doesn't double-bill customers. Ravi to coordinate with billing infra.

Action items (owners + rough ETA)
- Sofia: add numeric-answer focused tests to the eval harness; aim EOD 2028-05-18. (Jira: JIRA-QUANT-9140)
- Liu: land compact serialization guard for mergespan; add tracer for split frequency; PR by 2028-05-19. (PR: #284)
- Ethan: run 72h canary simulator with 10% traffic slice using 4-bit variant + compact kv-cache configuration; report perf and anomaly counts by 2028-05-24.
- Priya: draft canary rollout playbook (rollback, alerting thresholds, customer comms) and sync with product for constraints — draft by 2028-05-20.
- Ravi: confirm billing rollback semantics for cache serialization fallback and add spec to Ops runbook by 2028-05-22.

Notes to self / quick brainstorm fragments
- Consider a lightweight numeric-answer post-processor (regex + verifier) that triggers for short spans that look like numbers/dates — cheap guard, catches ~60% of current failures in sampled set.
- Could we opportunistically transcode very long KV segments to a compressed delta form when cold? Might buy more headroom for long-convo customers.
- Evaluate whether 4-bit quant + per-token fallback to fp16 for high-entropy token generation reduces visible errors while keeping cost savings.

Meeting follow-ups / references
- Slack thread: #quant-ops (see thread ts 1680001200 for early perf notes)\- Eval CI: https://ci.redwood.internal/quant-evals/12345\- PR: https://github.com/redwood-inference/quant-playground/pull/284

Quick status board (one-liners)
- Eval pipeline: green for baseline corpora, red for numeric-answer probe set.\- KV-cache compact: PR opened, needs guard fix.\- Canary readiness: blocked on numeric-answer test + billing fallback spec.

If you have time before EOD: skim the sample failures in the numeric probe (Sofia attached a csv in the PR), pick 3 quick heuristics and test locally. We'll surface best candidates in Monday sync.

-- end capture --

source.title: Precision cost curves: multi-region quantization & batching tradeoff (Q2 2028)
source.content:
Purpose:
This working sheet captures measured throughput, estimated cost, and quality delta for a set of candidate quantization + batching operating points across three target regions. The goal is to identify Pareto-efficient operating points that meet regional SLA latency targets while minimizing monthly GPU spend for Q2 2028 commitments.

How to read this sheet:
- Quant levels tested: FP16 (baseline), INT8 (calibrated), 4-bit (GPTQ-like), 3-bit (experimental, high degradation).
- Batching policies: fixed-batch-N, adaptive-batching (autosize by latency target), prefetched-KV (KV cache hit assumed), and background-emb (background embedding pool with relaxed latency).
- Quality delta: measured as relative eval score drop on a small internal prompt-set (range 0% = no degradation, larger negative values = worse). We provide both semantic embedding cosine drop and LLM generation regression score.
- Cost is estimated GPU-hours per 1M tokens (rounded), then converted to monthly spend for hypothetical traffic.

Summary matrix (CSV rows):
Region,GPU,GPUs_alloc,Quant,Batching_policy,Avg_tokens_s_per_GPU,Est_95p_latency_ms,Quality_delta_pct(LLM),Quality_delta_pct(emb),Cost_per_1M_tokens_USD,Avg_util_pct
us-west,g5-8x,64,FP16,fixed-16,38000,95,0.0,0.0,12.5,72
us-west,g5-8x,64,INT8,fixed-16,52000,110,-1.1,-0.6,8.9,84
us-west,g5-8x,64,4bit,adaptive-batching,74000,130,-3.8,-1.8,6.4,88
us-west,g5-8x,64,3bit,adaptive-batching,98000,185,-12.5,-4.6,4.9,93

eu-central,a100-40,48,FP16,fixed-12,33000,115,0.0,0.0,14.6,68

eu-central,a100-40,48,INT8,adaptive-batching,45500,135,-1.6,-0.9,10.2,79
eu-central,a100-40,48,4bit,adaptive-batching+KV,64500,155,-4.4,-2.1,7.1,86

ap-southeast,g5-16x,32,FP16,fixed-8,29000,140,0.0,0.0,18.8,61
ap-southeast,g5-16x,32,INT8,adaptive-batching,41000,165,-2.0,-1.2,13.5,74
ap-southeast,g5-16x,32,4bit,prefetched-KV+adaptive,59000,190,-5.9,-2.8,9.0,82

Notes on measurements and assumptions:
- Throughput per GPU measured using internal perf harness with 1k-step warmup, synthetic mix approximating production token distribution (20% long form, 60% medium, 20% short).
- Latency is 95th percentile measured for the synthetic mix under steady traffic at target utilization. Adaptive-batching experiments include a latency controller tuned to target percentile shown.
- Cost per 1M tokens computed with cloud list GPU-hour and amortized fixture: Cost_token = (GPU_hour_cost / (tokens_s_per_gpu * 3600)) * 1e6. We included a 5% overhead for orchestration/replica buffer.
- Quality deltas: benchmark is internal suite of 800 prompt pairs (mix of factual QA, summarization, and instruction tasks) for LLM regressions; embeddings measured using evaluation set of 20k pairs for cosine delta. These are small-sample estimates; see caveats below.

Interpretation & recommended operating points:
- Latency-sensitive regions (US-W, EU-C): FP16 or INT8 preferred if we must meet 95p <= 120ms. INT8 gives ~30–40% throughput uplift vs FP16 with sub-2% quality loss; operationally attractive for user-facing chat.
- Cost-first background workloads (batch-embed, rerank): 4-bit with prefetched-KV achieves best cost/token with modest embedding degradation (~2–3%). Suitable for nightly jobs and non-real-time rerank.
- Aggressive cost mode (3-bit) shows large quality regressions and 95p latencies that violate tight SLAs; consider only for experimental or extremely tolerant internal workloads.

Regional allocation example (Q2 commit proposal):
- US-West: commit 64 g5-8x; run 60% traffic on INT8 + adaptive fallback to FP16 for spikes; reserve 10% capacity for canary FP16 rollouts. Estimated monthly cost delta vs FP16-only: -18%.
- EU-Central: commit 48 a100-40; baseline FP16 for premium customers, INT8 for majority; deploy 4-bit for embedding pipelines. Estimated avoidable spend: -15% vs 100% FP16.
- APAC: smaller pool (32 g5-16x) skewed to 4-bit for lower-cost workloads; maintain cross-region routing for peak bursts.

Tradeoff charts included in the source sheet (referenced below) show convex hull of throughput vs quality drop; the steepest slope is between INT8 and 4-bit for our evaluation set — i.e., big throughput gains for small quality loss up to 4-bit, then rapidly diminishing returns.

Action items / next experiments:
- A1: Run larger A/B on production prompts (two-week) comparing INT8 vs FP16 for high-traffic chat route. Owner: Diego. Jira: INFRA-1843.
- A2: Increase KV cache CFD (cache hit variants 20/40/60%) for 4-bit experiments to model realistic prefix reuse. Owner: Hannah.
- A3: Add per-token hallucination metric to quality suite (automation + new eval harness). Owner: Omar.

Open questions / risks:
- Calibration drift: INT8 calibration currently done at model-load; drift under domain shift may increase quality delta. Need weekly sampling.
- Tail variance: adaptive-batching improves average throughput but increases tail variance under bursty short requests — watch SLA burn in first 30 days.
- Regional pricing sensitivity: APAC GPU egress/price changes could flip the cost calculus; include monthly re-run of this sheet in capacity cadence.

Appendix: quick formulas used in the sheet
- tokens_s_per_gpu_adjusted = measured_base * (1 + batching_efficiency) * (1 + kv_hit_rate * kv_speedup_factor)
- est_cost_per_1M = ((gpu_hour_price / tokens_s_per_gpu_adjusted) * 3600) * 1e6 * (1 + 0.05 orchestration overhead)

References and linked artifacts:
- JIRA-INFRA-1843 (INT8 production A/B)
- confluence:capacity-playbooks/quant-batch-standards (calibration notes, measurement harness)

Draft notes and provenance:
- This is a working draft for the Q2-2028 capacity commit discussion. Numbers are a mix of lab-measured datapoints (perf harness v4) and financial proformas. Treat quality deltas as indicative only; they must be validated in production A/B before rollout to customers.

Questions for review meeting:
- Are we comfortable using INT8 as the default for non-premium routes?
- How aggressive do we want the KV cache hit assumption to be in forecast spreadsheets (20% conservative vs 40% optimistic)?
- Which routes should be protected with FP16-only fallbacks?

source.title: Onboarding Edgecases Weekly Capture — Apr 18 2027
source.notes:
Weekly capture focused on a small set of weird/recurring edge-cases we've seen while onboarding mid-size LLM variants (13B-ish) into the serving runtime. This is not a polished runbook — more a scratchpad for follow-ups and decisions from today's sync.

Attendees: Priya, Jamal, Elena, Marco, Sasha (brief), Ops: Mina (SRE).

1) Summary of experiments (quick numbers)
- Model variants: redwood-v2-13b (fp16 baseline), redwood-v2-13b-gptq, llama2-13b-ptq, mistral-7b-instruct (for reference).
- Evals: conversational eval suite (100 prompts, mixed long-context), safety probes (50), factuality spot-check (30).
- Key metrics (baseline -> variant): GPTQ int4-ish gave -2.4% MRR on long-context QA, +18% throughput (tokens/s). PTQ gave -1.1% MRR but lower memory. Perplexity changed: fp16 12.2 -> gptq 14.0 -> ptq 12.8 (on holdout).
- Latency tail: quantized variants show 10–30ms improvement p50, but p95 inconsistent (spikes when KV cache warm-up + variable sequence lengths).

2) Edge cases observed during onboarding runs
- KV cache boundary flip: when we roll from fp16 to gptq during a canary, some sessions lost prefix-KV alignment resulting in corrupted continuations. Repro steps: 1) start session with fp16, 2) save KV with SeqLen=512, 3) switch routing to gptq variant, 4) resume -> tokens off by a prompt chunk. Hypothesis: gptq inference kernel uses chunked KV layout with different chunk alignment.
- Special-token generation regressions: Some instruction-following prompts that ended with structured JSON got truncated or emitted extra braces under int4 quant.; likely from less-stable temperature sampling post-quant.
- Safety flag regressions: small but measurable increase in unsafe-token flags on safety probes for gptq on one prompt family (policy-training mismatch?).
- Determinism drift: beam/greedy deterministic outputs drift ~1% after aggressive quantization for repeated runs with same seeds when sequence length > 1024.

3) Short-term mitigation & decisions (actionable)
- Rollback policy: Canary to keep sessions pinned to variant used at session start until we have safe KV compatibility tests. (Owner: Jamal)
- Add a KV-layout compatibility test in the onboarding CI that runs cross-variant resume traces with multiple seq lengths (256/512/1024). (Owner: Elena, due 2027-04-25)
- Create a lightweight deterministic regression harness (50 prompts focused on structured outputs) and add to nightly evals when onboarding any quantized variant. (Owner: Priya)
- Safety triage: add the failing safety probes to the safety team queue + ask model-ops for a calibration run on the dataset we used. (Owner: Marco)

4) Experiment notes / configs to reproduce
- Repro gist: /repo/model-onboard/experiments/edgecases/gptq-resume-repro.sh (PR #198) — uses 5 warm prompts, then save-kv, then switch route and resume 20 continuations.

5) Open questions / hypotheses
- Is the alignment problem a kernel-level packing change (kernel shape) or an input-padding off-by-one? Need a byte-level diff of saved KV structure across fp16 vs gptq. (Action: Jamal + Ops)
- Could tokenizer differences (bpe merges) between model builds be causing apparent KV misalignment? We pulled two builds from different conversion scripts last week. (Action: Elena to fetch conversion logs).
- Does smaller batch size reduce p95 spikes for quantized variants due to less fragmentation in the request queue? Quick ablation planned. (Owner: Sasha)

- Priya: built deterministic harness prototype; found 6 prompts where JSON output shape changed with int4; will add to regression set.
- Jamal: working on KV-compat test + kernel inspector; discovered a chunk-header mismatch in one gptq build.
- Elena: re-ran conversion with unified tokenizer pipeline, pushing new artifact to staging for tomorrow.
- Marco: opened JIRA-ML-2147 to track safety regression; flagged to safety triage.
- Mina (SRE): observed two production canaries hit cache-warm spikes; recommended increase of canary duration to 30m for quant rollouts.

7) TODOs / next steps (short list)
- Add KV-compat test to onboarding CI (Elena/Jamal) — due 4/25.
- Merge deterministic harness + nightly job (Priya) — target 4/26.
- Run heavy ablation: batch-size 1/4/8/16 across int4/ptq/fp16 and capture p95 (Sasha) — start 4/20.
- Add failing prompts to safety board + request calibration (Marco) — in progress.
- Draft a short postmortem template for any rollout that causes >1% user-facing regression (Priya + Ops) — due 5/2.

8) Notes to self / heuristics we've accumulated
- Pin sessions to the variant at start time unless explicit resume semantics are validated.
- Treat quantized variants as "probationary" for first 72h after onboarding in staging with extended monitoring.
- Structured-output prompts are brittle — add them early to the deterministic regression set.
