title: Onboarding Edgecases Weekly Capture — Apr 18 2027
preview:
Weekly capture focused on a small set of weird/recurring edge-cases we've seen while onboarding mid-size LLM variants (13B-ish) into the serving runtime. This is not a polished runbook — more a scratchpad for follow-ups and decisions from today's sync.

Attendees: Priya, Jamal, Elena, Marco, Sasha (brief), Ops: Mina (SRE).

1) Summary of experiments (quick numbers)
- Model variants: redwood-v2-13b (fp16 baseline), redwood-v2-13b-gptq, llama2-13b-ptq, mistral-7b-instruct (for reference).
- Evals: conversational eval suite (100 prompts, mixed long-context), safety probes (50), factuality spot-check (30).
- Key metrics (baseline -> variant): GPTQ int4-ish gave -2.4% MRR on long-context QA, +18% throughput (tokens/s). PTQ gave -1.1% MRR but lower memory. Perplexity changed: fp16 12.2 -> gptq 14.0 -> ptq 12.8 (on holdout).
- Latency tail: quantized variants show 10–30ms improvement p50, but p95 inconsistent (spikes when KV cache warm-up + variable sequence lengths).

2) Edge cases observed during onboarding runs
- KV cache boundary flip: when we roll from fp16 to gptq during a canary, some sessions lost prefix-KV alignment resulting in corrupted continuations. Repro steps: 1) start session with fp16, 2) save KV with SeqLen=512, 3) switch routing to gptq variant, 4) resume -> tokens off by a prompt chunk. Hypothesis: gptq inference kernel uses chunked KV layout with different chunk alignment.
- Special-token generation regressions: Some instruction-following prompts that ended with structured JSON got truncated or emitted extra braces under int4 quant.; likely from less-stable temperature sampling post-quant.
- Safety flag regressions: small but measurable increase in unsafe-token flags on safety probes for gptq on one prompt family (policy-training mismatch?).
- Determinism drift: beam/greedy deterministic outputs drift ~1% after aggressive quantization for repeated runs with same seeds when sequence length > 1024.

3) Short-term mitigation & decisions (actionable)
- Rollback policy: Canary to keep sessions pinned to variant used at session start until we have safe KV compatibility tests. (Owner: Jamal)
- Add a KV-layout compatibility test in the onboarding CI that runs cross-variant resume traces with multiple seq lengths (256/512/1024). (Owner: Elena, due 2027-04-25)
- Create a lightweight deterministic regression harness (50 prompts focused on structured outputs) and add to nightly evals when onboarding any quantized variant. (Owner: Priya)
- Safety triage: add the failing safety probes to the safety team queue + ask model-ops for a calibration run on the dataset we used. (Owner: Marco)

4) Experiment notes / configs to reproduce
- Repro gist: /repo/model-onboard/experiments/edgecases/gptq-resume-repro.sh (PR #198) — uses 5 warm prompts, then save-kv, then switch route and resume 20 continuations.
- Runtime flags used: --kv-chunk=auto (default), --continuous-batching=on, --quant-backend=gptq-fast. Try --kv-chunk=legacy in next run for comparison.
- Observed in both dedicated and hosted staging (dedicated slightly more reproducible).

5) Open questions / hypotheses
- Is the alignment problem a kernel-level packing change (kernel shape) or an input-padding off-by-one? Need a byte-level diff of saved KV structure across fp16 vs gptq. (Action: Jamal + Ops)
- Could tokenizer differences (bpe merges) between model builds be causing apparent KV misalignment? We pulled two builds from different conversion scripts last week. (Action: Elena to fetch conversion logs).
- Does smaller batch size reduce p95 spikes for quantized variants due to less fragmentation in the request queue? Quick ablation planned. (Owner: Sasha)

6) Standup snippets (this week)
- Priya: built deterministic harness prototype; found 6 prompts where JSON output shape changed with int4; will add to regression set.
- Jamal: working on KV-compat test + kernel inspector; discovered a chunk-header mismatch in one gptq build.
- Elena: re-ran conversion with unified tokenizer pipeline, pushing new artifact to staging for tomorrow.
- Marco: opened JIRA-ML-2147 to track safety regression; flagged to safety triage.
- Mina (SRE): observed two production canaries hit cache-warm spikes; recommended increase of canary duration to 30m for quant rollouts.

7) TODOs / next steps (short list)
- Add KV-compat test to onboarding CI (Elena/Jamal) — due 4/25.
- Merge deterministic harness + nightly job (Priya) — target 4/26.
- Run heavy ablation: batch-size 1/4/8/16 across int4/ptq/fp16 and capture p95 (Sasha) — start 4/20.
- Add failing prompts to safety board + request calibration (Marco) — in progress.
- Draft a short postmortem template for any rollout that causes >1% user-facing regression (Priya + Ops) — due 5/2.

8) Notes to self / heuristics we've accumulated
- Pin sessions to the variant at start time unless explicit resume semantics are validated.
- Treat quantized variants as "probationary" for first 72h after onboarding in staging with extended monitoring.
- Structured-output prompts are brittle — add them early to the deterministic regression set.

Links and quick refs: PR #198 (repro), JIRA-ML-2147 (safety), runbook: confluence://applied-ml/onboarding-playbook (section: quantization-quirks).

If anything above looks off or you have logs to add (full kernel dumps, saved-KV hex), drop them in the experiment folder or ping #applied-ml in Slack and tag Jamal/Elena.

-- Priya

(End of capture)

source.title: variant garden benchnotes — v2.4 vs v2.3 onboarding
source.content:
Summary:

Quick take: v2.4 (alpha) shows a modest overall win on our Variant Garden workload (+4.1% human-pref), but introduces a small regression on the invoice/format-preservation cohort and has a 12% higher median latency in the 2048-token regime. Recommendation below is a staged canary (5% -> 25%) with tight monitoring and a rollback trigger on >3% user-visible regression for two consecutive days.

Background / motivation:
- This was an onboarding evaluation for candidate model v2.4 (training/config changes: refactored attention block, updated tokenizer handling for emojis, mixed-precision kernel changes).
- Goal: determine whether v2.4 should be promoted from internal candidate to the hosted catalog variant replacing v2.3.
- Workload mix: Variant Garden (multi-domain promptset constructed by SE/Prod for broad coverage). Includes: chat assistant queries, code generation snippets, instruction transforms, enterprise templates (invoices, contracts), and few-shot summarization.

Evaluation setup (notes):
- Datasets: VG-100K balanced across domains; smaller focused cohorts used for targeted regressions: invoices-3k, code-smoke-2k, privacy-sensitive-1k.
- Promptsets: P0 (chat), P1 (instruction), P2 (completion/code), P3 (structured templates), P4 (regression harness with adversarial prefixes).
- Serving config: both models evaluated under identical serving runtime (dedicated test pool), fp16 quant path for v2.3, v2.4 used same path + new kernels. Batching config: dynamic batching with 50ms window.
- Metrics: human preference win-rate (crowd + internal raters), automated quality metrics (BLEU-style for code? we used CodeBLEU for code cohort), structure-fidelity (template field match %), hallucination proxy (truth-lookup mismatch rate), latency (p50/p95), memory footprint (peak GPU), and cost proxy (token compute ms).

Important methodology notes (don't skip):
- Human eval: two-phase. Phase A blind A/B on 6,000 items (crowd + staff); Phase B targeted triage on flagged failures. Raters instructed to prioritize instruction-following and factuality.
- Statistical tests: Wilson intervals for win-rate; paired t-test for latency. We require >2.5% absolute win with p<0.05 to claim superiority.
- Unknowns: small sample coverage for some enterprise templates (e.g., legal clause generation). Flagged as follow-up.

Leaderboard (top-level aggregates):
- Overall human pref (phase A): v2.4 52.05% vs v2.3 47.95% (n=6000, 95% CI ±1.3%)
- Avg latency (2048 tokens, median): v2.4 182 ms vs v2.3 163 ms
- Structure fidelity (templates): v2.4 89.1% vs v2.3 92.7%
- CodeBLEU (code cohort): v2.4 28.7 vs v2.3 26.9
- Hallucination proxy: v2.4 4.6% vs v2.3 4.1%
- Peak GPU memory: v2.4 12.6GB vs v2.3 12.2GB
- Cost proxy (tokens-per-ms): v2.4 slightly worse by ~6%

Win / Loss table (per-domain breakdown, simplified):

Domain         | v2.4 wins | v2.3 wins | ties | notes
-------------- | ---------:| --------:| ----:| -----
Chat (P0)      | 2100      | 1850     | 50   | v2.4 friendlier to follow-ups
Instruction(P1)| 900       | 720      | 30   | clearer step-by-step in v2.4
Code (P2)      | 320       | 250      | 10   | better variable naming, CodeBLEU up
Templates(P3)  | 150       | 270      | 5    | structure regressions concentrated here
Adversarial(P4) | 130      | 110      | 10   | mixed; v2.4 slightly more robust overall

Notes on the table:
- Counts are human pref decisions from Phase A (6k items split by domain).
- Templates (P3) is the outlier where v2.3 dominates — primary risk area.

Qualitative triage highlights (Phase B):
- Template failures: v2.4 more likely to omit optional fields or reformat dates (example: "InvoiceDate: 03/01/2024" -> "March 1st, 2024" causing parser failures downstream). Many of these are subtle formatting regressions not captured by blind quality metrics.
- Hallucination cases: few but notable — v2.4 occasionally invents citation-like strings for fact-check prompts. Rate small but non-zero.
- Code behavior: v2.4 produces slightly more concise solutions and fewer irrelevant comments; however a small class of edge-case off-by-one bugs were caught in unit smoke tests (patchable).
- Conversation flow: v2.4 handles multi-turn clarifications better (fewer repeated asks).

Regression signals and risk assessment:
- Severity: Templates regression is high impact for enterprise customers who pipe outputs into structured parsers. False-negative templates could break downstream ingestion (chargeback risk).
- Latency: v2.4 p95 increases were modest but visible — could push some SLAs for low-latency tiers.
- Coverage gaps: Legal clause generation and some non-English segments have sparse evaluation; more samples needed before full rollout.

Follow-ups (must do before wide rollout):
1) Quick fix pass: enforce tokenizer-normalization step for date formats and template-preserving tokenization (Owner: Carlos, target: 2027-03-29).
2) Add 5k more P3 template examples focusing on enterprise date/number formats and run focused A/B (Owner: Marta).
3) Run 72-hr canary with full observability: structure-fidelity alerts, end-to-end ingestion success rate, user-facing error-rate (Owner: Jesse).
4) Patch and unit-test code edge-cases identified in code-smoke cohort; include a regression test that runs on PRs (Owner: Anika).

Decision / recommendation:
- Promote v2.4 to a limited canary in hosted catalog with the following constraints:
  * Canary population: 5% of new requests for 48 hours, then if no critical regressions, ramp to 25% over 7 days.
  * Routes: exclude enterprise template-heavy routes (marked by route tag template-heavy) until template patch validated.
  * Monitoring: immediate daily human-sample checks (n=200) for structure-fidelity; automated alarms if structure-fidelity drop >2% absolute vs baseline for 24h.
  * Rollback triggers: any sustained (48h) user-visible regression >3% absolute on human-pref for core product flows OR structure-fidelity drop >4% absolute on templates.

Rollout playbook (high level):
- Day 0-2: Canary 5%. SRE and Applied ML on alert. Run additional template triage every 12h.
- Day 3-9: If stable, ramp to 25% with continued triage and a public changelog note for partner customers.
- Post-ramp: scheduled full rollout decision meeting with Product & CS (metrics review).

Monitoring dashboard (must-haves):
- Live human-pref sampling pipeline (daily).
- Structure-fidelity % by route and cohort (templates, invoices, contracts).
- End-to-end ingestion success rate for template consumers.
- Latency p50/p95 by token bucket and route tag.
- Error / exception traces linked to request ids.

Open questions / discussion items:
- Are we comfortable with the small hallucination uptick given improved conv/clari? trade-offs? (ask PM).
- Acceptance criteria for enterprise customers — do we require template parity before any traffic (probably yes for partners).
- Should we include an automatic template-preserve transform in the serving layer as a stop-gap? (ops feasibility).

Action items (owners & dates):
- Carlos: tokenizer-normalization patch and validation (by 2027-03-29).
- Marta: expand P3 template set and run targeted A/B (by 2027-04-02).
- Jesse: create canary runbook and observability dashboards (by 2027-03-26).
- Anika: land code edge-case unit tests and PR gating (by 2027-03-28).
- Priya (owner): convene rollout decision review (2027-04-05).

Notes to self / rough thoughts:
- Might be worth a small param-finetune on date/num representation rather than serving transforms — trade-off: model retrain cost vs quick fix complexity.
- Keep changelog copy transparent about templates being excluded from the initial canary.
- Save all triage spreadsheets into s3 bucket and tag INF-4521 for PSA.

Appendix A: sample failing template case (redacted)
- Prompt: "Fill invoice template with fields A,B,C"
- v2.3 output: "InvoiceDate: 2027-03-01; InvoiceNumber: 000123; Total: $1,234.00"
- v2.4 output: "InvoiceDate: March 1st, 2027; InvoiceNumber: 123; Total: 1,234 USD"
- Problem: date format and invoice number normalization broke downstream exact-match parser.

Appendix B: small stats dump (selected):
- Human-pref delta: +4.1% (v2.4) overall; domain spread: chat +5.7, instr +3.8, code +4.2, templates -8.6
- Latency p50 median (2048 toks): v2.3 163ms; v2.4 182ms; p-value 0.02
- Structure-fidelity: v2.3 92.7% -> v2.4 89.1%

End.

(working draft — please add more triage notes if you find other systemic patterns; flagged items should be tracked in INF-4521)

- Marta: expand P3 template set and run targeted A/B (by 2027-04-02).
- Jesse: create canary runbook and observability dashboards (by 2027-03-26).
- Anika: land code edge-case unit tests and PR gating (by 2027-03-28).
- Priya (owner): convene rollout decision review (2027-04-05).

Notes to self / rough thoughts:
- Might be worth a small param-finetune on date/num representation rather than serving transforms — trade-off: model retrain cost vs quick fix complexity.
- Keep changelog copy transparent about templates being excluded from the initial canary.
- Save all triage spreadsheets into s3 bucket and tag INF-4521 for PSA.

Appendix A: sample failing template case (redacted)
- Prompt: "Fill invoice template with fields A,B,C"
- v2.3 output: "InvoiceDate: 2027-03-01; InvoiceNumber: 000123; Total: $1,234.00"
- v2.4 output: "InvoiceDate: March 1st, 2027; InvoiceNumber: 123; Total: 1,234 USD"
- Problem: date format and invoice number normalization broke downstream exact-match parser.

Appendix B: small stats dump (selected):
- Human-pref delta: +4.1% (v2.4) overall; domain spread: chat +5.7, instr +3.8, code +4.2, templates -8.6
- Latency p50 median (2048 toks): v2.3 163ms; v2.4 182ms; p-value 0.02
- Structure-fidelity: v2.3 92.7% -> v2.4 89.1%

End.

text:
Summary:

Quick take: v2.4 (alpha) shows a modest overall win on our Variant Garden workload (+4.1% human-pref), but introduces a small regression on the invoice/format-preservation cohort and has a 12% higher median latency in the 2048-token regime. Recommendation below is a staged canary (5% -> 25%) with tight monitoring and a rollback trigger on >3% user-visible regression for two consecutive days.

Background / motivation:
- This was an onboarding evaluation for candidate model v2.4 (training/config changes: refactored attention block, updated tokenizer handling for emojis, mixed-precision kernel changes).
- Goal: determine whether v2.4 should be promoted from internal candidate to the hosted catalog variant replacing v2.3.
- Workload mix: Variant Garden (multi-domain promptset constructed by SE/Prod for broad coverage). Includes: chat assistant queries, code generation snippets, instruction transforms, enterprise templates (invoices, contracts), and few-shot summarization.

Evaluation setup (notes):
- Datasets: VG-100K balanced across domains; smaller focused cohorts used for targeted regressions: invoices-3k, code-smoke-2k, privacy-sensitive-1k.
- Promptsets: P0 (chat), P1 (instruction), P2 (completion/code), P3 (structured templates), P4 (regression harness with adversarial prefixes).
- Serving config: both models evaluated under identical serving runtime (dedicated test pool), fp16 quant path for v2.3, v2.4 used same path + new kernels. Batching config: dynamic batching with 50ms window.
- Metrics: human preference win-rate (crowd + internal raters), automated quality metrics (BLEU-style for code? we used CodeBLEU for code cohort), structure-fidelity (template field match %), hallucination proxy (truth-lookup mismatch rate), latency (p50/p95), memory footprint (peak GPU), and cost proxy (token compute ms).

Important methodology notes (don't skip):
- Human eval: two-phase. Phase A blind A/B on 6,000 items (crowd + staff); Phase B targeted triage on flagged failures. Raters instructed to prioritize instruction-following and factuality.
- Statistical tests: Wilson intervals for win-rate; paired t-test for latency. We require >2.5% absolute win with p<0.05 to claim superiority.
- Unknowns: small sample coverage for some enterprise templates (e.g., legal clause generation). Flagged as follow-up.

Leaderboard (top-level aggregates):
- Overall human pref (phase A): v2.4 52.05% vs v2.3 47.95% (n=6000, 95% CI ±1.3%)
- Avg latency (2048 tokens, median): v2.4 182 ms vs v2.3 163 ms
- Structure fidelity (templates): v2.4 89.1% vs v2.3 92.7%
- CodeBLEU (code cohort): v2.4 28.7 vs v2.3 26.9
- Hallucination proxy: v2.4 4.6% vs v2.3 4.1%
- Peak GPU memory: v2.4 12.6GB vs v2.3 12.2GB
- Cost proxy (tokens-per-ms): v2.4 slightly worse by ~6%

Win / Loss table (per-domain breakdown, simplified):

Domain         | v2.4 wins | v2.3 wins | ties | notes
-------------- | ---------:| --------:| ----:| -----
Chat (P0)      | 2100      | 1850     | 50   | v2.4 friendlier to follow-ups
Instruction(P1)| 900       | 720      | 30   | clearer step-by-step in v2.4
Code (P2)      | 320       | 250      | 10   | better variable naming, CodeBLEU up
Templates(P3)  | 150       | 270      | 5    | structure regressions concentrated here
Adversarial(P4) | 130      | 110      | 10   | mixed; v2.4 slightly more robust overall

Notes on the table:
- Counts are human pref decisions from Phase A (6k items split by domain).
- Templates (P3) is the outlier where v2.3 dominates — primary risk area.

Qualitative triage highlights (Phase B):
- Template failures: v2.4 more likely to omit optional fields or reformat dates (example: "InvoiceDate: 03/01/2024" -> "March 1st, 2024" causing parser failures downstream). Many of these are subtle formatting regressions not captured by blind quality metrics.
- Hallucination cases: few but notable — v2.4 occasionally invents citation-like strings for fact-check prompts. Rate small but non-zero.
- Code behavior: v2.4 produces slightly more concise solutions and fewer irrelevant comments; however a small class of edge-case off-by-one bugs were caught in unit smoke tests (patchable).
- Conversation flow: v2.4 handles multi-turn clarifications better (fewer repeated asks).

Regression signals and risk assessment:
- Severity: Templates regression is high impact for enterprise customers who pipe outputs into structured parsers. False-negative templates could break downstream ingestion (chargeback risk).
- Latency: v2.4 p95 increases were modest but visible — could push some SLAs for low-latency tiers.
- Coverage gaps: Legal clause generation and some non-English segments have sparse evaluation; more samples needed before full rollout.

Follow-ups (must do before wide rollout):
1) Quick fix pass: enforce tokenizer-normalization step for date formats and template-preserving tokenization (Owner: Carlos, target: 2027-03-29).
2) Add 5k more P3 template examples focusing on enterprise date/number formats and run focused A/B (Owner: Marta).
3) Run 72-hr canary with full observability: structure-fidelity alerts, end-to-end ingestion success rate, user-facing error-rate (Owner: Jesse).
4) Patch and unit-test code edge-cases identified in code-smoke cohort; include a regression test that runs on PRs (Owner: Anika).

Decision / recommendation:
- Promote v2.4 to a limited canary in hosted catalog with the following constraints:
  * Canary population: 5% of new requests for 48 hours, then if no critical regressions, ramp to 25% over 7 days.
  * Routes: exclude enterprise template-heavy routes (marked by route tag template-heavy) until template patch validated.
  * Monitoring: immediate daily human-sample checks (n=200) for structure-fidelity; automated alarms if structure-fidelity drop >2% absolute vs baseline for 24h.
  * Rollback triggers: any sustained (48h) user-visible regression >3% absolute on human-pref for core product flows OR structure-fidelity drop >4% absolute on templates.

Rollout playbook (high level):
- Day 0-2: Canary 5%. SRE and Applied ML on alert. Run additional template triage every 12h.
- Day 3-9: If stable, ramp to 25% with continued triage and a public changelog note for partner customers.
- Post-ramp: scheduled full rollout decision meeting with Product & CS (metrics review).

Monitoring dashboard (must-haves):
- Live human-pref sampling pipeline (daily).
- Structure-fidelity % by route and cohort (templates, invoices, contracts).
- End-to-end ingestion success rate for template consumers.
- Latency p50/p95 by token bucket and route tag.
- Error / exception traces linked to request ids.

Open questions / discussion items:
- Are we comfortable with the small hallucination uptick given improved conv/clari? trade-offs? (ask PM).
- Acceptance criteria for enterprise customers — do we require template parity before any traffic (probably yes for partners).
- Should we include an automatic template-preserve transform in the serving layer as a stop-gap? (ops feasibility).

Action items (owners & dates):
- Carlos: tokenizer-normalization patch and validation (by 2027-03-29).
- Marta: expand P3 template set and run targeted A/B (by 2027-04-02).
- Jesse: create canary runbook and observability dashboards (by 2027-03-26).
- Anika: land code edge-case unit tests and PR gating (by 2027-03-28).
- Priya (owner): convene rollout decision review (2027-04-05).

Notes to self / rough thoughts:
- Might be worth a small param-finetune on date/num representation rather than serving transforms — trade-off: model retrain cost vs quick fix complexity.
- Keep changelog copy transparent about templates being excluded from the initial canary.
- Save all triage spreadsheets into s3 bucket and tag INF-4521 for PSA.

Appendix A: sample failing template case (redacted)
- Prompt: "Fill invoice template with fields A,B,C"
- v2.3 output: "InvoiceDate: 2027-03-01; InvoiceNumber: 000123; Total: $1,234.00"
- v2.4 output: "InvoiceDate: March 1st, 2027; InvoiceNumber: 123; Total: 1,234 USD"
- Problem: date format and invoice number normalization broke downstream exact-match parser.

Appendix B: small stats dump (selected):
- Human-pref delta: +4.1% (v2.4) overall; domain spread: chat +5.7, instr +3.8, code +4.2, templates -8.6
- Latency p50 median (2048 toks): v2.3 163ms; v2.4 182ms; p-value 0.02
- Structure-fidelity: v2.3 92.7% -> v2.4 89.1%

End.

source.title: Tiling burst amortization playbook — latency vs cost sketches
source.content:
Purpose and scope:

This is a working playbook sketch for how platform should reason about “tiling burst amortization” — a phrasing we’re using for the pattern where short-duration high-concurrency spikes (bursts) are absorbed by dividing work into fixed-tiling slots (pre-warmed/reserved tiling) versus fully opportunistic continuous batching. The goal: give product/serving owners a compact set of tradeoffs, simple numeric heuristics, and example curves so PMs and SREs can pick a configuration with predictable SLO/cost tradeoffs.

Background / quick reminder:
- Bursty traffic (50–500 reqs/s for O(10s) windows) is common for interactive features (search, chat start).
- Two orthogonal levers we tune: slot-tiling (pre-reserving N tiling slots per pod with warm kernels) and continuous opportunistic batching (windowed gather on arrival).
- Key costs: steady-state reserved GPU minutes, amortized token compute per request, and percent of tokens served while waiting in queue (latency).

High-level heuristics (one-liners):
- If burst magnitude > 3x baseline and burst duration < 60s, prefer tiling slots sized to expected concurrency to avoid tail-percentile amplification.
- If traffic is highly variable but with long tails (sessions > 30s average), opportunistic batching yields better token efficiency; use slot-tiling only for a small canary cohort.
- Small models (<= 8B) tolerate more opportunistic batching before tail rises; large models (>= 70B) need proactive tiling to keep p95/p99 in check.

Representative microbench sketches (text charts):
1) Scenario A — short burst (30s), 4x peak, model: 13B FP16, baseline RPS=25, burst RPS=100

Latency percentiles (ms) by configuration:
- No tiling / continuous batching (window=10ms): p50=40, p95=210, p99=420
- Slot-tiling N=8 (prewarm, dedicated): p50=38, p95=95, p99=130
- Hybrid (N=3 tiling + opportunistic 10ms): p50=39, p95=110, p99=170

Cost-per-1000-requests (relative units, lower is cheaper):
- No tiling: 1.0 (baseline)
- Slot-tiling N=8: 1.35 (reserved GPU minutes up 30%)
- Hybrid: 1.12

Interpretation: tiling reduces tail latency dramatically at modest cost increase for short high-magnitude bursts. Hybrid captures much of the latency benefit while limiting reserved minutes.

2) Scenario B — sustained variable traffic (10min window), 2x peak, model: 70B quantized (4-bit), baseline RPS=60, burst RPS=120

Latency percentiles (ms):
- No tiling / continuous batching (window=20ms): p50=120, p95=260, p99=430
- Slot-tiling N=16: p50=110, p95=190, p99=290
- Opportunistic with longer window (50ms): p50=115, p95=170, p99=220

Cost-per-1000-requests (relative):
- No tiling (20ms): 1.0
- Slot-tiling N=16: 1.45
- Opportunistic 50ms: 0.92 (better amortization by larger windows; increases user-visible p50 moderately)

Interpretation: for large quantized models, increasing opport. window size can be very cost-effective if application tolerates slight p50 regressions; tiling still helps p99 but at higher reserved cost.

ASCII-style summary chart (p99 vs cost):

Configuration           | Cost (rel) | p99 (ms)  
------------------------|------------|----------
No-tiling 10ms window    | 1.00       | 420      
Hybrid N=3 + 10ms       | 1.12       | 170      
Tiling N=8              | 1.35       | 130      
Opport. 50ms window     | 0.92       | 220      
Tiling N=16 (large)     | 1.45       | 290      

Design considerations and risk notes:
- Cold kernel start penalty: tiling reduces the incidence of cold kernel start events but requires warm kernel memory footprint; on GPUs with limited memory, large tiling counts may crowd other tenants.
- Queue amplification: opportunistic long windows increase queueing; ensure per-tenant request budgets to avoid head-of-line blocking.
- Quantization sensitivity: lower-bit quant reduces token compute but can increase per-token latency variance; this makes p99 worse under opportunistic batching in some models.
- Observability: we need per-configuration telemetry (reserved-minutes, kernel-warm-count, queue-time histogram) instrumented and surfaced in Console to make tradeoffs visible.

Short checklist for product owners (operational recipe):
- Step 0: determine SLO target (p95 or p99) and acceptable cost uplift percent.
- Step 1: measure baseline RPS distribution for 24h; compute median burst factor and typical burst duration.
- Step 2: pick initial config:
   - burst factor >= 3 and duration <= 60s -> start with tiling N ~ expected concurrency, test with canary 5% traffic
   - sustained 2x bursts or long tails -> increase opportunistic window (20->50ms) and measure p50 impact
- Step 3: run 24–72h A/B capturing p50/p95/p99, reserved-minutes delta, and token throughput.
- Step 4: if p99 still > SLO, add small tiling pool (N=2–4) and re-run.

Short example config snippets (pseudo):
- small-chat-service: tiling: {enabled: true, slots: 6, prewarm: true}, batching: {window_ms:10}  # start config for 13B
- docs-summarizer: tiling: {enabled: false}, batching: {window_ms:40}  # cost-first for 70B quantized

Open questions / experiments to run (priority ordered):
1) Systematic sweep of tiling slot size vs memory pressure across p100 vs h100 variants (JIRA PLAT-8423).
2) Benchmark quantization-bitwidth vs p99 under long-window batching for models we serve (13B, 34B, 70B).
3) Implement low-overhead per-request kernel-tagging to measure cold-kernel incidence in prod canaries.

Next steps (owner responsibilities):
- Platform eng: implement dashboard widgets (queue-time histogram, reserved-minutes delta) — owner: Kenji — due 2027-03-10
- Applied ML: run quant-bit p99 sweep for prioritized models — owner: Marisol — due 2027-03-14
- Product/PM: trial hybrid config for chat-start on prod-traffic canary (5%) — owner: Ethan — due 2027-03-07

Notes to self / rough rough ideas (brain dump):
- Could we design a “micro-tiling” mode where tiny 1/2-slot reservations are shared across tenants with preemption? Hard but may reduce reserved cost for low-util customers.
- Consider user-visible metrics in Console: show “burst resilience score” with expected p99 at 3x, 5x bursts.

If you’re reading this and want more concrete charts, I can attach the raw CSVs and a quick notebook that generates the ASCII curves. For now this is a text-first playbook that teams can iterate against.

- Could we design a “micro-tiling” mode where tiny 1/2-slot reservations are shared across tenants with preemption? Hard but may reduce reserved cost for low-util customers.
- Consider user-visible metrics in Console: show “burst resilience score” with expected p99 at 3x, 5x bursts.

If you’re reading this and want more concrete charts, I can attach the raw CSVs and a quick notebook that generates the ASCII curves. For now this is a text-first playbook that teams can iterate against.

source.title: Ask Garden: Backyard Briefing Q1 2027
source.body:
Purpose:
This is a compact, lightly curated synthesis of customer threads we incubated during the last six weeks (Dec 2026 - Jan 2027). Goal is to surface the smallest set of low-to-medium effort probes that could materially reduce customer pain or unblock higher-priority Dedicated/Private deals. This is intentionally bootstrappy: notes, raw quotes, and quick triage rather than polished recommendations.

Snapshot (what we looked at):
- 48 support tickets flagged by CS as latency/consistency-related between 2026-12-01 and 2027-01-10.
- 7 partner-run performance audits (2 hosted API, 5 Dedicated).
- 12 CS call transcripts and three on-site research calls.
- Early telemetry: a non-trivial cohort (approx 12 customers) with repeated short-seq heavy-write patterns (chat systems, code assistants).

Quick summary (topline):
- Customers are seeing two consistent friction families: 1) tail latency spikes under mixed small/long sequences (often after KV cache misses), and 2) unpredictable billing spikes tied to retry/timeout behavior.
- Many customers know about batching/quantization knobs but want safer, opinionated defaults and easy rollback.
- There's appetite for a focused ‘runtime stabilization’ offering (small, config-driven changes + clear observability) that could be sold as a Professional Services + config bundle for Dedicated customers.

Representative customer quotes (verbatim snippets):
- "We get weird 10-20s tails randomly during peak — sometimes an hour goes fine then boom, everything stutters." — large SaaS search team.
- "Our infra team is okay to tune things, but we want a recommended safe profile we can enable without fear." — fintech engineering lead.
- "Billing jumped last week, looks like retries + long completions. Hard to tell whether to blame prompt or runtime." — product manager, conversational agent.

Key themes and tangible asks (ranked by frequency + impact):
1) Tail-latency & cache-friction (high frequency, high impact):
   - Ask: Improve KV cache hit diagnostics (expose simple per-customer hit/miss rate and a sample of evicted keys).
   - Why: Customers cannot tell whether latency is prompt-induced or runtime cache thrash.
   - Suggested probe: Add a 7-day per-tenant cache hit/miss metric in Console + one-click export of 50 example keys that missed (scrubbed).
   - Rough effort: Eng infra spike (2-3 wks) + Console UI card.

2) Retry & billing visibility (medium frequency, high customer anxiety):
   - Ask: Break down chargeable token usage by retry/timeout vs successful completions in billing UI.
   - Why: Teams are seeing surprising charges and escalate to finance.
   - Probe: Add billing flagging for sessions with >3 retries in 24h and automated alert to tenant admins.
   - Rough effort: Billing + backend tagging (1-2 sprints).

3) Safe default profiles & rollback (high frequency, medium impact):
   - Ask: Provide several vetted runtime profiles (low-latency, cost-saving, conservative) with an immediate rollback toggle.
   - Why: Many customers are comfortable with recommendations but fear being locked into a suboptimal config.
   - Probe: Ship 3 profiles in Dedicated console behind a feature flag for early adopters. Include A/B telemetry.
   - Rough effort: Product + Console + docs (2-4 wks).

4) Lightweight canary/gradual rollout for models & quantized variants (low frequency, medium impact):
   - Ask: Make a simple percentage-based rollout mechanism in Console for model variants with automatic fallback to previous variant on error rate spike.
   - Why: Prevents whole-tenant regressions and gives confidence to try cheaper quantized models.
   - Probe: Canary gating that checks 95th latency and total error rate; auto-fallback policy.
   - Rough effort: Serving-runtime + Console + policy glue (multi-team; ~1.5 months).

5) Prompt vs runtime differentiation playbook (internal docs need):
   - Ask: Customer-facing checklist and playbook that CS/SE can send customers to triage whether slow completions are prompt- or infra-driven.
   - Why: Reduces basic churn and repetitive support cycles.
   - Probe: Draft one-pager + triage flow we can A/B test in three accounts.
   - Rough effort: Low (2-3 days).

Prioritized micro-probes (minimum viable experiments):
- Probe A (Fast wins): Add a "Cache Health" card in Console for top 10 tenants by traffic. Show hit/miss, eviction rate, recent TTLs, and two suggested actions (increase max-kv, enable prefix caching). Owner: Liam O'Neill. Timebox: 2 weeks.
- Probe B (Billing clarity): Add retry-tagging in billing export CSV with a sample dashboard showing sessions with >2 retries. Owner: Sofia Martinez. Timebox: 3 weeks.
- Probe C (Profiles alpha): Expose a single recommended profile ("latency safe") with a rollback button; ship to 5 pilot tenants. Owner: Daniel Kim. Timebox: 4 weeks.

Risks, unknowns, and tradeoffs:
- Privacy: Exporting sample keys must be scrubbed — need legal signoff for any key/content exports.
- Cost telemetry fidelity: Tagging retries vs successful completions introduces potential accounting drift; finance/ops must validate.
- Engineering bandwidth: Runtime work competes with scheduled model-variant rollout features; both need serving-runtime attention.
- False positives: Auto-fallback policies must avoid flip-flopping on normal variance; need hysteresis and cooldown windows.

Suggested near-term communications:
- Share this note with eng-serving-runtime on Wed 2027-02-09 (sync request: 30min).
- Circulate a short CS one-pager for pilots with three tenants (ask for permission to enable profiles).
- Open two JIRA spikes: JIRA-CR-901 (Cache health metrics) and JIRA-CR-902 (Billing retry tagging).

Open questions for follow-up:
- Can serving runtime expose lightweight per-tenant eviction samples without heavy perf cost? (ENG answer required)
- Is finance comfortable accepting a billing delta tolerance of up to 0.5% while we roll out retry-tagging?
- Are there privacy/contract constraints for exporting any per-request keys/samples for customers in EU regulated verticals?

Next steps (week-by-week plan):
- Week 1: Kick PoC for Cache Health card (one backend metric + Console stub). Reach out to 5 pilot tenants.
- Week 2-3: Billing tagging PoC + sample CSV export for CS review.
- Week 3-4: Ship profiles alpha behind flag to pilot tenants; collect telemetry.
- Week 5: Consolidate learnings and decide go/no-go for broader rollouts.

Owners and contact points:
- CS lead / doc owner: Priya Natarajan (priya@redwood.ai)
- Metric/Console owner: Liam O'Neill (liam@redwood.ai)
- Billing owner: Sofia Martinez (sofia@redwood.ai)
- Runtime/Product liaison: Daniel Kim (daniel@redwood.ai)

Notes to self / loose scraps:
- When drafting Cache Health, consider a lightweight sampling approach: only sample on observed latency > X ms and limit to N keys per hour.
- Short-term messaging: "Enable latency-safe profile for 24h trial; rollback instantly if 95th latency rises" — simple language for SEs.
- Keep list of pilot tenants outside product repo to avoid auto-enablement mistakes.

End of briefing.

(If this doc is useful, I will convert to a short CS playbook with checklists and an email template for pilot outreach. For now this remains a working note.)

source.title: Keepalive suppression and NAT reflexive race — investigation notes
source.content:
Summary:

This is a working investigation into a recurring pattern we observed in three customer Private deployments (region-eu1, region-us-west2, customer-beta-cluster) where control-plane TCP sessions from control-plane proxies to customer firewalls/edge boxes were silently dropping after ~90s of idle time despite keepalives configured. Symptoms were asymmetric: the customer-side reported active sessions and responded to probes, but our control-plane reported connection resets/timeouts followed by immediate re-establishment attempts (which sometimes failed due to NAT translation race).

Why this doc exists (notes to self):
- capture timeline + packet-level observations in one place
- draft mitigations and runbook flips for oncall
- propose telemetry additions so next oncall can root faster

High-level hypothesis (short):
NAT device or inline firewall is doing aggressive connection pruning when it sees no midstream L4 keepalive frames; our proxy keepalives are being suppressed/aggregated by middleboxes (coalesced or blocked), creating a window where NAT binding expiration happens concurrently with our re-establish attempts. The re-establish hits a different ephemeral port / different 5-tuple and the customer firewall's state machine rejects it, causing an apparent outage until the NAT reflexive mapping stabilizes.

Incident timeline (UTC, best-effort correlated across logs and tcpdump):
- 2026-12-03 02:11:02  — Control-plane proxy (cp-proxy-17) last forwarded application packet to customer edge (10.3.12.1:443 -> 198.51.100.22:51023).
- 2026-12-03 02:12:35  — Customer-side firewall logged conntrack prune for tuple 198.51.100.22:51023 -> 10.3.12.1:443 (reason: idle-timeout).
- 2026-12-03 02:12:36  — Our proxy logged TCP RST to application; control-plane attempted connection re-open (SYN) from ephemeral port 61012.
- 2026-12-03 02:12:36.120 — tcpdump at customer-edge: saw SYN from 198.51.100.22:61012 but rejected (policy drop) because DNAT mapping not present; later an ICMP port-unreachable seen in one capture (inconsistent across captures).
- 2026-12-03 02:12:37.900 — Control-plane retried, used same ephemeral port; customer-edge accepted on second attempt (mapping refreshed). Service recovered ~1.8s after first SYN.

Packet excerpt (sanitized):
- Control-plane side (tcpdump): last keepalive from cp-proxy: tcp options TS val 123456 ecr 654321 + no payload.
- Customer-side (fw syslog): "conntrack: delete conn 198.51.100.22:51023 -> 10.3.12.1:443 idle_timeout=90s"

Observed patterns across 3 customers: 
- The idle timeout value varies (60–120s) depending on vendor/firmware.
- Middlebox aggregates tiny keepalives into larger bursts (we see our 30s keepalives only intermittently at customer capture points).
- When aggregation happens, NAT binding expiry is aligned with a control-plane SYN which uses a different ephemeral port (race).
- Some customer firewalls implement a "stealth-mode" that silently drops first SYN until an application-level trigger occurs (makes debugging hard).

Root cause analysis (RCA):
Primary cause: mismatch between our keepalive timing and customer-side NAT/firewall conntrack behavior + NAT mapping reliance on exact 5-tuple for acceptance. Secondary cause: lack of observable signals in our telemetry to detect suppressed keepalive at middleboxes; we thought keepalive was reaching endpoint, but it was being coalesced/blocked.

Contributing systemic issues:
- Control-plane proxies default keepalive interval set to 30s; some customer middleboxes remove or coalesce these, pushing effective interval > configured idle timers.
- No active probing path that verifies mapping stability across NAT (we trusted TCP handshakes only when an application send occurred).
- Oncall playbook lacked steps to quickly test NAT mapping behavior (we relied on packet captures which require customer cooperation).

Immediate mitigations applied during incident: 
- Added short-term aggressive TCP retransmit backoff tuning on cp-proxy (reduce syn retry interval, increase retry count briefly) — helped in two clusters where SYN retried before mapping aged out.
- For customers where we had orchestration access, pushed a 15s UDP keepalive across control-plane tunnel (to maintain NAT binding independent of TCP). This worked but requires customer consent.
- Temporary suggestion to customers: increase conntrack idle threshold to >=300s for control-plane peers (not always possible).

Permanent mitigations proposed (priority ordered):
1) Implement an active mapping-check probe on session idle: when control-plane observes N seconds of socket idle > X (e.g., 45s), send a small 0-byte TCP keepalive plus an application-layer "ping" to assert mapping — log success/failure. This should be telemetry-enabled and visible in Console.
2) Introduce optional UDP keepalive tunnel for on-prem customers behind aggressive NATs (configurable per-customer). Provide clear security guidance and config template.
3) Default keepalive policy update: expose per-tenant keepalive interval override in Dedicated/Private config; recommend 10–15s for customers running known aggressive NAT devices. Document detections that suggest lowering interval.
4) Console/obs: surface conn state churn metric and per-route "failed-first-syn" rates so we can detect races automatically and alert before user impact.
5) Run a small compatibility suite for top 6 firewall vendors: measure conntrack behavior for our default keepalive pattern and publish a vendor compatibility matrix.

Runbook additions (draft):
- Title: Control-plane keepalive suppression — triage steps
- Trigger: repeated control-plane TCP resets between cp-proxy and customer edge > 3 within 10m for same prefix
- Triage quick checks:
  * Check cp-proxy tcp_keepalive logs: grep "keepalive" /var/log/cp-proxy.log around incident time
  * Check control-plane socket stats: netstat -antp | grep <peer-ip> and ss -o state established to confirm probe frames
  * Request short tcpdump at customer-edge focused on 5-tuple and keepalive options (if customer cooperating)
  * If tcpdumps unavailable: ask customer to run conntrack -L | grep <our-ip> and report idle_timer values
- Immediate mitigations in-play (if safe): 
  * Toggle proxy aggressive-syn-retry temporarily: set cp-proxy /etc/cp-proxy/conf.d/keepalive: syn_retry_interval=200ms syn_retry_count=8 
  * If customer allows, enable UDP keepalive nat-pinger on the control-plane tunnel: cpctl enable-nat-pinger --dst <edge-ip> --interval 15s
- Escalation: if problem persists >15m and impacts >5% of control-plane sessions, escalate to eng-sre and reach out to customer infra with suggested conntrack tuning or firmware upgrade paths.

Telemetry & observability changes (what to add):
- Add metric: controlplane.tcp.first_syn_failed_count (labels: customer, region, fw_vendor)
- Add gauge: tcp.keepalive.frames_seen (by cp-proxy egress) vs frames_acked (by remote ack) to detect suppression gaps
- Add a synthetic probe job: for each Private customer, once per hour do a mapping-check that performs SYN+app-ping and records first-syn-accept latency.

Testing notes (how we validated mitigations):
- Reproduced on lab fw-A (vendor A) by setting conntrack idle to 90s and enabling aggregation. We saw identical pattern; enabling UDP nat-pinger eliminated the race.
- Reproduced with small TCP proxy shim that suppressed keepalive packets (simulated aggregation) and confirmed mapping expiration then SYN rejection.

Action items (owner, ETA):
- Priya: prototype mapping-check probe in cp-proxy (POC) — ETA 2027-01-10
- Xiaoming: add first_syn_failed_count metric and dashboard (Console) — ETA 2026-12-20
- Liam: vendor compatibility matrix doc + lab runs for top 6 firewalls — ETA 2027-02-01
- Rae: update oncall runbook page and publish quick triage checklist — ETA 2026-12-18
- Miguel: add public-facing KB article with recommended conntrack settings and UDP keepalive template (customer-facing) — ETA 2026-12-22

Open questions / follow-ups:
- Are there any regulatory or policy concerns with us proposing UDP keepalive (such as customer networks that forbid unsolicited UDP)? Add note in KB.
- Could middleboxes be doing TCP option stripping that removes our keepalive option signatures? We should add a small option-echo test to confirm.
- Should we offer an "auto-cure" where cp-proxy temporarily increases keepalive frequency when first_syn_failed_count exceeds threshold? Risk: more traffic and possible customer pushback. Discuss with product.

Notes for postmortem write-up (if we convert):
- Keep this narrative focused on the race condition and systemic visibility gaps rather than vendor finger-pointing. 
- Include reproducible lab test steps + pcap snippets (sanitized).
- Attach timeline with precise epoch timestamps pulled from cp-proxy logs (we have those, need to collate).

Quick commands collected (useful during oncall):
- cp-proxy logs: sudo journalctl -u cp-proxy -S "2026-12-03" | grep -i keepalive
- tcpdump sample on cp-proxy egress: sudo tcpdump -i eth0 host 198.51.100.22 and port 443 -w /tmp/cp-egress.pcap
- check conntrack on linux-based edge: sudo conntrack -L | grep 198.51.100.22 || echo "no conntrack entry"

Loose brainstorming (future ideas):
- Build a lightweight control-plane UDP heartbeat that uses minimal crypto (HMAC) so customers accept behavior; expose as opt-in and logged.
- When we detect first-syn-fail spike for a given customer, auto-open a small internal incident and surface suggested config changes to a customer portal with one-click instructions for known vendors.

Closing note (current state):
- This is a draft. Lab repro validated UDP nat-pinger mitigates race. Working on telemetry + runbook updates. We should schedule a short 30m sync with Solutions Eng + Customer (if affected) to propose non-invasive mitigations (conntrack tuning or opt-in UDP keepalive).

-- Priya

title: Keepalive suppression and NAT reflexive race — investigation notes
preview:
Summary:

This is a working investigation into a recurring pattern we observed in three customer Private deployments (region-eu1, region-us-west2, customer-beta-cluster) where control-plane TCP sessions from control-plane proxies to customer firewalls/edge boxes were silently dropping after ~90s of idle time despite keepalives configured. Symptoms were asymmetric: the customer-side reported active sessions and responded to probes, but our control-plane reported connection resets/timeouts followed by immediate re-establishment attempts (which sometimes failed due to NAT translation race).

Why this doc exists (notes to self):
- capture timeline + packet-level observations in one place
- draft mitigations and runbook flips for oncall
- propose telemetry additions so next oncall can root faster

High-level hypothesis (short):
NAT device or inline firewall is doing aggressive connection pruning when it sees no midstream L4 keepalive frames; our proxy keepalives are being suppressed/aggregated by middleboxes (coalesced or blocked), creating a window where NAT binding expiration happens concurrently with our re-establish attempts. The re-establish hits a different ephemeral port / different 5-tuple and the customer firewall's state machine rejects it, causing an apparent outage until the NAT reflexive mapping stabilizes.

Incident timeline (UTC, best-effort correlated across logs and tcpdump):
- 2026-12-03 02:11:02  — Control-plane proxy (cp-proxy-17) last forwarded application packet to customer edge (10.3.12.1:443 -> 198.51.100.22:51023).
- 2026-12-03 02:12:35  — Customer-side firewall logged conntrack prune for tuple 198.51.100.22:51023 -> 10.3.12.1:443 (reason: idle-timeout).
- 2026-12-03 02:12:36  — Our proxy logged TCP RST to application; control-plane attempted connection re-open (SYN) from ephemeral port 61012.
- 2026-12-03 02:12:36.120 — tcpdump at customer-edge: saw SYN from 198.51.100.22:61012 but rejected (policy drop) because DNAT mapping not present; later an ICMP port-unreachable seen in one capture (inconsistent across captures).
- 2026-12-03 02:12:37.900 — Control-plane retried, used same ephemeral port; customer-edge accepted on second attempt (mapping refreshed). Service recovered ~1.8s after first SYN.

Packet excerpt (sanitized):
- Control-plane side (tcpdump): last keepalive from cp-proxy: tcp options TS val 123456 ecr 654321 + no payload.
- Customer-side (fw syslog): \"conntrack: delete conn 198.51.100.22:51023 -> 10.3.12.1:443 idle_timeout=90s\"

Observed patterns across 3 customers: 
- The idle timeout value varies (60–120s) depending on vendor/firmware.
- Middlebox aggregates tiny keepalives into larger bursts (we see our 30s keepalives only intermittently at customer capture points).
- When aggregation happens, NAT binding expiry is aligned with a control-plane SYN which uses a different ephemeral port (race).
- Some customer firewalls implement a \"stealth-mode\" that silently drops first SYN until an application-level trigger occurs (makes debugging hard).

Root cause analysis (RCA):
Primary cause: mismatch between our keepalive timing and customer-side NAT/firewall conntrack behavior + NAT mapping reliance on exact 5-tuple for acceptance. Secondary cause: lack of observable signals in our telemetry to detect suppressed keepalive at middleboxes; we thought keepalive was reaching endpoint, but it was being coalesced/blocked.

Contributing systemic issues:
- Control-plane proxies default keepalive interval set to 30s; some customer middleboxes remove or coalesce these, pushing effective interval > configured idle timers.
- No active probing path that verifies mapping stability across NAT (we trusted TCP handshakes only when an application send occurred).
- Oncall playbook lacked steps to quickly test NAT mapping behavior (we relied on packet captures which require customer cooperation).

Immediate mitigations applied during incident: 
- Added short-term aggressive TCP retransmit backoff tuning on cp-proxy (reduce syn retry interval, increase retry count briefly) — helped in two clusters where SYN retried before mapping aged out.
- For customers where we had orchestration access, pushed a 15s UDP keepalive across control-plane tunnel (to maintain NAT binding independent of TCP). This worked but requires customer consent.
- Temporary suggestion to customers: increase conntrack idle threshold to >=300s for control-plane peers (not always possible).

Permanent mitigations proposed (priority ordered):
1) Implement an active mapping-check probe on session idle: when control-plane observes N seconds of socket idle > X (e.g., 45s), send a small 0-byte TCP keepalive plus an application-layer "ping" to assert mapping — log success/failure. This should be telemetry-enabled and visible in Console.
2) Introduce optional UDP keepalive tunnel for on-prem customers behind aggressive NATs (configurable per-customer). Provide clear security guidance and config template.
3) Default keepalive policy update: expose per-tenant keepalive interval override in Dedicated/Private config; recommend 10–15s for customers running known aggressive NAT devices. Document detections that suggest lowering interval.
4) Console/obs: surface conn state churn metric and per-route "failed-first-syn" rates so we can detect races automatically and alert before user impact.
5) Run a small compatibility suite for top 6 firewall vendors: measure conntrack behavior for our default keepalive pattern and publish a vendor compatibility matrix.

Runbook additions (draft):
- Title: Control-plane keepalive suppression — triage steps
- Trigger: repeated control-plane TCP resets between cp-proxy and customer edge > 3 within 10m for same prefix
- Triage quick checks:
  * Check cp-proxy tcp_keepalive logs: grep \"keepalive\" /var/log/cp-proxy.log around incident time
  * Check control-plane socket stats: netstat -antp | grep <peer-ip> and ss -o state established to confirm probe frames
  * Request short tcpdump at customer-edge focused on 5-tuple and keepalive options (if customer cooperating)
  * If tcpdumps unavailable: ask customer to run conntrack -L | grep <our-ip> and report idle_timer values
- Immediate mitigations in-play (if safe): 
  * Toggle proxy aggressive-syn-retry temporarily: set cp-proxy /etc/cp-proxy/conf.d/keepalive: syn_retry_interval=200ms syn_retry_count=8 
  * If customer allows, enable UDP keepalive nat-pinger on the control-plane tunnel: cpctl enable-nat-pinger --dst <edge-ip> --interval 15s
- Escalation: if problem persists >15m and impacts >5% of control-plane sessions, escalate to eng-sre and reach out to customer infra with suggested conntrack tuning or firmware upgrade paths.

Telemetry & observability changes (what to add):
- Add metric: controlplane.tcp.first_syn_failed_count (labels: customer, region, fw_vendor)
- Add gauge: tcp.keepalive.frames_seen (by cp-proxy egress) vs frames_acked (by remote ack) to detect suppression gaps
- Add a synthetic probe job: for each Private customer, once per hour do a mapping-check that performs SYN+app-ping and records first-syn-accept latency.

Testing notes (how we validated mitigations):
- Reproduced on lab fw-A (vendor A) by setting conntrack idle to 90s and enabling aggregation. We saw identical pattern; enabling UDP nat-pinger eliminated the race.
- Reproduced with small TCP proxy shim that suppressed keepalive packets (simulated aggregation) and confirmed mapping expiration then SYN rejection.

Action items (owner, ETA):
- Priya: prototype mapping-check probe in cp-proxy (POC) — ETA 2027-01-10
- Xiaoming: add first_syn_failed_count metric and dashboard (Console) — ETA 2026-12-20
- Liam: vendor compatibility matrix doc + lab runs for top 6 firewalls — ETA 2027-02-01
- Rae: update oncall runbook page and publish quick triage checklist — ETA 2026-12-18
- Miguel: add public-facing KB article with recommended conntrack settings and UDP keepalive template (customer-facing) — ETA 2026-12-22

Open questions / follow-ups:
- Are there any regulatory or policy concerns with us proposing UDP keepalive (such as customer networks that forbid unsolicited UDP)? Add note in KB.
- Could middleboxes be doing TCP option stripping that removes our keepalive option signatures? We should add a small option-echo test to confirm.
- Should we offer an \"auto-cure\" where cp-proxy temporarily increases keepalive frequency when first_syn_failed_count exceeds threshold? Risk: more traffic and possible customer pushback. Discuss with product.

Notes for postmortem write-up (if we convert):
- Keep this narrative focused on the race condition and systemic visibility gaps rather than vendor finger-pointing. 
- Include reproducible lab test steps + pcap snippets (sanitized).
- Attach timeline with precise epoch timestamps pulled from cp-proxy logs (we have those, need to collate).

Quick commands collected (useful during oncall):
- cp-proxy logs: sudo journalctl -u cp-proxy -S \"2026-12-03\" | grep -i keepalive
- tcpdump sample on cp-proxy egress: sudo tcpdump -i eth0 host 198.51.100.22 and port 443 -w /tmp/cp-egress.pcap
- check conntrack on linux-based edge: sudo conntrack -L | grep 198.51.100.22 || echo \"no conntrack entry\"

Loose brainstorming (future ideas):
- Build a lightweight control-plane UDP heartbeat that uses minimal crypto (HMAC) so customers accept behavior; expose as opt-in and logged.
- When we detect first-syn-fail spike for a given customer, auto-open a small internal incident and surface suggested config changes to a customer portal with one-click instructions for known vendors.

Closing note (current state):
- This is a draft. Lab repro validated UDP nat-pinger mitigates race. Working on telemetry + runbook updates. We should schedule a short 30m sync with Solutions Eng + Customer (if affected) to propose non-invasive mitigations (conntrack tuning or opt-in UDP keepalive).

-- Priya

source.title: Handoff checkpoint — micro-retro (Dec 2–6, 2026)
source.content:
Purpose:
Quick standalone micro-retro + handoff checkpoint for the private deployments installs we ran the past week. Goal: capture recurring operational gaps that are slowing handoffs, list immediate mitigations, and assign owners for next-week fixes. This is not a full postmortem — it focuses on handoff friction.

Attendees (sync + async comments):
- Priya Natarajan (owner)
- Ethan Cho (field engineer)
- Marco Alvarez (networking lead)
- Lina Zhang (install ops)
- Carlos Mendes (SRE oncall)
- Aisha Mbaye (solutions engineering)

High-level summary (1–2 lines):
- Two customer installs progressed to final validation; both blocked by network/CNI edge cases and cert rotation timing. One new customer (RetailCo) deferred acceptance due to unexpected firewall rules blocking health probes.

Key customer snapshots:
1) RetailCo (on-prem, dual-datacenter) — current status: validation stalled
   - What happened: HA pair brought up, control-plane pods stable, but health-checks intermittently failing from monitoring region B.
   - Primary blocker: corporate firewall silently dropping UDP probes (we were using UDP-based probe aggregator).
   - Temporary mitigation: switched probes to TCP/HTTP for health checks; confirmed stability on staging.
   - Next action: SE to coordinate with customer network team to get explicit allow rule for UDP if they want legacy probes (Aisha, due 2026-12-09).
   - Tickets: PD-3421 (network diagnostics for RetailCo) — escalated.

2) FinSys (VPC, customer-managed KMS) — current status: smoke tests passed; awaiting keys rotation window
   - What happened: during cert rotation test, node agents failed to fetch new wrapping keys due to role mapping TTL misconfiguration.
   - Primary blocker: TTL on instance role mapping (customer side) set to 1h; rotation script assumes sub-5m propagation.
   - Mitigation: performed manual refresh via scripted API call; documented the step in runbook.
   - Long-term: add explicit preflight check for Vault/KMS TTL and surface a warning in the installer UI (Lina + Marco, POC due 2026-12-13).
   - Tickets: PD-3589 (KMS rotation preflight check).

3) EdgeRetail (edge cluster, small site) — current status: complete, small post-install latency spike
   - Observation: image pull throttling from mirror in customer datacenter caused delayed container start for det-inference; recovered after mirror swap.
   - Suggestion: include mirror health probe and fallback in future edge-installer release (Carlos).

Recurring friction themes (observed across 4 installs this sprint):
- Network assumptions: we still assume symmetric rules for UDP probes and ephemeral outbound flows. Customers often have more restrictive firewall policies than documented.
- Installer visibility: the UI / CLI logs don't consistently highlight when external network ACLs block flows versus internal container issues — slows triage.
- Role/TTL mismatches: third-party KMS and identity systems have widely varying propagation windows; our scripts assume optimistic propagation.
- Knowledge transfer: new SEs onboarded this quarter lack a short checklist that ties common customer network patterns to exact remediation steps.

Immediate mitigations (short horizon, 1 week):
- Checklist drop-in: create a 1-page install checklist for SEs that covers 6 most common network firewall rules + example iptables/ACL snippets (owner: Aisha, due 2026-12-10).
- Probe fallback: change default health probe to try HTTP/TCP first then UDP (non-breaking toggle) in the next release candidate (owner: Marco, release branch: rc/edge-probes, due 2026-12-14).
- Runbook note: add explicit KMS TTL preflight step and a manual refresh command snippet (owner: Lina, due 2026-12-09).
- Installer logging: quick patch to add a tagged log line when outbound connections to well-known endpoints fail (owner: Carlos, PR draft by 2026-12-08).

Medium-term improvements (2–6 weeks):
- Installer preflight suite: extend automated preflight to test firewall behavior (bi-directional TCP health check + common UDP patterns) and return a clear recommendation.
- SE onboarding playbook: build a short workshop + checklist for common install failure modes, add to SE weekly training.
- Mirror resilience: edge-installer to include mirror health probe + automatic mirror failover for image pulls.

Action items (owners + dates):
- Aisha Mbaye: compile the 1-pager install checklist and share to /se-team/handbook (2026-12-10).
- Marco Alvarez: implement probe-fallback change and open PR against rc/edge-probes (2026-12-14).
- Lina Zhang: update runbook with KMS TTL preflight and manual-refresh instructions (2026-12-09).
- Carlos Mendes: add outbound-failure log telemetry + example troubleshooting queries to SRE dashboard (2026-12-08).
- Priya Natarajan: consolidate feedback and schedule a 30-minute sync with product to discuss adding preflight to installer backlog (2026-12-11).

Decisions made during sync:
- We will not make UDP probes the primary method by default; instead we will attempt TCP/HTTP first and leave UDP as optional for customers that require it.
- Quick wins (checklist + runbook note + logging) are prioritized for immediate delivery in the coming week.

Open questions / escalations:
- Should the installer ship an opinionated default set of firewall rules to show to customers during onboarding? Product input required (PRD ticket pending).
- For KMS integrations, do we enforce a minimum propagation window or just surface a warning? (need security/cryptography input).

Notes to SEs / field engineers (tips from this week):
- When hitting intermittent health-check failures, perform a packet capture from the cluster egress point and share a 10s pcap; this usually shows malformed probes or blocked ports.
- If cert rotation fails, attempt a manual auth refresh against the customer's KMS (documented command in runbook) before escalating.

Suggested backlog tickets (short names):
- preflight-firewall-probe
- edge-probe-fallback
- kms-ttl-preflight
- installer-outbound-log-tagging

Follow-ups / next sync:
- 2026-12-09: quick async check-in (progress updates in the doc comments).
- 2026-12-13: 30-minute retro + prioritization with product to decide on installer preflight deliverable (calendar invite queued).

Appendix (useful snippets):
- manual KMS refresh: curl -X POST https://vault.customer.example/v1/auth/refresh -H 'Authorization: Bearer <token>' # example only
- temporary health-check switch: kubectl patch svc monitoring -p '{"spec": {"healthCheck": {"protocol": "HTTP"}}}'

End of micro-retro. Notes: keep this doc live as owners update their sections; if you make a change, add a one-line summary and timestamp.

- Installer preflight suite: extend automated preflight to test firewall behavior (bi-directional TCP health check + common UDP patterns) and return a clear recommendation.
- SE onboarding playbook: build a short workshop + checklist for common install failure modes, add to SE weekly training.
- Mirror resilience: edge-installer to include mirror health probe + automatic mirror failover for image pulls.

Action items (owners + dates):
- Aisha Mbaye: compile the 1-pager install checklist and share to /se-team/handbook (2026-12-10).
- Marco Alvarez: implement probe-fallback change and open PR against rc/edge-probes (2026-12-14).
- Lina Zhang: update runbook with KMS TTL preflight and manual-refresh instructions (2026-12-09).
- Carlos Mendes: add outbound-failure log telemetry + example troubleshooting queries to SRE dashboard (2026-12-08).
- Priya Natarajan: consolidate feedback and schedule a 30-minute sync with product to discuss adding preflight to installer backlog (2026-12-11).

Decisions made during sync:
- We will not make UDP probes the primary method by default; instead we will attempt TCP/HTTP first and leave UDP as optional for customers that require it.
- Quick wins (checklist + runbook note + logging) are prioritized for immediate delivery in the coming week.

Open questions / escalations:
- Should the installer ship an opinionated default set of firewall rules to show to customers during onboarding? Product input required (PRD ticket pending).
- For KMS integrations, do we enforce a minimum propagation window or just surface a warning? (need security/cryptography input).

Notes to SEs / field engineers (tips from this week):
- When hitting intermittent health-check failures, perform a packet capture from the cluster egress point and share a 10s pcap; this usually shows malformed probes or blocked ports.
- If cert rotation fails, attempt a manual auth refresh against the customer's KMS (documented command in runbook) before escalating.

Suggested backlog tickets (short names):
- preflight-firewall-probe
- edge-probe-fallback
- kms-ttl-preflight
- installer-outbound-log-tagging

Follow-ups / next sync:
- 2026-12-09: quick async check-in (progress updates in the doc comments).
- 2026-12-13: 30-minute retro + prioritization with product to decide on installer preflight deliverable (calendar invite queued).

Appendix (useful snippets):
- manual KMS refresh: curl -X POST https://vault.customer.example/v1/auth/refresh -H 'Authorization: Bearer <token>' # example only
- temporary health-check switch: kubectl patch svc monitoring -p '{"spec": {"healthCheck": {"protocol": "HTTP"}}}'

End of micro-retro. Notes: keep this doc live as owners update their sections; if you make a change, add a one-line summary and timestamp.

Purpose:
Quick standalone micro-retro + handoff checkpoint for the private deployments installs we ran the past week. Goal: capture recurring operational gaps that are slowing handoffs, list immediate mitigations, and assign owners for next-week fixes. This is not a full postmortem — it focuses on handoff friction.

Attendees (sync + async comments):
- Priya Natarajan (owner)
- Ethan Cho (field engineer)
- Marco Alvarez (networking lead)
- Lina Zhang (install ops)
- Carlos Mendes (SRE oncall)
- Aisha Mbaye (solutions engineering)

High-level summary (1–2 lines):
- Two customer installs progressed to final validation; both blocked by network/CNI edge cases and cert rotation timing. One new customer (RetailCo) deferred acceptance due to unexpected firewall rules blocking health probes.

Key customer snapshots:
1) RetailCo (on-prem, dual-datacenter) — current status: validation stalled
   - What happened: HA pair brought up, control-plane pods stable, but health-checks intermittently failing from monitoring region B.
   - Primary blocker: corporate firewall silently dropping UDP probes (we were using UDP-based probe aggregator).
   - Temporary mitigation: switched probes to TCP/HTTP for health checks; confirmed stability on staging.
   - Next action: SE to coordinate with customer network team to get explicit allow rule for UDP if they want legacy probes (Aisha, due 2026-12-09).
   - Tickets: PD-3421 (network diagnostics for RetailCo) — escalated.

2) FinSys (VPC, customer-managed KMS) — current status: smoke tests passed; awaiting keys rotation window
   - What happened: during cert rotation test, node agents failed to fetch new wrapping keys due to role mapping TTL misconfiguration.
   - Primary blocker: TTL on instance role mapping (customer side) set to 1h; rotation script assumes sub-5m propagation.
   - Mitigation: performed manual refresh via scripted API call; documented the step in runbook.
   - Long-term: add explicit preflight check for Vault/KMS TTL and surface a warning in the installer UI (Lina + Marco, POC due 2026-12-13).
   - Tickets: PD-3589 (KMS rotation preflight check).

3) EdgeRetail (edge cluster, small site) — current status: complete, small post-install latency spike
   - Observation: image pull throttling from mirror in customer datacenter caused delayed container start for det-inference; recovered after mirror swap.
   - Suggestion: include mirror health probe and fallback in future edge-installer release (Carlos).

Recurring friction themes (observed across 4 installs this sprint):
- Network assumptions: we still assume symmetric rules for UDP probes and ephemeral outbound flows. Customers often have more restrictive firewall policies than documented.
- Installer visibility: the UI / CLI logs don't consistently highlight when external network ACLs block flows versus internal container issues — slows triage.
- Role/TTL mismatches: third-party KMS and identity systems have widely varying propagation windows; our scripts assume optimistic propagation.
- Knowledge transfer: new SEs onboarded this quarter lack a short checklist that ties common customer network patterns to exact remediation steps.

Immediate mitigations (short horizon, 1 week):
- Checklist drop-in: create a 1-page install checklist for SEs that covers 6 most common network firewall rules + example iptables/ACL snippets (owner: Aisha, due 2026-12-10).
- Probe fallback: change default health probe to try HTTP/TCP first then UDP (non-breaking toggle) in the next release candidate (owner: Marco, release branch: rc/edge-probes, due 2026-12-14).
- Runbook note: add explicit KMS TTL preflight step and a manual refresh command snippet (owner: Lina, due 2026-12-09).
- Installer logging: quick patch to add a tagged log line when outbound connections to well-known endpoints fail (owner: Carlos, PR draft by 2026-12-08).

- SE onboarding playbook: build a short workshop + checklist for common install failure modes, add to SE weekly training.
- Mirror resilience: edge-installer to include mirror health probe + automatic mirror failover for image pulls.

- Quick wins (checklist + runbook note + logging) are prioritized for immediate delivery in the coming week.

Open questions / escalations:

- For KMS integrations, do we enforce a minimum propagation window or just surface a warning? (need security/cryptography input).

Notes to SEs / field engineers (tips from this week):

- If cert rotation fails, attempt a manual auth refresh against the customer's KMS (documented command in runbook) before escalating.

- kms-ttl-preflight

Follow-ups / next sync:

source.title: Quant Pulse — Model Onboarding & Eval Standup (Nov 2–6, 2026)
source.content:
Purpose:
This note captures the Applied ML weekly standups and research updates for the quantization + model onboarding track. Focus this week: finish initial INT8/FP16 onboarding for two community models (Llama-3-13B variant + open-13B-mix), run cross-evals, and validate KV-cache interaction with our continuous batching path.

High-level summary (Nov 2–6):
- INT8 baseline builds completed for both targets; runtime artifacts pushed to staging nodes.
- Early eval: token-level perplexity +0.5–1.2% vs FP16 for conversational prompts; no major hallucination patterns detected in the sampled slices.
- Throughput: batching + KV-cache enabled increased steady-state tokens/sec by ~2.4x on A100-40G for short-chat workloads.
- Primary blocker: intermittent OOM when running sequence-length=4096 synthetic stress tests in combination with prefill caching (suspect KV-store eviction logic).
- Decision: hold roll-forward to canary until KV eviction bug is fixed; continue quality regressions monitoring with extended prompt set.

Day-by-day standup highlights:
Mon 2026-11-02 — "kickoff and build"
- Priya: finalized quantization config for Llama-3-13B-v1 (per-channel scale + symmetric clipping). Built kernels with relaxed-gemm path; pushed image tag quant/llama3-13b-int8:v20261102.
- Marco: integration test for continuous batching passed on small input (<=512). Noticed 6% slower prefill step after introducing fused attention kernel; suspect kernel dispatch overhead.
- Jia: started compilation matrix for open-13B-mix with 3 quant-profiles (int8-symmetric, int8-asymmetric, fp16).
- Blockers: CI flakiness on A100 staging due to outdated CUDA driver on one node.

Tue 2026-11-03 — "evals & regressions"
- Quick eval run: 1.5k prompts (mix of chat, code, summarization). Observations:
  * Llama-3-13B-int8: avg token perplexity change +0.8% vs fp16; p95 latency improvement 18% for streaming tokens.
  * open-13B-mix-int8-asym: perplexity +1.2%; showed slight repetition on long-code-generation tasks.
- Action: expand eval set to 10k prompts for statistical significance; schedule overnight run on larger cluster (Jia to own).
- Ops note: launched a dedicated profiling job to trace kernel hotpaths (Alexei).

Wed 2026-11-04 — "kv-cache & stress"
- Found OOM reproducer sequence when prefill + incremental decode combined for sequences > 3k tokens with batching multiplicity > 8. Root-cause hypothesis: stale pointer in KV eviction code path when compacting shards under load.
- Temporary mitigation: cap batch multiplicity to 6 for canary nodes and enable conservative cache retention policy.
- Experiment: with conservative cache, p95 latency increased by ~12% but memory stabilized.

Thu 2026-11-05 — "ablation and metrics"
- Ablation matrix completed for L3-13B-int8 (runs labeled A1..A6). Key results:
  * A1 (no-kv, batching 4) — peak throughput 9.6k tok/s, mem 21GB, quality baseline.
  * A2 (kv-enabled, batching 4) — throughput 18.3k tok/s, mem 26GB, small quality delta +0.4% perplexity.
  * A3 (kv-enabled, batching 8) — throughput 22.9k tok/s, mem 34GB, observed OOM in stress test.
  * A4 (kv-conservative, batching 8) — throughput 20.1k tok/s, mem 30GB, stable for 95% of test slices.
  * A5 (fp16 baseline, batching 8) — throughput 16.7k tok/s, mem 38GB.
  * A6 (int8-asym tuning, batching 6) — throughput 21.0k tok/s, mem 28GB, perplexity +0.6%.
- Note: memory numbers are peak process RSS measured under synthetic generator. Use with caution for production extrapolation.

Fri 2026-11-06 — "planning and next steps"
- Consensus: continue with int8-symmetric as primary quant profile for L3-13B, but keep int8-asymmetric as a fallback for workloads that show higher quality sensitivity (code-gen).
- PR opened to patch KV eviction (linked artifact). Patch includes additional atomic guards + improved compaction schedule. QA to validate under stress harness.
- Rollout plan for canary: after KV patch validated, deploy 3 canary nodes in eu-west-1 dedicated pool with conservative cache policy and telemetry hooks (cost + latency). If 7 days of stable metrics, expand.

Outstanding action items (owner + ETA):
- Jia: finish 10k prompt cross-eval and produce per-genre breakdown (ETA 2026-11-11).
- Alexei: complete end-to-end perf profile and propose two kernel dispatch micro-optimizations (ETA 2026-11-12).
- Marco: fix stale CUDA-driver nodes on staging and run full nightly integration (ETA 2026-11-09).
- Lena: add prompt-based hallucination checks to automated eval harness and run on last 50k chat samples (ETA 2026-11-15).
- Priya: prepare canary rollout checklist and updated monitoring dashboards (ETA 2026-11-13).

Risks & mitigation:
- Risk: Quality regressions in long-form code generation with int8-asymmetric. Mitigation: default route such traffic to fp16 variant via routing policy; enable automatic fallback when eval metrics cross threshold.
- Risk: KV eviction bug under high batch multiplicity causing OOM. Mitigation: conservative cache policy + atomic patch; add telemetry to emit detailed eviction traces to S3 for post-mortem.
- Risk: under-sampled eval set -> false positives/negatives. Mitigation: expand eval corpus, stratify by prompt length, genre, and temperature.

Notes for product/solutions teams:
- No immediate customer-facing changes this week; canary still gated on KV fix.
- For Dedicated customers who want reduced memory footprint, we can offer int8 variants behind a feature flag (requires acceptance testing). Prep a short one-pager after canary success.

Appendix — quick links and artifacts referenced:
- Internal PR: https://github.com/redwood-inference/serving-runtime/pull/582 (quant kernels + runtime flags)
- Jira: RDW-2123 (KV eviction bug)
- Confluence draft: https://confluence.redwood/internals/applied-ml/quant-onboard-nov2026
- Eval harness: /internal/storage/evals/2026-11/int8-crosseval-10k (index stored in Confluence page)

Quick decisions logged:
- Keep int8-symmetric as default for L3-13B onboarding.
- Block canary rollout until RDW-2123 validated on staging.
- Expand eval set to 10k prompts before any production traffic shift.

If anything missing or you want the raw experiment traces, ping in #applied-ml-quant or DM Priya."

source.title: Contract Forecast Runway — Ops Grid Q4 2026
source.content:
Sheet layout (rows are records). Columns: Account | Contract End | ARR | Renewal Probability (%) | Next Steps | Owner | Last Contact | Risk Notes | Playbook | Renewal Window | Target ARR Change

Header row: Account,Contract End,ARR,Renewal Probability (%),Next Steps,Owner,Last Contact,Risk Notes,Playbook,Renewal Window,Target ARR Change

Rows:
Acme Retail,2026-12-15,$480,000,45,Schedule exec QBR + price review; propose 6% upsell; confirm SSO status,Priya N.,2026-11-02,Usage plateauing in checkout flows; procurement expects PO by mid-November,Enterprise Upsell Play,Nov-Dec 2026,0-6%
