source.title: capacity-scan-hosted-api-peak-supplycurve-by-az-gpu-class-2027q2
source.content:
Summary:
This sheet defines a supply-curve approach to convert projected hosted API peak demand (tokens/sec and QPS) into an actionable GPU provisioning plan by availability zone (AZ) and GPU class. Aim: ensure 99th-percentile latency SLOs for conversational + completion workloads while minimizing over-provisioning across regions.

Key differences vs prior playbooks:
- Uses an explicit supply-curve (tokens/sec -> GPU instances) per AZ and per GPU family (T4, A100, H100) instead of global headroom buckets.
- Incorporates a microburst amplification factor (MAF) and prefix-caching reuse to model short-window peaks (1m, 5m, 15m).
- Explicit fallback mapping to cheaper GPU class under saturated conditions (A100 -> T4 fallback for low-context workloads).

Assumptions (conservative baseline):
- Baseline mix: 60% short-chat (avg 80 tokens/request), 30% assistant-long (avg 600 tokens/request), 10% embedding/rerank (avg 200 tokens/request).
- Avg tokens/sec per QPS = weighted avg tokens / second per request; use tokens-per-request / avg request duration. For planning, convert by tokens_per_request * QPS.
- Cold-start penalty: 0.5s p50 additional for on-demand slices; prewarm reduces to 0.05s.
- GPU throughput profiles (tokens/sec sustained per card at target latency): T4=10k tps, A100=55k tps, H100=110k tps (workload-dependent).
- Target utilization: 70% sustained average; allow 30% headroom for 1m bursts; allow extra 15% at AZ-level for P99 events.
- Microburst Amplification Factor (MAF): 1m=1.6x, 5m=1.35x, 15m=1.12x.
- Inter-AZ failover capacity reserve: 10% of region peak kept cold-warm.

Projected peak sweep (sample lines from the main table):
Columns: month, projected_qps_region (global-hosted), projected_tokens_per_sec_global, p95_tokens_1m, p99_tokens_1m, required_gpus_A100 (70% util), required_gpus_H100, required_gpus_T4, recommended_az_split
2027-04, 28,000 QPS, 4,200,000 tps, 4,200,000*1.35=5,670,000 p95 5m, 1m_p99 = 4,200,000*1.6=6,720,000, A100@70% -> ceil(6,720,000 / (55,000*0.7)) = ceil(6,720,000 / 38,500) = 175 A100, H100 equiv = ceil(6,720,000 / (110,000*0.7)) = ceil(6,720,000 / 77,000) = 88 H100, T4 fallback = ceil(6,720,000 / (10,000*0.7)) = 961 T4, recommended_az_split: 40% us-west, 40% us-east, 20% eu-west
2027-07, 36,500 QPS, 5,475,000 tps, p95_5m=5,475,000*1.35=7,386,000, p99_1m=5,475,000*1.6=8,760,000, A100s=ceil(8,760,000/38,500)=228, H100=ceil(8,760,000/77,000)=114, T4=ceil(8,760,000/7,000)=1252 (note: T4 lower perf in mixed workloads) , az_split: 35% us-east, 35% us-west, 20% eu-west, 10% apac

Supply curve explanation (how to use):
- For a given tokens/sec target T and burst window W, multiply T by MAF(W) to get burst-adjusted demand B.
- For each GPU class, compute effective tokens/sec capacity per card at target latency: Cg = gpu_throughput * target_utilization.
- Required cards = ceil(B / Cg).
- Apply AZ split and reserve fractions: reserve_AZ_local = ceil(required_cards * local_reserve_pct).
- Add inter-AZ cold-warm reserve (10% of regional peak) which can be spun up within 3–5 minutes.

Example supply-curve excerpt (tokens/sec -> cards needed at 1m window):
- 500k tps -> A100: ceil(500k*1.6 / 38.5k) = 21 A100
- 1.5M tps -> A100: ceil(1.5M*1.6 / 38.5k) = 63 A100
- 3.0M tps -> A100: ceil(3.0M*1.6 / 38.5k) = 126 A100
- 6.7M tps -> A100: 175 A100 (see projection row)

Regional allocation rules:
- Primary AZs (US-EAST, US-WEST, EU-WEST): keep active capacity sized to serve 80% of their projected share; 20% of region demand served via cross-AZ failover.
- APAC: conservative on-premise capacity + burst to cloud; keep higher prewarm for longer tail latencies (use H100 where latency-critical).
- Autoscale minimum slices: 8 cards per AZ for quick horizontal scaling boundary.
- Slice sizing heuristic: prefer multiples of 4 cards for A100 pools (infra constraints on packing).

Cost/Unit sanity check (approx):
- Effective cost per sustained million tokens (hosted mix) by GPU class (rough infra costs incl. amortized infra & power): T4 ~$18 /M tokens, A100 ~$6 /M tokens, H100 ~$3.5 /M tokens (very workload-dependent). Use these for allocation tradeoffs when considering fallback.

Fallback and graceful degradation policy (supply-curve driven):
- If A100 pool saturation > 95% for >3m, route low-context short-chat requests to T4 pool (degraded quality but lower cost).
- If regional availability < 80% of target for >5m, enable cross-region spill to nearest region with available H100 capacity and set token-limit per request to 75%.
- Maintain per-tenant QoS classes that can be throttled earlier to preserve critical customers (priority buckets).

Operational runbook snippets (operationalization notes for SRE/Capacity team):
1) Daily: refresh projection inputs from metrics pipeline (past 6wk rolling QPS and tokens per request distributions).
2) Weekly: recompute supply-curve breakpoints; publish recommended GPU pool changes to infra-provisioning channel by Tue 10:00 PT.
3) Pre-peak (72h): raise prewarm slices to cover 50% of predicted 1m P99 demand; tag nodes with allocation-id.
4) During peak: monitor 'burst_absorption_ratio' metric per AZ (target >0.8). If <0.6, trigger emergency pool reallocation (runbook ID: CAP-EMG-03).
5) Post-peak: scale pools down gradually over 30–90 min; run divergence analysis on projected vs actual tokens/sec.

Open questions / follow-ups:
- Validate H100 sustained tps numbers on mixed-length workloads (need microbench matrix across model families).
- Determine acceptable quality loss thresholds for T4 fallback for each tenant class.
- Assess spot/interruptible capacity options for burst absorb (cost vs reliability tradeoffs).

Action items (owners):
- Priya: run the 6-week historical sweep to populate month-by-month rows (due 2026-11-09).
- Liam: bench A100/H100 mixed workload throughput with realistic chat+long prompts (due 2026-11-05).
- Maya: update prewarm automation to support AZ-level targeted warming (PR#8342) (due 2026-11-16).
- Diego: draft tenant QoS throttling playbook and paging thresholds (due 2026-11-12).

Appendix: quick CSV-friendly table (copy into spreadsheet):
month,global_qps,global_tps,p95_5m_tps,p99_1m_tps,req_A100,req_H100,req_T4,az_split
2027-04,28000,4200000,5670000,6720000,175,88,961,us-west:40%|us-east:40%|eu-west:20%
2027-05,30000,4500000,6075000,7200000,187,94,1020,us-east:40%|us-west:35%|eu-west:20%|apac:5%
2027-07,36500,5475000,7386000,8760000,228,114,1252,us-east:35%|us-west:35%|eu-west:20%|apac:10%

Notes: this is a working planning sheet intended to be copied into the capacity planner spreadsheet. Figures are intentionally conservative; please question any number that changes by >10% from the rolling 6-week observed metric.

- If regional availability < 80% of target for >5m, enable cross-region spill to nearest region with available H100 capacity and set token-limit per request to 75%.
- Maintain per-tenant QoS classes that can be throttled earlier to preserve critical customers (priority buckets).

Operational runbook snippets (operationalization notes for SRE/Capacity team):
1) Daily: refresh projection inputs from metrics pipeline (past 6wk rolling QPS and tokens per request distributions).
2) Weekly: recompute supply-curve breakpoints; publish recommended GPU pool changes to infra-provisioning channel by Tue 10:00 PT.
3) Pre-peak (72h): raise prewarm slices to cover 50% of predicted 1m P99 demand; tag nodes with allocation-id.
4) During peak: monitor 'burst_absorption_ratio' metric per AZ (target >0.8). If <0.6, trigger emergency pool reallocation (runbook ID: CAP-EMG-03).
5) Post-peak: scale pools down gradually over 30–90 min; run divergence analysis on projected vs actual tokens/sec.

Open questions / follow-ups:
- Validate H100 sustained tps numbers on mixed-length workloads (need microbench matrix across model families).
- Determine acceptable quality loss thresholds for T4 fallback for each tenant class.
- Assess spot/interruptible capacity options for burst absorb (cost vs reliability tradeoffs).

Action items (owners):
- Priya: run the 6-week historical sweep to populate month-by-month rows (due 2026-11-09).
- Liam: bench A100/H100 mixed workload throughput with realistic chat+long prompts (due 2026-11-05).
- Maya: update prewarm automation to support AZ-level targeted warming (PR#8342) (due 2026-11-16).
- Diego: draft tenant QoS throttling playbook and paging thresholds (due 2026-11-12).

Appendix: quick CSV-friendly table (copy into spreadsheet):
month,global_qps,global_tps,p95_5m_tps,p99_1m_tps,req_A100,req_H100,req_T4,az_split
2027-04,28000,4200000,5670000,6720000,175,88,961,us-west:40%|us-east:40%|eu-west:20%
2027-05,30000,4500000,6075000,7200000,187,94,1020,us-east:40%|us-west:35%|eu-west:20%|apac:5%
2027-07,36500,5475000,7386000,8760000,228,114,1252,us-east:35%|us-west:35%|eu-west:20%|apac:10%

Notes: this is a working planning sheet intended to be copied into the capacity planner spreadsheet. Figures are intentionally conservative; please question any number that changes by >10% from the rolling 6-week observed metric.
text:
Summary:
This sheet defines a supply-curve approach to convert projected hosted API peak demand (tokens/sec and QPS) into an actionable GPU provisioning plan by availability zone (AZ) and GPU class. Aim: ensure 99th-percentile latency SLOs for conversational + completion workloads while minimizing over-provisioning across regions.

Key differences vs prior playbooks:
- Uses an explicit supply-curve (tokens/sec -> GPU instances) per AZ and per GPU family (T4, A100, H100) instead of global headroom buckets.
- Incorporates a microburst amplification factor (MAF) and prefix-caching reuse to model short-window peaks (1m, 5m, 15m).
- Explicit fallback mapping to cheaper GPU class under saturated conditions (A100 -> T4 fallback for low-context workloads).

Assumptions (conservative baseline):
- Baseline mix: 60% short-chat (avg 80 tokens/request), 30% assistant-long (avg 600 tokens/request), 10% embedding/rerank (avg 200 tokens/request).
- Avg tokens/sec per QPS = weighted avg tokens / second per request; use tokens-per-request / avg request duration. For planning, convert by tokens_per_request * QPS.
- Cold-start penalty: 0.5s p50 additional for on-demand slices; prewarm reduces to 0.05s.
- GPU throughput profiles (tokens/sec sustained per card at target latency): T4=10k tps, A100=55k tps, H100=110k tps (workload-dependent).
- Target utilization: 70% sustained average; allow 30% headroom for 1m bursts; allow extra 15% at AZ-level for P99 events.
- Microburst Amplification Factor (MAF): 1m=1.6x, 5m=1.35x, 15m=1.12x.
- Inter-AZ failover capacity reserve: 10% of region peak kept cold-warm.

Projected peak sweep (sample lines from the main table):
Columns: month, projected_qps_region (global-hosted), projected_tokens_per_sec_global, p95_tokens_1m, p99_tokens_1m, required_gpus_A100 (70% util), required_gpus_H100, required_gpus_T4, recommended_az_split
2027-04, 28,000 QPS, 4,200,000 tps, 4,200,000*1.35=5,670,000 p95 5m, 1m_p99 = 4,200,000*1.6=6,720,000, A100@70% -> ceil(6,720,000 / (55,000*0.7)) = ceil(6,720,000 / 38,500) = 175 A100, H100 equiv = ceil(6,720,000 / (110,000*0.7)) = ceil(6,720,000 / 77,000) = 88 H100, T4 fallback = ceil(6,720,000 / (10,000*0.7)) = 961 T4, recommended_az_split: 40% us-west, 40% us-east, 20% eu-west
2027-07, 36,500 QPS, 5,475,000 tps, p95_5m=5,475,000*1.35=7,386,000, p99_1m=5,475,000*1.6=8,760,000, A100s=ceil(8,760,000/38,500)=228, H100=ceil(8,760,000/77,000)=114, T4=ceil(8,760,000/7,000)=1252 (note: T4 lower perf in mixed workloads) , az_split: 35% us-east, 35% us-west, 20% eu-west, 10% apac

Supply curve explanation (how to use):
- For a given tokens/sec target T and burst window W, multiply T by MAF(W) to get burst-adjusted demand B.
- For each GPU class, compute effective tokens/sec capacity per card at target latency: Cg = gpu_throughput * target_utilization.
- Required cards = ceil(B / Cg).
- Apply AZ split and reserve fractions: reserve_AZ_local = ceil(required_cards * local_reserve_pct).
- Add inter-AZ cold-warm reserve (10% of regional peak) which can be spun up within 3–5 minutes.

Example supply-curve excerpt (tokens/sec -> cards needed at 1m window):
- 500k tps -> A100: ceil(500k*1.6 / 38.5k) = 21 A100
- 1.5M tps -> A100: ceil(1.5M*1.6 / 38.5k) = 63 A100
- 3.0M tps -> A100: ceil(3.0M*1.6 / 38.5k) = 126 A100
- 6.7M tps -> A100: 175 A100 (see projection row)

Regional allocation rules:
- Primary AZs (US-EAST, US-WEST, EU-WEST): keep active capacity sized to serve 80% of their projected share; 20% of region demand served via cross-AZ failover.
- APAC: conservative on-premise capacity + burst to cloud; keep higher prewarm for longer tail latencies (use H100 where latency-critical).
- Autoscale minimum slices: 8 cards per AZ for quick horizontal scaling boundary.
- Slice sizing heuristic: prefer multiples of 4 cards for A100 pools (infra constraints on packing).

Cost/Unit sanity check (approx):
- Effective cost per sustained million tokens (hosted mix) by GPU class (rough infra costs incl. amortized infra & power): T4 ~$18 /M tokens, A100 ~$6 /M tokens, H100 ~$3.5 /M tokens (very workload-dependent). Use these for allocation tradeoffs when considering fallback.

Fallback and graceful degradation policy (supply-curve driven):
- If A100 pool saturation > 95% for >3m, route low-context short-chat requests to T4 pool (degraded quality but lower cost).
- If regional availability < 80% of target for >5m, enable cross-region spill to nearest region with available H100 capacity and set token-limit per request to 75%.
- Maintain per-tenant QoS classes that can be throttled earlier to preserve critical customers (priority buckets).

Operational runbook snippets (operationalization notes for SRE/Capacity team):
1) Daily: refresh projection inputs from metrics pipeline (past 6wk rolling QPS and tokens per request distributions).
2) Weekly: recompute supply-curve breakpoints; publish recommended GPU pool changes to infra-provisioning channel by Tue 10:00 PT.
3) Pre-peak (72h): raise prewarm slices to cover 50% of predicted 1m P99 demand; tag nodes with allocation-id.
4) During peak: monitor 'burst_absorption_ratio' metric per AZ (target >0.8). If <0.6, trigger emergency pool reallocation (runbook ID: CAP-EMG-03).
5) Post-peak: scale pools down gradually over 30–90 min; run divergence analysis on projected vs actual tokens/sec.

Open questions / follow-ups:
- Validate H100 sustained tps numbers on mixed-length workloads (need microbench matrix across model families).
- Determine acceptable quality loss thresholds for T4 fallback for each tenant class.
- Assess spot/interruptible capacity options for burst absorb (cost vs reliability tradeoffs).

Action items (owners):
- Priya: run the 6-week historical sweep to populate month-by-month rows (due 2026-11-09).
- Liam: bench A100/H100 mixed workload throughput with realistic chat+long prompts (due 2026-11-05).
- Maya: update prewarm automation to support AZ-level targeted warming (PR#8342) (due 2026-11-16).
- Diego: draft tenant QoS throttling playbook and paging thresholds (due 2026-11-12).

Appendix: quick CSV-friendly table (copy into spreadsheet):
month,global_qps,global_tps,p95_5m_tps,p99_1m_tps,req_A100,req_H100,req_T4,az_split
2027-04,28000,4200000,5670000,6720000,175,88,961,us-west:40%|us-east:40%|eu-west:20%
2027-05,30000,4500000,6075000,7200000,187,94,1020,us-east:40%|us-west:35%|eu-west:20%|apac:5%
2027-07,36500,5475000,7386000,8760000,228,114,1252,us-east:35%|us-west:35%|eu-west:20%|apac:10%

source.title: Signal Ops: Weekly Spotlight - Tracing, Dashboards & Cost Telemetry (2026-08-20)
source.content:
Purpose:
A compact weekly note capturing the Observability (Signal Ops) sync for 2026-08-20. Focus areas: tracing sampling adjustments, dashboard owner responsibilities, and a short cost-telemetry anomaly triage. Notes are intentionally terse — operational handoff for next week.

Attendees:
- Priya Natarajan (owner)
- Evan Chu
- Luis Martinez
- Zara Khan
- Anya Patel (SRE liaison)

High-level summary / tl;dr:
- We validated a targeted trace-sampling experiment that reduced long-span ingestion from 1.8k/day to ~420/day for low-traffic backend services, with negligible loss in root-cause signal. Estimated 18% reduction in tracing ingestion cost for the staging cluster.
- Dashboard refresh: consolidated 9 low-usage dashboards into 3 composite views; set owner/rotation on remaining dashboards.
- Cost telemetry: detected a 12% week-over-week increase in exporter egress in region-3; root cause likely misconfigured batch sizes after sdk upgrade. Patch rolled to the SDK with backfill guard.

Detailed notes:
1) Tracing sampling experiment (low-traffic backends)
- Motivation: long-tail latency spikes were being drowned out by high-cardinality consumer traces for a small set of microservices (payments-connector-light, ml-workqueue-proxy). These services produce many long KV spans but low request volume.
- Experiment design: soft-sample by request-type + response-status. Keep full traces for 100% of latency>1s or error != 200. For successful <1s requests, sample at 5%. Implemented via the agent-level prefilter that tags traces with sampling-suggested rates and the ingest pipeline applies the rate.
- Duration: 14 days (started 2026-08-06), observed until 2026-08-20.
- Results: overall spans/day decreased from 1.8k to ~420 for target services. No observable regression in SLO incident detection for automated alerts (checked against last 6-week alert history).
- Risks/notes: edge case where multi-service transactions that include a sampled-light request may lose context. Plan to enable KV-cache rehydration metadata for sampled partial traces to improve correlation.
- Next steps: promote policy to staging->prod staging ramp: increase to 90% of traffic in staging cluster next week and monitor alert sensitivity (assigned Evan).

2) Dashboards and owner assignments
- Completed: consolidated the "trace-latency-explorer" + "per-service-span-sizes" + "kv-span-map" into a single composite "trace-economics" dashboard with dropdown for environment and model. This reduces cognitive switching and lowers dashboard duplication.
- Owners set:
  - trace-economics: Zara Khan (rotates quarterly)
  - dashboard-costs: Luis Martinez
  - exporter-health: Anya Patel
- Action: add an owner field to dashboard meta so the Console shows a hover tooltip with on-call and rotation dates (product ticket created: CONFLUENCE-OBS-239). Priya to coordinate UI copy with Console team.

3) Cost telemetry anomaly (region-3 exporter egress)
- Symptom: 12% W/W increase in telemetry egress bill line for region-3, manifesting on 2026-08-14.
- Quick triage: looked at exporter batch-size distribution and found a sudden drop in avg.batch_size from 512->128 after the agent SDK (v2.3.0) rolled on 2026-08-13. This increased per-request overhead and egress calls.
- Root cause: SDK changed default batch behavior to prioritize latency for small queues; no migration notes in the release.
- Remediation: reverted agent config to explicit batch_size=512 via config override for region-3 agents; opened PR to SDK repo with backward-compatible toggles (github.com/redwood/telemetry/pull/884).
- Follow-up: schedule a cross-team review of SDK release governance. Also propose a smoke test that verifies cost-impacted defaults in CI. (Owner: Luis)

4) Alerts and runbook updates
- Updated: "trace-ingest-backpressure" runbook with new thresholds and a checklist to reduce agent sampling to 1% for noisy tenants as an emergency mitigation.
- Added a diagnostic snippet to runbook: a query for 95th-span-size-by-service and a quick correlator for cost delta.
- TODO: validate runbook steps with SRE runbook-drill next sprint (Anya assigned).

5) Open experiments / stuff to watch
- Prefix KV cache: We started a small experiment enabling prefix-level caching for model-inference edges to reduce repeated prefix decoding. Early results: 6–9% token-cost reduction for repeated-prompt traffic (private build). Need 2 more weeks of data and a regression test for cache-staleness. (Owner: Priya)
- Trace retention vs. cost tradeoff analysis: drafting a Monte Carlo sim to estimate signal loss vs. dollars when moving retention from 30d->14d for low-traffic services. Draft is in CONFLUENCE-OBS-239.

6) Decisions made
- Approve targeted sampling policy for low-traffic backends (as described above) to be extended to prod after one more staging ramp.
- Reverted agent default batch-size in region-3 and created SDK PR for explicit config.

7) Blockers / ask from other teams
- Console: need a small metadata field for dashboard owner (Console product). Priya to escalate in product sync.
- SDK team: request a hotfix release cadence for agent defaults that materially affect cost. Luis to schedule post-mortem.

Action items (owners and due dates)
- Evan: promote sampling policy to 90% staging traffic and monitor alert sensitivity; due 2026-08-27.
- Zara: finalize trace-economics dashboard items and add owner rotation note; due 2026-08-25.
- Luis: lead SDK release post-mortem and propose a migration checklist; due 2026-08-30.
- Anya: schedule runbook drill and validate emergency mitigation; due 2026-09-03.
- Priya: coordinate Console product ticket for dashboard owner metadata; due 2026-08-24.

Notes to self / freeform:
- Keep an eye on correlation between prefix cache hits and downstream latency — if we see >3% 99p latency regressions on model-inference, pause experiment.
- Consider a small internal post outlining the sampling policy rationale to make it easier for teams to opt-in.

Meeting artifacts / references:
- Telemetry SDK PR: github.com/redwood/telemetry/pull/884
- Consolidated dashboard draft (link internal): CONFLUENCE-OBS-239
- Jira for sampling rollout: JIRA-OBS-1123

Next sync: 2026-08-27, same time.

End of note.

source.title: Applied ML standup — unplugged notes (Jul 19 2026)
source.content:
Meeting: Applied ML weekly — informal standup / sync (unrecorded)
Date: 2026-07-19
Location: Zoom + #applied-ml Slack
Facilitator: Priya
Attendees: Priya, Ethan, Marisol, Jesse, Sona (brief), Todd (30m)

Purpose: quick-fire sharing of experiment progress, small blockers, and decisions we can take before next sprint. Note: this is an operational capture intended as a working artifact — rough language OK.

Top-level TL;DR
- Quant int4 mixed-probe run on LlamaX-13B shows 1.8% EM degradation on prompt suite A but 22% latency reduction in steady-state serving sim. Good candidate for canary if we can validate hallucination deltas.
- Eval harness: added prompt perturbation tests (typos, truncated contexts) — early signal that cached KV-prefetch increases stability for long contexts.
- Onboarding: initial packaging of Elektra-7B failed due to custom rotary encodings; infra patch landed in serving-runtime branch, follow-up PR to model-catalog required.
- Actions: prioritize rebuttal evals for the int4 candidate (Marisol), schedule canary config for Dedicated tests (Todd), and complete gating checklist for Elektra (Ethan).

Notes by topic

1) Quantization experiments (Marisol lead)
- What we ran: mixed int4 asymmetric quant on LlamaX-13B with calibration on 2k tokens from conn-exchange dataset. Mixed scheme keeps first 12 layers in int8, last 8 layers int4.
- Results summary: throughput up ~22% (tokens/s), memory down ~28% on p4d sim. Perplexity delta on eval suite A: +0.9 ppl (small), EM on extraction tasks -1.8 points (edge but consistent).
- Risk signals: (a) instruction-following probes show occasional prompt truncation leading to wrong slot-filling in 3% of cases, (b) one-off verbatim recall tests fail on certain seed prompts.
- Next steps: run long-form generative coherence evals (90k tokens) and hallucination detector across 5 prompt families. Marisol will push scripts and run against cached and non-cached modes to see interaction with KV cache.
- Action item: Marisol — run rebuttal evals and post results by 2026-07-23.

2) Evals & harness improvements (Priya/Jesse)
- Added two new perturbation tests: character-drop (simulate OCR/errors) and context-shift (shuffle prior turns). Early finding: KV-prefetch reduces sensitivity to character-drop for >1024 context length.
- Metric additions: span-consistency, citation-presence (binary), and token-level divergence to baseline model (KL proxy). These are computed nightly now.
- Dataset curation: dropped 120 noisy examples from the longform bench after manual review — they were inflating hallucination rates.
- Open question: how to weight span-consistency vs. end-user perceived quality for rollout gating? Suggest product input next week.
- Action item: Jesse — update nightly eval dashboard with new metrics (due 2026-07-22).

3) Model onboarding (Ethan/Todd)
- Elektra-7B onboarding: blocker was custom rotary encoding and nonstandard tokenizer config. Ethan applied a small shim in the model-converter that maps rotary params to our runtime expectation. PR merged to infra/model-converters (link above).
- Canaries: Todd recommends a two-stage canary — (1) internal dedicated pool with synthetic load, (2) limited Hosted API route with traffic split 99/1. Need to define SLO backout thresholds (latency p95 + hallucination delta).
- Licensing check: legal confirmed Elektra license is compatible but flagged attribution metadata required in model-catalog.
- Action item: Ethan — finish model-catalog entry and sample tests (due 2026-07-21). Todd — schedule dedicated canary for next Monday, 2026-07-26.

4) KV-cache and microbatching (SRE + Runtime)
- Small ops note: observed cache eviction spikes at 02:00 UTC correlating with nightly batch jobs; increased cache TTL for small keys to 12h as a temporary mitigation.
- Prefetch experiments: enabling prefix prefetch improved tail-latency for long contexts by ~45ms p99 in the sim harness. However, increases peak memory by ~6% — trade-off noted.
- Microbatching: RPS-aware batching policy prototype deployed to dev; microbatch size auto-tunes between 4–32 based on concurrent token producers. Early numbers promising in latency vs cost tradeoff.
- Action: SRE (Jesse) to instrument a per-route memory delta metric for prefetch scenarios and add alert if >10% memory uplift.

5) Quick bug triage
- Observation: eval harness intermittently times out on large generative jobs — root cause likely the new retry logic interacting with streaming sockets. Workaround: increase eval timeout to 25m and patch retry backoff.
- Serving bug: model-pool drain race on shutdown. Repro steps captured in GH issue; assigned to Todd.

Decisions taken
- Proceed with int4 mixed candidate to a limited canary only after Marisol's rebuttal evals confirm hallucination delta under threshold (<=2.5% EM degradation and no >5% increase in hallucination score).
- Elektra onboarding allowed to continue but require catalog metadata and attribution fields completed before Hosted route exposure.

Risks & unresolved questions
- How to interpret small EM deltas for extraction tasks vs human-perceived quality for chat UI? (Product priority).
- Prefetch memory uplift trade-offs for Dedicated customers — need a per-customer opt-out or policy.
- Does microbatch auto-tuner interact badly with real-time low-latency routes? Need blackbox test.

Calendar & planning
- Marisol: rebuttal evals complete by 2026-07-23
- Jesse: dashboard updates by 2026-07-22; memory delta alert by 2026-07-24
- Ethan: catalog entry and sample tests by 2026-07-21 (in flight)
- Todd: schedule dedicated canary 2026-07-26
- Priya: coordinate product sync for gating criteria on 2026-07-22

Appendix (quick logs)
- Local run notes: mixed-int4 run command: run_quant_probe --model lmx-13b --scheme mixed-int4 --calib-samples 2000 --cache-mode prefetch
- Eval script: nightly_eval.sh --suite longform_v2 --perturbations char-drop,context-shift --metrics span-consistency,kl-proxy
- GH refs: PR #224 (quant-probe) contains the mixed-int4 runner; PR #227 (serving-runtime) contains prefetch memory instrumentation.

Notes-to-self: keep this doc portable — will copy to Confluence when gating decisions finalized. If anyone wants raw experiment logs, ping me for the S3 path.

End of notes.

source.title: Capacity Triage Rotations Queue — July 2026
source.content:
Tracker description: Lightweight intake + triage board used by infra on-call to prioritize short-term GPU allocations and surface medium-term capacity needs to planning. Intentionally compact to be used in async handoffs and daily triage standups.

Columns: Request ID | Team | Project | Use case | GPUs requested (type) | Preferred region | Desired start | Deadline | Priority | Owner (requestor) | Rotation owner | Approver | Approval status | Notes / follow-ups

Rows:
RT-2026-157 | serving-runtime | latency-cache-optim | KV cache perf test for 8k context | 4 x A100s (40GB) | us-west1 | 2026-07-10 | 2026-07-20 | High | Marcus Liu | Priya Natarajan (week A) | Cara Diaz | approved | Request validated; infra to carve from reserved pool. ETA 2d to provision. Add tagging for cost center: eng-serving.
RT-2026-158 | applied-ml | multi-model-fusion | pretraining evaluation (synthetic) | 16 x H100 | us-east4 | 2026-07-15 | 2026-08-01 | Critical | Elena Rodriguez | Priya Natarajan (week A) | Cara Diaz | pending | Needs security review for dataset. Suggested reduce to 8 H100s with longer runtime — ask team for phase gating. Jira: JIRA-ML-402.
RT-2026-159 | private-deployments | onprem-demo | customer PoC on private appliance | 2 x V100 | eu-west2 | 2026-07-20 | 2026-07-30 | Medium | Samir Gupta | Ruth Kim (week B) | N/A | needs-clarification | Clarify networking and bring-your-own-ssd. Estimate: 3 days install. Add ticket to solutions-eng.
RT-2026-160 | product-hosted-api | embeddings-cache | embedding sweep for cold-cache fill | 8 x A10G | us-west2 | 2026-07-05 | 2026-07-09 | Low | Cara Diaz | Ruth Kim (week B) | Cara Diaz | approved | Approved under nightly batch window. Use preemptible nodes. Monitor token throughput.
RT-2026-161 | platform-engineering | model-rollout-canary | canary rollout for q3 model (redwood-3b-q4) | 6 x A100s (80GB) | ap-south1 | 2026-07-18 | 2026-07-25 | High | Jacob Park | Priya Natarajan (week A) | Cara Diaz | pending | Request wants dedicated VLAN. Coordination needed with networking. Add follow-up call.
RT-2026-162 | observability | tracing-backfill | backfill traces for new tracing pipeline | 4 x T4 | us-east4 | 2026-07-07 | 2026-07-11 | Medium | Lina Morales | Ruth Kim (week B) | N/A | approved | Can run on spare low-priority nodes; schedule overnight.
RT-2026-163 | serving-runtime | kernel-benchmark-suite | microkernel perf validation | 2 x H100 | us-central1 | 2026-07-12 | 2026-07-14 | High | Aaron Cole | Priya Natarajan (week A) | Cara Diaz | rejected | Overlaps with infra maintenance window. Resubmit for window after 2026-07-21.

Triage rules (quick reference):
- Requests with "Critical" priority -> escalate to on-call lead and block out from reserved pool if start <= 7 days.
- "High" -> rotation owner must surface a carve decision within 48 hours or tag for capacity planning review.
- "Medium/Low" -> try to schedule into preemptible/burst capacity or off-peak windows.
- Any request that requires networking/VLAN/KMS must attach an RFC link and get an approval from platform-eng (approver column).

Rotation schedule (July):
- Week A (2026-07-01 to 2026-07-14): Priya Natarajan (triage lead), backup: Ruth Kim.
- Week B (2026-07-15 to 2026-07-28): Ruth Kim (triage lead), backup: Priya Natarajan.
- Handoff notes: rotation owner updates this sheet at end of shift; add a single-line summary to slack #infra-capacity with RT id + decision.

Capacity hints / reminders for requestors:
- Consider preemptible / spot instances for non-blocking experiments.
- If you need >12 GPUs for >7 days, file a mid-term capacity request (confluence: infra/capacity-requests) — this intake is for short (<14d) runs.
- Provide an estimated GPU-hours number to help cost tradeoffs.

Open action items (current):
- Elena (applied-ml) to confirm dataset access for RT-2026-158 by 2026-07-13.
- Jacob (platform) to open networking RFC for VLAN carve for RT-2026-161.
- Samir to supply on-prem appliance serial and network diagram for RT-2026-159.

Revision log: 2026-07-02 initial creation (Priya); 2026-07-10 added RT-2026-162 and RT-2026-160 decisions (Ruth); 2026-07-14 updated rotation schedule and rejected RT-2026-163 (Priya).

Notes to triage leads: Keep rows compact. Move approved/denied entries to the closed sheet (linked in confluence). Use the "Priority" tags consistently: Critical / High / Medium / Low.

RT-2026-162 | observability | tracing-backfill | backfill traces for new tracing pipeline | 4 x T4 | us-east4 | 2026-07-07 | 2026-07-11 | Medium | Lina Morales | Ruth Kim (week B) | N/A | approved | Can run on spare low-priority nodes; schedule overnight.
RT-2026-163 | serving-runtime | kernel-benchmark-suite | microkernel perf validation | 2 x H100 | us-central1 | 2026-07-12 | 2026-07-14 | High | Aaron Cole | Priya Natarajan (week A) | Cara Diaz | rejected | Overlaps with infra maintenance window. Resubmit for window after 2026-07-21.

Triage rules (quick reference):
- Requests with "Critical" priority -> escalate to on-call lead and block out from reserved pool if start <= 7 days.
- "High" -> rotation owner must surface a carve decision within 48 hours or tag for capacity planning review.
- "Medium/Low" -> try to schedule into preemptible/burst capacity or off-peak windows.
- Any request that requires networking/VLAN/KMS must attach an RFC link and get an approval from platform-eng (approver column).

Rotation schedule (July):
- Week A (2026-07-01 to 2026-07-14): Priya Natarajan (triage lead), backup: Ruth Kim.
- Week B (2026-07-15 to 2026-07-28): Ruth Kim (triage lead), backup: Priya Natarajan.
- Handoff notes: rotation owner updates this sheet at end of shift; add a single-line summary to slack #infra-capacity with RT id + decision.

Capacity hints / reminders for requestors:
- Consider preemptible / spot instances for non-blocking experiments.
- If you need >12 GPUs for >7 days, file a mid-term capacity request (confluence: infra/capacity-requests) — this intake is for short (<14d) runs.
- Provide an estimated GPU-hours number to help cost tradeoffs.

Open action items (current):
- Elena (applied-ml) to confirm dataset access for RT-2026-158 by 2026-07-13.
- Jacob (platform) to open networking RFC for VLAN carve for RT-2026-161.
- Samir to supply on-prem appliance serial and network diagram for RT-2026-159.

Revision log: 2026-07-02 initial creation (Priya); 2026-07-10 added RT-2026-162 and RT-2026-160 decisions (Ruth); 2026-07-14 updated rotation schedule and rejected RT-2026-163 (Priya).

Notes to triage leads: Keep rows compact. Move approved/denied entries to the closed sheet (linked in confluence). Use the "Priority" tags consistently: Critical / High / Medium / Low.

source.title: Model Curation Audit Runbook — Jun 2026
source.content:
Purpose:
This runbook documents a repeatable protocol for making onboarding decisions when multiple candidate model versions show near-parity across core metrics. It focuses on: (1) consistent prompt-set construction, (2) deterministic tie-breaking heuristics, (3) spot-check sampling and audit buckets, and (4) a short human-review escalation path. Goal: reduce flip-flopping between close models and provide traceable rationale for choosing a primary serving version or opting for staged rollouts.

Context and motivation:
We regularly see small deltas between candidate checkpoints (quantized vs fp16 variants, different tokenizers, small loRA adapter differences). Standard leaderboard averages often hide distributional failures and rare-but-important regressions. This runbook operationalizes an approval flow that balances automated metrics with low-cost human validation.

Datasets and prompt-sets used in the sweep:
- Core prompt matrix (P0): 2,400 in-domain prompts across completion, instruction-following, and dialog contexts. Stratified by length (short/medium/long) and task (summarization, instruction execution, code transformation, query-answering).
- Rare edge prompts (E1): 320 queries collected from customer bug reports and SRE/CS incident logs.
- Hallucination stress-set (H2): 200 prompts designed to trigger unsupported-knowledge responses and expose incorrect attribution.
- Safety check slices (S): 150 prompts focusing on toxic phrasing and policy-sensitive content.
- Calibration seeds: 5 deterministic seeds used for model sampling to ensure reproducible outputs in sampling-based variants.

Evaluation metrics (automated):
- EM-mean (exact match where applicable)
- ROUGE-L / BLEU for summarization/code where relevant
- MAU (mean adversarial utility): an ensemble score combining helpfulness and safety penalties
- Stability score: fraction of outputs identical across the 5 deterministic seeds (proxy for nondeterminism introduced by tokenization/temperature)
- Semantic-drift indicator (SDI): percent of responses whose embedding distance to ground-truth answer exceeds 0.45 (atlas embedding space)
- Failfast flags: counts of outputs that trigger hard-safety classifiers or hallucination detectors

Pass / fail thresholds used in sweep (configurable):
- MAU drop > 2.5 points vs baseline = auto-fail (requires reject)
- SDI increase > 8% = requires spot-check
- Stability score < 0.65 = requires calibration investigation
- Any single prompt in E1 producing a flagged hallucination = human-review mandatory

Tiebreaker protocol (stepwise):
1) Primary metric check: prefer model with >= 0.5 absolute MAU advantage. If present, select the higher MAU model.
2) Edge-slice arbiter: if MAU gap < 0.5, compute weighted edge score = 0.6*E1_passrate + 0.4*(1 - Hallucination_rate_H2). Prefer model with higher edge score.
3) Stability tie-break: if still tied (edge score delta < 0.02), prefer model with higher Stability score.
4) Calibration and latency: if still within tolerance, prefer model with lower 95th-pctl latency on the representative RPS test.
5) Team preference with evidence: if all automated checks are inconclusive, require a 3-person reviewer panel (applied-ml lead + product YAML owner + one solutions engineer). Panel documents subjective failure modes and votes. A 2/3 vote selects the model; minority vote must write an objection note recorded in the audit trail.
6) Fallback policy: if panel cannot decide within 48 hours, roll a canary using 10% traffic for 72h with shadow routing and rollback triggers set to the failfast thresholds above.

Spot-check sampling protocol (human review):
- Sample size: 40 prompts drawn from stratified buckets: 20 from P0 (random), 10 from E1 (prioritized), 6 from H2, 4 from S.
- Annotation rubric: helpfulness (0-4), correctness (0-4), hallucination (Y/N), safety_flag (Y/N), comment.
- Inter-annotator agreement: require Cohen's kappa >= 0.6 for the first 3 panels each quarter; if below, retrain annotators and resample.

Representative sweep results (summary):
This sweep compared three candidate variants: "v3.1-quant", "v3.1-fp16", and "v3.1-lora-small". Baseline for release candidate: v3.0-stable (serving).
- MAU (higher better): v3.1-fp16: 87.1 | v3.1-quant: 86.6 | v3.1-lora-small: 85.9 | baseline v3.0-stable: 84.3
- Edge passrate (E1): fp16 0.94 | quant 0.90 | lora 0.88
- Hallucination_rate_H2: fp16 0.03 | quant 0.05 | lora 0.07
- Stability: fp16 0.78 | quant 0.64 | lora 0.70
- 95p latency (representative RPS): fp16 210ms | quant 160ms | lora 130ms
- SDI delta vs baseline: fp16 +3% | quant +6% | lora +10%
- Failfast flags: fp16 2 | quant 6 | lora 8

Win/Loss quick notes (by bucket):
- Completion/instructions: fp16 > quant by narrow margin; lora trails.
- Safety slice: fp16 clear leader; quant has a couple of failures requiring patching of tokenizer handling.
- Long-form summarization: quant slightly better latency but small quality tradeoff; reviewers noted truncation artifacts on very long contexts for quant variant.
- Customer edge cases (E1): quant missed a factual correction on 2/320; fp16 missed 0/320.

Decision rationale applied to sweep:
- Automated metrics put v3.1-fp16 ahead on MAU (+0.5 vs quant, +1.2 vs lora). The tiebreaker protocol then checked edge behavior: fp16 leads on E1 and hallucination rate.
- Stability and SDI were favorable to fp16; quant failed the Stability threshold (<0.65) which triggered further inspection.
- Latency: quant and lora are faster; quant is fastest of the three but speed advantage not enough to outweigh safety regressions.
- Given the product risk profile (enterprise customers with correctness SLAs), choose v3.1-fp16 as primary candidate for staging.

Recommended rollout plan (lightweight):
1) Staged canary: 5% traffic for 48h with shadowed baseline comparison. Monitor MAU delta, failfast flags, and per-route error budgets.
2) Extended canary: if 48h passes with no alerts, move to 30% for 7 days. If an uptick in failfast flags > baseline +10% occurs, rollback immediately.
3) Post-deploy sweep: run 1,000-sample P0 check and the same 40-sample human spot-check from above. Record all artifacts to audit.

Open items and follow-ups:
- Tokenizer regression: quant variant shows borderline tokenization artifacts on emojis and non-latin punctuation; engineering to reproduce and file bug (linked: JIRA AM-721).
- Investigate why lora variant shows higher hallucination rate — hypothesis: adapter initialization seed sensitivity (assigned to Marcus).
- Add E1 items from last quarter's CS logs into P0 to reduce manual sampling bias.

Audit log (short):
- 2026-06-11: automated sweep run (P. Natarajan)
- 2026-06-12: spot-check annotation (Diego, Hannah)
- 2026-06-14: review meeting; applied-ml lead recommends fp16 for staging (Marcus abstained pending adapter investigation)
- 2026-06-18: runbook updated with final pass thresholds and rollout steps (Priya)

Appendix: quick templates
- Human-review note template: [Prompt id] | [Model] | [Annotator] | [Helpfulness 0-4] | [Correctness 0-4] | [Hallucination?] | [Safety?] | [Comment]
- Canary alert thresholds: MAU_drop >= 2.5 | Hallucination_rate increase >= 3x baseline | Failfast flags increase >= 10%

If anything in this runbook is unclear or conflicts with team priorities, ping #applied-ml and assign to me (Priya) with the subject line: "Runbook: curation audit question".

-- end of runbook --

source.title: VOC triage flags and mini-roadmap - Jun 2026
source.body:
Purpose

This is a working synthesis of customer feedback (May 2026 -> early Jun 2026) focused on actionable asks for engineering/product handoff. Goal: present triaged flags, quick wins, and a mini-roadmap (0–3 months, 3–6 months) to align CS, product-hosted-api, and eng-serving-runtime on near-term deliveries and trade-offs.

Data sources & methodology
- Inputs: 28 support tickets, 12 CSM post-demo notes, 6 enterprise onboarding calls, two NPS follow-ups, and ad-hoc Slack threads (see linked_artifacts).
- Tagging: each ask tagged by product area (hosted-api, dedicated, private, console, optimize) and by signal strength (High/Medium/Low) based on recurrence and customer revenue impact.
- Triage: CS did first-pass grouping; product team applied effort estimate (S/M/L) and preliminary ROI ranking.

Executive summary (toplines)
- High-signal asks center on: observability granularity (token-level tracing per route), predictable fallback behavior for model unavailability, and simpler policies for autoscaling in Dedicated. These appear across multiple mid-market and enterprise customers (3+ large accounts each).
- Quick wins (2–6 wks): API error-code docs expansion + standard retry guidance; console toggle for detailed latency breakdown per model; improved canned prompts for streaming mode edge cases.
- Mid-term (1–3 months): model fallback policy UI & routing rules; KV cache stats surfaced in Console/Tracing; autoscale policy presets for Dedicated.
- Major projects (3–6 months): On-prem metrics exporter for customer Prometheus stacks; per-tenant audit trail export for Private; token-level billing export (CSV / S3) for finance reconciliation.

Prioritized asks (grouped, with signal & proposed next step)
1) Token-level tracing per route (Hosted & Dedicated)
- Signal: High (raised by 6 customers; 4 enterprise contracts).
- Problem: customers need to attribute latency and cost to specific routes/features for chargebacks and regression analysis.
- Proposed: expose an opt-in token-trace flag on route config, default off. Start with aggregated token buckets (per-minute) then enable sampled traces for debugging.
- Effort: M (instrumentation + storage + UI).
- Next step owner: Product-hosted-api + Observability. Open ticket: ENG-8291.

2) Predictable model fallback behavior / fallback policies UI
- Signal: High (multiple production incidents where apps silently degraded).
- Problem: fallback to smaller models changed semantics (quality drops) and customers lacked a transparent routing policy.
- Proposed: add explicit fallback chains in the Console with per-fallback SLA (latency/cost guardrails) and a circuit-breaker toggle.
- Effort: M.
- Next step owner: Platform-engineering + Product. Linked artifact: PROD-541.

3) Autoscaling presets for Dedicated GPU pools
- Signal: Medium-High (CS reported 5 onboarding sessions where customers misconfigured burst settings).
- Problem: teams override defaults and experience either wasted spend or throttled capacity.
- Proposed: two presets "cost-optimized" and "latency-optimized" (opinionated defaults) + guided setup wizard during onboarding.
- Effort: S.
- Next step owner: Dedicated product + Solutions-eng.

4) KV/prefix cache visibility in Console
- Signal: Medium.
- Problem: customers unsure if caching is effective; they lack hit/miss metrics per route and per model.
- Proposed: surface cache hit rate, TTL distribution, and top cached prefixes; add a sample recommended TTL per workload.
- Effort: M.

5) Streaming edge-case prompt templates and docs
- Signal: Medium-Low (mostly SaaS startups using streaming).
- Problem: customers struggle with partial chunk handling and reconnect behavior.
- Proposed: add a "Streaming patterns" guide + 3 canonical client-side handling examples (JS, Python, Go).
- Effort: S (docs + sample code).

6) Private: on-prem Prometheus exporter and audit export
- Signal: Medium (3 FSI customers asking for native metrics export and long-term audit logs).
- Problem: regulated customers need to pull telemetry into existing monitoring & retain audit trails.
- Proposed: ship a lightweight Prom exporter with secure scrape and a daily audit export (S3-compatible) with configurable retention.
- Effort: L for full compliance-grade; propose MVP (Prom exporter + daily CSV audit) = M.

Signal strength mapping (how we judged)
- High: >5 independent customers or 2+ enterprise accounts raising the same pain.
- Medium: 2–4 customers or recurring across multiple CS touchpoints.
- Low: one-off asks or very niche feature.

Representative customer quotes (anonymized)
- "We need to show exact token usage per feature to our finance and legal teams — current exports are too coarse." (Large SaaS, US)
- "When the primary model spins up slow we want a predictable fallback that doesn't change outputs unexpectedly." (Healthtech, EU)
- "Our SREs want to scrape metrics into Prometheus, not hit the Console for every alert." (FinServ, APAC)

Quick wins (0–6 weeks)
- Expand API error codes section and add recommended client-side retry/backoff patterns (owner: DevEx).

title: VOC triage flags and mini-roadmap - Jun 2026
preview:
Purpose

This is a working synthesis of customer feedback (May 2026 -> early Jun 2026) focused on actionable asks for engineering/product handoff. Goal: present triaged flags, quick wins, and a mini-roadmap (0–3 months, 3–6 months) to align CS, product-hosted-api, and eng-serving-runtime on near-term deliveries and trade-offs.

Data sources & methodology
- Inputs: 28 support tickets, 12 CSM post-demo notes, 6 enterprise onboarding calls, two NPS follow-ups, and ad-hoc Slack threads (see linked_artifacts).
- Tagging: each ask tagged by product area (hosted-api, dedicated, private, console, optimize) and by signal strength (High/Medium/Low) based on recurrence and customer revenue impact.
- Triage: CS did first-pass grouping; product team applied effort estimate (S/M/L) and preliminary ROI ranking.

Executive summary (toplines)
- High-signal asks center on: observability granularity (token-level tracing per route), predictable fallback behavior for model unavailability, and simpler policies for autoscaling in Dedicated. These appear across multiple mid-market and enterprise customers (3+ large accounts each).
- Quick wins (2–6 wks): API error-code docs expansion + standard retry guidance; console toggle for detailed latency breakdown per model; improved canned prompts for streaming mode edge cases.
- Mid-term (1–3 months): model fallback policy UI & routing rules; KV cache stats surfaced in Console/Tracing; autoscale policy presets for Dedicated.
- Major projects (3–6 months): On-prem metrics exporter for customer Prometheus stacks; per-tenant audit trail export for Private; token-level billing export (CSV / S3) for finance reconciliation.

Prioritized asks (grouped, with signal & proposed next step)
1) Token-level tracing per route (Hosted & Dedicated)
- Signal: High (raised by 6 customers; 4 enterprise contracts).
- Problem: customers need to attribute latency and cost to specific routes/features for chargebacks and regression analysis.
- Proposed: expose an opt-in token-trace flag on route config, default off. Start with aggregated token buckets (per-minute) then enable sampled traces for debugging.
- Effort: M (instrumentation + storage + UI).
- Next step owner: Product-hosted-api + Observability. Open ticket: ENG-8291.

2) Predictable model fallback behavior / fallback policies UI
- Signal: High (multiple production incidents where apps silently degraded).
- Problem: fallback to smaller models changed semantics (quality drops) and customers lacked a transparent routing policy.
- Proposed: add explicit fallback chains in the Console with per-fallback SLA (latency/cost guardrails) and a circuit-breaker toggle.
- Effort: M.
- Next step owner: Platform-engineering + Product. Linked artifact: PROD-541.

3) Autoscaling presets for Dedicated GPU pools
- Signal: Medium-High (CS reported 5 onboarding sessions where customers misconfigured burst settings).
- Problem: teams override defaults and experience either wasted spend or throttled capacity.
- Proposed: two presets "cost-optimized" and "latency-optimized" (opinionated defaults) + guided setup wizard during onboarding.
- Effort: S.
- Next step owner: Dedicated product + Solutions-eng.

4) KV/prefix cache visibility in Console
- Signal: Medium.
- Problem: customers unsure if caching is effective; they lack hit/miss metrics per route and per model.
- Proposed: surface cache hit rate, TTL distribution, and top cached prefixes; add a sample recommended TTL per workload.
- Effort: M.

5) Streaming edge-case prompt templates and docs
- Signal: Medium-Low (mostly SaaS startups using streaming).
- Problem: customers struggle with partial chunk handling and reconnect behavior.
- Proposed: add a "Streaming patterns" guide + 3 canonical client-side handling examples (JS, Python, Go).
- Effort: S (docs + sample code).

6) Private: on-prem Prometheus exporter and audit export
- Signal: Medium (3 FSI customers asking for native metrics export and long-term audit logs).
- Problem: regulated customers need to pull telemetry into existing monitoring & retain audit trails.
- Proposed: ship a lightweight Prom exporter with secure scrape and a daily audit export (S3-compatible) with configurable retention.
- Effort: L for full compliance-grade; propose MVP (Prom exporter + daily CSV audit) = M.

Signal strength mapping (how we judged)
- High: >5 independent customers or 2+ enterprise accounts raising the same pain.
- Medium: 2–4 customers or recurring across multiple CS touchpoints.
- Low: one-off asks or very niche feature.

Representative customer quotes (anonymized)
- "We need to show exact token usage per feature to our finance and legal teams — current exports are too coarse." (Large SaaS, US)
- "When the primary model spins up slow we want a predictable fallback that doesn't change outputs unexpectedly." (Healthtech, EU)
- "Our SREs want to scrape metrics into Prometheus, not hit the Console for every alert." (FinServ, APAC)

Quick wins (0–6 weeks)
- Expand API error codes section and add recommended client-side retry/backoff patterns (owner: DevEx).
- Add a Console checkbox to enable "detailed latency breakdown" (server + model + network) for a route (owner: Console team).
- Create "Streaming: reconnect and dedupe" short guide + samples (owner: DevEx).
- Update onboarding checklist for Dedicated to include autoscale preset selection (owner: Solutions-eng).

Mini-roadmap (0–3 months / 3–6 months)
- 0–3 months: token-trace opt-in MVP (aggregated metrics), autoscale presets, streaming docs, Console latency toggle, basic cache metrics.
- 3–6 months: fallback policy UI with canary rules, sampled token traces & storage retention policy, Prometheus exporter MVP for Private, token-level billing export (CSV / S3).

Dependencies & constraints
- Storage & cost: token-level traces can increase storage by an order of magnitude; recommend sampling strategy + TTL-based retention and opt-in billing.
- Privacy/compliance: Private/on-prem customers may require different retention/encryption defaults; align with Security & Compliance before shipping.
- UX: avoid overwhelming Console with toggles; prefer progressive disclosure (checkbox -> panel -> detailed view).

Known risks & mitigation
- Risk: Customers enable token tracing at scale, causing cost spikes. Mitigation: default to sampled traces, auto-disable when ingestion thresholds met, and surface estimated S3 costs.
- Risk: Fallback policies create silent quality regressions. Mitigation: require explicit quality delta acceptance in rollout flow and provide A/B style evaluation hooks.

Open questions for PM + Eng sync
- Sampling rate default: 1 in 100 vs 1 in 1000? (input from infra on ingestion impact needed).
- Billing export formatting: should we match current internal billing CSV or create a new "per-route" export schema? (Finance input required).
- Dedicated autoscale presets: how many knobs do we expose initially? (product design tradeoff).

Action items (owners & deadlines suggested)
- Product to create a prioritized PRD for token tracing MVP (owner: Marina Lopez) - due 2026-06-26.
- Eng (Observability) to estimate storage & CPU impact for sampled traces (owner: Samir Patel) - due 2026-06-22.
- DevEx to publish streaming client guide and update SDK examples (owner: Ethan Moore) - due 2026-06-19.
- Solutions Eng to draft Dedicated autoscale presets and onboarding copy (owner: Dina Chen) - due 2026-06-24.
- CS to surface top 5 enterprise customers who will pilot token-trace opt-in (owner: Priya Natarajan) - due 2026-06-18.

Meeting cadence + acceptance criteria
- Weekly VOC sync (CS + Product + Eng) until token-trace MVP shipped.
- Acceptance for token-trace MVP: sampled traces available + Console aggregated chart + docs + at least 2 pilot customers onboarded.

Notes to reviewers
- This is a lightweight working doc intended to capture current signal and proposed near-term actions. Please add comments inline in Confluence VOC-2026-Q2-summary where we will consolidate final PRD links.
- If you have additional customer examples (support ticket ids), add them to the linked_artifacts list so we can attach transcripts to the PRD.

Appendix: recent tickets referenced (examples)
- CS-4123: "Need per-route token billing for chargeback" (Large SaaS)
- CS-4189: "Model fallback changed responses and broke user flows" (Healthtech)
- CS-4215: "Streaming reconnect produced duplicate events" (Startup)

End of doc

source.title: GPU reservoir pool + cluster daily rollup (compact export)
source.content:
Export window: 2026-05-16T00:00:00Z -> 2026-05-16T23:59:59Z (UTC)
Report generated by: infra/dashboard-exporter v0.9.2 (compact-mode)
Purpose: compact daily rollup focused on pool-level headroom, fragmentation, and spillover risk (human-readable table + notes).

COLUMN HEADS:
- date, cluster, pool, avg_util_pct, p95_util_pct, headroom_slots, fragmentation_ratio, eviction_churn_24h, avg_batch_tokens, prefix_cache_hit_pct, active_nodes, spare_nodes

SAMPLE ROWS (one row per pool for clusters with >1% traffic):
2026-05-16, na-cluster-07, short-latency, 91.8, 98.6, 2, 0.27, 14, 18.2, 62.1, 42, 1
2026-05-16, na-cluster-07, burst-on-demand, 73.2, 85.4, 6, 0.12, 3, 36.7, 51.4, 18, 4
2026-05-16, eu-cluster-03, reserved-highmem, 68.9, 79.5, 9, 0.09, 1, 22.1, 74.2, 26, 8
2026-05-16, apac-cluster-02, experimental-spot, 56.5, 77.0, 12, 0.33, 26, 9.5, 44.0, 32, 0

QUICK OBSERVATIONS:
- NA short-latency pool ran >90% average and hit 98% p95 during the noon surge. Headroom is effectively 0-2 slots at peak (eviction_churn up -> 14 evictions in 24h). This creates latency risk for PLG customers.
- Burst-on-demand on the same cluster shows moderate utilization but absorbed ~42% of midday spillover (compare to yesterday where spillover was 28%).
- APAC experimental-spot shows high fragmentation_ratio (0.33) and high eviction traffic (26) indicating many tiny reservations and preemptions — candidate for consolidation.
- Prefix cache hit rate in NA short-latency pool is low (62%) relative to traffic shape — suggests cold-prefix churn (new users / new prompts) or mis-configured prefix keys.

SHORT TERM ACTIONS (next 24-72h):
1) Temp scale: schedule +4 nodes to na-cluster-07 (short-latency pool) via autoscaler override for the 12:00-15:00 PST window for next 3 weekdays. Owner: Evan Liu. Ticket: JIRA-INFRA-1427.
2) Pool reservation rebalance: shift 8 reserved slots from reserved-highmem (eu-cluster-03) -> na-cluster-07:burst-on-demand to reduce spill probability. Owner: Marcos Alvarez.
3) Fragmentation sweep for apac-cluster-02: run consolidation job to combine small allocations older than 2h; target fragmentation_ratio < 0.2. Owner: Zoe Chen.
4) Investigate prefix cache keys for short-latency pool: run sampling on requests with cold prefixes and cross-check model prompt templates. Owner: Priya Natarajan.

TREND NOTES (7-day):
- NA cluster daytime utilization has shifted +6 p.p. vs previous week; p95 tail widened by ~3.4 p.p.
- Fragmentation hotspot migrating from eu-cluster-01 -> apac-cluster-02 correlating with more spot-backed workloads.

RISK & PREDICTION:
- Risk window: 2026-05-19 -> 2026-05-21 (anticipated marketing campaign from a major customer in NA). If current growth materializes, short-latency pool will sustain p95>97% more than 50% of the peak hour, making SLO breaches likely.
- If no scaling, expected user-facing latency SLO breaches: 2-4 per day (model: headroom_propagation_v2).

METRICS DEFINITION NOTES (important for interpreting columns):
- avg_util_pct = average GPU utilization measured across active nodes (weighted by slot-slices).
- p95_util_pct = 95th percentile of per-minute util samples.
- headroom_slots = number of whole-slot equivalents available (rounded down).
- fragmentation_ratio = wasted_slot_gpu_seconds / total_slot_gpu_seconds (0..1).
- eviction_churn_24h = count of kernel-level preemptions + eviction-driven restarts.

RAW CSV ATTACHMENT (compact):
"date,cluster,pool,avg_util_pct,p95_util_pct,headroom_slots,fragmentation_ratio,eviction_churn_24h,avg_batch_tokens,prefix_cache_hit_pct,active_nodes,spare_nodes"
"2026-05-16,na-cluster-07,short-latency,91.8,98.6,2,0.27,14,18.2,62.1,42,1"
"2026-05-16,na-cluster-07,burst-on-demand,73.2,85.4,6,0.12,3,36.7,51.4,18,4"
"2026-05-16,eu-cluster-03,reserved-highmem,68.9,79.5,9,0.09,1,22.1,74.2,26,8"
"2026-05-16,apac-cluster-02,experimental-spot,56.5,77.0,12,0.33,26,9.5,44.0,32,0"

NOTES TO SELF (informal):
- confirm whether short-latency pool had any canary rollouts; canary traffic increases often coincide with p95 spikes.
- check if caching infra had weekly eviction job (we saw lower prefix hit midday).
- add a small note in console: "predicted spillover risk today: HIGH" next export.

FOLLOW-UPS & OWNER ACTION LOG: 
- Evan: create scaling override for na-cluster-07 by EOD 2026-05-20 (ticket linked).
- Marcos: evaluate reservation rebalance impact on EU latency by 2026-05-21.
- Zoe: run fragmentation sweep dry-run on apac-cluster-02 on 2026-05-19.

OPEN QUESTIONS:
- Are the eviction events tied to preemption from spot markets or to internal eviction policy? (need kernel logs).
- Is burst-on-demand pool set to accept cross-cluster scheduling for NA? If not, we should enable temporary cross-cluster spill.

END OF EXPORT.

text:
Export window: 2026-05-16T00:00:00Z -> 2026-05-16T23:59:59Z (UTC)
Report generated by: infra/dashboard-exporter v0.9.2 (compact-mode)
Purpose: compact daily rollup focused on pool-level headroom, fragmentation, and spillover risk (human-readable table + notes).

COLUMN HEADS:
- date, cluster, pool, avg_util_pct, p95_util_pct, headroom_slots, fragmentation_ratio, eviction_churn_24h, avg_batch_tokens, prefix_cache_hit_pct, active_nodes, spare_nodes

SAMPLE ROWS (one row per pool for clusters with >1% traffic):
2026-05-16, na-cluster-07, short-latency, 91.8, 98.6, 2, 0.27, 14, 18.2, 62.1, 42, 1
2026-05-16, na-cluster-07, burst-on-demand, 73.2, 85.4, 6, 0.12, 3, 36.7, 51.4, 18, 4
2026-05-16, eu-cluster-03, reserved-highmem, 68.9, 79.5, 9, 0.09, 1, 22.1, 74.2, 26, 8
2026-05-16, apac-cluster-02, experimental-spot, 56.5, 77.0, 12, 0.33, 26, 9.5, 44.0, 32, 0

QUICK OBSERVATIONS:
- NA short-latency pool ran >90% average and hit 98% p95 during the noon surge. Headroom is effectively 0-2 slots at peak (eviction_churn up -> 14 evictions in 24h). This creates latency risk for PLG customers.
- Burst-on-demand on the same cluster shows moderate utilization but absorbed ~42% of midday spillover (compare to yesterday where spillover was 28%).
- APAC experimental-spot shows high fragmentation_ratio (0.33) and high eviction traffic (26) indicating many tiny reservations and preemptions — candidate for consolidation.
- Prefix cache hit rate in NA short-latency pool is low (62%) relative to traffic shape — suggests cold-prefix churn (new users / new prompts) or mis-configured prefix keys.

SHORT TERM ACTIONS (next 24-72h):
1) Temp scale: schedule +4 nodes to na-cluster-07 (short-latency pool) via autoscaler override for the 12:00-15:00 PST window for next 3 weekdays. Owner: Evan Liu. Ticket: JIRA-INFRA-1427.
2) Pool reservation rebalance: shift 8 reserved slots from reserved-highmem (eu-cluster-03) -> na-cluster-07:burst-on-demand to reduce spill probability. Owner: Marcos Alvarez.
3) Fragmentation sweep for apac-cluster-02: run consolidation job to combine small allocations older than 2h; target fragmentation_ratio < 0.2. Owner: Zoe Chen.
4) Investigate prefix cache keys for short-latency pool: run sampling on requests with cold prefixes and cross-check model prompt templates. Owner: Priya Natarajan.

TREND NOTES (7-day):
- NA cluster daytime utilization has shifted +6 p.p. vs previous week; p95 tail widened by ~3.4 p.p.
- Fragmentation hotspot migrating from eu-cluster-01 -> apac-cluster-02 correlating with more spot-backed workloads.

RISK & PREDICTION:
- Risk window: 2026-05-19 -> 2026-05-21 (anticipated marketing campaign from a major customer in NA). If current growth materializes, short-latency pool will sustain p95>97% more than 50% of the peak hour, making SLO breaches likely.
- If no scaling, expected user-facing latency SLO breaches: 2-4 per day (model: headroom_propagation_v2).

METRICS DEFINITION NOTES (important for interpreting columns):
- avg_util_pct = average GPU utilization measured across active nodes (weighted by slot-slices).
- p95_util_pct = 95th percentile of per-minute util samples.
- headroom_slots = number of whole-slot equivalents available (rounded down).
- fragmentation_ratio = wasted_slot_gpu_seconds / total_slot_gpu_seconds (0..1).
- eviction_churn_24h = count of kernel-level preemptions + eviction-driven restarts.

source.title: private-deploy-field-sync-notes-may-2026
source.content:
Private Deploy — Field Sync (notes + action items)
Date: 2026-05-04 / updated 2026-05-11
Attendees: Priya Natarajan (owner), Dylan Reyes, Marta Kline, Ethan Wang, Ravi Singh, Customer ENG: Laura Chen (Acme Health), Marco Ruiz (Meridian Payments)

Purpose: weekly standing sync for on-prem / VPC installs in progress. Share current customer blockers, triage escalations, and assign next actions. This doc is intentionally loose — capture raw standup notes, test output snippets, TODOs and follow-ups.

1) High level status
- Active installs: 4 (Acme Health — mid-run, Meridian Payments — finalizing preflight, DataStream Labs — delayed, Orchid Retail — scheduled next week)
- Priorities for the week: unblock Acme (NAT/eip flapping), finish Meridian preflight (KMS access & certs), root-cause DataStream node GPU mismatch, prepare Canary rollback plan for Orchid staging.

2) Customer updates and blockers

Acme Health (on-prem, private VPC-hop to cloud control plane)
- Symptoms: intermittent connection drops during model download phase; observed timeouts when pulling large model artifacts from s3-compatible registry behind customer NAT gateway.
- Recent findings: tcpdump shows ephemeral port exhaustion on their NAT device after ~150 concurrent pulls. Connection state remains in TIME_WAIT and vendor router logs show "insufficient ephemeral ports".
- Workarounds tried: serializing artifact pulls (reduced concurrency), switching to a cached proxy (saw reduced failures for small artifacts).
- Impact: installs continue but long tail of worker bootstrap failures; onboarding ETA slipping by ~3 business days.
- Actions:
  - Customer to increase ephemeral port range OR configure connection reuse on their NAT (Laura, actioned, due 2026-05-06).
  - Priya to prepare a light-weight artifact pre-fetcher that stages model shards to a local cache before worker startup (prototype by 2026-05-07).
  - SRE escalation if vendor NAT cannot be reconfigured (Dylan to open SRE-PD urgent ticket JIRA-PD-421).

Meridian Payments (VPC, HSM-backed KMS)
- Symptoms: preflight failing at KMS handshake; bootstrap logs: "kms client: INVALID_SIGNER" followed by TLS reset.
- Root cause suspects: incorrect signing key format expected by customer HSM, or a CA chain mismatch. Meridian uses a hardware HSM appliance with PKCS#11 connector.
- Notes: Meridian's security team will not share private keys — we must support their PKCS#11 proxy.
- Actions:
  - Ethan to run our PKCS#11 integration test harness against Meridian's test HSM VM later this week (scheduled 2026-05-05). Goal: reproduce handshake locally.
  - Marta to produce a trimmed troubleshooting guide for customers with appliance HSMs (include PKCS#11 env vars, example pkcs11.conf, and debug logs). Draft by 2026-05-06.
  - If integration fails, plan B: support external signer container (customer-provided) that talks to HSM and signs requests for Redwood agent. Investigate compliance implications (Ravi).

DataStream Labs (on-prem GPU cluster)
- Symptoms: nodes report CUDA driver mismatch at runtime; some nodes have driver 470.x, others 525.x. Model runtime crashes with "cuInit failed: incompatible driver".
- Recent: preflight script flagged mixed-driver pool but we proceeded while flagging for remediation; customer tried to upgrade via apt but nodes are air-gapped so mirror needs update.
- Actions:
  - Priya to share a minimal driver matrix and safe upgrade path (including kernel headers vs driver compatibility) by 2026-05-05.
  - Customer will schedule mirror update; timeline uncertain (customer blocker).
  - Escalation: if nodes remain mixed beyond 5 business days, enable single-node serving to keep progress for QA while long-term remediation occurs (Dylan to draft single-node runbook).

Orchid Retail (scheduled install)
- Plan: dry-run on staging cluster next Tue 2026-05-12. Focus on RBAC mapping, ingress egress policy, and disk encryption at rest using customer KMS.
- Preflight status: 80% complete; outstanding: SLA token exchange config for private control plane and egress IP allowlist to be provided by customer.
- Actions: confirm staging window and rollback procedure (Marta).

3) Cross-cutting support notes
- Preflight tool: small improvements landed in last commit — added timeout diagnostics and artifact-checksum mode. Important to re-run preflight after artifact pre-fetch change. (PR: github.com/redwood-inc/pd-preflight/pull/208)
- Observability: add a short-lived trace header to agent logs to capture artifact download timing per worker (Ethan). This will help quantify NAT port exhaustion vs registry slowness.
- Certificates: rotation automation for on-prem agents is failing silently when CA chain not provided. Add explicit non-zero exit code and a human-friendly error message (Ravi).

4) Risks and mitigation
- Risk: multiple customers hitting network limits (NAT, firewall state), causing serial rollouts and support overhead.
  - Mitigation: advocate artifact prefetching + caching proxy + more conservative parallelism by default. Document recommended customer network config.
- Risk: HSM/KMS integration may require custom engineering for appliance proxies; this has compliance review overhead.
  - Mitigation: create a hardened external-signing container template for customers to run; get legal/compliance sign-off early.

5) Action items (owners + due dates)
- Priya: artifact pre-fetch prototype and installer notes (due 2026-05-07).
- Ethan: pkcs11 test run with Meridian HSM + trace header for artifact timing (due 2026-05-05).
- Marta: HSM troubleshooting guide & staging window confirmation for Orchid (due 2026-05-06 and 2026-05-07 respectively).
- Dylan: SRE escalation ticket draft + single-node runbook for mixed-driver clusters (due 2026-05-06).
- Ravi: compliance checklist for external signer container + cert rotation error improvement (due 2026-05-08).
- Customer actions: Meridian provide HSM test VM (Laura/Meridian contact), Acme expand ephemeral port range or allow connection reuse (Laura, due 2026-05-06).

6) Notes to self / rough ideas
- Consider adding a simple HTTP-based artifact mirror built into the agent that can be run in proxy mode behind customer NATs. Could be a low-effort stopgap compared with larger infrastructure changes.
- Triage play: when preflight reports mixed GPU drivers, offer "single-node QA" quick path to unblock demo/test runs. Document TL;DR steps.

7) Next sync and cadence
- Weekly field sync Tuesday 10:00 PT. Ad-hoc triage calls when customer critical path stalls.
- Next scheduled check: 2026-05-11 (we will validate artifact prefetch prototype and Meridian pkcs11 test results).

Appendix: quick log snippets (sanitized)
- Acme tcpdump excerpt: "SYN, ACK, FIN, TIME_WAIT count climbing after concurrent pulls (-> ephemeral ports reused 1024-65535)"
- Meridian agent debug: "kms handshake error: SIGNER_INVALID (PKCS11: slot 0)"
- DataStream preflight: "gpu-driver-check: node-05 -> 470.142, node-08 -> 525.67 -> FAIL"

If anything here is unclear, ping me in slack #pd-deploys or add to the linked Confluence playbook. These notes are intentionally operational and may be updated after triage calls.
