source.title: Buffer Sizing Sensitivity Study — Autoscaler Trigger Experiments
source.content:
Overview:
Purpose: quantify sensitivity of latency, cold-start events, and unit cost to different 'buffer' sizing strategies and min/max node bounds across regions. This is a lightweight spreadsheet-style experiment log and the distilled numbers we discussed in the infra sync (4/20).

Hypotheses:
- Small buffer (1-2 spare GPUs) reduces cost but may increase cold-starts during bursty traffic, hurting p95 latency.
- Larger buffer (3-5) stabilizes p95 at the expense of steady-state cost.
- Region-level traffic patterns (APAC burstiness vs NA steady) change the optimal min setting.

Experiment matrix (each row = single experiment run, 48h window, synthetic workload with 30m peak bursts every 3h):

Columns: experiment_id | region | min_gpus | max_gpus | trigger_up_util% | trigger_down_util% | buffer_size_spare_gpus | observed_cold_starts_total | p95_latency_ms | avg_util_peak% | cost_delta_vs-baseline% | notes

E1 | us-west1 | 2 | 20 | 75 | 45 | 1 | 48 | 210 | 86 | -7.1 | small buffer -> frequent cold starts during first burst after idle
E2 | us-west1 | 2 | 20 | 80 | 50 | 3 | 8 | 145 | 82 | +2.8 | 3 spare GPUs reduces cold-starts significantly, slight cost up
E3 | us-west1 | 4 | 20 | 80 | 50 | 4 | 3 | 132 | 79 | +6.5 | higher min reduces autoscale churn; p95 best but cost higher
E4 | europe-west1 | 1 | 12 | 70 | 40 | 1 | 22 | 235 | 88 | -9.4 | EU bursts shorter but more frequent -> 1 spare too low
E5 | europe-west1 | 2 | 12 | 75 | 45 | 2 | 7 | 160 | 84 | -0.9 | moderate buffer balances cost/latency
E6 | asia-east1 | 1 | 16 | 85 | 55 | 1 | 72 | 320 | 92 | -12.7 | APAC traffic spiky; min=1 leads to many cold starts and high p95
E7 | asia-east1 | 2 | 16 | 85 | 55 | 3 | 12 | 175 | 88 | -3.0 | 3 spare heads helps but cost still down vs baseline due to short idle windows
E8 | multi-region (us+eu) | 3 | 28 | 80 | 50 | 2 | 18 | 150 | 81 | +1.2 | cross-region routing smoothing reduces peak in single region; moderate buffer ok
E9 | us-west1 | 0 | 10 | 70 | 35 | 0 | 150 | 410 | 95 | -18.6 | min=0 leads to large latency spikes and many cold starts -> unacceptable for SLO apps
E10 | us-west1 | 1 | 10 | 70 | 35 | 2 | 40 | 210 | 89 | -9.9 | small min with raised buffer halves cold-starts compared to min=0

Metric notes:
- observed_cold_starts_total: counted node-level cold-starts that caused >200ms extra token latency (aggregate over run).
- p95_latency_ms: generation p95 for target benchmark prompt set (w/ 8k context).
- cost_delta_vs-baseline%: baseline = fleet with min=3, max=20, buffer=2 in us-west baseline run from March 2026 billing sample. Negative = cheaper.

Qualitative observations from logs and traces:
- Cold-starts correlated with async batch queue growth: when queue hits threshold and scaler kicks, first evictions/loads cause KV cache warms and 150-500ms token delays for initial requests.
- Node churn increased when trigger_up <= 75 and trigger_down <= 45 with low min; in practice hysteresis at controller level had to be tuned (see linked MR 472).
- APAC region's short bursts (~6-12s spikes) cause repeated scale-ups when buffer < 2 even if average utilization low.

Quick sensitivity summaries:
- p95 latency is highly sensitive to buffer_size when min_gpus <= 2 (delta up to +200–300ms p95).
- Cost increases roughly linearly with min_gpus; buffer contributes a smaller steady cost but yields disproportionate latency improvements.
- Raising trigger_up from 75 to 80 decreased unnecessary scale-ups in US experiments by ~12% at the expense of slightly higher peak util.

Operational recommendations (practical runbook items):
1) Region presets: default min for APAC = 2, EU = 1, NA = 2 (for production-facing model routes). Rationale: APAC burstiness requires at least 2 warm GPUs.
2) Buffer policy: prefer spare buffer of 2–3 GPUs for high-SLO routes. Use 1 spare for low-cost dev/experimental routes.
3) Trigger tuning: use trigger_up=80 / trigger_down=50 for NA/EU; trigger_up=85 / trigger_down=55 for APAC where bursts are shortest but frequent.
4) Hysteresis: add 3-minute stabilization window before scale-down to avoid thrash on short bursts (had notable effect in E2/E5).
5) Canary rollout plan: apply preset to 10% of production routes (non-critical) for 1 week, measure cold-starts and p95 regressions, then expand.

Follow-ups / open questions:
- Repeat runs with mixed-model workloads (long context vs short) — current runs used single 8k prompt profile.
- Evaluate KV cache pre-warming strategies (warm snapshots) vs buffer-only economics; warm snapshots may reduce buffer need by 1–2 GPUs.
- Validate with real customer traces (requested from CS) for two enterprise customers with APAC-heavy traffic.

Raw experiment CSV snippet (first 5 rows):
experiment_id,region,min_gpus,max_gpus,trigger_up,trigger_down,buffer_spare, cold_starts,p95_ms,avg_util,cost_delta
E1,us-west1,2,20,75,45,1,48,210,86,-7.1
E2,us-west1,2,20,80,50,3,8,145,82,2.8
E3,us-west1,4,20,80,50,4,3,132,79,6.5
E4,europe-west1,1,12,70,40,1,22,235,88,-9.4

Appendix: quick definitions and why they matter:
- buffer_spare: extra GPUs kept warm beyond scheduling for immediate absorption of bursts. A higher buffer reduces cold-starts but raises steady-state cost.
- min_gpus: absolute fleet floor (scales the baseline cost and reduces probability of cold starts after long idle).
- trigger_up/down: utilization thresholds for scaling decisions; tightening them reduces risk of crossing into overloaded state but increases extra scaling activity.

Draft tag: this is a working sheet — not yet aligned with product rollout docs. Comments welcome inline.

Owners for follow-up: @Priya (experiment reruns), @Elena (billing delta sanity), @Kyle (playbook updates).

title: Buffer Sizing Sensitivity Study — Autoscaler Trigger Experiments
preview:
Overview:
Purpose: quantify sensitivity of latency, cold-start events, and unit cost to different 'buffer' sizing strategies and min/max node bounds across regions. This is a lightweight spreadsheet-style experiment log and the distilled numbers we discussed in the infra sync (4/20).

Hypotheses:
- Small buffer (1-2 spare GPUs) reduces cost but may increase cold-starts during bursty traffic, hurting p95 latency.
- Larger buffer (3-5) stabilizes p95 at the expense of steady-state cost.
- Region-level traffic patterns (APAC burstiness vs NA steady) change the optimal min setting.

Experiment matrix (each row = single experiment run, 48h window, synthetic workload with 30m peak bursts every 3h):

Columns: experiment_id | region | min_gpus | max_gpus | trigger_up_util% | trigger_down_util% | buffer_size_spare_gpus | observed_cold_starts_total | p95_latency_ms | avg_util_peak% | cost_delta_vs-baseline% | notes

E1 | us-west1 | 2 | 20 | 75 | 45 | 1 | 48 | 210 | 86 | -7.1 | small buffer -> frequent cold starts during first burst after idle
E2 | us-west1 | 2 | 20 | 80 | 50 | 3 | 8 | 145 | 82 | +2.8 | 3 spare GPUs reduces cold-starts significantly, slight cost up
E3 | us-west1 | 4 | 20 | 80 | 50 | 4 | 3 | 132 | 79 | +6.5 | higher min reduces autoscale churn; p95 best but cost higher
E4 | europe-west1 | 1 | 12 | 70 | 40 | 1 | 22 | 235 | 88 | -9.4 | EU bursts shorter but more frequent -> 1 spare too low
E5 | europe-west1 | 2 | 12 | 75 | 45 | 2 | 7 | 160 | 84 | -0.9 | moderate buffer balances cost/latency
E6 | asia-east1 | 1 | 16 | 85 | 55 | 1 | 72 | 320 | 92 | -12.7 | APAC traffic spiky; min=1 leads to many cold starts and high p95
E7 | asia-east1 | 2 | 16 | 85 | 55 | 3 | 12 | 175 | 88 | -3.0 | 3 spare heads helps but cost still down vs baseline due to short idle windows
E8 | multi-region (us+eu) | 3 | 28 | 80 | 50 | 2 | 18 | 150 | 81 | +1.2 | cross-region routing smoothing reduces peak in single region; moderate buffer ok
E9 | us-west1 | 0 | 10 | 70 | 35 | 0 | 150 | 410 | 95 | -18.6 | min=0 leads to large latency spikes and many cold starts -> unacceptable for SLO apps
E10 | us-west1 | 1 | 10 | 70 | 35 | 2 | 40 | 210 | 89 | -9.9 | small min with raised buffer halves cold-starts compared to min=0

Metric notes:
- observed_cold_starts_total: counted node-level cold-starts that caused >200ms extra token latency (aggregate over run).
- p95_latency_ms: generation p95 for target benchmark prompt set (w/ 8k context).
- cost_delta_vs-baseline%: baseline = fleet with min=3, max=20, buffer=2 in us-west baseline run from March 2026 billing sample. Negative = cheaper.

Qualitative observations from logs and traces:
- Cold-starts correlated with async batch queue growth: when queue hits threshold and scaler kicks, first evictions/loads cause KV cache warms and 150-500ms token delays for initial requests.
- Node churn increased when trigger_up <= 75 and trigger_down <= 45 with low min; in practice hysteresis at controller level had to be tuned (see linked MR 472).
- APAC region's short bursts (~6-12s spikes) cause repeated scale-ups when buffer < 2 even if average utilization low.

Quick sensitivity summaries:
- p95 latency is highly sensitive to buffer_size when min_gpus <= 2 (delta up to +200–300ms p95).
- Cost increases roughly linearly with min_gpus; buffer contributes a smaller steady cost but yields disproportionate latency improvements.
- Raising trigger_up from 75 to 80 decreased unnecessary scale-ups in US experiments by ~12% at the expense of slightly higher peak util.

Operational recommendations (practical runbook items):
1) Region presets: default min for APAC = 2, EU = 1, NA = 2 (for production-facing model routes). Rationale: APAC burstiness requires at least 2 warm GPUs.
2) Buffer policy: prefer spare buffer of 2–3 GPUs for high-SLO routes. Use 1 spare for low-cost dev/experimental routes.
3) Trigger tuning: use trigger_up=80 / trigger_down=50 for NA/EU; trigger_up=85 / trigger_down=55 for APAC where bursts are shortest but frequent.
4) Hysteresis: add 3-minute stabilization window before scale-down to avoid thrash on short bursts (had notable effect in E2/E5).
5) Canary rollout plan: apply preset to 10% of production routes (non-critical) for 1 week, measure cold-starts and p95 regressions, then expand.

Follow-ups / open questions:
- Repeat runs with mixed-model workloads (long context vs short) — current runs used single 8k prompt profile.
- Evaluate KV cache pre-warming strategies (warm snapshots) vs buffer-only economics; warm snapshots may reduce buffer need by 1–2 GPUs.
- Validate with real customer traces (requested from CS) for two enterprise customers with APAC-heavy traffic.

Raw experiment CSV snippet (first 5 rows):
experiment_id,region,min_gpus,max_gpus,trigger_up,trigger_down,buffer_spare, cold_starts,p95_ms,avg_util,cost_delta
E1,us-west1,2,20,75,45,1,48,210,86,-7.1
E2,us-west1,2,20,80,50,3,8,145,82,2.8
E3,us-west1,4,20,80,50,4,3,132,79,6.5
E4,europe-west1,1,12,70,40,1,22,235,88,-9.4

Appendix: quick definitions and why they matter:
- buffer_spare: extra GPUs kept warm beyond scheduling for immediate absorption of bursts. A higher buffer reduces cold-starts but raises steady-state cost.
- min_gpus: absolute fleet floor (scales the baseline cost and reduces probability of cold starts after long idle).
- trigger_up/down: utilization thresholds for scaling decisions; tightening them reduces risk of crossing into overloaded state but increases extra scaling activity.

source.title: Mini handback econ roster + dollar sampler (Apr 2026)
source.content:
Purpose:
Quick, ad-hoc table to surface who we loop for short handbacks and a lightweight $/effort sample per owner. Intended for product, SRE, and SEs to decide temporary coverage vs. escalations for next 7 days. Not an SLA—just a working econ sketch.

Notes about approach:
- We bucket owners by likely effort (low/med/high) for a one-week coverage window.
- Dollar sampler is back-of-envelope: assume blended fully-burdened hourly rate = $85/hr for contributor time, 40% probability-of-involvement for med, 80% for high, 20% for low.
- This spreadsheet is intentionally compact — use as tie-breaker when assigning rapid handbacks.

Roster table (rows are manual entries from quick calls):
owner,role,primary-contact,email,proximity-to-sprint,estimated-hours-week,effort-bucket,probability,sample-dollar-week,notes
Priya Natarajan,Product Ops,priya@redwood.ai,priya@redwood.ai,close,6,low,0.2,102.0,can cover async; needs SE backup for infra requests
Ethan Morales,Platform Eng,ethan@redwood.ai,ethan@redwood.ai,onsite,14,med,0.4,476.0,burst support likely day-1
Zoe Fischer,Solutions Eng,zoe@redwood.ai,zoe@redwood.ai,close,10,med,0.4,340.0,customer-facing checkins expected
Ravi Desai,Observability,ravi@redwood.ai,ravi@redwood.ai,remote,18,high,0.8,1224.0,may need SRE pairing for logs
Marta Lopez,Customer Success,marta@redwood.ai,marta@redwood.ai,close,4,low,0.2,68.0,owns customer comms

Summary metrics (computed inline):
- Sum estimated-hours-week: 52
- Raw sample-dollar-week (sum of sample-dollar-week column): 2210.0
- Average probability-weighted hours per owner: ~9.2

Interpretation / quick recommendations:
1) If we need to keep the product route stable for 7 days (high-risk window), prioritize creating a paired rota of Ethan + Ravi for the first 72 hours and keep Zoe on standby for customer-facing escalations. Combined sample-dollar-week for that primary pair = 1700-ish (back-of-envelope).
2) Low-effort owners (Priya, Marta) should handle async and comms; do not pages unless SLO breach.
3) If we expect >2x inbound from customers, escalate to Dedicated ops (ENG-4823) and trigger the mini-playbook on Confluence.

Assumptions and caveats:
- Hourly rate used is illustrative; for procurement/finance-level decisioning use actual blended rates from payroll.
- Probability buckets are subjective; this is deliberately conservative for quick triage.
- Does not include infra costs (GPUs, endpoint spend). For run-costs see linked ticket ENG-4823.

Open questions / follow-ups:
- Can SRE provide an on-call float for next 7 days? (ask: @oncall-sre)
- Should we pre-authorize a $2k surge fund for customer tickets if ticket volume > 25/day? (Finance call)
- Confirm if Solutions Eng can push templated troubleshooting guides to customers prior to escalation.

Next steps (owner assignments):
- Priya to assemble async playbook doc and share to slack by EOD Apr 15 (owner: Priya Natarajan).
- Ethan to confirm availability and define handoff window (owner: Ethan Morales).
- Ravi to triage observability alerts for one-off dashboards and hand over to Zoe for customer w/o infra changes (owner: Ravi Desai).

Quick log of touches (light audit):
2026-04-14: initial mini-roster compiled from standup and two SE calls (Priya).
2026-04-15: pulled in observability estimate after Ravi convo (Ethan).
2026-04-16: added customer comms owner and final sample-dollar-week numbers (Priya).

If you want a formal sheet with formulas and pivoting, I can convert this to a full Google Sheet and add live links to oncall schedules and spend dashboards. For now this is the minimal handback econ sketch to move fast.

source.title: Edge deploy impediments and next steps (week of 2026-03-22)
source.body:
Edge deploy - Impediments & Next Steps
Week: 2026-03-22 to 2026-03-26
Owner: Priya Natarajan
Note: working doc for ops/eng coordination on current VPC/on-prem installs; mixes standup notes, quick triage findings, and concrete action items.

1) High-level summary
- Two installs in active rollout this week (Acadia Health on-prem, Meridian Analytics VPC). One delayed due to networking/infra misconfigurations (Acadia). A third customer (Zephyr Labs) hit a credential + KMS egress block during canary.
- Recurring theme: subtle network policy (MTU & iptables) + customer firewall rules that break gRPC streaming and artifact fetches. Also gaps in telemetry on host-level disk and kernel OOM signals.

2) Standup highlights (daily blurbs, consolidated)
- Mon 2026-03-22: Acadia initial install completed but agent never reached control-plane. Observed TLS handshake failures when talking to private-control endpoint (SNI mismatch). Ticket PD-8721 opened.
- Tue 2026-03-23: Meridian VPC test passes throughput smoke but sustained load causes occasional tokenization stalls; repro on our staging when MTU forced to 9000 on NIC.
- Wed 2026-03-24: Zephyr canary failed during bootstrap due to KMS egress being blocked by corporate firewall; KMS endpoint traffic attempted to use IP range not permitted by their proxy.
- Thu 2026-03-25: Investigated driver mismatch on an on-prem GPU node; node reported CUDA 11.2 vs runtime expected 11.8 leading to XLA fallback and degraded perf.
- Fri 2026-03-26: Nightly telemetry job revealed missed disk pressure events on two installs; no alerts fired because node-exporter config excluded /var/lib/redwood/cache.

3) Deep dives (per-customer)
- Acadia Health (on-prem, phase: post-install)
  Problem: TLS handshake failures between on-prem gateway and Redwood private control plane.
  Root cause hypothesis: Customer used a transparent proxy that rewrote SNI leading to cert mismatch; additional NAT performed on egress which changed source IP expectations for SLA routing.
  Impact: control-plane connection never established; install stalled.
  Mitigation so far: Solutions Eng performed a manual proxy bypass test (worked). Eng infra suggested adding explicit SNI host header fallback in agent (PD-8734).
  Outstanding: need a small agent change to send an explicit Host header and retry with alternate TLS port if SNI fails. Also ask Acadia infra to allow target SNI or enable proxy passthrough.
  Action items:
    - Priya to propose agent fallback patch (branch: sni-fallback) — due 2026-03-31 (Jira PD-8734)
    - Ethan to schedule call with Acadia network team to get proxy config or whitelist — due 2026-03-29

- Meridian Analytics (VPC, phase: load testing)
  Problem: intermittent gRPC stalls and long tail latency under sustained token churn.
  Observations: Problem repro'd when MTU on test NIC set to 9000; fragmentation resulted in higher packet loss under our simulated load. When MTU forced to 1500, stalls disappeared.
  Root cause hypothesis: Large MTU + middlebox fragmentation + TCP reassembly bug in their virtual NIC drivers.
  Impact: Potential for production stalls at customers who enable jumbo frames.
  Mitigation: Document MTU as an explicit preflight check; provide workaround to pin MTU to 1500 or enable MSS clamping at the VPC gateway.
  Action items:
    - Maya to add MTU preflight check to the installer and emit a WARN that blocks rollout unless explicitly overridden — due 2026-04-06
    - Liam to open guidance doc for Solutions Eng describing MSS clamping steps for major cloud providers — due 2026-04-02

- Zephyr Labs (on-prem air-gapped candidate)
  Problem: Bootstrapping failed because KMS traffic could not egress; their firewall/proxy requires egress by DNS name only and blocks known IPs (intermittent).
  Observations: Agent attempted TLS connections to KMS that resolved to multiple IP addresses; their egress rules were IP-only.
  Mitigation: Provide clear instructions on allowing domain names or use a customer-side egress proxy that performs outbound DNS resolution. Offer an offline KMS envelope workflow for air-gapped installs as short-term workaround.
  Action items:
    - Sofia to prototype offline KMS envelope flow and doc example — due 2026-04-09
    - Solutions Eng to run a preflight checklist with Zephyr infra to document egress patterns — due 2026-03-31

4) Cross-cutting technical findings (things worth shipping platform-wide)
- Agent resiliency: Add explicit Host/SNI fallback + secondary TLS port attempt to handle transparent proxies. This is low-risk small change, unblocks at least two customers this week. (PD-8734)
- Installer telemetry: include /var/lib/redwood/cache and /var/log/redwood in node-exporter to avoid blindspots for disk pressure + OOM patterns.
- MTU/fragmentation: Default installer should detect jumbo-frames and emit clear guidance. Also add an option to force MSS clamping via iptables/tc for the agent's traffic.

5) Observability & alerting gaps discovered
- Missing disk metrics on two on-prem installs since node-exporter excluded cache path by default; created PD-8748 to auto-include our key directories in preflight.
- No explicit alert for repeated TLS handshake failures (SNI mismatch). Currently surfaces as repeated ConnectError — needs a specific alert to avoid noisy paging.
- KV cache invalidations were correlated with intermittent NTP drift on one customer node. Need to add NTP health check to preflight.

6) Quick wins we can ship this sprint (priority order)
- SNI fallback in agent (small patch) — owner: Priya — high priority
- MTU preflight check + installer WARN — owner: Maya — medium
- node-exporter default dirs include — owner: Ethan — high
- Documentation: preflight checklist update (network, KMS, MTU, NTP) — owner: Liam — low

7) Open questions / decisions needed
- Do we want agent to perform active egress path tests (connect to KMS, control plane, S3 endpoints) during install or keep preflight passive? (tradeoff: active increases friction in air-gapped environments). Please voice preferences in thread.
- For customers who insist on jumbo frames, do we support MSS clamping in our agent or require infra change? If we ship MSS clamping we must QA across cloud images.

8) Triage log excerpts (condensed)
- "tls: handshake failure: sni mismatch" — repeated 40x in Acadia logs over 2 hours; connection reset by proxy.
- "grpc: stream stalled: partial send timeout" — correlated with 9000 MTU test.
- "KMS unreachable: ECONNREFUSED at 10.11.12.13" — resolved as firewall blocking DNS-resolved IPs.

9) Meeting cadence & next syncs
- Daily standup 09:30 PST (private-deployments)
- Deep technical sync for agent-fallback patch: Thu 2026-03-31 16:00 PST (Priya, Ethan, Maya)
- Customer call: Acadia network review w/ Solutions Eng: 2026-03-29 10:00 PST (Ethan assigned)

10) Action item tracker (compact)
- PD-8734: agent SNI fallback patch — Priya — due 2026-03-31
- PD-8721: Acadia proxy/TLS investigation — Ethan + Acadia infra — due 2026-03-29
- PD-8748: node-exporter default inclusion of redwood dirs — Ethan — due 2026-03-30
- Docs: MTU & preflight guide — Liam — due 2026-04-02

Notes / rough thoughts to self:
- Consider a small egress diagnostic binary shipped with agent that can be enabled in installs to capture DNS->IP resolution, SNI, and TLS fingerprinting. Could be opt-in for privacy.
- Revisit our installer's default firewall guidance: most customers ignore docs and let their infra team run defaults; pack a short checklist that is copy-paste friendly.

If you're reviewing: please comment inline or drop notes in Slack thread #private-deployments. Mark any action items you pick up by adding yourself to the Jira or pinging me directly.

End of week note.

source.title: Observability sync capture - triage and next actions
source.body:
Meeting: Observability weekly sync (lightweight capture)
Date: 2026-03-26 10:00-10:40 PT
Facilitator: Priya Natarajan
Attendees: Priya, Ethan, Lina, Marco, Janelle (joined late), Tanya (shadow)
Purpose: fast triage of tracing sampling anomalies, dashboard refresh plan, and cost telemetry rollup work. Capture quick decisions and action owners — this is a working note, not polished.

Agenda (10 min each topic + 5 min wrap):
- Tracing: sampling drift & tag normalization
- Dashboards: cost dashboard redesign + mobile view constraints
- Cost telemetry: per-route token aggregation pipeline and anomaly alerts
- Housekeeping: oncall coverage, deploy windows, follow-ups

Notes
1) Tracing: sampling drift observed in prod
- Symptom: last 72h shows increased sampled spans from the serving layer for small p99 tail requests; overall sampled rate jumped from ~8% to ~18% on March 24 evening.
- Quick hypothesis: a change in the gateway routing (canary rollout last night) caused more short-lived requests to take the low-latency path which sets sampling override to 1. Alternatively, a metrics pipeline bug expanded the sample flag during retention rewrite.
- Data we pulled: span counts by service (serving, gateway, frontend) + sampling flag breakdown, correlated with rollout tags (git sha + rollout-id).
- Temporary mitigation: reverted rollout to previous canary (rolled back 2026-03-25 02:30 PT) — sampling rate returned to baseline but history still contains the spike.
- Decision: keep current rollback in prod, but keep the canary paused until trace flags are instrumented. We need an automated guard.
- Action items:
  - Ethan: add a pre-rollout check that validates sampling flag distribution on a small synthetic traffic run. Blocker: needs a cheap synthetic harness (OBS-342). Due 2026-04-02.
  - Lina: write a short doc for expected sampling behavior per route and add to model-serving runbook. Due 2026-03-30.
  - Marco: write a query (stored in analytics dashboard) to show sampling rate by service and rollout-id for last 7 days. Make it a chart on the infra-tracing dashboard. Due 2026-03-29.

2) Tag normalization & span size concerns
- Problem: inconsistent span tags from Python and Rust serving stacks cause combinatorial explosion on indexing (user_id vs user.id vs uid).
- Brainstorm notes: add a tag-normalizer transform in the ingestion path to collapse synonyms into canonical keys; avoid changing upstream code right before a big release.
- Tradeoffs: doing normalization in-ingest saves downstream dashboard work but costs CPU at ingest (we have headroom if we batch transforms). Alternative: enforce tagging schema with linter + pre-commit + integration tests.
- Decision: implement dual approach: short-term ingest-normalizer + medium-term SDK lint rules and docs.
- Action items:
  - Janelle: prototype a tag-normalizer lambda (transformer) and run on a 48h replay of sampled spans to measure CPU and failure modes. Due 2026-04-06.
  - Priya: open a docs PR for tagging schema and add examples for common ML serving tags. Due 2026-04-01.

3) Dashboards: cost dashboard redesign and mobile constraints
- Current state: cost dashboard mixes per-model token cost (hosted), infra GPU burn, and anomalies in a single view. Engineers complain it's noisy and slow to load on the RW console.
- Proposal: split into 3 lightweight views: "Unit Economics (per model)", "Infra Burn & Capacity", "Alerts & Anomalies". Each should be query-light for console mobile view.
- UX notes: mobile view should precompute aggregated metrics once per hour and cache results for 10 minutes. Avoid full join queries.
- Decision: implement split views and add an aggregated hourly cache layer for mobile. Start with Unit Economics and Infra Burn.
- Action items:
  - Marco: mockup new Unit Economics view and wireframe low-card mobile layout. Use storybook components for preview. Due 2026-04-02.
  - Ethan: add hourly aggregator job (k8s cron) that materializes token-cost aggregates into a precomputed table. This will back mobile queries. Due 2026-04-09.
  - Lina + Priya: prioritize 5 metrics for first release: tokens_total, tokens_billed_usd, requests_p50/p95, avg_latency_ms, cache_hit_rate. Finalize list by 2026-03-30.

4) Cost telemetry: per-route token aggregation pipeline and anomaly alerts
- Goal: surfaced per-customer and per-route token cost within 2 hours of incident with anomaly detection on spend spikes (>3x baseline within 60m).
- Current gaps: aggregation job is delayed (failed overnight job Mar 23), and alerting rules are noisy because baseline window too short.
- Immediate fix: make aggregation job resilient (retry logic + backoff) and add deduplication of events in the ingestion layer.
- Longer-term: build a streaming downsampler that maintains a rolling 24h baseline per route with configurable sensitivity.
- Action items:
  - Ethan: investigate failed aggregation job and add robust retry + alert on job failures. Ticket: OBS-350. Due 2026-03-31.
  - Marco: implement baseline window tuning knob in the anomaly detector and test with controlled synthetic spike. Due 2026-04-07.
  - Priya: coordinate with CS (customer-success) to get a list of top 20 routes to prioritize in per-route rollouts. Ask CS for list by 2026-03-28.

5) Housekeeping & ops
- Oncall rotate: Tanya will finish her oncall shadow run next week; Janelle takes primary oncall starting 2026-03-29 for 2 weeks.
- Deploy windows: agreed to avoid large observability infra changes during major platform deploys on 2026-04-01 and 2026-04-08 (product-driven).
- Data retention: cost telemetry raw logs retention discussion deferred to next week (need finance inputs).

Open questions / risks
- Risk that ingest-normalizer adds unacceptable CPU cost during traffic spikes. Mitigation: run replay and adjust batch sizing; if necessary throttle normalizer to sampled streams only.
- Risk that hourly aggregates miss short-lived spikes important to CS. Trade: set anomaly sensitivity and have fallback to raw-stream alerts for top 20 routes.

Decisions summary
- Rollback kept for gateway canary until sampling check is added.
- Implement ingest-side tag normalizer (short-term) + tagging linter (medium-term).
- Split cost dashboard into three views and add hourly precompute for mobile.
- Make aggregation job resilient and tune anomaly detector baseline window.

Next sync: 2026-04-02 10:00 PT (quick 30 min) — focus: tag-normalizer prototype and hourly aggregator progress.

Quick links & references
- OBS-342 (sampling pre-rollout check)
- PR: github.com/redwood-inference/observability/pull/128 (dashboard pagination improvements)
- Slack: #observability thread (sampling spike)

Notes to self: keep this doc lightweight; once tag-normalizer prototype is green turn this into a short RFC and route to platform-eng for review.

-- end of capture --

- Data we pulled: span counts by service (serving, gateway, frontend) + sampling flag breakdown, correlated with rollout tags (git sha + rollout-id).
- Temporary mitigation: reverted rollout to previous canary (rolled back 2026-03-25 02:30 PT) — sampling rate returned to baseline but history still contains the spike.
- Decision: keep current rollback in prod, but keep the canary paused until trace flags are instrumented. We need an automated guard.
- Action items:
  - Ethan: add a pre-rollout check that validates sampling flag distribution on a small synthetic traffic run. Blocker: needs a cheap synthetic harness (OBS-342). Due 2026-04-02.
  - Lina: write a short doc for expected sampling behavior per route and add to model-serving runbook. Due 2026-03-30.
  - Marco: write a query (stored in analytics dashboard) to show sampling rate by service and rollout-id for last 7 days. Make it a chart on the infra-tracing dashboard. Due 2026-03-29.

2) Tag normalization & span size concerns
- Problem: inconsistent span tags from Python and Rust serving stacks cause combinatorial explosion on indexing (user_id vs user.id vs uid).
- Brainstorm notes: add a tag-normalizer transform in the ingestion path to collapse synonyms into canonical keys; avoid changing upstream code right before a big release.
- Tradeoffs: doing normalization in-ingest saves downstream dashboard work but costs CPU at ingest (we have headroom if we batch transforms). Alternative: enforce tagging schema with linter + pre-commit + integration tests.
- Decision: implement dual approach: short-term ingest-normalizer + medium-term SDK lint rules and docs.
- Action items:
  - Janelle: prototype a tag-normalizer lambda (transformer) and run on a 48h replay of sampled spans to measure CPU and failure modes. Due 2026-04-06.
  - Priya: open a docs PR for tagging schema and add examples for common ML serving tags. Due 2026-04-01.

3) Dashboards: cost dashboard redesign and mobile constraints
- Current state: cost dashboard mixes per-model token cost (hosted), infra GPU burn, and anomalies in a single view. Engineers complain it's noisy and slow to load on the RW console.
- Proposal: split into 3 lightweight views: "Unit Economics (per model)", "Infra Burn & Capacity", "Alerts & Anomalies". Each should be query-light for console mobile view.
- UX notes: mobile view should precompute aggregated metrics once per hour and cache results for 10 minutes. Avoid full join queries.
- Decision: implement split views and add an aggregated hourly cache layer for mobile. Start with Unit Economics and Infra Burn.
- Action items:
  - Marco: mockup new Unit Economics view and wireframe low-card mobile layout. Use storybook components for preview. Due 2026-04-02.
  - Ethan: add hourly aggregator job (k8s cron) that materializes token-cost aggregates into a precomputed table. This will back mobile queries. Due 2026-04-09.
  - Lina + Priya: prioritize 5 metrics for first release: tokens_total, tokens_billed_usd, requests_p50/p95, avg_latency_ms, cache_hit_rate. Finalize list by 2026-03-30.

4) Cost telemetry: per-route token aggregation pipeline and anomaly alerts
- Goal: surfaced per-customer and per-route token cost within 2 hours of incident with anomaly detection on spend spikes (>3x baseline within 60m).
- Current gaps: aggregation job is delayed (failed overnight job Mar 23), and alerting rules are noisy because baseline window too short.
- Immediate fix: make aggregation job resilient (retry logic + backoff) and add deduplication of events in the ingestion layer.
- Longer-term: build a streaming downsampler that maintains a rolling 24h baseline per route with configurable sensitivity.
- Action items:
  - Ethan: investigate failed aggregation job and add robust retry + alert on job failures. Ticket: OBS-350. Due 2026-03-31.
  - Marco: implement baseline window tuning knob in the anomaly detector and test with controlled synthetic spike. Due 2026-04-07.
  - Priya: coordinate with CS (customer-success) to get a list of top 20 routes to prioritize in per-route rollouts. Ask CS for list by 2026-03-28.

5) Housekeeping & ops
- Oncall rotate: Tanya will finish her oncall shadow run next week; Janelle takes primary oncall starting 2026-03-29 for 2 weeks.
- Deploy windows: agreed to avoid large observability infra changes during major platform deploys on 2026-04-01 and 2026-04-08 (product-driven).
- Data retention: cost telemetry raw logs retention discussion deferred to next week (need finance inputs).

Open questions / risks
- Risk that ingest-normalizer adds unacceptable CPU cost during traffic spikes. Mitigation: run replay and adjust batch sizing; if necessary throttle normalizer to sampled streams only.
- Risk that hourly aggregates miss short-lived spikes important to CS. Trade: set anomaly sensitivity and have fallback to raw-stream alerts for top 20 routes.

Decisions summary
- Rollback kept for gateway canary until sampling check is added.
- Implement ingest-side tag normalizer (short-term) + tagging linter (medium-term).
- Split cost dashboard into three views and add hourly precompute for mobile.
- Make aggregation job resilient and tune anomaly detector baseline window.

Next sync: 2026-04-02 10:00 PT (quick 30 min) — focus: tag-normalizer prototype and hourly aggregator progress.

Quick links & references
- OBS-342 (sampling pre-rollout check)
- PR: github.com/redwood-inference/observability/pull/128 (dashboard pagination improvements)
- Slack: #observability thread (sampling spike)

Notes to self: keep this doc lightweight; once tag-normalizer prototype is green turn this into a short RFC and route to platform-eng for review.

-- end of capture --

source.title: Auriga Burst AI Labs — Account Brief (Draft)
source.content:
Account summary (CS-facing draft)
Company: Auriga Burst AI Labs (aurigaburst.ai)
Industry: Adtech (personalization + reranking + some chat-assistant surfaces)
Account tier target: Enterprise
HQ region: NA
Employee band: 1000+
Primary products in scope: Dedicated, Private, Optimize (plus new proposed Burst Reserve add-on)

Current stage (updated): Late evaluation → commercial negotiation pending final throughput + burst guarantees.
Forecast close (updated): 2026-04 (was 2025-12; slipped due to budget freeze + champion availability)
Estimated ARR (updated): $150k–$320k depending on reserved baseline + burst reserve sizing

Key contacts (updated):
- CTO: Maya Singh — original champion; returned from extended leave on a limited schedule as of early March. Still supportive but delegating day-to-day.
- Eng Lead: Samir Patel — day-to-day technical owner; effectively current champion.
- Procurement: (TBD) — waiting on security runbook + explicit SLA language.
Redwood team:
- AE: Avery Chen
- SE: Diego Ramirez
- CS (draft owner): Priya Natarajan

Use cases (mostly unchanged):
- Reranking for ad/promo selection (most latency-sensitive)
- Batch embeddings for catalog/user features
- Chat assistant for internal ops + limited customer-facing chat flows

Traffic + performance requirements (updated based on Feb/March follow-ups):
- Peak concurrency target: 750 qps sustained for ~20s bursts remains the baseline ask.
- Worst-case bursts: now stated as 2000–2500 qps during flash promotions (previously “up to ~2000”).
- Throughput target: ~500k tokens/min baseline; updated stretch goal discussed: ~650k tokens/min during peak windows if they expand chat surfaces.
- Latency SLOs:
  - Reranking: p95 <= 250ms (unchanged)
  - Chat flows: p99 <= 600ms (unchanged)
- Hard requirement: deterministic behavior under burst (avoid queueing cascades); willing to accept controlled degradation if explicitly specified (e.g., per-route throttling + overflow policy).

Deployment requirements (unchanged): VPC + public cloud acceptable.
Security requirements (largely unchanged, clarified):
- SSO/SAML required
- Audit logging required (customer asked for explicit event list and retention options)
- KMS integration required (runbook + key rotation expectations)
- Data residency: US-only (confirmed). They asked for written confirmation that support access is US-restricted for incident response.

Competitive context (updated):
- CompetitorX-cloud remains primary competitor; they offered reserved cluster + “guaranteed bursting” with aggressive pricing.
- In-house inference is a fallback option for non-peak workloads.
- New nuance: Auriga is evaluating a hybrid plan (reserve in CompetitorX + keep Redwood for specific reranking routes) unless we can provide stronger burst/capacity commitments.

What happened so far (timeline; mostly carried over + a couple of new items):
- 2025-10-20: Inbound; Hosted API demo (Avery).
- 2025-11-05: Qualified; requested Dedicated pricing + capacity model.
- 2025-11-18: POC kickoff — small-scale reranking tests on hosted.
- 2025-12-02: POC results: quality strong; queueing at ~600 qps sustained; latency spikes during bursts. CTO quote: “can’t risk queueing during campaigns.”
- 2026-01-07: Security review call; shared security FAQ + KMS diagram.
- 2026-01-15: CompetitorX offer: reserved cluster + guaranteed bursting at lower price; Auriga asked for Redwood SLA ramp plan.
- 2026-01-25: Internal budget freeze; CTO went on leave; cadence slowed.
- 2026-02-10: Follow-up demo on Dedicated autoscaling + burst policies; asked for hard queueing guarantees + per-route throttling.
- 2026-02-20: Last strong touchpoint pre-freeze: “evaluating options; price + capacity still deciding factors.”
- 2026-03-07 (new): Samir re-engaged; requested updated proposal including (a) reserved baseline, (b) burst reserve sizing guidance, (c) explicit queueing/overload behavior.
- 2026-03-14 (new): Internal Redwood sync: propose Dedicated + Burst Reserve add-on + written overload policy + joint load test plan. New infra ticket created for burst regression validation (INFR-2338).

Key blockers / risks (refreshed):
1) Throughput + burst/queueing risk (primary): their traffic is extremely bursty. They want capacity guarantees, or at minimum an explicit, testable overload policy.
2) Commercial pressure: CompetitorX pricing + “guaranteed bursting” language is currently more concrete.
3) Org dynamics: CTO is back but limited; Samir is executing. Need to ensure we’re not single-threaded.
4) Procurement readiness: wants audit logging + KMS operational runbook and explicit data residency/support-access language.

What we’ve done / proposals in motion (carried over, updated):
- Scoped Dedicated POC with autoscaling rules and batching/kv-cache recommendations.
- Optimize team provided kernel/quantization guidance; Diego proposed tuned batching profile.
- Proposed overflow behaviors (cross-region fallback discussed earlier), but customer preference is to avoid cross-region due to data residency + latency; now leaning toward in-region burst reserve + explicit throttling for non-critical routes.
- INFR-2145: investigating queueing root cause; engineering shared tuning notes; customer still wants deterministic SLA.
- INFR-2338 (new): validate burst regression under updated batching profile + isolate noisy-neighbor risks in Dedicated config.

Customer “must-haves” for next step (what they said explicitly):
- A re-run throughput POC with tuned batching profile AND clear methodology (traffic shape, duration, burst ramp).
- A written description of overload behavior (what happens at/beyond capacity) and what controls they get (per-route throttles, priority, circuit breakers).
- Security artifacts: audit log event taxonomy + KMS integration runbook + data residency/support access statement.

Next steps (CS + GTM alignment; updated):
- Technical: schedule joint load test plan review with Auriga SRE/Perf and Redwood infra leads; run the burst profile they specified (baseline 750 qps w/ 20s bursts; spikes to 2000–2500 qps) and capture p95/p99 lat + queue depth behavior.
- Commercial: deliver two options:
  (1) Dedicated baseline + Burst Reserve add-on (3-month pilot) with explicit burst capacity window
  (2) Dedicated baseline only, with a documented overload policy + optional future reserve
- Security: package a single procurement bundle (audit logging details + KMS runbook + residency/support-access statement). Track against SEC-307.
- Executive alignment: re-engage Maya (CTO) for sign-off once Samir validates the POC results; avoid stalling on “champion availability.”

CS stance (draft): High strategic value (enterprise adtech, big volume) with elevated reliability expectations. If we can make burst behavior explicit and repeatably demonstrable, likelihood improves; if not, high probability they split workloads or default to CompetitorX for peak campaigns.

Open questions (to resolve before finalizing brief):
- Do we have approved standard language for “burst reserve” and overload behavior that legal will accept?
- Can we commit to any queueing/latency SLO under burst, or only best-effort + documented degradation?
- What is the smallest credible reserved baseline that meets their campaign risk tolerance without pricing us out?

1) Throughput + burst/queueing risk (primary): their traffic is extremely bursty. They want capacity guarantees, or at minimum an explicit, testable overload policy.
2) Commercial pressure: CompetitorX pricing + “guaranteed bursting” language is currently more concrete.
3) Org dynamics: CTO is back but limited; Samir is executing. Need to ensure we’re not single-threaded.
4) Procurement readiness: wants audit logging + KMS operational runbook and explicit data residency/support-access language.

What we’ve done / proposals in motion (carried over, updated):
- Scoped Dedicated POC with autoscaling rules and batching/kv-cache recommendations.
- Optimize team provided kernel/quantization guidance; Diego proposed tuned batching profile.
- Proposed overflow behaviors (cross-region fallback discussed earlier), but customer preference is to avoid cross-region due to data residency + latency; now leaning toward in-region burst reserve + explicit throttling for non-critical routes.
- INFR-2145: investigating queueing root cause; engineering shared tuning notes; customer still wants deterministic SLA.
- INFR-2338 (new): validate burst regression under updated batching profile + isolate noisy-neighbor risks in Dedicated config.

Customer “must-haves” for next step (what they said explicitly):
- A re-run throughput POC with tuned batching profile AND clear methodology (traffic shape, duration, burst ramp).
- A written description of overload behavior (what happens at/beyond capacity) and what controls they get (per-route throttles, priority, circuit breakers).
- Security artifacts: audit log event taxonomy + KMS integration runbook + data residency/support access statement.

Next steps (CS + GTM alignment; updated):
- Technical: schedule joint load test plan review with Auriga SRE/Perf and Redwood infra leads; run the burst profile they specified (baseline 750 qps w/ 20s bursts; spikes to 2000–2500 qps) and capture p95/p99 lat + queue depth behavior.
- Commercial: deliver two options:
  (1) Dedicated baseline + Burst Reserve add-on (3-month pilot) with explicit burst capacity window
  (2) Dedicated baseline only, with a documented overload policy + optional future reserve
- Security: package a single procurement bundle (audit logging details + KMS runbook + residency/support-access statement). Track against SEC-307.
- Executive alignment: re-engage Maya (CTO) for sign-off once Samir validates the POC results; avoid stalling on “champion availability.”

CS stance (draft): High strategic value (enterprise adtech, big volume) with elevated reliability expectations. If we can make burst behavior explicit and repeatably demonstrable, likelihood improves; if not, high probability they split workloads or default to CompetitorX for peak campaigns.

Open questions (to resolve before finalizing brief):
- Do we have approved standard language for “burst reserve” and overload behavior that legal will accept?
- Can we commit to any queueing/latency SLO under burst, or only best-effort + documented degradation?
- What is the smallest credible reserved baseline that meets their campaign risk tolerance without pricing us out?

source.title: Evals harness implementation notes (scripts, loaders, scoring, CI)
source.content:
Goal:
- Capture pragmatic implementation details for the evals harness used in model onboarding (what we run during onboarding pipelines and nightly regression checks).
- Focus areas: scripts, dataset loaders, scoring functions, integration into CI, and ops notes for troubleshooting.

High-level constraints and priorities:
- Deterministic reproducibility: seeds, stable tokenization, normalized whitespace.
- Lightweight dev iteration: small local mode with sample shards + full-run mode on infra.
- Clear failure modes: test should fail loudly for regressions in both quality (metrics drop) and performance (latency/cost increases).
- Keep scoring functions pure and small (easy to unit test).

Repository layout (expected files / modules)
- evals_harness/
  - cli.py                # thin CLI wrapper to run jobs locally / CI
  - runner.py             # orchestrates loader -> model client -> scorer -> reporter
  - loaders/
    - common.py           # base loader, utils for sharding, sampling
    - qa_loader.py        # SQuAD-like loader example (jsonl with fields: id, context, question, answers)
    - promptset_loader.py  # CSV/TSV loader for prompt sets (prompt, expected_regex, tags)
  - scorers/
    - base.py             # Scorer interface: accumulate -> finalize -> serialize
    - exact_match.py      # exact match for QA answers (normalize text)
    - fuzzy_rogue.py      # lightweight paraphrase-aware scorer (BLEU-ish heuristic)
  - scripts/
    - build_shard.py      # sampling and sharding scripts used in infra to produce balanced shards
    - backfill_results.py # re-run shards for missing results recovery
  - ci/
    - evals-ci.yml        # GitHub Actions workflow that runs smoke evals on PRs
  - tests/                # unit tests for loaders and scorers

Important implementation notes and gotchas:
- Tokenization parity:
  - We pin the tokenizer version and serialize tokenizer metadata into the job directory (tokenizer_name + version + vocab_hash).
  - Loader must expose input_ids (optional) for deterministic reruns in backfills.

- Deterministic seeds and sampling:
  - CLI accepts --seed, default 42. All random ops (sample negatives, shuffle) must use numpy.random.default_rng(seed) and expose the seed in metadata.json.

- Loader contract (methods and expectations):
  - init(path, split, limit=None, seed=None) -> loader instance
  - __iter__() yields dicts with: id, prompt, ground_truth (optional), metadata dict
  - meta: total_examples, split_name, tokenizer_info
  - Shards: loader.shard(index, num_shards) returns a new loader instance tied to that shard

- Example loader edge cases:
  - QA loader: answers might be list or string; normalize to list of short strings, strip whitespace, lower-case if config.normalization.
  - Promptset loader: multi-turn prompts should be encoded as single string with "\n--- TURN BREAK ---\n" separator to avoid confusion with natural newlines.

Scoring function patterns:
- Keep small: accumulate per-example predictions and reference, implement a finalize() to compute aggregate metrics.
- Preferred metrics: exact_match, normalized F1, tolerate synonyms via small synonym glossaries (per-domain).
- Evaluate calibration separately: compute confidence bins and Brier score when model returns probabilities.

Example scorer pseudocode (exact match normalizer):
- normalizer(s): remove punctuation, collapse whitespace, lower-case, strip leading articles (a, an, the)
- exact_match(preds, refs): any(normalizer(pred) == normalizer(ref) for ref in refs)

CI wiring (GitHub Actions)
- Goals: fast PR feedback (smoke tests), nightly full-run, and manual backfill jobs.
- evals-ci.yml (summary):
  - on: pull_request (paths: 'evals_harness/**')
  - jobs:
    - smoke-evals:
      runs-on: ubuntu-latest
      steps:
      - checkout
      - setup-python
      - pip install -r requirements-dev.txt
      - run: python -m evals_harness.cli --mode smoke --shard 0 --num-shards 1 --limit 50 --model ephemeral-local
      - upload-artifact: smoke-results-<sha>.json
- Nightly full regression (separate workflow):
  - heavier infra, spins up dedicated GPU runner (self-hosted); runs full promptsets and stores results in GCS path: gs://evals-results/<model>/<date>/

Thresholding and regression detection:
- Onboarding pipeline accepts a manifest with golden baselines (metric + acceptable delta).
- Regresion rule examples:
  - exact_match drop > 2% on core-latency-critical set -> block rollout
  - latency p95 increase > 20% -> alert SRE and fail gating job
- Store historical metrics as time-series (Prometheus or BigQuery table) for trend detection and post-mortem.

Artifacts produced by a run (saved in run_dir):
- predictions.jsonl  (id, prompt, pred, raw_model_output, tokens, duration_ms)
- scores.json       (aggregated metrics, per-split breakdown)
- metadata.json     (loader, seed, model_hash, tokenizer_info, git_sha)
- sample-failures/  (up to k examples per error type with diff view)

Operational playbook / troubleshooting
- Common failures:
  - Token mismatch: predictions look garbled; check metadata.tokenizer_name + vocab_hash and the model client tokenizer.
  - Flaky test due to non-deterministic sampling: confirm seed is set and passed to loader + runner.
  - CI timeouts: smoke mode uses small limits; increase timeout in GH Actions if installing dev deps is slow.

- Repro steps for local dev:
  - export REDWOOD_ENV=dev
  - python -m venv .venv && source .venv/bin/activate
  - pip install -e .[dev]
  - python -m evals_harness.cli --mode local --limit 20 --seed 123 --model local-qa-stub

- Backfill process (when results missing or new metric added):
  - identify missing shards via results index in GCS or BigQuery
  - run backfill with: python scripts/backfill_results.py --manifest gs://... --target-date YYYY-MM-DD --recompute-scorers

Testing guidance:
- Unit tests for loaders: test with small synthetic files (tempfile), check that shard sizes add up, and that normalization rules are deterministic.
- Tests for scorers: use parametrized cases with edge-case answers (punctuation, casing, whitespace-only answers).
- Integration smoke tests in CI should mock external model endpoint with a deterministic stub that returns consistent outputs.

Open TODOs / wishlist (short-term):
- Add a snippet-based comparator for long-form generation (ROUGE-L variant with length penalty tuned for our product).
- Store token-level traces (KV-cache hits / generation timestep) for latency debugging on Dedicated.
- Build small UI for inspecting sample-failures (ideally in Redwood Console as a temporary panel).

Notes from earlier experiments (context):
- We tried a heavy BLEURT-based scorer but it made CI brittle (model weights changed behavior); smaller heuristic scorers + periodic human eval was more stable.
- Prefix caching in the serving runtime changes tokenization boundaries slightly because of truncation strategy; hence recommendation to evaluate with production truncation rules.

Contacts / owners:
- Priya Natarajan (primary author) - overall harness and CI wiring
- Ethan Morales - scorer implementations and metric definitions
- Liu Chen - dataset ingestion and sharding scripts

If you take this doc and implement, please append a short changelog to the top of content with: date, author, and short note of what was added.

End of notes.

source.title: Microseq regression ledger — KV fragmentation & promptset diffs
source.summary: Short summary: After toggling the new paged-KV allocator (PR 8a7f3d1c) and enabling the continuous-batching scheduler on canary, we observed a small but consistent regression on a subset of micro-sequence prompt sets. The regression manifests as token-level latency spikes at sequence boundaries and a 1.8% increase in hallucination triggers on targeted QA prompts. Behavioral diffs are concentrated on prompts with repeated entity co-reference and short context windows (<96 tokens). This ledger captures the prompt-level diffs, reproduction notes, metric snapshots, and proposed mitigations for the release team.
source.background_and_context: What changed: two runtime changes merged into canary last week: (1) paged-KV allocator switches to 8k page-size fragmentation strategy (PR 8a7f3d1c) intended to reduce memory overhead for long KV caches; (2) continuous-batching scheduler introduced a micro-batching warmup phase that merges short-lived requests aggressively for throughput. Both changes passed unit and integration tests. Why this doc: applied-ml onboarding evs found micro-regressions when running canonical prompt-set battery (promptset: microseq-edgecases-v1). We need a documented ledger for triage and to guide quick rollback/mitigation decisions.
source.observed_behavior:
- Latency: p50 unchanged but p95 increased by ~7–12ms on short sequences (<=96 tokens) for flash-attn-enabled runs.
- Throughput: token/sec unchanged in steady-state for long sequences; throughput improved slightly for batched short sequences.
- Functional diffs: for 85/5,000 prompts (1.7%) we see altered answer phrasing; for 90/5,000 prompts (1.8%) safety/hallucination flags raised where none existed.
- Repro pattern: diffs correlate with (a) repeated named-entity co-ref prompts, (b) prompts that hit KV page boundary at inference start, (c) user prompts submitted in <20ms burst spacing that are merged by continuous-batching.
- Non-deterministic amplification: when both paged-KV fragmentation and continuous-batching are enabled together, diffs double compared to enabling either alone.
source.reproduction_steps:
1) Environment: canary cluster tag runtime-2026-03-cbc, model: redwood-open-13b-v3, quantization: q4f16, flash-attn: enabled.
2) Pull promptset microseq-edgecases-v1 from confluence link.
3) Run batch job with 5,000 canonical prompts at applied-ml/eval harness commit 3b4e9a2 (use seed 42).
4) Collect outputs and run diff tool (scripts/eval/diff_promptsets.py) to compare against baseline runtime-2026-02-stable outputs.
5) Toggle features: (A) paged-KV only, (B) continuous-batching only, (C) both on. Measure latency p50/p95, token throughput, exact-match, rouge-l, hallucination-flag rate.
6) Repeat with flash-attn disabled as control.
Notes: reproduce on a 1-GPU canary node may undercount diff amplification; run on 4-node canary pool for reliable amplification of KV page boundary conditions.
source.metric_snapshots:
Baseline (runtime-2026-02-stable): p95 latency (short seq) = 112ms, hallucination-flag rate = 0.9%, altered-answer rate = 1.2%.
Canary (both changes enabled): p95 latency (short seq) = 123ms, hallucination-flag rate = 2.7%, altered-answer rate = 2.9%.
Delta (canary - baseline): p95 +11ms, hallucination +1.8pp, altered-answer +1.7pp.
Isolation runs: paged-KV only => p95 +6ms, hallucination +0.9pp; continuous-batching only => p95 +4ms, hallucination +0.6pp. Combined effects are roughly additive with a small supra-linear interaction when flash-attn is on.
source.promptset_insights:
- Prompts that triggered diffs include: short QA with entity chaining (e.g., 'Where is Dr. K heading? She left school earlier; where will she be at noon?'), index/lookup-style prompts that rely on stable tokenization boundaries, and prompts that intentionally stress KV cache warm-start (prefix-prime sequences).
- Failure modes: slight decoding path differences cause different beam re-rank ordering and in some cases different punctuation that flips safety/hallucination heuristics.
- Sensitivity: tokenization offsets near page boundaries are high-sensitivity points; a single off-by-one KV alignment can change attention scope for a few tokens and cascade into answer framing differences.
source.raw_examples:
(example 1) Prompt id: ms-472
Input: 'Alice met Boris in 3 towns. She asked him about the museum. Did Boris remind her about the tickets?'
Baseline output: 'Yes — Boris reminded Alice about the tickets before noon.'
Canary output: 'It seems Boris had not reminded Alice, he planned to earlier.'
Observation: shifted coreference resolution, downstream safety classifier flagged low confidence.

(example 2) Prompt id: ms-1981
Input: short lookup prompt that maps a code to a place. Baseline returns exact canonical string; canary returns paraphrase with missing field leading to downstream parser failure.

(example 3) Token-boundary case: prompt primes KV cache with 92 tokens, model crossing into a new page on inference step 1; slight attention change when paged-KV uses new page boundary leads to different top-k tokens in early decoding steps.
source.analysis_and_hypotheses:
1) Paged-KV change: 8k page-size fragmentation reduces memory but introduces page-alignment effects that alter initial KV indexing for short sequences that prime the cache at runtime start; this is a likely root cause for boundary-sensitive prompts.
2) Continuous-batching: aggressive merge of sub-20ms inter-arrival requests changes micro-ordering of request processing and may alter RNG seed progression or internal cache reuse ordering, producing non-deterministic behavior on edge prompts.
3) Flash-attn interaction: optimized attention kernel can change numerical accumulation order and rounding; combined with page-aligned KV offsets, small numeric differences amplify into top-token reorderings.
Conclusion: multiple small changes interact to cross a threshold for a subset of prompts. None alone is catastrophic, but combined they create a measurable behavioral surface change.
source.proposed_mitigations:
Short-term (hotfix window):
- Roll back continuous-batching merge heuristics for bursts <40ms (owner: Avery).
- Revert paged-KV page-size change on canary and re-run full promptset (owner: Lina).
- Add a targeted regression-suite gate: microseq-edgecases-v1 must show <0.5pp hallucination delta before rollout.

Medium-term:
- Implement KV page-alignment invariants in runtime (ensure consistent KV indexing independent of page size) (owner: Ethan).
- Add determinism logging hooks to capture request-processing ordering and RNG seed evolution for merged batches.
- Augment eval harness to include token-boundary stress tests and a 'page-boundary' synthetic prompt generator.

Long-term:
- Improve attention numerics testing matrix (floating accumulation ordering) and add micro-benchmarks that compare decoding prefix token sequences across kernel implementations.
source.action_items:
- [ ] Rollback continuous-batching merge heuristics on canary (Avery) — target: 2026-03-13
- [ ] Revert paged-KV page-size change on canary (Lina) — target: 2026-03-14
- [ ] Add microseq-edgecases-v1 to master gating suite (Priya) — target: 2026-03-16
- [ ] Implement KV indexing invariants spike (Ethan) — target PR by 2026-03-21
- [ ] Run full redwood-open-13b-v3 onboarding eval with flash-attn toggles and post results to confluence (Priya, Lina) — target: 2026-03-18
- [ ] Postmortem draft (owner: Priya) — target: 2026-03-20
source.notes_and_open_questions:
- Is the hallucination classifier itself sensitive to punctuation/phrasing changes around tokens of interest? We should confirm classifier stability with synthetic paraphrases.
- Are there telemetry gaps that prevent us from tracing page-boundary alignment for individual requests? Need a small instrumentation patch.
- Could quantization scheme (q4f16) amplify numeric differences when combined with flash-attn internal reorderings? Run an isolation matrix varying quantization.
- Does continuous-batching alter RNG seeding for top-k sampling when merging requests? Add deterministic-seed option in scheduler for test runs.
source.footer: Prepared by: Priya Natarajan (applied-ml) — initial triage notes for runtime/serving teams. If you take ownership of any action items above, add your name under collaborators and update status. This document is a working ledger; expect follow-ups and attached diffs in linked_artifacts.

title: Microseq regression ledger — KV fragmentation & promptset diffs
preview: Short summary: After toggling the new paged-KV allocator (PR 8a7f3d1c) and enabling the continuous-batching scheduler on canary, we observed a small but consistent regression on a subset of micro-sequence prompt sets. The regression manifests as token-level latency spikes at sequence boundaries and a 1.8% increase in hallucination triggers on targeted QA prompts. Behavioral diffs are concentrated on prompts with repeated entity co-reference and short context windows (<96 tokens). This ledger captures the prompt-level diffs, reproduction notes, metric snapshots, and proposed mitigations for the release team.
text: Short summary: After toggling the new paged-KV allocator (PR 8a7f3d1c) and enabling the continuous-batching scheduler on canary, we observed a small but consistent regression on a subset of micro-sequence prompt sets. The regression manifests as token-level latency spikes at sequence boundaries and a 1.8% increase in hallucination triggers on targeted QA prompts. Behavioral diffs are concentrated on prompts with repeated entity co-reference and short context windows (<96 tokens). This ledger captures the prompt-level diffs, reproduction notes, metric snapshots, and proposed mitigations for the release team.

source.title: Allocator + Routing experiments: notes and action items
source.content:
Attendees: Priya, Liam, Sara, Omar, Nadia
Meeting: ad-hoc sync (lab notes) — focused on allocator behaviour when routing rules + quota throttles are active
Context / why: last week we saw a spike in ephemeral allocation churn tied to the new "steady-bucket" allocator mode + an unusual pattern in routing fallbacks that doubled small-req latency at p99. Goal: capture quick experimental setup + observed signals so SRE + runtime can triage.

Summary of experiments run (2026-03-10):
- Experiment A: steady-bucket allocator enabled, incremental backoff on node acquisition = 50ms -> 200ms. Load: synthetic trace from production-sample-2026-03-09 (mixed short/long sequences). Duration: 2h.
  - Observed: avg allocation latency +12%, p95 allocation latency +45% under bursty arrival; p99 token-latency increased ~18ms for short requests (<= 64 tokens).
  - Hypothesis: backoff delays caused queueing at request-router layer leading to more routing fallbacks to compatible model variants.

- Experiment B: steady-bucket OFF, keeper-reserve=2 for each pool, routing policy changed to prefer local-region capacity for small requests. Duration: 1.5h.
  - Observed: allocation churn reduced ~30%, p99 latency for small requests dropped back to baseline, but overall utilization dropped by 8% (worse cost efficiency).
  - Tradeoff: lower churn vs higher idle capacity.

- Experiment C: throttle-quota simulation enabled (soft quotas at 80% of configured). Throttle reaction time shortened from 120s -> 30s to emulate aggressive quota enforcement.
  - Observed: routing layer triggered emergency fallback earlier; saw increased cross-region traffic for ~3min windows after throttle kicks in. Cross-region fallbacks added ~30ms median and +60ms p99 for those routed requests.

Key signals captured (metrics / logs):
- allocator.request_latency_ms: spike in 95/99 when steady-bucket + backoff active
- router.fallback_count: correlated with allocator spikes (pearson ~0.78)
- quotas.enforcement_events: clustered, often followed by a small-window surge in cross-region routing
- trace sample: threadstarve events in host-agent logs coinciding with allocation retry loops (see linked PR logs)

Immediate conclusions:
- The combination of steady-bucket + aggressive backoff increases tail latency by queueing at the router level and increasing fallback pressure.
- Faster quota enforcement reduces time-to-fallback but amplifies cross-region traffic and tail latency for those fallbacks.
- Keeper-reserve mitigates churn but at cost of utilization.

Open questions / unknowns: 
- Is queueing occurring in the router request queue or in the allocator's local pending list? Need deeper trace correlation (span-level) to confirm.
- Can we implement a non-blocking fast-path for small requests (<=64 tokens) to bypass allocator backoff?
- How do these behaviors change under real multi-tenant traffic (we used a synthetic mixed trace)?

Decisions proposed (quick):
- Short-term: disable steady-bucket + backoff in prod for regions with recent p99 spikes until we have an improved backoff algorithm. (Proposed owner: Priya)
- Medium-term: add keeper-reserve toggle per pool type (small-request pools default to 1 reserve). (Owner: Omar)
- Long-term: implement a router-side fast-path for low-seq-length requests to reduce allocator dependencies. (Owner: Liam + eng-serving-runtime)

Action items (owners + rough due dates):
- Priya: roll back steady-bucket/backoff in eu-west-1 and us-west-2 tonight (2026-03-12 EOD) — notify SRE + update status page.
- Omar: prototype pool keeper-reserve toggle PR and load-test in lab (due 2026-03-18). Link: https://github.com/redwood-inference/controlplane/pull/678 (draft).
- Liam: add span correlation instrumentation between router.enqueue and allocator.acquire to identify where queueing happens (due 2026-03-15).
- Sara: run multi-tenant replay with 5 customer traces and report diff vs synthetic (due 2026-03-17).
- Nadia: run an experiment with quota enforcement windows extended to 90s to see fallback smoothing (due 2026-03-16).

Notes to self / rough TODOs:
- capture a sample trace where router.fallback_count jumps and attach host-agent thread stacks (ticket RINF-4321).
- check whether autoscaler interacts with keeper-reserve unexpectedly (possible double-eviction).
- follow up with product on acceptable utilization hit for keeper-reserve for small-request pools.

Risks / customer impact:
- If steady-bucket remains enabled in regions with bursty traffic we risk elevated p99 client latency for latency-sensitive flows (chat/small completions).
- Aggressive quota enforcement without a controlled fallback path can increase cross-region traffic and incur bandwidth costs + higher latencies.

Next sync: quick standup follow-up Friday 2026-03-13 09:30 PT — plan to review initial rollback metrics and decide on keeper-reserve rollout.

Attaching: short raw metric extracts and the synthetic trace we used (shared in lab bucket), plus links above.

source.title: Engagement Slate: Sentinel Logistics Q2 Renewal Brief
source.content:
Purpose and meeting goals:
- Run a tight 45–50 minute Quarterly Engagement Slate with Sentinel Logistics (Key contacts: Claire Morgan - Head of AI, Raj Patel - Platform Ops).
- Demonstrate measurable value from Redwood over past 6 months and align on the renewal posture for Aug 2026.
- Move the commercial conversation from reactive support credits to a recommended Dedicated capacity proposal with a clear ROI story.

Top-line ask (for renewal owner to prime):
- Recommend: 12-month Dedicated Starter commit (4 A100-equivalent GPUs) + 6 months of Redwood Optimize professional services for quantization and routing tuning.
- Why: predictable latency for SLO-bound shipment-tracking features, 22% lower per-token cost in our simulation vs hosted at current volumes, and stronger data isolation for procurement compliance.

Agenda (45–50m):
1) 0–5m: Quick opener and success highlight (Claire + Raj in the room)
2) 5–15m: Account health & adoption highlights (CS lead)
3) 15–25m: Product performance deep-dive (eng lead) with live latency/cost snapshot
4) 25–35m: Risk review & mitigation plan (support + SRE)
5) 35–45m: Renewal proposal & commercial ask (rev/AM + CS)
6) 45–50m: Decision table and immediate next steps (confirm next meeting)

Account health snapshot (what we’ll show):
- Active product surfaces: anomaly detection pipelines (Logi-Alert), customer message assistant (LogiAssist), routing prompt engine for ETA predictions.
- Key metrics (last 90 days):
  - Monthly active endpoints: 12 (up from 4 at baseline)
  - Peak qps: 37 (p95 latency target missed 3% of the time in last 30 days)
  - Token volume: ~1.3B tokens/month (growth 2.5x YoY)
  - Embeddings queries: 1.1M / month (used for semantic search in returns workflow)
- Health signal: Usage growth strong, but two operational pain points: latency spikes during end-of-day batch jobs, and rising inference cost for high concurrency windows.

Slide-by-slide narrative and presenter notes:
Slide 1 — Title + Outcomes (CS lead)- Headline: “Sentinel Logistics — Q2 Engagement Slate: From pilot growth to predictable operations”- Talking points: short bullet on adoption, one clear metric (token growth) and the ask for the meeting (decide on renewal approach).

Slide 2 — Business context (AM)- One-liners on Sentinel’s business priorities: lower ETA variance, faster returns processing, and cost predictability.- Tie their FY26 OKRs to ML product reliability.

Slide 3 — Adoption timeline (CS)- Visual timeline (text bullets): pilot (Q3 ’25) -> production rollout (Q4 ’25) -> multi-region expansion (Q1 ’26).- Note: call out integration with their event bus and the 2-week rollout cadence.

Slide 4 — Value realized (CS + AM)- Quantified wins: 18% reduction in false-positive ETA alerts; 28% faster returns triage; estimated $72k monthly cost avoidance in customer support through LogiAssist automation.- Show an anecdote: “Case: US trucking line — 36hr SLA improvement on returns.”

Slide 5 — Usage & performance snapshot (Eng)- Present top-line graphs (we’ll include screenshots): tokens/day, embeddings/day, p50/p95 token latency, and error rate.- Talking points: explain why p95 drifted last month (concurrent bulk inference jobs at 03:00 UTC) and what we fixed (KV cache fence and batching policy change).

Slide 6 — Cost breakdown (Eng + Optimize)- Per-feature cost: Logi-Alert 42% of tokens, LogiAssist 35%, embeddings 23%.- Show host vs dedicated price simulation at current volume with quantization (simulated 22% lower unit cost for ded).

Slide 7 — Reliability incidents & remediation (SRE)- Incident log (last 6 months): 3 Sev2s tied to burst scaling, one config drift.- Remediation: autoscaling policy tune, queue backpressure control, recommend Dedicated capacity to cap variance.

Slide 8 — Roadmap alignment (Product)- Upcoming features that align: streaming token metrics exports (Q3), model pinning per-route (Q3), and real-time cost alerts (Q4).- Ask for product input: gain approval to run a short A/B test on quantized model for returns routing.

Slide 9 — Risk / dependency table (CS)- Risk: spikes during nightly ETL jobs -> Mitigation: move heavy batch offline or route to lower-cost model variant.- Risk: procurement timeline -> Mitigation: draft SOW + early pricing window.

Slide 10 — Renewal proposal (AM)- Option A (Recommended): Dedicated Starter — 12 months — 4 GPU commit — includes reserve capacity + 99.9% uptime target + 3 on-call rollback days / month.- Option B: Hosted + cost cap (short-term) — 6 months.- Commercial math callout: break-even vs hosted at 9 months given projected growth.

Slide 11 — Implementation plan & milestones (Solutions Eng)- Week 0–2: legal + SOW signatures- Week 2–4: env prep + baseline performance tuning- Week 4–8: migration and canary, A/B quantized model test- Week 8–12: full cutover and handoff to Sentinel platform team

Slide 12 — Decision table & next steps (CS + AM)- Options we want by end of meeting: verbal buy-in to pursue Dedicated or request for option B timeline.- Action items: assign technical POC, schedule procurement check-in, and confirm pricing sheet delivery by EOW.

Tailored talking points and rebuttals (for AMs):
- If they ask about short-term cost: emphasize predictable unit economics and the ability to tune batching/quant. We’ll run a 2-week cost simulation on their exact traces at no charge.
- If they push for self-host in their cloud: highlight rollout & SRE burden and that Redwood Private onboarding timeline is 10–12 weeks vs Dedicated 4–8 weeks.
- If procurement asks for SLA language: have the draft SLA section ready and call out rollback policy & incident credits.

Open questions to surface in meeting (to prepare ahead):
- Which internal teams will be decision-makers for FY26 budget (Procurement, Security, Platform)? Confirm names and timelines.
- Are there specific compliance checkpoints that would make Private deployment required?
- Any planned traffic spikes (e.g., Black Friday-like events) between now and renewal?

Playbook: immediate prep checklist (before the meeting)
- Run cost-simulation with last 90 days of traces and prepare 2 scatter charts (hosted vs ded). — Owner: Maya
- Prepare dedicated migration runbook and SRE on-call rotation proposal. — Owner: Aaron
- Draft short SOW + pricing annex for Option A; get initial legal read. — Owner: Priya
- Validate that embeddings index can be warmed during migration (to avoid cold-start latency). — Owner: Diego

Notes to self / meeting cadence ops
- Keep the meeting tight: practice the 45-minute run once with internal stakeholders.
- Bring chart with 3-year TCO vs self-host + an appendix with sensitivity analysis.
- Ask for a quick customer testimonial quote for the pitch deck if they are willing.

Appendix (ready content to attach to slides)
- Cost simulation CSV (we will attach post-run)
- Incident timeline with root cause summaries
- Short case-study blurb (US trucking line)

Draft email to send to Claire + Raj (to book the slate):
Subject: Sentinel Logistics — Q2 Engagement Slate (45m) 
Hi Claire / Raj,

We’d like to run a focused 45-minute engagement slate to review the production health of your Redwood deployments, share a direct cost & performance analysis, and present a recommended renewal path that reduces variance and unit cost. Suggested times: Tue 2026-03-23 10:00–10:45 PT or Wed 2026-03-24 13:00–13:45 PT.

Agenda attached. Please let me know which slot works and if you want any additional stakeholders included.

Thanks,
Priya

-- end of draft --

Slide 9 — Risk / dependency table (CS)- Risk: spikes during nightly ETL jobs -> Mitigation: move heavy batch offline or route to lower-cost model variant.- Risk: procurement timeline -> Mitigation: draft SOW + early pricing window.

Slide 10 — Renewal proposal (AM)- Option A (Recommended): Dedicated Starter — 12 months — 4 GPU commit — includes reserve capacity + 99.9% uptime target + 3 on-call rollback days / month.- Option B: Hosted + cost cap (short-term) — 6 months.- Commercial math callout: break-even vs hosted at 9 months given projected growth.

Slide 11 — Implementation plan & milestones (Solutions Eng)- Week 0–2: legal + SOW signatures- Week 2–4: env prep + baseline performance tuning- Week 4–8: migration and canary, A/B quantized model test- Week 8–12: full cutover and handoff to Sentinel platform team

Slide 12 — Decision table & next steps (CS + AM)- Options we want by end of meeting: verbal buy-in to pursue Dedicated or request for option B timeline.- Action items: assign technical POC, schedule procurement check-in, and confirm pricing sheet delivery by EOW.

Tailored talking points and rebuttals (for AMs):
- If they ask about short-term cost: emphasize predictable unit economics and the ability to tune batching/quant. We’ll run a 2-week cost simulation on their exact traces at no charge.
- If they push for self-host in their cloud: highlight rollout & SRE burden and that Redwood Private onboarding timeline is 10–12 weeks vs Dedicated 4–8 weeks.
- If procurement asks for SLA language: have the draft SLA section ready and call out rollback policy & incident credits.

Open questions to surface in meeting (to prepare ahead):
- Which internal teams will be decision-makers for FY26 budget (Procurement, Security, Platform)? Confirm names and timelines.
- Are there specific compliance checkpoints that would make Private deployment required?
- Any planned traffic spikes (e.g., Black Friday-like events) between now and renewal?

Playbook: immediate prep checklist (before the meeting)
- Run cost-simulation with last 90 days of traces and prepare 2 scatter charts (hosted vs ded). — Owner: Maya
- Prepare dedicated migration runbook and SRE on-call rotation proposal. — Owner: Aaron
- Draft short SOW + pricing annex for Option A; get initial legal read. — Owner: Priya
- Validate that embeddings index can be warmed during migration (to avoid cold-start latency). — Owner: Diego

Notes to self / meeting cadence ops
- Keep the meeting tight: practice the 45-minute run once with internal stakeholders.
- Bring chart with 3-year TCO vs self-host + an appendix with sensitivity analysis.
- Ask for a quick customer testimonial quote for the pitch deck if they are willing.

Appendix (ready content to attach to slides)
- Cost simulation CSV (we will attach post-run)
- Incident timeline with root cause summaries
- Short case-study blurb (US trucking line)

Hi Claire / Raj,

We’d like to run a focused 45-minute engagement slate to review the production health of your Redwood deployments, share a direct cost & performance analysis, and present a recommended renewal path that reduces variance and unit cost. Suggested times: Tue 2026-03-23 10:00–10:45 PT or Wed 2026-03-24 13:00–13:45 PT.

Agenda attached. Please let me know which slot works and if you want any additional stakeholders included.

Thanks,
Priya
