source.title: Orchestrated SDK Onramp — Chronicle (draft)
source.content:
Purpose:
This doc captures an experimental, orchestrated approach to SDK onramp—mixing automated checks, short mentor sessions, and telemetry gates to accelerate integrator success while catching early regressions. It is a working chronicle: notes from three pilots, a proposed recipe (playbook), and a pocket quickstart for field engineers and solutions reps.

Principles (short):
- Teach by doing: 20–30 minute guided walkthroughs where the user ships a mini-integration.
- Telemetry-first: lightweight events define success signals and gating for rollouts.
- Fast recovery: provide tiny fallbacks and clear rollback paths in the first commit.
- Low friction: aim for zero config for the first demo; expose opt-in complexity later.

Pilot highlights (what we observed):
- Pilot A (marketplace integration): most teams hit auth friction at step 2. Quick fix: provide a prefilled env file and CLI helper `redwood sdk link --demo`.
- Pilot B (chat embed): prefix caching confusion — added a visible cache-status header in responses and a small slide that explains cache semantics helped reduce questions.
- Pilot C (mobile prototype): network flakiness surfaced that our sample app hid — we added a togglable network throttler in the sample app and a troubleshooting card.

Recommended Playbook (orchestrated flow):
1) Preflight (automated): run `redwood-cli onboard check` — verifies API key, region, basic quotas, and local env. Returns a short summary and a set of remediation commands.
2) Guided lab (mentor-led, 20–30m): mentor shares screen or uses co-pilot mode. Tasks: init sample app, call text.generate, inspect streaming, toggle cache, add telemetry.
3) Self-run quickstart (5–10m): user repeats the same steps with a checklist.
4) Telemetry gate (automated): once onboarding succeeds, the SDK auto-sends a namespaced event sample to our staging collector; if errors > 10% in the first 100 events, flag for follow-up.
5) Handoff assets: provide the user with a small artifact bundle (env file, sample unit tests, a minimal mock server).

Telemetry contract (minimal):
We require a tiny, stable set of events emitted by the SDK during onboarding. Each event should contain: event, timestamp, sdk_version, route, latency_ms, sample_id (for correlation).
Example event names:
- sdk.onramp.start
- sdk.onramp.auth_ok
- sdk.onramp.first_request_ok
- sdk.onramp.streaming_enabled
- sdk.onramp.error

Quickstart snippet (developer-facing):
1) Clone starter: `git clone git@github.com:redwood-inference/sdk-starters.git && cd sdk-starters/minimal-chat`
2) Install & init: `npm ci && REDWOOD_API_KEY=pk_test x redwood-cli init --demo`
3) Run the sample: `npm start` (open http://localhost:3000)
4) Smoke test: run `redwood-cli onboard check` — expect `OK: auth, region, demo-routes`

Mentor runbook (cheats):
- If `onboard check` fails auth: confirm API key (ask user to run `echo $REDWOOD_API_KEY | cut -c1-8` to confirm prefix), region mismatch -> suggest `export REDWOOD_REGION=us-west`
- If streaming hangs: ask for `--trace` output from `redwood-cli logs` and check `client.keepAlive` in sample config.
- If high latency: check local network, then toggle `PREFETCH_CACHE=false` to see baseline.

First-commit checklist (what we want the user to ship fast):
- [ ] SDK initialized with API key (not hardcoded in repo).
- [ ] One end-to-end test that hits a non-prod route.
- [ ] Telemetry events visible in staging console.
- [ ] Feature flags configured for safe rollout.

Failure modes & mitigations (practical):
- Auth tokens are rotated mid-onboarding: provide a `redwood-cli token refresh` that prints helper command for envs and CI.
- Model fallback triggers too early (user sees quality drop): increase canary weight and add user-visible fallback banner in UI explaining temporary downgrade.
- Cache incoherence on mobile: expose a `clearKVCache()` helper and a single-shot toggle in the sample app.

Open questions & next experiments: 
- Can we make `onboard check` run in 500ms for most networks? Likely needs parallelism + shorter telemetry sample.
- Is a short LLM-generated walkthrough (auto-created from repo files) helpful as a follow-up? Pilot a PoC that drafts a 3-step checklist.
- What privacy constraints for telemetry in EU customers? Add opt-out and an anonymized mode for EU pilots.

Assets to produce (next 6 weeks):
- `redwood-cli onboard check` v0.2 (automated fix links) — owner: Liam
- Mentor session deck (10 slides + notes) — owner: Sofia
- Sample app update: network toggles + clear cache button — owner: Devon
- Telemetry schema doc + example collector script — owner: Priya

Notes to self (short):
- Keep onboarding micro-sessions to 30 minutes max.
- Collect a small set of annotated screen recordings from pilots (2–3 minutes each).
- Make the quickstart path the default in the starter repo; advanced paths behind a `--advanced` flag.

Appendix (short checklist for field engineers):
- Bring a second monitor for mentor sessions to share logs.
- Have a forked starter repo ready with an inserted demo API key (revocable).
- After session: create a follow-up ticket tagged `onramp-followup` with artifacts and the telemetry sample id.

End of chronicle — feedback welcome.

(If you want to iterate, ping me in #eng-developer-experience or add inline comments to this doc.)

source.title: Sourcing Calibration + 90-day Onboarding Playbook (WIP)
source.content:
Purpose:
This is a working playbook to (1) align sourcers, hiring managers, and interviewers on candidate profile and evaluation, and (2) provide a repeatable 30/60/90 onboarding checklist for technical hires joining infra/serving-runtime teams. Focus role examples: Senior/Lead Technical Program Manager (Serving Runtime), Senior Software Engineer (Inference), Infra SRE.

Summary/Goals:
- Reduce time-to-offer for infra org roles from 42 days -> 28 days by standardizing scorecards and triage steps.
- Improve interview consistency: define 4 core evaluation buckets (technical depth, systems judgment, program execution, collaboration & communication).
- Ensure new hires hit meaningful milestones by week 4/8/12 with clear owner tasks and early feedback loops.

Sourcing Calibration (short):
- Ideal profile shorthand: 5+ years building/operating GPU-backed services or distributed CPU-heavy workloads; demonstrated cross-team program ownership; has shipped optimizations (cost/latency) not just features.
- Red flags: only ML research experience w/out production exposure, no quantifiable impact on latency/cost, vague descriptions of "ownership".
- Primary channels: internal referrals, specialized infra channels (HPC/ML infra Slack communities), alumni networks from scale infra orgs.
- Quick boolean checklist for sourcers:
  - Has experience with production model serving or distributed caching? (Y/N)
  - Example of measurable perf improvement? (Y/N)
  - Program/project across >2 teams? (Y/N)
  - Comfortable with ambiguity + on-call history? (Y/N)
- Sourcing note: prioritize candidates who can explain tradeoffs (latency vs cost, batching vs real-time) in concrete terms. Wildcards: strong SRE candidates who lack ML infra but have real-time systems experience.

Interview Rubric (calibration draft):
Score 1-4 for each dimension (1=miss, 2=basic, 3=good, 4=strong) with examples to anchor.
1) Technical Depth (architecture, system design) - 30%
  - 4: Designs a sharded KV + cache + fallback system, enumerates failure modes, can sketch KV cache eviction impacts on latency and correctness, suggests mitigations.
  - 2: Gives high-level system blocks but misses critical failure modes or cost implications.
2) Systems Judgment & Tradeoffs - 25%
  - 4: Discusses batching tradeoffs, memory vs throughput, quantization impacts, and tradeoffs for multi-tenant GPU pools.
3) Program Execution & Delivery - 25%
  - 4: Lays out clear rollout plan (canary, metrics, rollback), risk matrix, stakeholder map; examples of shipping cross-team rollout.
4) Communication & Collaboration - 15%
  - 4: Communicates clearly to both engineers and product stakeholders; shows examples of resolving ambiguous requirements.
5) Cultural / Redwoods Fit (bias for shipping, reliability focus) - 5% (pass/fail soft gate)

Interview loop template (for senior infra roles):
- Recruiter screen (20m): logistics, compensation, basic fit, red flags.
- Hiring manager technical screen (45m): architecture depth + recent project deep-dive.

preview:
Purpose:
This is a working playbook to (1) align sourcers, hiring managers, and interviewers on candidate profile and evaluation, and (2) provide a repeatable 30/60/90 onboarding checklist for technical hires joining infra/serving-runtime teams. Focus role examples: Senior/Lead Technical Program Manager (Serving Runtime), Senior Software Engineer (Inference), Infra SRE.

Summary/Goals:
- Reduce time-to-offer for infra org roles from 42 days -> 28 days by standardizing scorecards and triage steps.
- Improve interview consistency: define 4 core evaluation buckets (technical depth, systems judgment, program execution, collaboration & communication).
- Ensure new hires hit meaningful milestones by week 4/8/12 with clear owner tasks and early feedback loops.

Sourcing Calibration (short):
- Ideal profile shorthand: 5+ years building/operating GPU-backed services or distributed CPU-heavy workloads; demonstrated cross-team program ownership; has shipped optimizations (cost/latency) not just features.
- Red flags: only ML research experience w/out production exposure, no quantifiable impact on latency/cost, vague descriptions of "ownership".
- Primary channels: internal referrals, specialized infra channels (HPC/ML infra Slack communities), alumni networks from scale infra orgs.
- Quick boolean checklist for sourcers:
  - Has experience with production model serving or distributed caching? (Y/N)
  - Example of measurable perf improvement? (Y/N)
  - Program/project across >2 teams? (Y/N)
  - Comfortable with ambiguity + on-call history? (Y/N)
- Sourcing note: prioritize candidates who can explain tradeoffs (latency vs cost, batching vs real-time) in concrete terms. Wildcards: strong SRE candidates who lack ML infra but have real-time systems experience.

Interview Rubric (calibration draft):
Score 1-4 for each dimension (1=miss, 2=basic, 3=good, 4=strong) with examples to anchor.
1) Technical Depth (architecture, system design) - 30%
  - 4: Designs a sharded KV + cache + fallback system, enumerates failure modes, can sketch KV cache eviction impacts on latency and correctness, suggests mitigations.
  - 2: Gives high-level system blocks but misses critical failure modes or cost implications.
2) Systems Judgment & Tradeoffs - 25%
  - 4: Discusses batching tradeoffs, memory vs throughput, quantization impacts, and tradeoffs for multi-tenant GPU pools.
3) Program Execution & Delivery - 25%
  - 4: Lays out clear rollout plan (canary, metrics, rollback), risk matrix, stakeholder map; examples of shipping cross-team rollout.
4) Communication & Collaboration - 15%
  - 4: Communicates clearly to both engineers and product stakeholders; shows examples of resolving ambiguous requirements.
5) Cultural / Redwoods Fit (bias for shipping, reliability focus) - 5% (pass/fail soft gate)

source.title: Signal dependency mapping for latency & quality - dashboard stitching canvas
source.content:
Purpose:
This is a working canvas to map signal dependencies (metrics + traces) that feed our inference dashboards and SLI calculations. The focus is on framing which signals are direct vs derived, how dashboards should stitch traces to metrics, and where sampling or reduced fidelity is acceptable.

Context / problem statement:
- Observability sprawl: multiple dashboards show similar latency/quality stories but use different metric aggregations and trace anchors. Operators get different answers depending on which dashboard they consult.
- Root-cause gaps: when investigating latency spikes we often lack a clear hop-by-hop dependency map (model load, KV cache, routing, compilation). Traces exist but are not consistently linked to metricisers.

Goals for this canvas:
1) Produce a pragmatic signal dependency graph for the most-used dashboards (Ops, Rollouts, Cost).
2) Define widget contracts: each dashboard panel must declare its primary signal, fallback signals, and required trace anchors.
3) Agree on sampling policy for traces associated with derived metrics and SLI windows.
4) Create a compact drillflow pattern so operators can pivot from high-level SLI to span-level context in <3 clicks.

Scope (what this doc covers):
- Token-serving path for hosted + dedicated deployments (request routing -> model -> kv-cache -> token generation).
- Latency, error, and quality signals related to generation and embedding endpoints.
- Dashboard stitching patterns and required query snippets.
- NOT in scope: billing chargeback policy, long-term retention policy (separate doc).

Signal catalog (candidate list, with intent and owner):
- request.latency.p95 (derived): p95 of end-to-end request latency measured at API ingress. Owner: eng-api. Use as SLI anchor for user-facing latency. Query sketch: percentile_over_time(api_latency_ms, 95, 5m).
- kvcache.hit_rate (primary): percent of requests served from KV cache. Owner: serving-runtime. Intent: explain cold-start/cold-cache spikes. Query sketch: sum(kv_hits)/sum(kv_lookups) by tenant.
- model.inf_time.p99 (primary): p99 of model forward pass (excl. enqueue). Owner: serving-runtime/research. Use to detect model/compilation regressions.
- routing.queue_ms (derived): median and 95th of queuing time pre-exec. Owner: platform-engineering. Important for batching/backpressure investigations.
- token.gen.rate (derived): tokens/sec per request. Useful to interpret perceived latency vs tokenization patterns.
- prompt.len.dist (primary): distribution of prompt lengths (buckets). Used to normalize latency and cost.
- endpoint.errors.rate (primary): % of errored responses at ingress. Owner: eng-sre.

Trace anchor design (recommendations):
- Anchor spans to session_id + request_id + model_variant. Traces without session_id should be rejected from correlation unless explicitly for synthetic probes.
- Span naming conventions: "api.ingress", "router.dispatch", "kvcache.lookup", "model.forward", "kernel.execute". Keep consistent tags: tenant_id, route, model_variant, seq_len, kv_hit=true/false.
- KV-cache trace enrichment: when kvcache.lookup returns miss, include a reason tag (cold_prefix, expired, mem_pressure). This lets metric->trace mapping quickly show cause.

Metric to trace mapping rules (stitching):
- Panels that show derived metrics (e.g., end-to-end p95) must include a small drilldown panel that re-queries raw traces filtered by the same timeframe + the highest contributing partition (top tenant or top model_variant).
- When showing cohorted SLIs (by tenant or route), dashboards must pass the cohort tag into the trace query so the operator lands directly on relevant spans.

Sampling & fidelity guidance:
- Default adaptive sampling: 1% global base + targeted 100% sampling for traces where request.latency > p95 and endpoint.errors.rate > threshold. Rationale: keep base cost low, capture anomalies fully.
- For model-forward spans where model.inf_time.p99 > baseline: increase sampling to 10% for that model_variant for 30 minutes automatically.
- Sensitive data redaction: traces must avoid storing prompt text. Use hashed prompt fingerprints only.

Dashboard widget contract (mini-template every panel should include):
- title: plain text
- primary_signal: metric id used as the truth source
- fallback_signals: list (short)
- trace_anchor: which span name(s) + tags required to link to traces
- recommended_sampling_policy: e.g., "adaptive anomaly sampling"
- owner: team/person

Example widget (Ops SLI panel):
- title: API E2E p95 (prod, US)
- primary_signal: request.latency.p95{region=us, env=prod}
- fallback_signals: model.inf_time.p95, routing.queue_ms.p95
- trace_anchor: spans with name = api.ingress and tag request_id (pass request_id to trace query)
- recommended_sampling_policy: 1% base + full capture for requests > p95
- owner: eng-sre

Investigation workflows (target <3 clicks):
1) From p95 spike panel -> click into widget drillflow -> view top-10 tenant/model_variant contributors -> click contributor -> open pre-filtered trace stream (time window = spike window).
2) In trace stream -> auto-annotate spans with metric deltas (kvcache hit/miss, queue time).
3) Option to replay prompt fingerprint (no PII) into sandbox for reproduction (requires permission).

Open questions / tradeoffs:
- How aggressive should cohort-based trace sampling be for low-volume tenants? (cost vs debug value)
- Do we need a separate lightweight trace shard retained longer for slow-moving rollouts?
- How to surface quantization/compilation events (these are internal to runtime) to dashboard without exposing implementation noise?

Action items / next steps:
- (Priya) Draft a widget contract template + JSON schema for adoption (due 2026-03-17).
- (Ethan) Implement adaptive sampling prototype for p95 anomaly capture (OD-342).
- (Zara) Update kvcache span to include miss_reason tag + sample events (PR: github.com/redwood/obs/PRs/221).
- Schedule a 60-min sync with SRE + Serving Runtime to validate the signal list (owner: Priya)

Notes to self / informal reminders:
- Keep this canvas intentionally lightweight; we want operator adoption, not more panels.
- When in doubt: make the trace link explicit in the panel UI (not buried).

---
If useful, I can convert the widget contract template to a JSON schema and push a sample dashboard JSON to the obs repo next week.

2) In trace stream -> auto-annotate spans with metric deltas (kvcache hit/miss, queue time).
3) Option to replay prompt fingerprint (no PII) into sandbox for reproduction (requires permission).

Open questions / tradeoffs:
- How aggressive should cohort-based trace sampling be for low-volume tenants? (cost vs debug value)
- Do we need a separate lightweight trace shard retained longer for slow-moving rollouts?
- How to surface quantization/compilation events (these are internal to runtime) to dashboard without exposing implementation noise?

Action items / next steps:
- (Priya) Draft a widget contract template + JSON schema for adoption (due 2026-03-17).
- (Ethan) Implement adaptive sampling prototype for p95 anomaly capture (OD-342).
- (Zara) Update kvcache span to include miss_reason tag + sample events (PR: github.com/redwood/obs/PRs/221).
- Schedule a 60-min sync with SRE + Serving Runtime to validate the signal list (owner: Priya)

Notes to self / informal reminders:
- Keep this canvas intentionally lightweight; we want operator adoption, not more panels.
- When in doubt: make the trace link explicit in the panel UI (not buried).

---
If useful, I can convert the widget contract template to a JSON schema and push a sample dashboard JSON to the obs repo next week.
text:
Purpose:
This is a working canvas to map signal dependencies (metrics + traces) that feed our inference dashboards and SLI calculations. The focus is on framing which signals are direct vs derived, how dashboards should stitch traces to metrics, and where sampling or reduced fidelity is acceptable.

Context / problem statement:
- Observability sprawl: multiple dashboards show similar latency/quality stories but use different metric aggregations and trace anchors. Operators get different answers depending on which dashboard they consult.
- Root-cause gaps: when investigating latency spikes we often lack a clear hop-by-hop dependency map (model load, KV cache, routing, compilation). Traces exist but are not consistently linked to metricisers.

Goals for this canvas:
1) Produce a pragmatic signal dependency graph for the most-used dashboards (Ops, Rollouts, Cost).
2) Define widget contracts: each dashboard panel must declare its primary signal, fallback signals, and required trace anchors.
3) Agree on sampling policy for traces associated with derived metrics and SLI windows.
4) Create a compact drillflow pattern so operators can pivot from high-level SLI to span-level context in <3 clicks.

Scope (what this doc covers):
- Token-serving path for hosted + dedicated deployments (request routing -> model -> kv-cache -> token generation).
- Latency, error, and quality signals related to generation and embedding endpoints.
- Dashboard stitching patterns and required query snippets.
- NOT in scope: billing chargeback policy, long-term retention policy (separate doc).

Signal catalog (candidate list, with intent and owner):
- request.latency.p95 (derived): p95 of end-to-end request latency measured at API ingress. Owner: eng-api. Use as SLI anchor for user-facing latency. Query sketch: percentile_over_time(api_latency_ms, 95, 5m).
- kvcache.hit_rate (primary): percent of requests served from KV cache. Owner: serving-runtime. Intent: explain cold-start/cold-cache spikes. Query sketch: sum(kv_hits)/sum(kv_lookups) by tenant.
- model.inf_time.p99 (primary): p99 of model forward pass (excl. enqueue). Owner: serving-runtime/research. Use to detect model/compilation regressions.
- routing.queue_ms (derived): median and 95th of queuing time pre-exec. Owner: platform-engineering. Important for batching/backpressure investigations.
- token.gen.rate (derived): tokens/sec per request. Useful to interpret perceived latency vs tokenization patterns.
- prompt.len.dist (primary): distribution of prompt lengths (buckets). Used to normalize latency and cost.
- endpoint.errors.rate (primary): % of errored responses at ingress. Owner: eng-sre.

Trace anchor design (recommendations):
- Anchor spans to session_id + request_id + model_variant. Traces without session_id should be rejected from correlation unless explicitly for synthetic probes.
- Span naming conventions: "api.ingress", "router.dispatch", "kvcache.lookup", "model.forward", "kernel.execute". Keep consistent tags: tenant_id, route, model_variant, seq_len, kv_hit=true/false.
- KV-cache trace enrichment: when kvcache.lookup returns miss, include a reason tag (cold_prefix, expired, mem_pressure). This lets metric->trace mapping quickly show cause.

Metric to trace mapping rules (stitching):
- Panels that show derived metrics (e.g., end-to-end p95) must include a small drilldown panel that re-queries raw traces filtered by the same timeframe + the highest contributing partition (top tenant or top model_variant).
- When showing cohorted SLIs (by tenant or route), dashboards must pass the cohort tag into the trace query so the operator lands directly on relevant spans.

Sampling & fidelity guidance:
- Default adaptive sampling: 1% global base + targeted 100% sampling for traces where request.latency > p95 and endpoint.errors.rate > threshold. Rationale: keep base cost low, capture anomalies fully.
- For model-forward spans where model.inf_time.p99 > baseline: increase sampling to 10% for that model_variant for 30 minutes automatically.
- Sensitive data redaction: traces must avoid storing prompt text. Use hashed prompt fingerprints only.

Dashboard widget contract (mini-template every panel should include):
- title: plain text
- primary_signal: metric id used as the truth source
- fallback_signals: list (short)
- trace_anchor: which span name(s) + tags required to link to traces
- recommended_sampling_policy: e.g., "adaptive anomaly sampling"
- owner: team/person

Example widget (Ops SLI panel):
- title: API E2E p95 (prod, US)
- primary_signal: request.latency.p95{region=us, env=prod}
- fallback_signals: model.inf_time.p95, routing.queue_ms.p95
- trace_anchor: spans with name = api.ingress and tag request_id (pass request_id to trace query)
- recommended_sampling_policy: 1% base + full capture for requests > p95
- owner: eng-sre

Investigation workflows (target <3 clicks):
1) From p95 spike panel -> click into widget drillflow -> view top-10 tenant/model_variant contributors -> click contributor -> open pre-filtered trace stream (time window = spike window).
2) In trace stream -> auto-annotate spans with metric deltas (kvcache hit/miss, queue time).
3) Option to replay prompt fingerprint (no PII) into sandbox for reproduction (requires permission).

Open questions / tradeoffs:
- How aggressive should cohort-based trace sampling be for low-volume tenants? (cost vs debug value)
- Do we need a separate lightweight trace shard retained longer for slow-moving rollouts?
- How to surface quantization/compilation events (these are internal to runtime) to dashboard without exposing implementation noise?

Action items / next steps:
- (Priya) Draft a widget contract template + JSON schema for adoption (due 2026-03-17).
- (Ethan) Implement adaptive sampling prototype for p95 anomaly capture (OD-342).
- (Zara) Update kvcache span to include miss_reason tag + sample events (PR: github.com/redwood/obs/PRs/221).
- Schedule a 60-min sync with SRE + Serving Runtime to validate the signal list (owner: Priya)

Notes to self / informal reminders:
- Keep this canvas intentionally lightweight; we want operator adoption, not more panels.
- When in doubt: make the trace link explicit in the panel UI (not buried).

---
If useful, I can convert the widget contract template to a JSON schema and push a sample dashboard JSON to the obs repo next week.

source.title: mixed-script-sanity-sampler-cjk-rtl-2026
source.summary: Working prompt sampler and spot-check checklist for onboarding new LLM variants against mixed-script production expectations (focus: CJK + RTL intersections, translation fidelity, and locale-specific formatting).
source.purpose: Provide a compact, high-signal prompt set and run instructions for quick sanity checks during model onboarding and regression runs. Target is to catch formatting regressions, script-mixing decode issues, and major translation artifacts before wider evals are triggered.
source.scope: Not exhaustive. Designed as a lightweight gate used by solutions engineering and eval engineers when bringing model candidates into Redwood's serving catalog. Covers: mixed-script boundaries (Latin+CJK, Arabic+Latin), RTL shaping/mirroring, punctuation & numeric formatting across locales, key translation quality edgecases, and tokenizer-driven whitespace/width issues.
prompt_set_overview: CJK-with-Latin-product-names: short UI strings mixing English product names with Chinese/Japanese/Korean surrounding text
Arabic-with-LTR-code-snippet: Arabic instruction sentence containing inline LTR code and identifiers
Date-and-number-formatting: same logical date expressed in multiple locale formats and numeral systems
Currency-and-space-width: tests for halfwidth/fullwidth currency symbols and spacing in CJK context
Bidi-embedded-URL: URL inside RTL text and expected rendering/ordering
Punctuation-mirroring: quotes, parentheses, and chevrons in RTL vs LTR surrounding text
Transliteration-control: names that should be transliterated vs names that should be preserved
Idiomatic-translation-short: single-sentence prompts where literal vs idiomatic translation must be distinguished
Long-prefix-cache: same prompt repeated with growing prefix to exercise KV-cache/shard boundaries
Ambiguous-segmentation: mixed ideographs + ASCII digits to test tokenizer splitting and spacing
source.sampling_instructions: Run each prompt with temperature 0.0 and 0.7, top_p 0.95. Use streaming disabled for deterministic reference runs. For quick gate, use top_k 40 and max_tokens padding of +40 beyond expected response length. Record tokenization offsets and raw token sequence for first 3 outputs per temp setting.
example_prompts: 简短的说明：请描述 iPhone 13 在中国的主要卖点。
请用阿拉伯语写一条短信通知：Server 42 (ID: 0x7fa) 已重启。
Translate to English: "我在2026年3月5日提交的发票金额是¥12,345.67。"
Arabic: أضف هذا الرابط داخل الجملة https://example.com/page?id=42 وتأكد من ترتيب الأحرف صحيح
Mixed: 東京駅 - arrival 14:30 (GMT+9) — 请确认时间格式。
Name handling: Translate but keep proper nouns intact: "President Nguyễn Phú Trọng will speak tomorrow."
expected_output_signatures: No mirrored Latin characters in RTL outputs (A-B order preserved unless bidi markers present)
Dates normalized to locale when asked; keep original numeral system unless instruction requests conversion
Currency shows correct symbol spacing for CJK (ideographic space where appropriate) and halfwidth in LTR contexts
Transliterated names follow configured transliteration table or are left verbatim when flagged
No dropped or duplicated punctuation when switching bidi contexts
Tokenizer offsets align with visible characters; no phantom whitespace or unexpected fullwidth tokens
evaluation_metrics: chrF++ for short translation quality (sensitive to character-level errors in CJK)
COMET-L2 for semantic fidelity on longer items
Mirroring-error-rate: fraction of prompts where punctuation/parenthesis are mirrored incorrectly
Numeric-normalization-fail: percent where numeral system or separators change unexpectedly
Tokenization-drift: count of cases where token boundaries produce leading/trailing spaces in output
regression_checks: Run baseline model X (ref commit) and candidate model. Fail gate if chrF++ drops by > 3 points for any CJK prompt subset.
Mirroring-error-rate increase > 2% triggers in_review and manual glance deck
Any increase in numeric-normalization-fail should block shipping for billing/invoice flows
If transliteration table mismatch occurs for >5 names in sample, create ticket and attach examples
source.data_requirements: Source strings include UTF-8 with explicit bidi markers in a handful of probes. Provide golden translations for each prompt in both languages where applicable. Annotated expected ranges for dates/currency per locale (zh-CN, ja-JP, ko-KR, ar-SA, he-IL).
runbook_steps: 1) Pull latest candidate model and baseline IDs from repo referenced in linked_artifacts
2) Run deterministic batch (temp 0.0) and collect raw outputs and token sequences
3) Run stochastic batch (temp 0.7) for diversity checks, 3 seeds
4) Compute metrics (chrF++, COMET) and mirroring/numeric checks with provided scripts
5) Populate quick-check spreadsheet and flag regressions per regression_checks
6) If failures, capture 10 failing examples, add to ticket with attached tokenizer offset dump
quick_todo: Get updated transliteration table from L10n (Jing)
Hook mirroring check into CI job (Omar)
Draft a short explainer for CS on what to look for in customer reports (Eliot)
Schedule 15m sync with solutions-engineering before next onboarding run
known_issues: Models with aggressive pretokenization sometimes split ideographic numerals into separate tokens, altering chrF++ scoring
Some older models output U+200F/U+200E invisibles inconsistently — need explicit strip step in postproc
Transliteration preferences vary by locale (Vietnamese names often ambiguous); golden set is not exhaustive
Date formatting tests rely on ICU version; CI runners must match prod ICU to avoid false positives
change_log: 2026-02-10: Initial draft, collected seed prompts from field reports
2026-02-18: Added numeric and currency subtests per finance feedback
2026-03-05: Incorporated transliteration table from L10n, updated runbook steps

source.title: Compatibility quickshots: rollforward plan and experimental log
source.body:
Purpose:
Quick experimental log and rollforward playbook for validating K8s + NVIDIA stack upgrades in private (VPC/on-prem) clusters. This is intentionally lightweight — rapid iteration notes, short TF knobs we tried, and a minimal canary/rollback plan. Meant to sit alongside the formal upgrade playbook as ‘fast feedback’ artifacts.

Context and goals:
- We have multiple customers running Dedicated/private clusters with mixed GPU generations (V100, A100, A30).
- Goal: identify low-risk upgrade path to kernel + driver + CUDA + NCCL that keeps inference runtime (token latency) within SLOs and avoids pod eviction/kernel panics.
- Secondary: exercise Terraform module changes for node image and driver daemonset rollout.

High-level approach (quickshots):
1) Small isolated lab cluster (3 nodes, 1 GPU each) per GPU family.
2) Run smoke inference workload (small LLM toy model + KV cache pressure) for 2 hours pre-upgrade to baseline.
3) Apply single variable change: either kernel update, driver daemonset image, or CUDA container image.
4) Monitor with perf counters + dmesg + kubelet logs for 30–90 minutes.
5) Record pass/fail and rough root cause notes.

Quick matrix snapshot (flattened text, last run 2026-03-04):
GPU | OS kernel | nvidia-driver | CUDA runtime | NCCL | k8s | containerd | result | notes
A100 | 5.15.0-58 | 525.85.12 | 12.1 | 2.14 | 1.27.3 | containerd 1.7 | PASS | small mem spike during pod startup, stable after warmup
A100 | 6.1.0-12 | 530.30.02 | 12.2 | 2.16 | 1.27.3 | containerd 1.7 | FAIL | kernel oops on nvidia_uvm, trace to driver ABI mismatch; reproduction 2/3
V100 | 5.15.0-58 | 470.161.03 | 11.4 | 2.10 | 1.26.3 | docker 20.10 | PASS | baseline perf within SLO
A30  | 5.10.0-102 | 510.47.03 | 11.8 | 2.12 | 1.27.3 | containerd 1.6 | FLAKY | intermittent NVML timeouts under heavy batch

Observed patterns and heuristics (fast notes):
- Kernel upgrades to mainline 6.x can break older 52x/53x drivers; prefer driver >= 525 for 6.x kernels on A100s.
- NCCL 2.16 showed improved cross-socket throughput but introduced hangs when combined with older drivers and cgroup v2 CPU throttling.
- containerd 1.7 + cgroup v2 is the cleanest path for modern kernels; older docker runtime may mask cgroup regressions.
- MIG-enabled A100 nodes need driver 470+? (depends on CUDA) — always validate MIG mode state in lab before rollout.

Terraform/infra experiment notes (snippets kept as prose):
- tf variable tried: enable_kernel_tune = true — toggles a small systemd unit to apply vm.max_map_count and swappiness. Worked harmlessly in lab.
- node_image_map change: added "ubuntu-22.04-kernel-6.1" entry and new node-pool definition. During apply we saw instance replacement delays (cloud-init timeout) — increased cloud-init timeout to 25m.
- driver_daemonset_image was changed from quay/redwood/nvidia-driver:525-ubi to quay/redwood/nvidia-driver:530-ubi in a single TF plan. Recommendation: split into two PRs: terraform change + separate canary rollout script.
- sample terraform vars we tried (for record):
  - gpu_node_pool_count = 3
  - driver_daemonset_strategy = "rollingUpdate"
  - driver_image_tag = "525-ubi-fastprobe"
  - enable_node_label_mig = "auto"

Sanity checks and smoke tests (checklist for each quickshot):
- [ ] nvidia-smi shows expected GPUs and MIG slices (if applicable)
- [ ] kubelet does not log driver registration errors within 10m
- [ ] small inference job (10 concurrent requests) completes with 95th latency below baseline + 30%
- [ ] no dmesg OOPS or kernel panics seen in 30m window
- [ ] NCCL tests (allreduce/single-GPU) pass at basic level

Repro and debugging notes (concrete ties):
- For A100 + kernel 6.1 failure: collected /var/log/kern.log + /var/log/syslog + driver pod logs. Repro steps: boot node with 6.1, install 530.30.02 driver via daemonset, start workload — first OOPS occurred at time ~ 00:14 with stack trace referencing nvidia_uvm. Suspect mismatch between driver kernel module and kernel ABI (module built against different kernel headers).
- Immediate mitigation: pin kernel to 5.15 in terraform node_image_map for canary pools and escalate to kernel team if 6.1 required.

Canary/rollforward plan (short):
1) Create canary node pool (2 nodes) per GPU family using new node image + driver pod image but with driver_daemonset.strategy = OnDelete.
2) Drain a single canary node, delete driver pods to trigger new image lifecycle, run smoke tests from CI (smoke-infra job).
3) If smoke passes for 2h, increment canary pool by 1 and repeat.
4) Full rollforward only after 3 consecutive successful canaries over 24h.
5) Rollback: terraform to previous node_image_map + driver_image_tag, cordon/evict replacement nodes. Maintain old AMI snapshot for quick reprovision.

Open questions / action items:
- Which customers require kernel 6.x features? (ACTION: solutions-eng to triage list)
- Do we have an automated binary compatibility check between driver image and kernel headers during CI? (ACTION: build infra to mount headers and verify modinfo)
- How does cgroup v2 throttling interact with NCCL — add a micro-benchmark that toggles cpu quota in lab.

Appendix: quick failure Triage checklist (for on-call/private deploy ops)
- Collect: dmesg, kubectl describe daemonset nvidia-driver, kubectl logs -n kube-system <driver-pod>, /var/log/cloud-init-output.log, nvidia-smi -q
- Quick triage heuristics: if 'nvidia_uvm' oops -> likely kernel-driver module mismatch; if NVML timeouts -> possible driver handshake or firmware problem; if NCCL hang with high CPU steal -> check cgroups and IO saturation.

Notes to self (loose):
- Keep these quickshots near the TF branch until stabilization.
- Convert successful quickshot matrix rows into definitive entries in the canonical compat matrix doc.
- Add a small CI job that boots a vm with target kernel and runs kernel module build check against driver image (low effort, high payoff).

End of quick log.

  - enable_node_label_mig = "auto"

Sanity checks and smoke tests (checklist for each quickshot):
- [ ] nvidia-smi shows expected GPUs and MIG slices (if applicable)
- [ ] kubelet does not log driver registration errors within 10m
- [ ] small inference job (10 concurrent requests) completes with 95th latency below baseline + 30%
- [ ] no dmesg OOPS or kernel panics seen in 30m window
- [ ] NCCL tests (allreduce/single-GPU) pass at basic level

Repro and debugging notes (concrete ties):
- For A100 + kernel 6.1 failure: collected /var/log/kern.log + /var/log/syslog + driver pod logs. Repro steps: boot node with 6.1, install 530.30.02 driver via daemonset, start workload — first OOPS occurred at time ~ 00:14 with stack trace referencing nvidia_uvm. Suspect mismatch between driver kernel module and kernel ABI (module built against different kernel headers).
- Immediate mitigation: pin kernel to 5.15 in terraform node_image_map for canary pools and escalate to kernel team if 6.1 required.

Canary/rollforward plan (short):
1) Create canary node pool (2 nodes) per GPU family using new node image + driver pod image but with driver_daemonset.strategy = OnDelete.
2) Drain a single canary node, delete driver pods to trigger new image lifecycle, run smoke tests from CI (smoke-infra job).
3) If smoke passes for 2h, increment canary pool by 1 and repeat.
4) Full rollforward only after 3 consecutive successful canaries over 24h.
5) Rollback: terraform to previous node_image_map + driver_image_tag, cordon/evict replacement nodes. Maintain old AMI snapshot for quick reprovision.

Open questions / action items:
- Which customers require kernel 6.x features? (ACTION: solutions-eng to triage list)
- Do we have an automated binary compatibility check between driver image and kernel headers during CI? (ACTION: build infra to mount headers and verify modinfo)
- How does cgroup v2 throttling interact with NCCL — add a micro-benchmark that toggles cpu quota in lab.

Appendix: quick failure Triage checklist (for on-call/private deploy ops)
- Collect: dmesg, kubectl describe daemonset nvidia-driver, kubectl logs -n kube-system <driver-pod>, /var/log/cloud-init-output.log, nvidia-smi -q
- Quick triage heuristics: if 'nvidia_uvm' oops -> likely kernel-driver module mismatch; if NVML timeouts -> possible driver handshake or firmware problem; if NCCL hang with high CPU steal -> check cgroups and IO saturation.

Notes to self (loose):
- Keep these quickshots near the TF branch until stabilization.
- Convert successful quickshot matrix rows into definitive entries in the canonical compat matrix doc.
- Add a small CI job that boots a vm with target kernel and runs kernel module build check against driver image (low effort, high payoff).

End of quick log.

source.title: Build-and-Test Refactor Probe: prompt set + regression notes
source.content:
Purpose:
This is a focused probe for onboarding models on refactoring/codegen tasks where the downstream gating criteria are: successful build (compilation), unit test preservation, and adherence to style constraints (lint). The goal is to capture a compact, repeatable prompt set + evaluation notes that the harness can run nightly as a regression check.

Background / context notes (quick):
- We need a suite that catches: broken imports, API signature drift, missing dependencies in generated code, test regressions, accidental behavior changes (logic), and style regressions (formatting, naming).
- Targeted at model candidates during onboarding and for weekly regression runs on LLM serving stack.
- This doc is intentionally lightweight — it should be easy to port into the harness as a new prompt dataset (jsonl) and map to existing compile/test runners.

Prompt set design (intent):
- Each item pairs: (1) short refactor instruction, (2) code snippet to transform, (3) unit test file(s) to preserve, (4) constraints (lint rules, max diff lines).
- Prompts should be explicit about the build system (pyproject/requirements.txt, node package.json, go.mod) and required python version or toolchain.
- Add explicit rubric instructions: DO NOT change function signatures; keep public API; preserve behavior for edge-case tests; minimize additional dependencies.

Sample prompts (representative) - these are intentionally terse (harness will expand):
1) Python: "Refactor utils/str_utils.py to remove duplication across functions, preserve all unit tests under tests/test_str_utils.py, keep public function signatures unchanged. Project uses Python 3.10, run pytest -q. Lint with ruff --select E,F,W. Maximum allowed diff: 40 lines."
2) TypeScript: "Simplify and extract helper from src/auth/session.ts into src/auth/session_helpers.ts, keep integration test tests/session.spec.ts passing under jest --runInBand, preserve exported names. Project: Node 18, tsconfig target ES2021. ESLint must have 0 errors."
3) Go: "Consolidate duplicated error handling in pkg/store/kvstore.go; keep all exported functions' signatures, unit tests in kvstore_test.go must pass (go test ./... -run KvStore). go 1.20. go vet should report no issues."

Rubric / automation metrics (what the harness measures):
- compile_success: binary/interpretation: pass/fail (string boolean).
- unit_test_pass_rate: percent (tests passed / tests run).
- lint_pass_rate: percent of lints suppressed vs total; primary pass if no errors.
- behavior_delta_count: number of failing tests introduced (integer as string in report).
- diff_size: total added+deleted lines (integer as string).
- introduced_dependencies: list of new packages detected (string list stored as pipe-separated string in the report).
- timeout_flag: whether generation exceeded token/time budget.
- human_note: freeform string for reviewer comments.

Example harness results (nightly run - snapshot):
- model-a-7b-quant-v2:
  compile_success: "true"
  unit_test_pass_rate: "92%"
  lint_pass_rate: "83%"
  behavior_delta_count: "2"
  diff_size: "58"
  introduced_dependencies: ""
  timeout_flag: "false"
  human_note: "Fails on an edge-case assert in tests/test_str_utils.py::test_strip_unicode; creates an extra helper that mutates input bytes in place. Needs prompt soft-constraint to avoid in-place mutation."

- model-b-16b-fp16:
  compile_success: "false"
  unit_test_pass_rate: "0%"
  lint_pass_rate: "10%"
  behavior_delta_count: "12"
  diff_size: "180"
  introduced_dependencies: "urllib3|dateparser"
  timeout_flag: "false"
  human_note: "Massive rewrites, missing imports (requests vs urllib3 mismatch), introduces questionable runtime dependency dateparser. Fails to preserve public API. Not acceptable for onboarding."

Observed failure modes and triage notes:
- Silent signature changes: model slightly renames arguments (e.g., `user_id` -> `uid`) which breaks call sites; need to add an explicit KeepSignatures: true line in the instruction templates.
- Missing imports when refactoring into new files: harness should run a static import check (python -m compileall) after generation to catch unresolved names early.
- Tests passing locally but flakey in CI: some refactors change timing assumptions in integration tests; isolate unit-only tests for gating.
- Style overreach: model reflows long lines into single-line lambdas that fail lint rules; add explicit lint rule list per prompt to reduce ambiguity.

Operational checklist for onboarding a new model with this probe:
- Add model id + config to nightly-harness/registry.csv
- Upload prompt JSONL to s3://redwood-evals/refactor-probe/<model-id>/<date>.jsonl
- Run harness container: ./run_probe.sh --dataset refactor-probe --model <model-id> --timeout 180s
- Review generated patch in build/artifacts/<model-id>/<run-id>/patch.diff and the compiled/test summary report <run-id>.json

Open questions / TODOs:
- How strict should allowed diff_size be for larger repos? (currently conservative: 200 lines total per item)
- Should we auto-assign minor formatting fixes (black/ruff) or treat them as failures to preserve original style?
- Add a negative test: prompt asks to remove a deprecated public function — harness must ensure removal propagates to callers (or flag as behavioral change).

Next steps:
- Convert these sample prompts into jsonl lines (working branch: PR #412).
- Add static import checker and a 'signature tokenizer' that verifies public API names unchanged.
- Run the probe against candidate models on the onboarding checklist and collect 2 weeks of data before promoting any model to the 'refactor-allowed' cohort.

Notes to reviewer:
This doc is intentionally pragmatic and should be easy to iterate. If you add or remove constraints, please include a short rationale and update the linked PR.

-- Priya

source.title: airgap-site-audit-technical-readiness
source.body:
Executive summary:

These are raw notes from the 2026-02-26 customer workshop (on-site) and the follow-up technical audit for an air-gapped, on-prem Redwood Private POC. The customer is a regulated healthcare platform (PHI scope) planning to host inference inside a locked datacenter with zero outbound internet. Primary goals: run latency-sensitive LLM inference (<150ms p95 for short prompts), key material managed by customer HSM, and strict audit/log retention for 7 years.

Attendees (on-site + remote):
- Priya Natarajan (Redwood SE)
- Luis Ortega (Redwood SRE)
- Maya Chen (Redwood Solutions Eng)
- Customer: CTO, Head of Infra (Michael R.), Network Engineer (Sofia L.), Security Lead (Anita G.)

Customer context / constraints observed:
- Air-gapped facility: no outbound internet, egress through controlled jump-host only for approved artifact sync windows.
- Hardware is heterogeneous across racks: two nodes with A100 80GB, three nodes with A10/A30, two CPU-only for control plane. RAID ZFS on local storage.
- No existing Kubernetes cluster; customer prefers VM-based orchestration or Kube via private registry mirror.
- HSM (Luna/Moflex) available in datacenter, accessible via internal network and PKCS#11.
- Strict change-control: any binaries must be signed and verified; change windows weekly.
- Monitoring/telemetry must be kept local; only aggregated reports (non-PII metrics) can be exported after review.
- Time sync works via internal NTP, but some nodes are behind by seconds; this will break cert validation for short-lived TLS certs if not reconciled.

Site inventory (high-level):
- Compute:
  - 2x DGX-style nodes (A100 80GB) with NVLink
  - 3x servers with NVIDIA A10/A30 (mixed 24–40GB VRAM)
  - 4x control VMs (32 vCPU, 256GB RAM)
- Storage:
  - Local NVMe on GPU nodes (1–4TB) used for model shards/KV cache
  - Shared NFS backed by RAID ZFS for artifacts and shared volumes
- Network:
  - Leaf-spine fabric, 100Gb internal, 10Gb to management VLANs
  - Firewall appliance blocks outbound except via jump host (bastion) during sync windows
  - No public DNS; internal DNS provided by customer
- Security appliances:
  - HSM cluster (PKCS#11), SIEM on-site, internal CA for TLS certs
- Ops:
  - Strict ticketing process for changes, vulnerability scanning on a monthly cadence

Constraints & immediate blockers:
1) No container registry mirror currently configured. We need an offline OCI registry mirror (registry:2 or Harbor) and a validated process for signing & verifying images. Blocker for k8s-based rollout.
2) GPU driver/CUDA versions inconsistent across nodes; runtime must either standardize drivers or provide multi-version support in images.
3) Time skew on some nodes (~5s) — affects cert validation and distributed cache coherency.
4) HSM integration requires network ACLs to allow Redwood control-plane components to reach HSM endpoints and a documented PKCS#11 token policy.
5) Backup/restore: model weights >1TB per model; no validated plan to pull/restore large artifacts in-airgap quickly.

Proposed technical approach (feasibility-minded):
- Bootstrap plan (high level):
  1) Deliver signed artifact bundle (OCI images, helm charts, VM images) on encrypted NVMe or verified via secure copy over jump host.
  2) Customer runs verification script (checks signatures + SBOMs) before ingestion into private registry/mirror.
  3) Deploy a lightweight private registry (Harbor recommended) on the management VLAN with RBAC; mirror artifacts there.
  4) For orchestration: prefer kube (k8s) for production parity with hosted flows — use kubeadm + airgap helm charts. If customer resists, provide a VM-based docker-compose fallback for POC only.
  5) Enable containerd registry mirrors with OCI signed images and cosign verification at runtime.

- Model and weights distribution:
  - Use a chunked tarball format + rsync-friendly layout so restores can be resumed if interrupted. Provide a CRC + SHA256 manifest and signed manifest for verification.
  - Recommend starting POC with a quantized model variant that fits A10/A30 to reduce weight size; if A100s available, enable larger variants later.

- Networking and certs:
  - Validate NTP across nodes prior to cert issuance; recommend tolerances <1s.
  - Use customer internal CA to sign Redwood TLS certs. Provide CSR automation tool that runs inside airgap (offline CSR generator + signed cert ingestion).
  - DNS: require internal DNS entries for service discovery; if not available, support static /etc/hosts as short-term workaround.

- HSM/KMS integration:
  - Support PKCS#11 for signing keys and for disk-encryption key wrapping. Documented workflow: hold private keys in HSM, Redwood control-plane holds only public material and KMS references.
  - Audit requirement: all KMS/HSM operations must be logged to SIEM locally. Redwood must support emitting structured audit events (file) to an on-prem location.

Operational considerations and local observability:
- Metrics/logs: run Prometheus + Thanos sidecar with local long-term storage (S3-compatible MinIO) and retention policy; no external scrape targets.
- Traces: run Jaeger locally; ensure sampling rates are adjusted to avoid PII capture.
- Alerting: local PagerDuty bridge or email routing; Redwood can provide rule templates but all alert outputs stay on-prem for POC.

Risk matrix (quick):
- Model corruption during transfer: Likelihood medium; Impact high. Mitigation: signed manifests, chunked transfers, post-transfer checksum + verification script.
- Unsupported driver mismatch: Likelihood high; Impact high. Mitigation: pre-audit drivers and provide driver bundles & install playbooks; plan downtime windows.
- HSM connectivity failure: Likelihood low–medium; Impact critical. Mitigation: test HSM connectivity during pre-install checks; fallback to customer-approved temporary key escrow policy for POC only.
- Certificate expiry/clock drift issues: Likelihood medium; Impact medium. Mitigation: force time sync check in bootstrap; set longer cert TTL during POC.

Minimum-prep checklist for next workshop (pre-PoC readiness):
- [ ] Customer to provision a jump host with SFTP for initial artifact sync (owner: Michael R., due: 2026-03-10)
- [ ] Provide hardware serials + GPU driver versions for all candidate nodes (owner: Sofia L., due: 2026-03-05)
- [ ] HSM contact + test credentials (PKCS#11 endpoint + test token) in a secure vault (owner: Anita G., due: 2026-03-12)
- [ ] Internal CA contact for CSR flow + certificate signing window (owner: Infra lead, due: 2026-03-08)
- [ ] Validate NTP across cluster and confirm <1s skew (owner: Infra, due: 2026-03-05)
- [ ] Confirm storage export speed for 1TB model pull (owner: Storage team, due: 2026-03-09)

Action items for Redwood (SE/SRE):
- Prep an airgap delivery artifact package and a concise runbook (Priya/Luis, due: 2026-03-14)
- Create driver-install automation and verification playbook for A100/A10 nodes (Luis, due: 2026-03-18)
- Build a minimal offline monitoring bundle (Prometheus + MinIO + Grafana dashboards) and test restore (Maya, due: 2026-03-20)
- Draft HSM integration guide (incl. PKCS#11 examples) and produce audit event schema (Priya, due: 2026-03-22)

Open questions to resolve with customer (short list):
1) Can customer allow outbound HTTPS to a signed Redwood artifact endpoint during a short maintenance window? (would simplify registry sync)
2) Are there any disallowed cryptographic algorithms (FIPS/hardware restrictions)?
3) Who owns long-term backups for model weights and what is the restore SLA?
4) Acceptable throttling for inference if we must limit token-per-second per GPU to preserve QoS?
5) Any legal restrictions on logs/metrics leaving the datacenter even in anonymized form?

Notes-to-self / follow-ups:
- Need to produce a small demo image (<4GB) to validate mirror+cosign flow during on-site validation.
- Verify MinIO version compatibility with Thanos sidecar offline mode.
- Produce a template signed manifest + verification tool that integrates with the customer's change-control process.

Appendix: Helpful technical check commands (to run during pre-audit)
- nvidia-smi --query-gpu=name,memory.total,driver_version --format=csv
- timedatectl status && ntpq -p - verify stratum and offset
- openssl s_client -connect <hsm-endpoint>:<port> -showcerts (for network reachability)
- sha256sum <artifact> && cosign verify --key <public-key> <image-ref>

End of notes.

(Will iterate this into a cleaner pre-install checklist and ship the POC runbook once blockers above are cleared.)

Site inventory (high-level):
- Compute:
  - 2x DGX-style nodes (A100 80GB) with NVLink
  - 3x servers with NVIDIA A10/A30 (mixed 24–40GB VRAM)
  - 4x control VMs (32 vCPU, 256GB RAM)
- Storage:
  - Local NVMe on GPU nodes (1–4TB) used for model shards/KV cache
  - Shared NFS backed by RAID ZFS for artifacts and shared volumes
- Network:
  - Leaf-spine fabric, 100Gb internal, 10Gb to management VLANs
  - Firewall appliance blocks outbound except via jump host (bastion) during sync windows
  - No public DNS; internal DNS provided by customer
- Security appliances:
  - HSM cluster (PKCS#11), SIEM on-site, internal CA for TLS certs
- Ops:
  - Strict ticketing process for changes, vulnerability scanning on a monthly cadence

Constraints & immediate blockers:
1) No container registry mirror currently configured. We need an offline OCI registry mirror (registry:2 or Harbor) and a validated process for signing & verifying images. Blocker for k8s-based rollout.
2) GPU driver/CUDA versions inconsistent across nodes; runtime must either standardize drivers or provide multi-version support in images.
3) Time skew on some nodes (~5s) — affects cert validation and distributed cache coherency.
4) HSM integration requires network ACLs to allow Redwood control-plane components to reach HSM endpoints and a documented PKCS#11 token policy.
5) Backup/restore: model weights >1TB per model; no validated plan to pull/restore large artifacts in-airgap quickly.

Proposed technical approach (feasibility-minded):
- Bootstrap plan (high level):
  1) Deliver signed artifact bundle (OCI images, helm charts, VM images) on encrypted NVMe or verified via secure copy over jump host.
  2) Customer runs verification script (checks signatures + SBOMs) before ingestion into private registry/mirror.
  3) Deploy a lightweight private registry (Harbor recommended) on the management VLAN with RBAC; mirror artifacts there.
  4) For orchestration: prefer kube (k8s) for production parity with hosted flows — use kubeadm + airgap helm charts. If customer resists, provide a VM-based docker-compose fallback for POC only.
  5) Enable containerd registry mirrors with OCI signed images and cosign verification at runtime.

- Model and weights distribution:
  - Use a chunked tarball format + rsync-friendly layout so restores can be resumed if interrupted. Provide a CRC + SHA256 manifest and signed manifest for verification.
  - Recommend starting POC with a quantized model variant that fits A10/A30 to reduce weight size; if A100s available, enable larger variants later.

- Networking and certs:
  - Validate NTP across nodes prior to cert issuance; recommend tolerances <1s.
  - Use customer internal CA to sign Redwood TLS certs. Provide CSR automation tool that runs inside airgap (offline CSR generator + signed cert ingestion).
  - DNS: require internal DNS entries for service discovery; if not available, support static /etc/hosts as short-term workaround.

- HSM/KMS integration:
  - Support PKCS#11 for signing keys and for disk-encryption key wrapping. Documented workflow: hold private keys in HSM, Redwood control-plane holds only public material and KMS references.
  - Audit requirement: all KMS/HSM operations must be logged to SIEM locally. Redwood must support emitting structured audit events (file) to an on-prem location.

Operational considerations and local observability:
- Metrics/logs: run Prometheus + Thanos sidecar with local long-term storage (S3-compatible MinIO) and retention policy; no external scrape targets.
- Traces: run Jaeger locally; ensure sampling rates are adjusted to avoid PII capture.
- Alerting: local PagerDuty bridge or email routing; Redwood can provide rule templates but all alert outputs stay on-prem for POC.

Risk matrix (quick):
- Model corruption during transfer: Likelihood medium; Impact high. Mitigation: signed manifests, chunked transfers, post-transfer checksum + verification script.
- Unsupported driver mismatch: Likelihood high; Impact high. Mitigation: pre-audit drivers and provide driver bundles & install playbooks; plan downtime windows.
- HSM connectivity failure: Likelihood low–medium; Impact critical. Mitigation: test HSM connectivity during pre-install checks; fallback to customer-approved temporary key escrow policy for POC only.
- Certificate expiry/clock drift issues: Likelihood medium; Impact medium. Mitigation: force time sync check in bootstrap; set longer cert TTL during POC.

Minimum-prep checklist for next workshop (pre-PoC readiness):
- [ ] Customer to provision a jump host with SFTP for initial artifact sync (owner: Michael R., due: 2026-03-10)
- [ ] Provide hardware serials + GPU driver versions for all candidate nodes (owner: Sofia L., due: 2026-03-05)
- [ ] HSM contact + test credentials (PKCS#11 endpoint + test token) in a secure vault (owner: Anita G., due: 2026-03-12)
- [ ] Internal CA contact for CSR flow + certificate signing window (owner: Infra lead, due: 2026-03-08)
- [ ] Validate NTP across cluster and confirm <1s skew (owner: Infra, due: 2026-03-05)
- [ ] Confirm storage export speed for 1TB model pull (owner: Storage team, due: 2026-03-09)

Action items for Redwood (SE/SRE):
- Prep an airgap delivery artifact package and a concise runbook (Priya/Luis, due: 2026-03-14)
- Create driver-install automation and verification playbook for A100/A10 nodes (Luis, due: 2026-03-18)
- Build a minimal offline monitoring bundle (Prometheus + MinIO + Grafana dashboards) and test restore (Maya, due: 2026-03-20)
- Draft HSM integration guide (incl. PKCS#11 examples) and produce audit event schema (Priya, due: 2026-03-22)

Open questions to resolve with customer (short list):
1) Can customer allow outbound HTTPS to a signed Redwood artifact endpoint during a short maintenance window? (would simplify registry sync)
2) Are there any disallowed cryptographic algorithms (FIPS/hardware restrictions)?
3) Who owns long-term backups for model weights and what is the restore SLA?
4) Acceptable throttling for inference if we must limit token-per-second per GPU to preserve QoS?
5) Any legal restrictions on logs/metrics leaving the datacenter even in anonymized form?

Notes-to-self / follow-ups:
- Need to produce a small demo image (<4GB) to validate mirror+cosign flow during on-site validation.
- Verify MinIO version compatibility with Thanos sidecar offline mode.
- Produce a template signed manifest + verification tool that integrates with the customer's change-control process.

Appendix: Helpful technical check commands (to run during pre-audit)
- nvidia-smi --query-gpu=name,memory.total,driver_version --format=csv
- timedatectl status && ntpq -p - verify stratum and offset
- openssl s_client -connect <hsm-endpoint>:<port> -showcerts (for network reachability)
- sha256sum <artifact> && cosign verify --key <public-key> <image-ref>

End of notes.

(Will iterate this into a cleaner pre-install checklist and ship the POC runbook once blockers above are cleared.)

source.title: Secrets eviction and graceful failover checklist (v0.1 draft)
source.content:
Purpose and scope:
This is a working checklist + module notes for handling secrets eviction, graceful failover, and stateful Terraform upgrades during KMS/HSM integration and private deployment upgrades. Target audience: SREs and infra engineers running Redwood Private (VPC/on-prem) installs.

Why this exists (quick summary):
- We have recurring incidents where node terminations or HSM failovers cause short-lived access failures because secrets were evicted or the KMS proxy lost affinity.
- Upgrade windows (Terraform module bumps, provider changes) need a safe, repeatable sequence that preserves key provenance and auditability.
- On-prem customers require deterministic audit chains and non-destructive rollback patterns for key material and operator actions.

High-level goals:
- Evict secrets safely so workloads fail-open with minimum exposure (deny-by-default with controlled recovery path).
- Ensure HSM/KMS rotation/upgrade does not orphan data-encryption-keys (DEKs) in object stores or KV stores.
- Maintain immutable audit records (WORM-style checkpoints) during upgrades.
- Provide a clear Terraform upgrade sequence that avoids resource replacement for critical key material.

Important assumptions (do not violate):
- HSM devices are single-tenant in customer-controlled racks or cloud HSM instances; their firmware/keys are sensitive and subject to vendor constraints.
- Vault or our secrets backplane sits behind a KMS proxy for envelope encryption.
- Operators have access to an offline escrow procedure for root/unsealing keys.

Eviction and failover checklist (operator runbook):
1) Pre-checks (N-48h):
   - Confirm backups of Vault (snapshot + seal-keys escrow). Document GPG fingerprint used for escrow.
   - Verify HSM health: vendor reported firmware ok, no degraded slots. (Vendor support ticket id recorded).
   - Check audit pipeline: test ingest via audit-proxy with test event and confirm storage write and immutability flags.
   - Snapshot Terraform state (terraform state pull > tfstate.backup). Store in secure artifact store.

2) Eviction strategy decision (N-4h):
   - Is this planned maintenance? If yes => graceful eviction. If unplanned => emergency path.
   - For graceful: schedule rolling eviction groups, 10% nodes at a time, prefer low-traffic AZs.
   - For emergency: trigger short TTL rekey policy and failover to KMS-fallback (local-wrapped keys) for read-only critical paths.

3) Rolling eviction procedure (operator):
   - Mark node group as draining in orchestration (label nodes with "secrets-draining=true").
   - Wait for KV-cache flush: call /internal/cache/flush for service instances on node (artifact: cache-flush-log-<ts>).
   - Revoke long-lived in-memory tokens: rotate ephemeral-session-token broadcast via control-plane.
   - Confirm consumers re-request secrets from Vault (audit events should show re-fetch within 60s).

4) HSM attestation and reseeding (post-eviction):
   - Re-attest HSM node against local TPM; collect PCRs and push to attestation service.
   - If entropy pools might have been reseeded, run quick entropy health check on HSM (vendor tool).
   - Seed DEK derivation metadata: include HSM-serial and attestation-timestamp into key metadata to ensure provenance.

5) Failover to fallback KMS (if HSM unreachable):
   - Flip KMS routing policy in the config-store to "fallback-local" (note: this is a config flag that should be toggled via the console/operator-api; do NOT change provider plugins at this stage).
   - Enable read-only unwrap policy: only allow unwrapping DEKs needed for in-flight operations, deny new envelope key generation.
   - Emit high-priority audit event that includes operator, reason, and expected window.

Terraform module notes and upgrade plan:
- Core principle: never replace explicit key resources in-place. Avoid terraform resource replacement for HSM-managed keys (resource reliant on provider should be imported/migrated rather than recreated).
- Use provider version pinning: provider "hsm" { version = "~> 2.4.1" } and lockfile checked in.
- Keep a small, separate terraform workspace for key material (infra/keys) so state changes for compute/network do not mix with keys. This makes state recoverable and reduces blast radius.
- Example migration steps for provider upgrade: 
  1) Create a new terraform plan in a "keys-migration" workspace with targetted imports.
  2) For any HSM key resource that will change schema, run: terraform import hsm_key.k1 <existing-key-id> then terraform apply.
  3) If provider version requires resource renaming, add the new resource with lifecycle { create_before_destroy = true } and manually copy metadata tags.
- Avoid automatic lifecycle ignore_changes for key material except when necessary; prefer explicit migration PRs and review.

Safe apply pattern (recommended):
1) terraform plan -var-file=secrets.tfvars -out=upgrade.plan
2) Review plan with security lead (signoff in ticket).
3) terraform apply "upgrade.plan" --lock-timeout=15m 
4) Run post-apply validation script (see smoke tests below).

Rollback and emergency restore:
- If an apply replaces a key inadvertently, the recommended rollback is: 
  1) Immediately lock access to compute nodes (taint or cordon) to stop further unwraps.
  2) Restore the tfstate from tfstate.backup and run terraform apply against that state (state should reflect original key resource).
  3) If the key material is lost at the HSM level, escalate to offline escrow (follow escrow playbook).

Audit logs and WORM checkpoints:
- During upgrades, we must create immutable audit checkpoints: 
  - Before any key operation, emit audit-checkpoint with sequence id and object snapshot stored in immutable storage (S3 with retention policy or equivalent on-prem archive).
  - Include operator identity, ticket id, git commit, terraform plan hash, and machine-readable attestation blob.
  - Audits must be verifiable: provide a simple script that computes a SHA256 over the checkpoint bundle and records it in the audit index.

Secrets handling policy (during upgrade windows):
- Shorten TTLs for ephemeral secrets (from 24h -> 15m) during active key ops window. This reduces exposure if keys are rotated.
- For long-lived tokens, require re-issuance and manual approval.
- Track token lineage in the audit events so we can map unwrapped ciphertexts to DEK versions.

Smoke tests (post-apply):
- Try unwrapping 5 sample DEKs and verify plaintext can decrypt a stored object in the object-store.
- Run the e2e request against service with simulated high-latency to ensure KV cache fallback triggers correctly.
- Confirm audit pipeline has 100% delivery for the past 10 minutes of events and that the checkpoint hash computed earlier is present.

Automated test matrix to add to CI (short list):
- terraform plan/apply in a sandbox workspace with HSM emulator (happy path).
- HSM emulator detach and failover to software-KMS; assert unwrap-only policy.
- Secret eviction + node termination test: randomly terminate 10% nodes and assert no data loss for read-only flows.

Open questions / TODOs:
- Decide final retention policy for audit checkpoints (90d vs 365d) — customer requirements differ.
- Confirm governance for offline escrow access (who signs escrow releases).
- Validate vendor HSM import workflows for our Terraform provider (some vendors do not expose importable identifiers).
- Add a no-op dry-run mode to our KMS proxy to simulate routing changes without changing live state.

Appendix: helpful commands and snippets
- Snapshot tfstate: terraform state pull > tfstate.backup-$(date +%Y%m%dT%H%M%SZ)
- Import HSM key: terraform import hsm_key.customer_master kms://hsm/serial-1234/key-uuid
- Force plan output: terraform plan -out=plan.tfout && terraform show -json plan.tfout > plan.json (attach plan.json to PR)
- Audit checkpoint sketch: 
  checkpoint_bundle=$(mktemp -d)/bundle.json
  jq -n '{operator: "${OP}", ticket: "${TICKET}", ts: "'$(date -u +%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ)'"}' > $checkpoint_bundle/meta.json
  tar czf checkpoint-$TS.tgz $checkpoint_bundle && sha256sum checkpoint-$TS.tgz > checkpoint-$TS.sha256

Contact and escalation:
- Primary: Priya Natarajan (eng-private-deployments)
- Secondary: Jonah Park (eng-security)
- Vendor escalation: HSM vendor support (see vendor contact list in Confluence)

Notes to self (raw):
- We should add a terraform module that emits an audit-checkpoint resource type so that audits are tied to plans.
- Consider short-circuiting reads by allowing an in-memory cached DEK for 30s during controlled failover to avoid mass rewraps.
- Need a KMS routing simulator in staging to exercise "fallback-local" vs "hsm-primary" rules.

Last edited: 2026-03-01 by Priya — major rewrite to include eviction sequence and terraform safe-apply pattern.

Secrets handling policy (during upgrade windows):
- Shorten TTLs for ephemeral secrets (from 24h -> 15m) during active key ops window. This reduces exposure if keys are rotated.
- For long-lived tokens, require re-issuance and manual approval.
- Track token lineage in the audit events so we can map unwrapped ciphertexts to DEK versions.

Smoke tests (post-apply):
- Try unwrapping 5 sample DEKs and verify plaintext can decrypt a stored object in the object-store.
- Run the e2e request against service with simulated high-latency to ensure KV cache fallback triggers correctly.
- Confirm audit pipeline has 100% delivery for the past 10 minutes of events and that the checkpoint hash computed earlier is present.

Automated test matrix to add to CI (short list):
- terraform plan/apply in a sandbox workspace with HSM emulator (happy path).
- HSM emulator detach and failover to software-KMS; assert unwrap-only policy.
- Secret eviction + node termination test: randomly terminate 10% nodes and assert no data loss for read-only flows.

Open questions / TODOs:
- Decide final retention policy for audit checkpoints (90d vs 365d) — customer requirements differ.
- Confirm governance for offline escrow access (who signs escrow releases).
- Validate vendor HSM import workflows for our Terraform provider (some vendors do not expose importable identifiers).
- Add a no-op dry-run mode to our KMS proxy to simulate routing changes without changing live state.

Appendix: helpful commands and snippets
- Snapshot tfstate: terraform state pull > tfstate.backup-$(date +%Y%m%dT%H%M%SZ)
- Import HSM key: terraform import hsm_key.customer_master kms://hsm/serial-1234/key-uuid
- Force plan output: terraform plan -out=plan.tfout && terraform show -json plan.tfout > plan.json (attach plan.json to PR)
- Audit checkpoint sketch: 
  checkpoint_bundle=$(mktemp -d)/bundle.json
  jq -n '{operator: "${OP}", ticket: "${TICKET}", ts: "'$(date -u +%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ)'"}' > $checkpoint_bundle/meta.json
  tar czf checkpoint-$TS.tgz $checkpoint_bundle && sha256sum checkpoint-$TS.tgz > checkpoint-$TS.sha256

Contact and escalation:
- Primary: Priya Natarajan (eng-private-deployments)
- Secondary: Jonah Park (eng-security)
- Vendor escalation: HSM vendor support (see vendor contact list in Confluence)

Notes to self (raw):
- We should add a terraform module that emits an audit-checkpoint resource type so that audits are tied to plans.
- Consider short-circuiting reads by allowing an in-memory cached DEK for 30s during controlled failover to avoid mass rewraps.
- Need a KMS routing simulator in staging to exercise \"fallback-local\" vs \"hsm-primary\" rules.
