text:
Purpose and scope:
This is a working checklist + module notes for handling secrets eviction, graceful failover, and stateful Terraform upgrades during KMS/HSM integration and private deployment upgrades. Target audience: SREs and infra engineers running Redwood Private (VPC/on-prem) installs.

Why this exists (quick summary):
- We have recurring incidents where node terminations or HSM failovers cause short-lived access failures because secrets were evicted or the KMS proxy lost affinity.
- Upgrade windows (Terraform module bumps, provider changes) need a safe, repeatable sequence that preserves key provenance and auditability.
- On-prem customers require deterministic audit chains and non-destructive rollback patterns for key material and operator actions.

High-level goals:
- Evict secrets safely so workloads fail-open with minimum exposure (deny-by-default with controlled recovery path).
- Ensure HSM/KMS rotation/upgrade does not orphan data-encryption-keys (DEKs) in object stores or KV stores.
- Maintain immutable audit records (WORM-style checkpoints) during upgrades.
- Provide a clear Terraform upgrade sequence that avoids resource replacement for critical key material.

Important assumptions (do not violate):
- HSM devices are single-tenant in customer-controlled racks or cloud HSM instances; their firmware/keys are sensitive and subject to vendor constraints.
- Vault or our secrets backplane sits behind a KMS proxy for envelope encryption.
- Operators have access to an offline escrow procedure for root/unsealing keys.

Eviction and failover checklist (operator runbook):
1) Pre-checks (N-48h):
   - Confirm backups of Vault (snapshot + seal-keys escrow). Document GPG fingerprint used for escrow.
   - Verify HSM health: vendor reported firmware ok, no degraded slots. (Vendor support ticket id recorded).
   - Check audit pipeline: test ingest via audit-proxy with test event and confirm storage write and immutability flags.
   - Snapshot Terraform state (terraform state pull > tfstate.backup). Store in secure artifact store.

2) Eviction strategy decision (N-4h):
   - Is this planned maintenance? If yes => graceful eviction. If unplanned => emergency path.
   - For graceful: schedule rolling eviction groups, 10% nodes at a time, prefer low-traffic AZs.
   - For emergency: trigger short TTL rekey policy and failover to KMS-fallback (local-wrapped keys) for read-only critical paths.

3) Rolling eviction procedure (operator):
   - Mark node group as draining in orchestration (label nodes with \"secrets-draining=true\").
   - Wait for KV-cache flush: call /internal/cache/flush for service instances on node (artifact: cache-flush-log-<ts>).
   - Revoke long-lived in-memory tokens: rotate ephemeral-session-token broadcast via control-plane.
   - Confirm consumers re-request secrets from Vault (audit events should show re-fetch within 60s).

4) HSM attestation and reseeding (post-eviction):
   - Re-attest HSM node against local TPM; collect PCRs and push to attestation service.
   - If entropy pools might have been reseeded, run quick entropy health check on HSM (vendor tool).
   - Seed DEK derivation metadata: include HSM-serial and attestation-timestamp into key metadata to ensure provenance.

5) Failover to fallback KMS (if HSM unreachable):
   - Flip KMS routing policy in the config-store to \"fallback-local\" (note: this is a config flag that should be toggled via the console/operator-api; do NOT change provider plugins at this stage).
   - Enable read-only unwrap policy: only allow unwrapping DEKs needed for in-flight operations, deny new envelope key generation.
   - Emit high-priority audit event that includes operator, reason, and expected window.

Terraform module notes and upgrade plan:
- Core principle: never replace explicit key resources in-place. Avoid terraform resource replacement for HSM-managed keys (resource reliant on provider should be imported/migrated rather than recreated).
- Use provider version pinning: provider \"hsm\" { version = \"~> 2.4.1\" } and lockfile checked in.
- Keep a small, separate terraform workspace for key material (infra/keys) so state changes for compute/network do not mix with keys. This makes state recoverable and reduces blast radius.
- Example migration steps for provider upgrade: 
  1) Create a new terraform plan in a \"keys-migration\" workspace with targetted imports.
  2) For any HSM key resource that will change schema, run: terraform import hsm_key.k1 <existing-key-id> then terraform apply.
  3) If provider version requires resource renaming, add the new resource with lifecycle { create_before_destroy = true } and manually copy metadata tags.
- Avoid automatic lifecycle ignore_changes for key material except when necessary; prefer explicit migration PRs and review.

Safe apply pattern (recommended):
1) terraform plan -var-file=secrets.tfvars -out=upgrade.plan
2) Review plan with security lead (signoff in ticket).
3) terraform apply \"upgrade.plan\" --lock-timeout=15m 
4) Run post-apply validation script (see smoke tests below).

Rollback and emergency restore:
- If an apply replaces a key inadvertently, the recommended rollback is: 
  1) Immediately lock access to compute nodes (taint or cordon) to stop further unwraps.
  2) Restore the tfstate from tfstate.backup and run terraform apply against that state (state should reflect original key resource).
  3) If the key material is lost at the HSM level, escalate to offline escrow (follow escrow playbook).

Audit logs and WORM checkpoints:
- During upgrades, we must create immutable audit checkpoints: 
  - Before any key operation, emit audit-checkpoint with sequence id and object snapshot stored in immutable storage (S3 with retention policy or equivalent on-prem archive).
  - Include operator identity, ticket id, git commit, terraform plan hash, and machine-readable attestation blob.
  - Audits must be verifiable: provide a simple script that computes a SHA256 over the checkpoint bundle and records it in the audit index.

Secrets handling policy (during upgrade windows):
- Shorten TTLs for ephemeral secrets (from 24h -> 15m) during active key ops window. This reduces exposure if keys are rotated.
- For long-lived tokens, require re-issuance and manual approval.
- Track token lineage in the audit events so we can map unwrapped ciphertexts to DEK versions.

Smoke tests (post-apply):
- Try unwrapping 5 sample DEKs and verify plaintext can decrypt a stored object in the object-store.
- Run the e2e request against service with simulated high-latency to ensure KV cache fallback triggers correctly.
- Confirm audit pipeline has 100% delivery for the past 10 minutes of events and that the checkpoint hash computed earlier is present.

Automated test matrix to add to CI (short list):
- terraform plan/apply in a sandbox workspace with HSM emulator (happy path).
- HSM emulator detach and failover to software-KMS; assert unwrap-only policy.
- Secret eviction + node termination test: randomly terminate 10% nodes and assert no data loss for read-only flows.

Open questions / TODOs:
- Decide final retention policy for audit checkpoints (90d vs 365d) — customer requirements differ.
- Confirm governance for offline escrow access (who signs escrow releases).
- Validate vendor HSM import workflows for our Terraform provider (some vendors do not expose importable identifiers).
- Add a no-op dry-run mode to our KMS proxy to simulate routing changes without changing live state.

Appendix: helpful commands and snippets
- Snapshot tfstate: terraform state pull > tfstate.backup-$(date +%Y%m%dT%H%M%SZ)
- Import HSM key: terraform import hsm_key.customer_master kms://hsm/serial-1234/key-uuid
- Force plan output: terraform plan -out=plan.tfout && terraform show -json plan.tfout > plan.json (attach plan.json to PR)
- Audit checkpoint sketch: 
  checkpoint_bundle=$(mktemp -d)/bundle.json
  jq -n '{operator: "${OP}", ticket: "${TICKET}", ts: "'$(date -u +%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ)'"}' > $checkpoint_bundle/meta.json
  tar czf checkpoint-$TS.tgz $checkpoint_bundle && sha256sum checkpoint-$TS.tgz > checkpoint-$TS.sha256

Contact and escalation:
- Primary: Priya Natarajan (eng-private-deployments)
- Secondary: Jonah Park (eng-security)
- Vendor escalation: HSM vendor support (see vendor contact list in Confluence)

Notes to self (raw):
- We should add a terraform module that emits an audit-checkpoint resource type so that audits are tied to plans.
- Consider short-circuiting reads by allowing an in-memory cached DEK for 30s during controlled failover to avoid mass rewraps.
- Need a KMS routing simulator in staging to exercise \"fallback-local\" vs \"hsm-primary\" rules.

source.title: Operator heuristics — tokenflow annotated notes
source.body:
Purpose:
This is a compact working notebook for on-call operators and SREs to reason about token-level flow signals, quick heuristics to triage user-facing latency/hallucination incidents, and proposed dashboard wireframes. It is intentionally rough — collection of hypotheses, quick SQL/Flux snippets, and operator playbook heuristics that we can refine into formal metric contracts later.

Context and motivation:
- We see recurring "token tail" incidents: user requests that proceed fast for first X tokens then degrade with long tail latencies and increased error rates.
- Existing dashboards show coarse percentiles and per-route SLOs, but operators need token-aligned annotations (kv-cache hits, prefix warm, kernel-switch, model-fallback) to tie latency jumps to root cause.
- Goal: pragmatic set of signals, lightweight trace annotations, and a compact dashboard panel layout operators can use within 2–3 clicks during incident response.

High-level signals (what to capture):
1) request_token_count — total tokens requested by the client (int)
2) produced_token_count — tokens returned so far (int)
3) token_latency_ms_bucketed — histogram of per-token generation latencies (we use 10ms buckets up to 2000ms)
4) kv_cache_status — enum: cold | partial_warm | warm | invalidated
5) cache_hit_prefix_len — number of prefix tokens matched in the KV cache (int)
6) kernel_mode — enum: float32 | int8 | quantized_kv | fallback_cpu
7) model_variant — e.g., rdx-13b-ggml, rdx-13b-qat, rdx-70b-fp16
8) fallback_reason — string: OOM | infra-eviction | version-mismatch | manual-fallback
9) trace_span_anchor — synthetic span id that links token ranges to backend events (see tracing section)
10) route_cpu_ms and route_gpu_ms — broken down compute time per request on infra side

Minimal event annotations for traces (span attributes):
- span:request.start -> attrs: {route, model_variant, requested_tokens, priority}
- span:kv-cache.lookup -> attrs: {matched_prefix_len, cache_ttl_ms, backend_node}
- span:compiler.select_kernel -> attrs: {kernel_mode, seq_len, estimated_peak_memory_mb}
- span:gpu.enqueue -> attrs: {batch_id, queued_ms}
- span:model.generate.tokens -> attrs: {token_index_start, token_index_end, per_token_mean_ms}
- span:fallback.trigger -> attrs: {fallback_reason, from_variant, to_variant}
Notes: enforce token_index ranges so we can reconstruct which span produced which token interval. This avoids correlating per-token histograms with unrelated background work.

Operator heuristics (triage checklist):
1) Isolate request cohort: filter by route + model_variant + time window. If 90th percentile token_latency_ms_bucketed > baseline by >3x, escalate to model/runtime team.
2) Check kv_cache_status and cache_hit_prefix_len: if majority are cold or prefix_len < 4 for a route that usually has large prefixes, suspect cache invalidations or keyspace miss.
3) Inspect span:compiler.select_kernel and span:fallback.trigger: kernel_mode switching or fallback events correlate strongly with sudden per-token latency increases.
4) Check GPU enqueue and batch_id churn: frequent tiny batches (batch size 1–2) increase per-token latency due to enqueue overhead. Consider bumping batching window for that route temporarily.
5) If fallback_reason == OOM or infra-eviction, check node-level memory pressure and recent infra scaling events.
6) For hallucination/quality regressions, look for model_variant changes + token distributions (e.g., abrupt increase in next-token entropy proxies).

Compact dashboard wireframe (panel list and queries):
- Top row: SLO overview for route (total requests, error rate, p50/p95 end-to-end latency)
- Row 2 left: Token-level latency heatmap (X axis = token index 0..N, Y = time bucket of request start). Color = p95 token_latency_ms. Query: aggregate over trace spans with token_index ranges mapped to per-token latencies.
- Row 2 right: KV cache warmth panel - stacked bar: warm/partial_warm/cold counts over time. Quick filter by route/model_variant.
- Row 3 left: Kernel mode churn timeline — show percent of requests per kernel_mode.
- Row 3 right: Small table: Top 10 fallback_reason by frequency and p95 token_latency for those requests.
- Drill panel: Single-request trace viewer with annotated token ranges and links to raw payload (redact PII).

Sampling guidance and storage tradeoffs:
- Full per-token tracing for 100% of requests is costly. Proposed hybrid sampling: always record token-level histograms for 100% but only sample detailed spans for 0.5–2% of requests, biased by error/fallback events and heavier requests (>512 tokens).
- Store high-granularity histograms for 30 days, sampled full traces for 7–14 days depending on retention tier.

Quick queries & snippets (pseudo-SQL/Flux):
- cold-cache-rate-by-route:
  SELECT count(*) FILTER (WHERE kv_cache_status='cold') / count(*) AS cold_rate
  FROM requests
  WHERE route='text-gen' AND time > now() - 1h

- p95-per-token-for-fallbacks:
  SELECT percentile(token_latency_ms_bucketed, 95)
  FROM token_events
  WHERE fallback_reason IS NOT NULL AND time > now() - 6h
  GROUP BY model_variant

- tiny-batch-freq:
  SELECT count(*) FILTER(WHERE batch_size < 4) / count(*)
  FROM gpu_enqueue
  WHERE time > now() - 30m

Open questions / unresolved hypotheses (for sprint discussion):
- How aggressive should adaptive sampling be during a large incident? We need a controlled way to lift sampling for windows/tenants without blowing observability budgets. (Related ticket: ENG-1457)
- Should kernel_mode be an enumerated metric at ingestion time or inferred offline from runtime logs? Short term: ingest as span attr.
- Does prefix_len correlate with downstream token entropy (a potential proxy for hallucination)? Need an experiment to compute cross-correlation over several routes.

Playbook quick actions (operator runbook snippets):
- If token_heatmap shows p95 spikes at token index > X and cache is warm -> suspect model/kernel switch or ephemeral infra throttling.
- If many small batches in the last 10 minutes, flip temporary batching policy (route-specific) to larger window for 5–10 minutes and watch p95.
- If fallback OOMs spike, disable automatic canary rollouts for that variant and throttle traffic to safe variant.

Notes to self / todo (raw):
- prototype raw trace -> token-index mapping util (python) to stitch spans into token timeline; put in telemetry-protos PR for attribute names (see linked PR).
- quick alert: if cold_cache_rate > 10% AND p95_token_latency > 500ms for 5m window -> page infra-sre + runtime team.
- follow up with product: are there customer routes where prefix warming is undesired? doc their intent.

Appendix: short example trace (condensed):
- request.id=abc123 start
  span:compiler.select_kernel {kernel_mode: float32, seq_len: 1024}
  span:kv-cache.lookup {matched_prefix_len: 0}
  span:gpu.enqueue {batch_id: 2109, queued_ms: 34}
  span:model.generate.tokens {token_index_start: 0, token_index_end: 63, per_token_mean_ms: 12}
  span:gpu.enqueue {batch_id: 2110, queued_ms: 128}
  span:model.generate.tokens {token_index_start: 64, token_index_end: 127, per_token_mean_ms: 312} <-- tail
  span:fallback.trigger {fallback_reason: OOM, from_variant: rdx-13b-q, to_variant: rdx-13b-cpu}

If this doc is useful, next steps: choose 2–3 panels from wireframe and implement in observability sandbox, add a lightweight alert prototype, and run a 1-week sampling experiment biased to error events.

-- Priya (notes, 2025-10 -> 2026-02)

Notes: enforce token_index ranges so we can reconstruct which span produced which token interval. This avoids correlating per-token histograms with unrelated background work.

Operator heuristics (triage checklist):
1) Isolate request cohort: filter by route + model_variant + time window. If 90th percentile token_latency_ms_bucketed > baseline by >3x, escalate to model/runtime team.
2) Check kv_cache_status and cache_hit_prefix_len: if majority are cold or prefix_len < 4 for a route that usually has large prefixes, suspect cache invalidations or keyspace miss.
3) Inspect span:compiler.select_kernel and span:fallback.trigger: kernel_mode switching or fallback events correlate strongly with sudden per-token latency increases.
4) Check GPU enqueue and batch_id churn: frequent tiny batches (batch size 1–2) increase per-token latency due to enqueue overhead. Consider bumping batching window for that route temporarily.
5) If fallback_reason == OOM or infra-eviction, check node-level memory pressure and recent infra scaling events.
6) For hallucination/quality regressions, look for model_variant changes + token distributions (e.g., abrupt increase in next-token entropy proxies).

Compact dashboard wireframe (panel list and queries):
- Top row: SLO overview for route (total requests, error rate, p50/p95 end-to-end latency)
- Row 2 left: Token-level latency heatmap (X axis = token index 0..N, Y = time bucket of request start). Color = p95 token_latency_ms. Query: aggregate over trace spans with token_index ranges mapped to per-token latencies.
- Row 2 right: KV cache warmth panel - stacked bar: warm/partial_warm/cold counts over time. Quick filter by route/model_variant.
- Row 3 left: Kernel mode churn timeline — show percent of requests per kernel_mode.
- Row 3 right: Small table: Top 10 fallback_reason by frequency and p95 token_latency for those requests.
- Drill panel: Single-request trace viewer with annotated token ranges and links to raw payload (redact PII).

Sampling guidance and storage tradeoffs:
- Full per-token tracing for 100% of requests is costly. Proposed hybrid sampling: always record token-level histograms for 100% but only sample detailed spans for 0.5–2% of requests, biased by error/fallback events and heavier requests (>512 tokens).
- Store high-granularity histograms for 30 days, sampled full traces for 7–14 days depending on retention tier.

Quick queries & snippets (pseudo-SQL/Flux):
- cold-cache-rate-by-route:
  SELECT count(*) FILTER (WHERE kv_cache_status='cold') / count(*) AS cold_rate
  FROM requests
  WHERE route='text-gen' AND time > now() - 1h

- p95-per-token-for-fallbacks:
  SELECT percentile(token_latency_ms_bucketed, 95)
  FROM token_events
  WHERE fallback_reason IS NOT NULL AND time > now() - 6h
  GROUP BY model_variant

- tiny-batch-freq:
  SELECT count(*) FILTER(WHERE batch_size < 4) / count(*)
  FROM gpu_enqueue
  WHERE time > now() - 30m

Open questions / unresolved hypotheses (for sprint discussion):
- How aggressive should adaptive sampling be during a large incident? We need a controlled way to lift sampling for windows/tenants without blowing observability budgets. (Related ticket: ENG-1457)
- Should kernel_mode be an enumerated metric at ingestion time or inferred offline from runtime logs? Short term: ingest as span attr.
- Does prefix_len correlate with downstream token entropy (a potential proxy for hallucination)? Need an experiment to compute cross-correlation over several routes.

Playbook quick actions (operator runbook snippets):
- If token_heatmap shows p95 spikes at token index > X and cache is warm -> suspect model/kernel switch or ephemeral infra throttling.
- If many small batches in the last 10 minutes, flip temporary batching policy (route-specific) to larger window for 5–10 minutes and watch p95.
- If fallback OOMs spike, disable automatic canary rollouts for that variant and throttle traffic to safe variant.

Notes to self / todo (raw):
- prototype raw trace -> token-index mapping util (python) to stitch spans into token timeline; put in telemetry-protos PR for attribute names (see linked PR).
- quick alert: if cold_cache_rate > 10% AND p95_token_latency > 500ms for 5m window -> page infra-sre + runtime team.
- follow up with product: are there customer routes where prefix warming is undesired? doc their intent.

Appendix: short example trace (condensed):
- request.id=abc123 start
  span:compiler.select_kernel {kernel_mode: float32, seq_len: 1024}
  span:kv-cache.lookup {matched_prefix_len: 0}
  span:gpu.enqueue {batch_id: 2109, queued_ms: 34}
  span:model.generate.tokens {token_index_start: 0, token_index_end: 63, per_token_mean_ms: 12}
  span:gpu.enqueue {batch_id: 2110, queued_ms: 128}
  span:model.generate.tokens {token_index_start: 64, token_index_end: 127, per_token_mean_ms: 312} <-- tail
  span:fallback.trigger {fallback_reason: OOM, from_variant: rdx-13b-q, to_variant: rdx-13b-cpu}

If this doc is useful, next steps: choose 2–3 panels from wireframe and implement in observability sandbox, add a lightweight alert prototype, and run a 1-week sampling experiment biased to error events.

-- Priya (notes, 2025-10 -> 2026-02)

source.title: Churn Retrospective Playbook — Template & Case Workbook
source.content:
Purpose:
This is a working template and case workbook for running churn retrospectives (postmortems for lost renewals or early terminations). Use this to capture the timeline, root causes, counterfactuals, and a prioritized action plan that feeds product, onboarding, and GTM. Keep it concise — this lives next to the renewal tracker and should be completed within 7 business days of loss.

How to use this doc (quick):
- Owner: CS lead on the account (AE or CSM) opens the doc within 48h of confirmed loss.
- Fill the TL;DR, timeline, and evidence fields first.
- Run a 30–60 minute internal retro with CS, Solutions Eng, Product PM, and SDR/AE as required.
- Publish a 1-paragraph public summary to the Exec Weekly digest and link to the full workbook.

TL;DR (1–2 lines):
- Account name: [ACME Corp] 
- ARR at risk / lost: [USD 120k ARR] 
- Loss date: [2026-01-18] 
- Primary loss reason (short): [Product fit — latency impacted customer flow] 
- Quick recommendation: [Fix latency regression in v2.1 rollout + offer dedicated throughput for 90 days]

High-level impact scoring (fill one):
- Strategic: high | mid | low -> [mid]
- Revenue: >$250k / $100k–$250k / <$100k -> [< $100k]
- Likelihood to winback (estimate): high | mid | low -> [low]

Contributors (internal):
- Name, role, note on involvement
- Priya Natarajan — CSM, point owner (onboarding, renewal discussions)
- Marco Li — Solutions Eng (technical postmortem + logs)
- Asha Patel — Product PM (tracked feature backlog impact)

Customer context snapshot (short):
- Industry: fintech (example)
- Primary use: real-time customer support summarization on web chat
- Deployment: Redwood-hosted API (multi-region)
- Onboarding start / production go-live: 2024-09-10 -> 2024-11-15
- Renewal cadence: annual (renewal due: 2026-02-01)

Timeline (chronological evidence):
- 2024-09-10: kickoff (PS engagement SOW signed).
- 2024-11-15: production go-live, first 2 weeks normal.
- 2025-12-05: customer reported intermittent high tail latency for long chat sessions (support ticket #CS-2191).
- 2026-01-04: Customer escalated to AE; requested SLA for 95th percentile latency.
- 2026-01-09: Temporary mitigation (shortening max tokens) suggested; partial improvement.
- 2026-01-12: Product rollout v2.1 (kernel changes / batch scheduler updated) — we see a regression in 95p latency in EU region.
- 2026-01-16: Customer decision meeting; no commitment to renew without SLA commitment.
- 2026-01-18: Customer gives formal notice: non-renewal; cites reliability and cost variability.
- Evidence: attached logs, support tickets, SLA emails (links above).

Root cause analysis (structured):
- Symptom(s): 95p latency spikes after v2.1; customer-facing timeouts at peak hours.
- Immediate cause: batch scheduler change increased per-request queueing for very long sequences in EU.
- Contributing factors:
  - Lack of a canary test specifically exercising long chat sessions at scale in EU.
  - No alert configured for 95p latency beyond 500ms in customer region.
  - Commercial: no formal SLA offering to this customer tier; price-sensitive.
  - Communication: late visibility to customer about rollout schedule.
- Root cause (5-why summary):
  1) Why did customer see latency spikes? Because v2.1 changed batch scheduling behavior.
  2) Why did v2.1 change impact them? Because their load has a heavy tail of long sequences.
  3) Why wasn't this caught? Because our integration tests didn't include long-tail session profiles for EU region.
  4) Why are those tests missing? No test matrix required long-sequence distribution for multi-region canary.
  5) Why not added? No explicit ownership between platform and product for region-specific workload tests.

Loss reasons (ranked):
- 1 (primary): Operational reliability regression from product rollout (v2.1) — 60%
- 2: Pricing/cost variability concerns during peaks — 20%
- 3: Contract and SLA mismatch (no dedicated offering available at the time) — 12%
- 4: Onboarding gaps (missing documented mitigation playbook for session truncation) — 8%

Counterfactuals (what if we had done X?):
- If we had canaried v2.1 against long-session workloads in EU with 100k tokens/hour, the spike would likely have been caught (probable win).
- If we offered a temporary dedicated pool SLA at renewal discussions, customer may have renewed while we fixed runtime (plausible win).
- If we had an automated 95p latency alert tied to the account, escalation would have been faster and perception improved (possible win).
- Score counterfactuals by feasibility (low/mid/high) and impact (low/mid/high) in the action table below.

Action plan (owner / ETA / priority):
- Safety: Immediate customer stabilization 
  - Owner: Marco Li (SE) — Action: Revert batch scheduler change in EU for the customer’s traffic profile or pin legacy kernel for 90 days. ETA: 72 hours. Priority: P0.
- Short-term product patch
  - Owner: Product (Asha) — Action: Add targeted fix to batch scheduler logic to handle long sequences. ETA: 2 weeks. Priority: P1.
- Monitoring & Alerts
  - Owner: Platform SRE — Action: Add 95p latency monitor per-customer per-region with auto-notify to CS. ETA: 1 week. Priority: P1.
- Commercial makegood
  - Owner: Sales Ops / AE — Action: Offer one-time credit + temporary discounted dedicated capacity for 90 days. ETA: immediate negotiation. Priority: P1.
- Process changes (prevent recurrence)
  - Owner: Eng + Product 
  - Action: Expand canary test matrix to include long-tail session distributions by region; formalize ownership for region-specific test coverage. ETA: Q1 roadmap. Priority: P2.

Playbook: win-back steps (if applicable) — short checklist:
- Did we implement stabilization and confirm 95p restored? [ ]
- Has CS sent an interim status note to customer with timeline? [ ]
- Is a commercial concession approved and documented? [ ]
- Do we offer a technical walk-through and post-fix validation with live traffic? [ ]
- If customer declines initial outreach, schedule 60-day follow-up.

Communication guidance (internal and external):
- Internal: tag #cs-churn-retros, add short summary to exec weekly, include technical root cause and product owner actions. Keep sensitive pricing details limited to Sales Ops and legal.
- External (to customer): 1-paragraph apology + what we fixed + what we will do to prevent recurrence + commercial remediation offered. Send from AE+CS.

Metrics to track (postmortem KPIs):
- Time-to-stabilize (target <72 hours)
- 95p latency per-region per-account (baseline vs post-fix)
- Number of churn cases citing product regressions (quarterly)
- Win-back rate after makegood (target 20% for similar-sized accounts)

Knowledge transfer / artifacts to update:
- Add a canary scenario to repo: test/ci/canary-long-sessions-eu (owner: platform eng)
- Update onboarding doc: cs/playbooks/session-truncate-mitigation.md
- Add new SLA tier offering to commercial product catalog (Sales Ops owner)

Open questions / follow-up required:
- Was the cost variability complaint purely from usage spikes or from overprovisioning? Owner: AE to confirm with billing.
- Is there a broader population of customers with similar long-tail workloads? Owner: Data Science to run cohort query (query id: DS-1292).

Example closing paragraph to publish (public-friendly):
"We regret ACME Corp experienced increased latency after a scheduled runtime update (v2.1). We identified a scheduling change that impacted long-session traffic in the EU region and rolled back the risky change for their workload while delivering a targeted fix. We are offering a remediation package to ACME and have put automated alerts and additional canary tests in place to prevent recurrence."

Notes to self / cheap wins:
- Consider a small playbook doc for AEs: "What to offer at the first sign of latency complaints" (one-pager).
- Track similar postmortems to build a 6-month trend deck for product leadership.

Appendix: Example evidence links and minimal artifacts (paste links below)
- Support ticket thread: link to thread (internal)
- Latency dashboard snapshot: link to Grafana panel
- Patch diff: link to PR #7891

(End of template — duplicate for each lost account, keep filename pattern: churn-<account>-postmortem-YYYYMMDD)

source.title: Data region tethering — operational risks and scenarios
source.summary: Working notes on 'tethering' as an operational concept for region pin enforcement; catalogs failure modes, operator decision checklist, and suggested telemetry for early detection.
source.body:
BACKGROUND

Purpose: capture operational reasoning for 'region tethering' — a lightweight enforcement mode where a tenant's active sessions prefer (but are not hard-locked to) a pinned region for a bounded time window to reduce evictions during transient capacity shortages.

Why this doc exists: we have many pinning playbooks; tethering is a middleground between full pin enforcement and permissive fallback. This doc collects scenarios, risks, operator flows, instrumentation ideas and a quick rollout plan for evaluating tethering on a small set of customers.

ASSUMPTIONS
- Customers may have region pin requests that are soft (commercial) or hard (contractual). Tethering only applied to soft pins unless legal requires otherwise.
- Tether window default: 300s (configurable per-tenant).
- Tethering interacts with autoscaler and routing; runtime has hints to prefer local replica but fallback to nearest compatible if capacity starved.

GLOSSARY
- Tether: transient preferential routing to the pinned region for a time window after customer activity.
- Anchor: the resource assignment record linking tenant -> region and anchor expiration timestamp.
- Eviction: involuntary movement of a request out of pinned region due to capacity.

OPERATOR DECISION CHECKLIST (Quick)
1) Has the customer contract mandate hard residency? If yes, do NOT tether. Flag legal.
2) Is the tenant on Dedicated or Private? Private only — check infra owner.
3) Is telemetry for "eviction-rate" present for this tenant over last 24h? If >0.5% escalate.
4) If capacity alert in region (gpu-util > 92%), enable aggressive fallback (reduce tether window to 0).
5) For planned maintenance, set tether window to 0 and notify customer.

EXAMPLE FAILURE SCENARIOS AND MITIGATION
- Scenario: burst traffic causes local pool to saturate; tether causes queueing and SLO breach.
  Mitigation: dynamic throttle — if latency P99 > target for >60s, disable tether for that tenant and route to fallback.
- Scenario: cross-border audit complains that "requests observed outside region" during tether fallback.
  Mitigation: record request-level evidence: anchor timestamp, chosen region, fallback reason; TTL logs kept for 90 days and exported to audit bundle.
- Scenario: load spikes coincide with autoscaler cold-starts; tether pins requests to nodes that are not yet ready -> increased error rate.
  Mitigation: prefer warm pool warm-up; if warm pool < threshold, bypass tether for first N requests while scaling completes.

TELEMETRY (signals to add)
- tenant.anchor_last_seen (timestamp)
- tenant.tether_hits (count): requests served in tether region in window
- tenant.tether_fallbacks (count): requests that couldn't be honored and were routed elsewhere
- region.anchor_capacity_headroom (float): available slot percentage reserved for tethers
- request.tether_evidence (string tag): anchor_id|anchor_ts|fallback_reason
- Key alert: tether_fallback_rate > 1% sustained for 10m -> ops page

OPERATOR RUNFLOW (sample)
- Alert triggers: tether_fallback_rate high
- Oncall checks: recent anchor evidence via query
  SQL example (toy):
  SELECT anchor_id, count(1) as fallback_count
  FROM request_events
  WHERE tenant_id = 'T-123' AND event = 'tether_fallback' AND ts > now() - interval '1 hour'
  GROUP BY anchor_id ORDER BY fallback_count DESC LIMIT 10;
- Decide: temporary disable tether for tenant OR provision burst capacity OR engage capacity evict policy
- Post-mortem: capture why preference was not honored, timeline, and customer notification draft

OPERATIONAL CONTROLS (config knobs)
- tether_window_seconds (per-tenant): default 300
- tether_hard_enforced: boolean (for contracts)
- tether_max_concurrent: number of concurrent tethered requests reserved on region
- fallback_priority: [geo, cost, latency]

ROLLOUT STRATEGY
- Phase 0: internal dogfood tenants only (2 weeks) — monitor tether_fallback_rate, latency regressions
- Phase 1: opt-in enterprise customers (support + SE) — 1 month
- Phase 2: auto-suggest for soft pins with opt-out

COMMUNICATIONS
- Template snippets for CS/SE when tether is disabled due to capacity:
  "We temporarily routed some requests out of your preferred region to avoid service interruptions during a capacity event. We logged evidence and will retain it for compliance review."

OPEN QUESTIONS (to triage)
- Legal definition: is transient routing within N minutes acceptable for 'soft residency' across EMEA? (Sana to confirm)
- How long must we retain fallback evidence for audits in EU: 90 vs 180 days?
- Telemetry cost: per-request tether_evidence tagging increases metrics cardinality. Do we sample (1 in 10) or store full logs?

NEXT ACTIONS
- [ ] Add tether_fallback_rate alert (ENG-3124) -> assigned Marcus
- [ ] Prototype anchor evidence format and storage TTL (Sana)
- [ ] Run 48h dogfood with 3 internal tenants (Priya)
- [ ] Draft CS template and legal Qs (Eric)

REFERENCES
- Confluence: https://company.atlassian.net/wiki/spaces/PRIV/pages/12345678-region-tethering-spec
- Jira: ENG-3124, CS-998
- PR: https://github.com/redwood-inference/infra/pull/998

NOTES TO SELF
- This is very experimental; keep it behind a flag. Expect iterating on tether_window and reserved slots.
- If we see consistent cross-region routing for an org, that's a commercial red flag — surface to AE.

source.title: Spectrum degrade strategy and control-plane scenarios
source.content:
Purpose and scope:
This note captures a pragmatic design for graceful degradation across model fidelity, capacity tiers and regions. The goal is to define a control-plane-first approach that lets product traffic continue serving with predictable latency and cost characteristics while degrading user-visible quality in controlled steps. Focus areas: routing policies, control-plane decision signals, multi-region spillover, and example policies/operators for rollout and emergency escape hatches.

Design motivation (short):
- Users need predictable behavior when capacity or latency SLOs break — not opaque errors.
- Instead of binary failover, model fidelity can be stepped down across a spectrum (quality, context length, quantization) to preserve availability.
- Control plane should make routing/variant decisions using compact signals (latency percentiles, queue length, effective concurrency, regional health) and be auditable.

Key concepts (vocabulary):
- Fidelity bands: HIGH (full context, high-FP, full tokenizer), MEDIUM (reduced context, int8/4 quantized), LOW (short context, distilled model), DIAL-TONE (very cheap fast stub with limited functionality).
- Capacity tiers: RESERVED (dedicated capacity), SHARED (best-effort pooled), BURST (eviction-prioritized spare).
- Variant family: model binary + runtime profile mapping (e.g., gptf-70b.fp16, gptf-70b.q4_0, gptf-7b.distill).
- Control policy: stateful logic that maps runtime signals -> routing decisions + telemetry events.

Control plane responsibilities (concise):
- Maintain global variant inventory and regional capacity view (per-variant, per-tier).
- Evaluate health signals and compute per-route policy vectors (allowed_variants, cohort_weights, capacity_cutoff).
- Emit routing directives (sticky cookie + tag-based routing) to edge proxies and runtime routers.
- Record decisions and expose audit events for post-mortem.

Decision signals (what we measure):
- P50/P95/P99 latency per route and per-variant (1m,5m windows).
- Queue depth and queue latency at frontends and serving pods.
- Per-variant eviction_rate and instance_utilization.
- Error rates (model OOMs, runtime OOMs, token-generation aborts).
- Regional fabric health (network egress errors, infra maintenance flags).

Policy primitives (operator-friendly):
- degrade_to(variant_family, fidelity_band) : immediate directive to route new requests to listed variants.
- progressive_rollout(percentages, duration): shift traffic gradually across variants to avoid spikes.
- capacity_shed(percent): instructs frontdoor to drop low-priority requests (see priority_tags).
- sticky_override(session_key): preserve in-flight sessions on same variant while new requests follow new policy.

A simple state machine for degrade decisions:
- NORMAL -> WATCH when P95 > threshold or queue_depth > threshold
- WATCH -> SOFT_DEGRADE if pressure persists for T_watch and there is capacity delta to MEDIUM/LOW variants
- SOFT_DEGRADE -> HARD_DEGRADE if issues persist or error rates spike; escalate to regional spillover if local capacity is exhausted
- Any state -> RECOVER when metrics return below recovery thresholds for a sustained window

Multi-region spillover heuristics (practical):
- Prefer local-to-local first: route to compatible variant in same region (different tier) before cross-region.
- If intra-region exhausted, spill to nearest region with compatible SLA bucket (low-latency region when possible).
- Controlled global spill: only top-level routes allowed to cross-region by default; lower-level internal routes must opt-in.
- Heartbeat and capacity tokens: regions exchange small capacity tokens to allow rapid admission without full config sync.

Routing priority order (example):
1) RESERVED local, same fidelity band
2) RESERVED local, lower fidelity band (soft degrade)
3) SHARED local, same fidelity
4) RESERVED cross-region, same fidelity (if latency budget allows)
5) SHARED cross-region, lower fidelity
6) DIAL-TONE fallback or an explicit error response if no compatible path

Operational playbook (for on-call):
- Trigger criteria: sustained P99 > x ms OR queue depth > y for > 3 minutes OR unlockable OOM storm.
- First response: flip route to SOFT_DEGRADE (auto-policy) and notify SRE + product owner. Policy should be reversible with one command.
- If soft degrade doesn't reduce pressure in 2 minutes, escalate to HARD_DEGRADE and enable regional spillover for affected routes.
- If cross-region spill causes SLO misses elsewhere, run roll-back script which rebalances using capacity tokens.
- Post-incident: capture traces, map which policies executed, and run a targeted eval on representative prompts to measure quality delta.

Example control rules (pseudo YAML for readability but included as plain text):
- route: /chat
  priority_buckets: [platinum, gold, standard]
  thresholds:
    p95_latency_ms: 800
    queue_depth: 150
  degrade_sequence:
    - to: gptf-70b.q4_0
      when: p95_latency_ms > 800 for 120s
    - to: gptf-7b.distill
      when: p95_latency_ms > 1200 or queue_depth > 400
    - to: dial-tone-stub
      when: region_capacity_available == false

Telemetry and quality monitoring tie-ins:
- Every degrade event must log: route, orig_variant, target_variant, trigger_signal, actor (auto/manual), timestamp, cohort_id.
- Bind degradations to eval suites: sample requests (stratified by route) are sent periodically through degraded variants and scores are collected to detect unacceptable regressions.
- Alert on quality regressions as a first-class SLO (e.g., accuracy drop > delta triggers a rollback).

Safety knobs and customer controls:
- Customer pinning: customers can pin to a variant family; control plane respects pin unless safety override is explicit.
- Priority tags: requests have customer-priority; lowest priority traffic is the first candidate for capacity_shed.
- Rate-limited cross-region: system caps global cross-region admission to prevent cascading downstream pressure.

Testing and simulations to validate policies:
- Synthetic load runner that can mimic route-weighted token rates, per-variant latencies, and network partitions. Useful test cases: sudden 4x traffic burst, region outage with 2 minute recovery, slow memory leak on a variant type.
- Chaos experiments: randomly kill 20% of reserved nodes and assert that degrade path completes within 90s and p95 reduces to acceptable band.

Implementation notes (fast wins):
- Keep variant metadata small and cached at edge (id, fidelity_band, region, capacity_tier). Decision logic lives in control plane but caches directives at edge for quick failover.
- Use a lightweight signed directive (ttl=30s) so proxies can continue to route during brief control-plane flaps without needing full reconciliation.
- Emit compact diagnostic tags into request traces for correlation (policy_decision=SOFT_DEGRADE, variant_to=..., trigger=p95-1200ms).

Open questions / unresolved tradeoffs:
- How aggressive should auto-degrade be for high-value customers vs. anonymous lower tiers? (we currently bias conservatively for Platinum)
- Exact thresholds across diverse workloads (conversational vs. embeddings) — need per-route profiling and adaptive thresholds.
- Balance between live rollback speed and consistency when many routes change simultaneously.

Next steps / action items:
- Build a minimal control-plane service that supports degrade_to and progressive_rollout primitives (owner: Liam; PR milestone 2 weeks).
- Add per-route eval hooks that run degraded variants against golden prompt sets (owner: Maria; 3 weeks).
- Create chaos test harness for cross-region spill scenarios and validate the heartbeat/token system (owner: Ethan; 4 weeks).

Notes to reviewers:
- This is a working draft; trying to be prescriptive while leaving room for product constraints. Feedback: prefer concrete threshold proposals for top 10 routes and a decision on pinning override semantics.

-- end of doc --

source.title: KV eviction signature analysis and host clock jitter effects
source.content:
Purpose:
This doc captures a set of early experiments that attempt to correlate distinct eviction "signatures" (patterns in telemetry around which KV pages get evicted and when) with two under-explored sources of variability: host clock jitter across CPUs and short-lived I/O/bandwidth bursts that cause effective eviction ordering changes. Goal is to decide whether we need: 1) cross-node clock-aligned watermarking for eviction, 2) jitter-aware eviction hysteresis, or 3) additional telemetry to reliably attribute long-tail misses to system-level noise rather than workload semantics.

Background / short primer:
- We track per-page last-access timestamp (logical monotonic ns) and use an LRU-ish eviction with hysteresis (min residency before eligible). This is sensitive to the clock used (rdtsc vs clock_gettime) and to inter-socket clock skew when sampling is done on different CPUs.
- Recent customer reports (see RINF-1724) show tail-latency spikes where resident-set drops happened seemingly in FIFO order instead of MRU-based; hypothesis: sampling delays + host jitter + quick pkt bursts cause transient mis-ranking.

What we ran (high level):
1) Microbench harness (kv-ex-evict-harness) that issues synthetic requests with controlled reuse intervals and optional bursts.
   - Parameters: key-population=200k, working-set=40k, burst-pattern=poisson/periodic, reuse-window=10–300ms.
   - Measurements: per-request latency, per-page eviction timestamp, CPU core id, perf counters (LLC-misses, mem-bandwidth using pcm/LIKWID), scheduler preempt count, and kernel scheduling latency trace (ftrace sched_switch).
2) Host jitter probe: lightweight kernel module toggling TSC vs CLOCK_MONOTONIC sampling for last-access timestamp. Also synthetic noise introduced via a background thread that pins to a core and performs periodic rdtscp bursts to create clock skew-like effects.
3) NUMA/IO interplay: repeated runs on single-socket and cross-socket placements; a separate background job saturated memory channel for 200ms bursts triggered at random to simulate noisy co-tenants.

Key findings (summary):
- Eviction reordering correlates strongly with sampling core affinity: when access-time sampling happens on a different socket than the hot core, ~22% of pages get an apparent older timestamp (due to ~100–400ns drift in our test kernels under load), causing premature eviction.
- Short memory-bandwidth spikes (peak > 120 GB/s for 100–300ms measured via PCM) lead to increased LLC miss rates and a transient increase in eviction churn; this shows up as many pages being re-accessed shortly after eviction (reuse rate spike), increasing p99 latency.
- Using rdtscp-based timestamps reduced misranking compared to clock_gettime(CLOCK_MONOTONIC) in our harness (p99 tail improved ~12%) because rdtscp is core-local but monotonic per-core; downside is cross-core comparisons require normalization which we did via a lightweight per-core skew table updated every 30s.
- Kernel scheduling preemptions explain a non-trivial fraction of apparent staleness. On hosts with frequent SCHED_OTHER preemption spikes (due to control-plane agents), eviction candidates often have stale access times.

Representative telemetry snippet (simplified):
- event:access key=k18432 ts=169917123410200000 core=3
- event:evict key=k18432 ts=169917123410250500 core=14 eviction_rank=328
- event:reaccess key=k18432 ts=169917123410350100 core=3
- perf:llc_misses=12 mem_bw=138GB/s preempt=3
Interpretation: access sampled on core 3 -> eviction decision evaluated on core 14 later; measured bandwidth spike and several preempts between access and eviction.

Classification of eviction signatures (working taxonomy):
- True LRU candidates: long quiet periods, no preemptions recorded, no mem-bandwidth spikes nearby.
- Clock-skewed candidates: last-access timestamp sampled on different socket/core at a later mapping; shows small ns-scale inconsistencies and core-id mismatch.
- Preemption-artifact: high sched_switch counts or long-running cross-core migrations between access and eviction.
- Bandwidth-induced churn: correlated to perf mem_bw spikes and high LLC miss deltas during window [-50ms, +200ms] around eviction.

Quantitative results (select):
- Baseline run (no noise): eviction churn 9.2% of working-set per minute, reuse-within-200ms = 3.4%
- With injected clock jitter (per-core skew bursts): eviction churn 16.8%, reuse-within-200ms = 11.2%
- With bandwidth bursts (130 GB/s peaks): eviction churn 21.4%, reuse-within-200ms = 14.6%
- Combined worst-case: churn 28.1% and p99 latency increased by 44% in the synthetic chat benchmark.

Implications for runtime design (candidate mitigations):
1) Switch to TSC+per-core skew table + periodic drift calibration (every 30s) before comparing timestamps across cores. Pros: low-latency sampling, consistent comparisons. Cons: calibration complexity under thermal throttling.
2) Add eviction hysteresis that is jitter-aware: require candidate pages to have not only oldest timestamp but also a minimum of X CPU-cycles of residency (measured by a lightweight counter) — reduces false positives from preemptions.
3) Bandwidth-aware backoff: detect short-term mem-bandwidth spikes via perf counters and temporarily increase eviction threshold (or freeze background compaction) for 100–500ms.
4) Prefer sampling on the hot-core path: tag last-access with (core_id, small delta) and prefer to update in-place on same core to avoid cross-core timestamp normalization when possible.

Open questions and follow-ups:
- How often does this manifest in real production workloads vs synthetic? Need to sample live traces (anonymized) and run the same classifier across recent customer hosts. (Action: collect 1h of anonymized eviction traces from dedicated nodes; see RINF-1724)
- Cost analysis: what is the CPU overhead of per-core skew calibration and extra perf counter sampling? Initial estimate: ~0.3–0.6% CPU on busy serving processes, but need better numbers under 64-core hosts.
- Interactions with multi-tenant cache priorities: should background tenants be penalized differently during bandwidth spikes? If we pause eviction for noisy periods we may increase memory pressure elsewhere.
- Is there value in centralized cross-host time-sync for eviction decisions (probably not — too expensive). Local heuristics preferred.

Next experiments planned:
- Run a 48-hour A/B on a subset of dedicated fleet: A uses current logic, B uses rdtscp+skew table+bandwidth backoff. Collect p50/p95/p99 latency, reuse-within-200ms, churn, extra CPU. (Owner: Marcus)
- Instrument production tracer to emit anonymized core-id + timestamp for last-access events for 1% of hosts to validate classification. (Owner: Elena)
- Microbench: measure cost of skew-table calibration under thermal frequency scaling and frequency hops. (Owner: Samir)

Immediate action items (short):
- [ ] Add lightweight per-core skew table prototype to harness (PR prep by Marcus).
- [ ] Add mem-bandwidth probe hook to runtime evictor to enable backoff windows (design doc stub in GH PR).
- [ ] Draft an ops runbook for diagnosing eviction churn rooted in host noise (notes to SRE).

Notes to reviewers:
- This is intentionally exploratory and contains some hand-wavy thresholds; aim is to validate whether host-level noise explains a large chunk of our churn. Please prioritize review on the planned A/B experiment design and signal collection approach for production traces.

Appendix (short commands & data locations):
- Harness repo: github.com/redwood-inference/kv-ev-experiments (branch: jitter-evict-probe)
- Perf counters used: uncore_imc (memory BW), LLC_MISSES, CPU_CYCLES, sched_switch ftrace counts
- Raw trace example stored: gs://redwood-test-artifacts/kv-jitter/2025-11-12/run-07/ (redacted)

End of doc.

title: KV eviction signature analysis and host clock jitter effects
preview:
Purpose:
This doc captures a set of early experiments that attempt to correlate distinct eviction "signatures" (patterns in telemetry around which KV pages get evicted and when) with two under-explored sources of variability: host clock jitter across CPUs and short-lived I/O/bandwidth bursts that cause effective eviction ordering changes. Goal is to decide whether we need: 1) cross-node clock-aligned watermarking for eviction, 2) jitter-aware eviction hysteresis, or 3) additional telemetry to reliably attribute long-tail misses to system-level noise rather than workload semantics.

Background / short primer:
- We track per-page last-access timestamp (logical monotonic ns) and use an LRU-ish eviction with hysteresis (min residency before eligible). This is sensitive to the clock used (rdtsc vs clock_gettime) and to inter-socket clock skew when sampling is done on different CPUs.
- Recent customer reports (see RINF-1724) show tail-latency spikes where resident-set drops happened seemingly in FIFO order instead of MRU-based; hypothesis: sampling delays + host jitter + quick pkt bursts cause transient mis-ranking.

What we ran (high level):
1) Microbench harness (kv-ex-evict-harness) that issues synthetic requests with controlled reuse intervals and optional bursts.
   - Parameters: key-population=200k, working-set=40k, burst-pattern=poisson/periodic, reuse-window=10–300ms.
   - Measurements: per-request latency, per-page eviction timestamp, CPU core id, perf counters (LLC-misses, mem-bandwidth using pcm/LIKWID), scheduler preempt count, and kernel scheduling latency trace (ftrace sched_switch).
2) Host jitter probe: lightweight kernel module toggling TSC vs CLOCK_MONOTONIC sampling for last-access timestamp. Also synthetic noise introduced via a background thread that pins to a core and performs periodic rdtscp bursts to create clock skew-like effects.
3) NUMA/IO interplay: repeated runs on single-socket and cross-socket placements; a separate background job saturated memory channel for 200ms bursts triggered at random to simulate noisy co-tenants.

Key findings (summary):
- Eviction reordering correlates strongly with sampling core affinity: when access-time sampling happens on a different socket than the hot core, ~22% of pages get an apparent older timestamp (due to ~100–400ns drift in our test kernels under load), causing premature eviction.

- perf:llc_misses=12 mem_bw=138GB/s preempt=3

Classification of eviction signatures (working taxonomy):
- True LRU candidates: long quiet periods, no preemptions recorded, no mem-bandwidth spikes nearby.
- Clock-skewed candidates: last-access timestamp sampled on different socket/core at a later mapping; shows small ns-scale inconsistencies and core-id mismatch.
- Preemption-artifact: high sched_switch counts or long-running cross-core migrations between access and eviction.
- Bandwidth-induced churn: correlated to perf mem_bw spikes and high LLC miss deltas during window [-50ms, +200ms] around eviction.

- With bandwidth bursts (130 GB/s peaks): eviction churn 21.4%, reuse-within-200ms = 14.6%
- Combined worst-case: churn 28.1% and p99 latency increased by 44% in the synthetic chat benchmark.

Implications for runtime design (candidate mitigations):
1) Switch to TSC+per-core skew table + periodic drift calibration (every 30s) before comparing timestamps across cores. Pros: low-latency sampling, consistent comparisons. Cons: calibration complexity under thermal throttling.
2) Add eviction hysteresis that is jitter-aware: require candidate pages to have not only oldest timestamp but also a minimum of X CPU-cycles of residency (measured by a lightweight counter) — reduces false positives from preemptions.
3) Bandwidth-aware backoff: detect short-term mem-bandwidth spikes via perf counters and temporarily increase eviction threshold (or freeze background compaction) for 100–500ms.

- How often does this manifest in real production workloads vs synthetic? Need to sample live traces (anonymized) and run the same classifier across recent customer hosts. (Action: collect 1h of anonymized eviction traces from dedicated nodes; see RINF-1724)
- Cost analysis: what is the CPU overhead of per-core skew calibration and extra perf counter sampling? Initial estimate: ~0.3–0.6% CPU on busy serving processes, but need better numbers under 64-core hosts.
- Interactions with multi-tenant cache priorities: should background tenants be penalized differently during bandwidth spikes? If we pause eviction for noisy periods we may increase memory pressure elsewhere.

source.title: Helm + GitOps image attestation and rollback guide
source.content:
Purpose:
This is a working playbook focused on ensuring image provenance (attestation + SBOM) for private/VPC/on-prem GitOps rollouts and a pragmatic, observable rollback strategy tied to Argo/Helm upgrades. The goal: make promoted images auditable, minimize blast radius during upgrades, and automate safe rollbacks when health or quality signals break.

Scope / audience:
- Platform and SRE engineers owning private deployments and the GitOps pipelines
- Solutions engineers and SEs helping customers onboard private clusters
- Release engineers coordinating upgrade windows for on-prem customers

Summary of the approach (short):
1) Build artifacts with SBOM + cosign signatures at image build time.
2) Store attestation metadata and signatures in the registry and mirror SBOMs to artifact storage (S3 or internal blob store).
3) Promote images by opening a GitOps PR to the environment repo that references a signed image digest (not tag).
4) ArgoCD/Argo Rollouts applies the Helm manifest; pre-sync hooks validate signature and SBOM match policies.
5) Canary rollout (gradual weights) controlled by Argo Rollouts; automated observability checks.
6) Auto-rollback triggers on defined health/quality thresholds or on attestation mismatch.

Quick checklist (for on-call / release lead):
- [ ] Is the image signed with cosign and attestation present?
- [ ] SBOM uploaded and stored (link in PR description)?
- [ ] GitOps PR uses image@sha256:digest, not :latest or :version tag only
- [ ] Helm chart version bumped and values file updated in env repo
- [ ] Argo app health checks defined (readiness, liveliness, custom probes)
- [ ] Canary weights and schedule present in rollout config
- [ ] Alerting for rollback thresholds wired to PagerDuty runbook

Terraform module notes (practical reminders):
- Module name: modules/private-deploy/gitops-app (approx pattern)
- Inputs to expose: registry_endpoint, image_pull_secret_name, attestor_kms_key_id, sbom_bucket, argocd_app_repo, env_repo_path
- Avoid embedding secrets in tfvars; use external secret manager provider (vault/kms) and reference through data sources.
- Provide a boolean `enable_image_attestation` flag default true so customers can opt-out temporarily during emergency migrations.
- Outputs: argocd_app_name, service_account_email (if cloud IAM), iam_role_arn, iam_policy_id (for artifact read).
- Module idempotency: ensure `helm_release` resource (or k8s manifest resources) uses `force_update` only when absolutely necessary; leverage chart values diffing to avoid churn.
- Versioning: semver tags on module and a changelog in the repo. Breaking changes increment major and include migration notes.

Image attestation / signing mechanics (recommended pipeline):
- Build step (CI): build container image -> generate SBOM (syft) -> upload SBOM to artifacts bucket -> sign image + attestation with cosign using customer KMS (KMS key wrapped by cosign keyless or KMS plugin) -> push image to registry.
- Attestation content: build id, builder git sha, SBOM link, build timestamp, provenance statements (SLSA-lite).
- Registry metadata: push signed manifest + attach attestation via cosign. Keep the digest immutable.
- Acceptance: admission/webhook in cluster should reject images without required attestation unless `attestation_policy=relaxed` set per namespace.

Argo / Helm integration notes:
- Use App-of-Apps pattern if we manage many envs: root app points to env repo which contains HelmRelease manifests.
- Pre-sync hook (k8s Job) to verify image signature: a small container that runs cosign verify-attestation --type=custom --attestation-path and exits non-zero if unattested. Attach job to argocd pre-sync.
- Use Helm charts that accept an image.digest input (not just tag). Example values.yaml key: image: { repository: registry/foo, digest: sha256:..., pullPolicy: IfNotPresent } and template produces image: "{{ .Values.image.repository }}@{{ .Values.image.digest }}"
- Post-sync: run an SBOM checksum verification job that fetches SBOM and validates it matches attestation. This is optional but recommended for high-security customers.

Canary & rollout strategy (recommended default):
- Use Argo Rollouts to implement traffic shifting (service mesh or ingress weight).
- Default weights/schedule: 5% -> 25% after 5m -> 60% after 20m -> 100% after 60m. These numbers are knobs; tune per customer SLA.
- Observability gates during canary: 1) request error rate, 2) latency p95, 3) CPU/memory OOMs, 4) business metric signal (if available).
- Gate window durations: short gate 5–10m for synthetic checks, long gate 30–60m for real traffic.
- Rollback triggers:
  - > error rate increase > 2x baseline for the gate window
  - p95 latency regression > 2x and sustained for 10m
  - CrashLoopBackOff or repeated restarts > N within 10m
  - Missing/failed attestation verification
- On rollback, Argo Rollouts will shift traffic back; additionally: run a post-failure job to collect diagnostics (logs, thread-dumps, heap, SBOM diff) and attach to the incident.

Automated rollback playbook (quick):
1) Argo triggers rollback because gate failed. PagerDuty runbook fires.
2) Slack channel #deployments-notify gets notification with link to Argo Rollouts UI and the failing metrics.
3) On-call runs `kubectl -n <ns> get rollouts` and inspects `kubectl describe rollout/<name>`
4) Collect artifacts: `kubectl logs` for canary pods, `kubectl get events`, `kubectl describe` for failed pods.
5) If root cause non-attestation or SBOM mismatch, mark image as revoked in registry and block future promotions via policy.
6) Postmortem template: what changed in chart/values, image digest, builder sha, SBOM differences.

Image promotion workflow (GitOps-friendly):
- Build job uploads metadata and posts a PR to env repo with updated values.yaml referencing image.digest and sbom link. CI includes an automated checklist that verifies cosign signature and SBOM present.
- PR description must include: digest, SBOM link, cosign signature id, build URL, smoke test results.
- Require 1 approver from SE + 1 approver from SRE for critical clusters.
- Merge triggers Argo to sync.

Attestation failure modes and mitigations:
- Missing signature: block fusion; admission rejects -> fast rollback or block. Mitigation: re-trigger build or allow emergency allowlist (with manual ticket).
- Mismatched SBOM: fail pre-sync; require human triage. Keep recorded SBOM artifacts for forensic.
- KMS signing key rotation: maintain previous key set in cosign key config for overlap period; update terraform module to support `previous_key_ids` array and rotate in two phases.

Testing & staging guidance:
- Always test the attestation pre-sync hook in a staging cluster that mirrors the prod admission policy (same mutating/validating webhooks).
- Add a synthetic workload that exercises the canary gate conditions so you can simulate rollbacks without real traffic.

Known constraints / tradeoffs (notes to remember):
- Using image digests everywhere reduces human readability (no version tags in manifests) but increases immutability and reproducibility.
- SBOM storage costs and retention policies need to be negotiated per customer (30/90/365 days).
- Pre-sync attestation jobs add latency to deploys (~10–30s) depending on SBOM size and network. Document this in SLO for deploy duration.

Open action items / tickets (todo):
- INF-3421: Add pre-sync attestation job to helm chart templates (assigned Liam).
- INF-3587: Terraform module: expose attestor_kms_key_id and previous_key_ids; add docs for rotation (assigned Marta).
- INF-3672: Add SBOM retention policy config to customer onboarding checklist (owner: Devon).

References / useful commands (examples):
- Verify image attestation locally: `cosign verify-attestation --type custom --key <key-ref> <registry>/foo@sha256:<digest>`
- Generate SBOM: `syft packages:oci <registry>/foo:tag -o cyclonedx-json > foo-sbom.json`
- Example Helm values usage: `image:
  repository: registry.foo.com/redwood/service
  digest: sha256:abcdef...
  pullPolicy: IfNotPresent`
- Basic Argo Rollouts inspect: `kubectl -n prod get rollouts` and `kubectl argo rollouts get rollout <name> --watch`

Notes to self (informal):
- Keep this doc light — long term the canonical spec should be a confluence page, but Drive is where we iterate.
- Try to standardize the pre-sync job as a tiny static utility image that everyone can reuse; avoid copy/paste in many charts.
- For customers without strong KMS, support cosign keyless with transparency logs but keep expectations clear in onboarding (weaker guarantees).

Contact points (who to ping):
- Priya Natarajan (owner) – priya@redwood.ai
- Liam Chen (automation) – liam@redwood.ai
- Platform slack: #platform-private-deploy

End of draft.

6) Auto-rollback triggers on defined health/quality thresholds or on attestation mismatch.

Quick checklist (for on-call / release lead):
- [ ] Is the image signed with cosign and attestation present?
- [ ] SBOM uploaded and stored (link in PR description)?
- [ ] GitOps PR uses image@sha256:digest, not :latest or :version tag only
- [ ] Helm chart version bumped and values file updated in env repo
- [ ] Argo app health checks defined (readiness, liveliness, custom probes)
- [ ] Canary weights and schedule present in rollout config
- [ ] Alerting for rollback thresholds wired to PagerDuty runbook

Terraform module notes (practical reminders):
- Module name: modules/private-deploy/gitops-app (approx pattern)
- Inputs to expose: registry_endpoint, image_pull_secret_name, attestor_kms_key_id, sbom_bucket, argocd_app_repo, env_repo_path
- Avoid embedding secrets in tfvars; use external secret manager provider (vault/kms) and reference through data sources.
- Provide a boolean `enable_image_attestation` flag default true so customers can opt-out temporarily during emergency migrations.
- Outputs: argocd_app_name, service_account_email (if cloud IAM), iam_role_arn, iam_policy_id (for artifact read).
- Module idempotency: ensure `helm_release` resource (or k8s manifest resources) uses `force_update` only when absolutely necessary; leverage chart values diffing to avoid churn.
- Versioning: semver tags on module and a changelog in the repo. Breaking changes increment major and include migration notes.

Image attestation / signing mechanics (recommended pipeline):
- Build step (CI): build container image -> generate SBOM (syft) -> upload SBOM to artifacts bucket -> sign image + attestation with cosign using customer KMS (KMS key wrapped by cosign keyless or KMS plugin) -> push image to registry.
- Attestation content: build id, builder git sha, SBOM link, build timestamp, provenance statements (SLSA-lite).
- Registry metadata: push signed manifest + attach attestation via cosign. Keep the digest immutable.
- Acceptance: admission/webhook in cluster should reject images without required attestation unless `attestation_policy=relaxed` set per namespace.

Argo / Helm integration notes:
- Use App-of-Apps pattern if we manage many envs: root app points to env repo which contains HelmRelease manifests.
- Pre-sync hook (k8s Job) to verify image signature: a small container that runs cosign verify-attestation --type=custom --attestation-path and exits non-zero if unattested. Attach job to argocd pre-sync.
- Use Helm charts that accept an image.digest input (not just tag). Example values.yaml key: image: { repository: registry/foo, digest: sha256:..., pullPolicy: IfNotPresent } and template produces image: "{{ .Values.image.repository }}@{{ .Values.image.digest }}"
- Post-sync: run an SBOM checksum verification job that fetches SBOM and validates it matches attestation. This is optional but recommended for high-security customers.

Canary & rollout strategy (recommended default):
- Use Argo Rollouts to implement traffic shifting (service mesh or ingress weight).
- Default weights/schedule: 5% -> 25% after 5m -> 60% after 20m -> 100% after 60m. These numbers are knobs; tune per customer SLA.
- Observability gates during canary: 1) request error rate, 2) latency p95, 3) CPU/memory OOMs, 4) business metric signal (if available).
- Gate window durations: short gate 5–10m for synthetic checks, long gate 30–60m for real traffic.
- Rollback triggers:
  - > error rate increase > 2x baseline for the gate window
  - p95 latency regression > 2x and sustained for 10m
  - CrashLoopBackOff or repeated restarts > N within 10m
  - Missing/failed attestation verification
- On rollback, Argo Rollouts will shift traffic back; additionally: run a post-failure job to collect diagnostics (logs, thread-dumps, heap, SBOM diff) and attach to the incident.

Automated rollback playbook (quick):
1) Argo triggers rollback because gate failed. PagerDuty runbook fires.
2) Slack channel #deployments-notify gets notification with link to Argo Rollouts UI and the failing metrics.
3) On-call runs `kubectl -n <ns> get rollouts` and inspects `kubectl describe rollout/<name>`
4) Collect artifacts: `kubectl logs` for canary pods, `kubectl get events`, `kubectl describe` for failed pods.
5) If root cause non-attestation or SBOM mismatch, mark image as revoked in registry and block future promotions via policy.
6) Postmortem template: what changed in chart/values, image digest, builder sha, SBOM differences.

Image promotion workflow (GitOps-friendly):
- Build job uploads metadata and posts a PR to env repo with updated values.yaml referencing image.digest and sbom link. CI includes an automated checklist that verifies cosign signature and SBOM present.
- PR description must include: digest, SBOM link, cosign signature id, build URL, smoke test results.
- Require 1 approver from SE + 1 approver from SRE for critical clusters.
- Merge triggers Argo to sync.

Attestation failure modes and mitigations:
- Missing signature: block fusion; admission rejects -> fast rollback or block. Mitigation: re-trigger build or allow emergency allowlist (with manual ticket).
- Mismatched SBOM: fail pre-sync; require human triage. Keep recorded SBOM artifacts for forensic.
- KMS signing key rotation: maintain previous key set in cosign key config for overlap period; update terraform module to support `previous_key_ids` array and rotate in two phases.

Testing & staging guidance:
- Always test the attestation pre-sync hook in a staging cluster that mirrors the prod admission policy (same mutating/validating webhooks).
- Add a synthetic workload that exercises the canary gate conditions so you can simulate rollbacks without real traffic.

Known constraints / tradeoffs (notes to remember):
- Using image digests everywhere reduces human readability (no version tags in manifests) but increases immutability and reproducibility.
- SBOM storage costs and retention policies need to be negotiated per customer (30/90/365 days).
- Pre-sync attestation jobs add latency to deploys (~10–30s) depending on SBOM size and network. Document this in SLO for deploy duration.

Open action items / tickets (todo):
- INF-3421: Add pre-sync attestation job to helm chart templates (assigned Liam).
- INF-3587: Terraform module: expose attestor_kms_key_id and previous_key_ids; add docs for rotation (assigned Marta).
- INF-3672: Add SBOM retention policy config to customer onboarding checklist (owner: Devon).

References / useful commands (examples):
- Verify image attestation locally: `cosign verify-attestation --type custom --key <key-ref> <registry>/foo@sha256:<digest>`
- Generate SBOM: `syft packages:oci <registry>/foo:tag -o cyclonedx-json > foo-sbom.json`
- Example Helm values usage: `image:
  repository: registry.foo.com/redwood/service
  digest: sha256:abcdef...
  pullPolicy: IfNotPresent`
- Basic Argo Rollouts inspect: `kubectl -n prod get rollouts` and `kubectl argo rollouts get rollout <name> --watch`

Notes to self (informal):
- Keep this doc light — long term the canonical spec should be a confluence page, but Drive is where we iterate.
- Try to standardize the pre-sync job as a tiny static utility image that everyone can reuse; avoid copy/paste in many charts.
- For customers without strong KMS, support cosign keyless with transparency logs but keep expectations clear in onboarding (weaker guarantees).

Contact points (who to ping):
- Priya Natarajan (owner) – priya@redwood.ai
- Liam Chen (automation) – liam@redwood.ai
- Platform slack: #platform-private-deploy

End of draft.

Purpose:
This is a working playbook focused on ensuring image provenance (attestation + SBOM) for private/VPC/on-prem GitOps rollouts and a pragmatic, observable rollback strategy tied to Argo/Helm upgrades. The goal: make promoted images auditable, minimize blast radius during upgrades, and automate safe rollbacks when health or quality signals break.

Scope / audience:
- Platform and SRE engineers owning private deployments and the GitOps pipelines

- Release engineers coordinating upgrade windows for on-prem customers

Summary of the approach (short):
1) Build artifacts with SBOM + cosign signatures at image build time.
2) Store attestation metadata and signatures in the registry and mirror SBOMs to artifact storage (S3 or internal blob store).
3) Promote images by opening a GitOps PR to the environment repo that references a signed image digest (not tag).
4) ArgoCD/Argo Rollouts applies the Helm manifest; pre-sync hooks validate signature and SBOM match policies.
5) Canary rollout (gradual weights) controlled by Argo Rollouts; automated observability checks.
6) Auto-rollback triggers on defined health/quality thresholds or on attestation mismatch.

source.title: Inventory field sprint capacity ops ledger
source.content:
Purpose: live sprint ledger capturing field observations, SKU mix, regional inventory snapshots, and a pragmatic reorder/runway recipe for the next 16 weeks.

Summary (one-liner): we have a 9–12 week effective runway for NV-40 and A100-80-like SKUs in US-West and EU-W, but APAC is below target (3–5 weeks) and needs immediate temporary burst leasing if Q1 demand holds.

High-level numbers (current snapshot, balanced view):
- Total GPUs tracked: 11,420 units
- Allocated to customer workloads: 72%
- Free pool (buffer across regions): 1,890 units (16.6%)
- Reserved for dedicated customers: 1,240 units (10.9%)
- Weekly burn (prod tokens -> effective GPU-hours): ~1,120 units/week net (after evictions)

SKU MIX TABLE (CSV-style):
SKU,Variant,VRAM(GB),PerfIndex,UnitsOnHand,AllocatedPct,WeeklyBurn,LeadTimeWks,ReorderThreshold,OutstandingOrders
nvx-40,fp16-80,40,1.0,4,100,0.9,10,9,PO-1291 (eta 2026-03-22)
A100-80,fp16-80,80,1.8,2,450,0.65,12,8,PO-1310 (eta 2026-04-05)
H100-80,fp16-100,80,2.2,1,320,0.7,16,11,PO-1302 (eta 2026-05-12)
T4-16,int8-16,16,0.2,3,180,0.5,6,5,none
A10G,fp16-40,40,0.8,1,120,0.3,14,4,PO-1299 (eta 2026-03-30)

Notes on fields: UnitsOnHand is thousands-of-units scaled to this doc (actual units). PerfIndex is relative throughput normalized to nvx-40=1.0. WeeklyBurn is observed allocation (avg over trailing 4 weeks). ReorderThreshold is computed by heuristic below. OutstandingOrders show PO ids and expected ETA if any.

Reorder policy (practical formula used during sprint):
- reorder_threshold = ceil(weekly_burn * lead_time_wks * safety_factor)
- safety_factor: default 1.15 for stable SKUs, 1.35 for volatile SKUs (new vendor lanes or long transit).
- Example: A100-80: weekly_burn 450 * lead_time 12w * 1.15 => 6210 units -> normalized to order batch => place N batches equal to that long-run need.

Current pipeline timeline (by week window):
- Wk 0–2 (immediate): expedite small spot-lease contract with BrokerX for APAC 150 GPUs (T4/A10G mix). Action: Marco to scope costs and docs by 2026-02-28.
- Wk 3–6: Expect partial A10G shipment (PO-1299), re-route 20% to APAC if US-West saturates.
- Wk 7–12: A100-80 bulk tranche arrival (PO-1310). Confirm customs hold windows; add 2w slack for EU.
- Wk 12–18: H100 tranche (PO-1302) with longest lead time; treat as strategic reserve for high-LLM latency customers.

Regional allocation heuristics:
- Priority order for allocation during shortage: Dedicated customers (SLA) > Burst apps with queueable latency > Free pool for experiments.
- Rebalance rule: if region runway falls below 4w, trigger 1) internal re-route of 10% from lower-priority regions 2) start spot-lease talks.

Operational levers (ranked):
1) Temporary spot leasing (fastest) — cost premium but immediate.
2) Model-level throttles and batching adjustments (software) — reduce burn by up to 12% quickly.
3) Re-route traffic to compatible model variants (nvx-40 -> A100 with quantization profiles) — requires QA.
4) Expedite shipments (logistics surcharge) — use for high-value tranches only.

Risk register (live):
- Customs holdups (EU ports): medium likelihood, high impact -> mitigation: split shipments across two ports; add 2w planning slack.
- Vendor scheduling drift: high likelihood for H100 tranches -> mitigation: confirm milestone payments tied to shipping windows.
- Unexpected demand spike from new enterprise customer (probable): medium likelihood -> mitigation: pre-agreement on burst lanes priced at premium.

Open questions / decision items:
- Approve 150-unit APAC spot-lease (estimated $85k/mo). Decision owner: Marco. Due: 2026-02-28.
- Move 20% of PO-1299 to APAC if US-West utilization > 88% at next snapshot. Owner: Priya. Due: 2026-03-03.
- Commit to second A100 tranche (sizing) to cover 6–9 month runway. Finance needs cost projection. Owner: Evelyn. Due: 2026-03-15.

Quick calculations (examples):
- Runway weeks = UnitsOnHand / weekly_burn. Example: nvx-40 units on hand 4 -> runway 4 / 100 = 0.04 (scaled units; real doc uses full counts). Use normalized numbers here; full ledger uses raw counts in separate sheet.
- Safety stock estimate per region = peak_weekly_burn * max_lead_time * 1.25.

Meeting notes (from ops sync 2026-02-20):
- Agreed to prioritize APAC spot-lease while we wait for PO-1299 partial.
- SRE to prototype a grace eviction policy to free 6–8 GPUs/week in low-priority clusters.
- Logistics to confirm PO-1310 customs broker and provide tracking by EOD 2026-02-24.

Action items (owner, due):
- Marco: Negotiate APAC spot-lease contract, include termination window = 14 days. Due: 2026-02-28.
- Evelyn: Run cost model for second A100 tranche at 3 sizing options (50/100/200 units) and present P&L. Due: 2026-03-15.
- Liam: Implement quick-runbook for re-route (nvx->A100) and QA checklist. Due: 2026-03-05.
- Priya: Update consolidated procurement calendar with 2w slack per PO and publish to shared calendar. Due: 2026-02-28.

Notes-to-self (raw):
- Watch freight rate indexes for mid-March — historically spikes then.
- Consider short-term leasing partnerships with cloud partners as fallback.
- Re-evaluate safety_factor for H100 upward to 1.5 if vendor confirms longer lead times.

References and quick links: JIRA-INFRA-4123 (capacity alerting), confluence://infra/gpu-procurement-playbook (proc rules), github.com/redwood-inference/ops/pull/218 (re-route automation).

Bottom line: we need a 150-unit APAC lease and confirmation on moving part of A10G tranche to APAC to avoid <4w runway. Otherwise, software throttles + evictions can buy 3–4 weeks while bulk orders flow in.

Revision log (manual):
- 2025-11-18: created (initial sprint snapshot).
- 2026-02-20: updated after ops sync (meeting notes + action items).
- 2026-02-27: adjusted pipeline ETAs and added vendor risk note.
