text:
Purpose: live sprint ledger capturing field observations, SKU mix, regional inventory snapshots, and a pragmatic reorder/runway recipe for the next 16 weeks.

Summary (one-liner): we have a 9–12 week effective runway for NV-40 and A100-80-like SKUs in US-West and EU-W, but APAC is below target (3–5 weeks) and needs immediate temporary burst leasing if Q1 demand holds.

High-level numbers (current snapshot, balanced view):
- Total GPUs tracked: 11,420 units
- Allocated to customer workloads: 72%
- Free pool (buffer across regions): 1,890 units (16.6%)
- Reserved for dedicated customers: 1,240 units (10.9%)
- Weekly burn (prod tokens -> effective GPU-hours): ~1,120 units/week net (after evictions)

SKU MIX TABLE (CSV-style):
SKU,Variant,VRAM(GB),PerfIndex,UnitsOnHand,AllocatedPct,WeeklyBurn,LeadTimeWks,ReorderThreshold,OutstandingOrders
nvx-40,fp16-80,40,1.0,4,100,0.9,10,9,PO-1291 (eta 2026-03-22)
A100-80,fp16-80,80,1.8,2,450,0.65,12,8,PO-1310 (eta 2026-04-05)
H100-80,fp16-100,80,2.2,1,320,0.7,16,11,PO-1302 (eta 2026-05-12)
T4-16,int8-16,16,0.2,3,180,0.5,6,5,none
A10G,fp16-40,40,0.8,1,120,0.3,14,4,PO-1299 (eta 2026-03-30)

Notes on fields: UnitsOnHand is thousands-of-units scaled to this doc (actual units). PerfIndex is relative throughput normalized to nvx-40=1.0. WeeklyBurn is observed allocation (avg over trailing 4 weeks). ReorderThreshold is computed by heuristic below. OutstandingOrders show PO ids and expected ETA if any.

Reorder policy (practical formula used during sprint):
- reorder_threshold = ceil(weekly_burn * lead_time_wks * safety_factor)
- safety_factor: default 1.15 for stable SKUs, 1.35 for volatile SKUs (new vendor lanes or long transit).
- Example: A100-80: weekly_burn 450 * lead_time 12w * 1.15 => 6210 units -> normalized to order batch => place N batches equal to that long-run need.

Current pipeline timeline (by week window):
- Wk 0–2 (immediate): expedite small spot-lease contract with BrokerX for APAC 150 GPUs (T4/A10G mix). Action: Marco to scope costs and docs by 2026-02-28.
- Wk 3–6: Expect partial A10G shipment (PO-1299), re-route 20% to APAC if US-West saturates.
- Wk 7–12: A100-80 bulk tranche arrival (PO-1310). Confirm customs hold windows; add 2w slack for EU.
- Wk 12–18: H100 tranche (PO-1302) with longest lead time; treat as strategic reserve for high-LLM latency customers.

Regional allocation heuristics:
- Priority order for allocation during shortage: Dedicated customers (SLA) > Burst apps with queueable latency > Free pool for experiments.
- Rebalance rule: if region runway falls below 4w, trigger 1) internal re-route of 10% from lower-priority regions 2) start spot-lease talks.

Operational levers (ranked):
1) Temporary spot leasing (fastest) — cost premium but immediate.
2) Model-level throttles and batching adjustments (software) — reduce burn by up to 12% quickly.
3) Re-route traffic to compatible model variants (nvx-40 -> A100 with quantization profiles) — requires QA.
4) Expedite shipments (logistics surcharge) — use for high-value tranches only.

Risk register (live):
- Customs holdups (EU ports): medium likelihood, high impact -> mitigation: split shipments across two ports; add 2w planning slack.
- Vendor scheduling drift: high likelihood for H100 tranches -> mitigation: confirm milestone payments tied to shipping windows.
- Unexpected demand spike from new enterprise customer (probable): medium likelihood -> mitigation: pre-agreement on burst lanes priced at premium.

Open questions / decision items:
- Approve 150-unit APAC spot-lease (estimated $85k/mo). Decision owner: Marco. Due: 2026-02-28.
- Move 20% of PO-1299 to APAC if US-West utilization > 88% at next snapshot. Owner: Priya. Due: 2026-03-03.
- Commit to second A100 tranche (sizing) to cover 6–9 month runway. Finance needs cost projection. Owner: Evelyn. Due: 2026-03-15.

Quick calculations (examples):
- Runway weeks = UnitsOnHand / weekly_burn. Example: nvx-40 units on hand 4 -> runway 4 / 100 = 0.04 (scaled units; real doc uses full counts). Use normalized numbers here; full ledger uses raw counts in separate sheet.
- Safety stock estimate per region = peak_weekly_burn * max_lead_time * 1.25.

Meeting notes (from ops sync 2026-02-20):
- Agreed to prioritize APAC spot-lease while we wait for PO-1299 partial.
- SRE to prototype a grace eviction policy to free 6–8 GPUs/week in low-priority clusters.
- Logistics to confirm PO-1310 customs broker and provide tracking by EOD 2026-02-24.

Action items (owner, due):
- Marco: Negotiate APAC spot-lease contract, include termination window = 14 days. Due: 2026-02-28.
- Evelyn: Run cost model for second A100 tranche at 3 sizing options (50/100/200 units) and present P&L. Due: 2026-03-15.
- Liam: Implement quick-runbook for re-route (nvx->A100) and QA checklist. Due: 2026-03-05.
- Priya: Update consolidated procurement calendar with 2w slack per PO and publish to shared calendar. Due: 2026-02-28.

Notes-to-self (raw):
- Watch freight rate indexes for mid-March — historically spikes then.
- Consider short-term leasing partnerships with cloud partners as fallback.
- Re-evaluate safety_factor for H100 upward to 1.5 if vendor confirms longer lead times.

References and quick links: JIRA-INFRA-4123 (capacity alerting), confluence://infra/gpu-procurement-playbook (proc rules), github.com/redwood-inference/ops/pull/218 (re-route automation).

Bottom line: we need a 150-unit APAC lease and confirmation on moving part of A10G tranche to APAC to avoid <4w runway. Otherwise, software throttles + evictions can buy 3–4 weeks while bulk orders flow in.

Revision log (manual):
- 2025-11-18: created (initial sprint snapshot).
- 2026-02-20: updated after ops sync (meeting notes + action items).
- 2026-02-27: adjusted pipeline ETAs and added vendor risk note.

source.title: Onprem GPU Upgrade Sanity Playbook — Labbook
source.content:
Purpose and scope:
This document is a working labbook-style playbook focused on safe, test-driven upgrades of NVIDIA drivers, CUDA, and NCCL across heterogeneous on-prem Kubernetes clusters that host Redwood Private. It complements the canonical upgrade playbooks (see linked Confluence) and surfaces field observations, validation checks, terraform module notes, and an automated sanity harness we use in the lab.

High-level upgrade strategy (fail-safe, rollback-first):
- Plan the rollout at node-pool granularity. Treat each node pool (by instance type + GPU family + kernel version) as a separate upgrade unit.
- Canary-first, rollback-first: apply the minimum change to a small canary pool and immediately run automated verification; if verification fails, trigger fast rollback automation.
- Keep a driver image snapshot and a node image snapshot (OS + kubelet + drivers) for instant reprovisioning.
- Do not upgrade CUDA and NCCL on the same pass as the driver except for explicitly validated permutations.

Compatibility validation matrix (operational view):
- Goal: a compact checklist to quickly determine whether a (kernel, driver, CUDA, NCCL, k8s) permutation is candidate for canary.
- Minimal fields we check: Kubernetes minor version, kubelet flags (CRI), OS kernel version, NVIDIA driver version, CUDA toolkit ABI, NCCL package version, container-runtime (containerd/docker), MIG configured (yes/no), interconnect (ethernet/infiniband), multigpu topology (NVLink present).

Representative matrix snapshot (lab canonical permutations) — summarized as text rows (full matrix in Confluence):
- Ubuntu 22.04, kernel 5.15.x, k8s 1.27.x, NVIDIA 542.59, CUDA 12.1, NCCL 2.18 — PASS for inference load (batch size <=16, seq_len <=1024)
- Ubuntu 22.04, kernel 6.1.x, k8s 1.28.x, NVIDIA 555.30, CUDA 12.2, NCCL 2.19 — WARNING: observed rare KV cache eviction stalls under heavy concurrent warmup (see test harness logs)
- Rocky 8.8, kernel 4.18 backported, k8s 1.25.x, NVIDIA 470.x, CUDA 11.4, NCCL 2.10 — PASS with reduced host memory settings; not recommended for Dedicated scale
- Mixed node-pool with A100 and H100 families: H100 needs driver >= 535 for full feature set; validate MIG/compute-mode separately.

Key validation checks (automated):
- Driver probe: nvidia-smi queries + driver-module load/unload test (modprobe --remove) inside a maintenance window.
- CUDA container smoke: run a lightweight cuda-memtest + a sample inference microbench (open-source runner) validating correct FP16/FP32 paths.
- NCCL health: run NCCL tests across 2–4 nodes (all-reduce latency and bandwidth) using our NCCL harness with target thresholds based on interconnect.
- KV-cache persistence: run a multi-actor kv-cache load (short sequences) and validate warmup latency distribution percentiles — watch for 95/99 spikes.
- Prometheus/metrics baseline regression: check node-exporter + nvidia-dcgm metrics for error counters, PCIe errors, GPU resets within 24h of upgrade.
- Kube-level validation: cordon + drain + unschedulable check, custom readiness probe behavior for Redwood agent, pod restart loops, eviction events.

Sanity test harness (lab automation) — runbook steps:
1) Provision canary node-pool with terraform (see terraform notes below).
2) Apply driver update via node-image pipeline (image build -> image promote -> node pool rolling update).
3) Run automated probes: driver-probe -> cuda-smoke -> nccl-run -> inference-warmup -> prometheus-snapshot.
4) Wait 30–90 minutes; analyze automated results (scripted thresholds).
5) If any single critical probe fails, execute automated rollback (image-bounce to previous snapshot) and open an incident with prefilled investigation template.

Terraform module notes (practical snippets and gotchas):
- We maintain a node-image module that builds AMIs/Images with baked driver/CUDA artifacts. Prefer image-centric upgrades vs in-place apt-driver-upgrades to reduce drift.
- Keep image tags immutable (image:drivers/v542-cuda12.1-202601). Module example (pseudo):
  module "node_image_gpu" {
    source = "git::ssh://git@github.com/redwood-inference/infra-modules//node-image?ref=main"
    os_distro = "ubuntu-22.04"
    kernel_version = "5.15.0-63-generic"
    nvidia_driver = "542.59"
    cuda_tag = "cuda-12.1"
  }
- Important: expose a boolean `enable_inplace_driver_upgrade` default false. We only set true for lab instances.
- For heterogeneous pools, include `gpu_family` map in the module to avoid implicit assumptions in instance templates.
- Add lifecycle rules to ensure image replacement happens only after successful validation: use a `validation_passed` remote state flag or a simple semaphored tag on the image resource.

Observed field anomalies (notes for triage):
- Rare PCIe link flaps when kernel auto-suspends a PCI device; mitigated by blacklisting autosuspend for nvidia devices in tuned/udev rules.
- NCCL hangs correlated with mismatched user-space udd drivers (container base image with older CUDA libs). Solution: enforce a runtime check that container CUDA libs <= host CUDA ABI compatibility table.
- Kubelet OOM with containerd + cgroup v2 when driver injects large kernel memory; raise system reserved memory for kubelet on kernel 6.x.

Rollback playbook (fast path):
- Mark node-pool:cordoned and scale up stable node-pool.
- Trigger image revert via terraform: set node pool image to previous immutable tag and run rolling update with maxUnavailable=1.
- If node-provision fails, run maintenance automation to reprovision via PXE or image-based recovery.
- Post-rollback: attach logs and heartbeat metrics to the rollback incident (auto-upload to S3 and link in JIRA).

Telemetry to capture during and after upgrade (minimum dataset):
- driver_version, cuda_version, kubelet_version (per node)
- nvidia_pci_errors, gpu_reset_count, gpu_temp, mem_used_percent (1m/5m/1h rolls)
- inference_tail_latency_p95/p99, throughput (tokens/s), batch_aborts
- container_restart_count, kube_eviction_events

Operational runbook snippets (commands):
- Check driver and modules: `nvidia-smi --query-gpu=driver_version,name --format=csv && lsmod | grep nvidia`
- CUDA container smoke: `ctr run --rm --net-host --env NVIDIA_VISIBLE_DEVICES=all docker.io/library/cuda:12.1-base /bin/bash -c "./cuda-smoke -i 3"`
- NCCL probe (lab harness): `./nccl-test --gpus 4 --iters 100 --pattern all_reduce`

Notes on observability thresholds (example):
- NCCL all-reduce bandwidth < 70% of baseline on IB: FAIL
- GPU ECC errors > 0 within 1h: FAIL
- Inference p99 latency increase > 30% relative to baseline: WARNING -> potentially reject rollout for latency-sensitive routes

Open questions & TODOs (working, needs owner):
- (Ethan) Add an automated preflight that validates container CUDA ABI vs host drivers before scheduling (deadline: 2026-03-10).
- (Priya) Formalize the `validation_passed` image tagging flow and document the terraform remote state interactions.
- (Marco) Add MIG-aware NCCL tuning profiles for H100 in the infra repo.

Appendix A — example incident template (pre-filled by automation):
- Incident title: `driver-upgrade-failure: <cluster>-<node-pool>`
- Triage checklist: reproduce driver probe -> check kernel logs (dmesg) -> collect nvidia-smi output -> gather last 1h prometheus snapshot -> capture kube events for the pool.

Appendix B — lab harness location and how to run locally:
- Repo: github.com/redwood-inference/infra-sanity-harness (see PR 712).
- Quick start: clone, `make lab-provision`, edit `lab/vars.yml` to point to the canary subnet, run `./harness/run_canary.sh --profile nccl,cuda,kvcache`

This document is a living labbook. Expect frequent updates as we expand permutations and codify more automation. Use the linked artifacts for the canonical matrix and open PRs. If you add a new test permutation, append the short results row to the compatibility matrix section above and ping the team for review.

- (Ethan) Add an automated preflight that validates container CUDA ABI vs host drivers before scheduling (deadline: 2026-03-10).
- (Priya) Formalize the `validation_passed` image tagging flow and document the terraform remote state interactions.
- (Marco) Add MIG-aware NCCL tuning profiles for H100 in the infra repo.

Appendix A — example incident template (pre-filled by automation):
- Incident title: `driver-upgrade-failure: <cluster>-<node-pool>`
- Triage checklist: reproduce driver probe -> check kernel logs (dmesg) -> collect nvidia-smi output -> gather last 1h prometheus snapshot -> capture kube events for the pool.

Appendix B — lab harness location and how to run locally:
- Repo: github.com/redwood-inference/infra-sanity-harness (see PR 712).
- Quick start: clone, `make lab-provision`, edit `lab/vars.yml` to point to the canary subnet, run `./harness/run_canary.sh --profile nccl,cuda,kvcache`

This document is a living labbook. Expect frequent updates as we expand permutations and codify more automation. Use the linked artifacts for the canonical matrix and open PRs. If you add a new test permutation, append the short results row to the compatibility matrix section above and ping the team for review.

source.title: KV window coherence probes — long-context retrieval stress (8k/32k/128k)
source.body:
Summary:
This note collects exploratory probes and regression-style tests focused on KV-cache window coherence under retrieval-heavy workloads across 8k, 32k and 128k context budgets. Goal is to exercise prefetch / sliding-window semantics, measure when prefix state decays (or is read incorrectly), and reproduce attention-sink patterns when retrieval chunks interleave with model-generated prefixes.

Motivation / why: 
- We've seen intermittent hallucination/regression on models when recent retrieved chunks are reintroduced after forced truncation. Suspect a KV windowing coherence hole where older KV segments are partially present but stale.
- Need a reproducible harness to compare model variants and runtime prefetch heuristics.

Experiment overview (high level):
- Models: test on two candidate checkpoints (v2.1, v2.1-quant8) and a third compatibility variant (v1.9-fp16) as baseline.
- Context budgets: 8k, 32k, 128k.
- Workloads: dense retrieval (top-k chunked inserts), sparse retrieval (single large doc reintroductions), and prefix replay stress (long generated prefix followed by short retrieval update).
- Prefetch timing variants: immediate (prefetch on retrieval call), delayed (prefetch on token N after retrieval), and lazy (on next-attention-sweep).

Harness setup and configs (notes-to-self):
- Runner: inference/harness/kvcache-probe.py (see linked GH).
- Synthetic corpus: 10k short docs (1–3 paragraphs) + 500 long docs (~50k tokens) used to generate retrieval chains.
- Retrieval chunk size: 512 tokens with overlaps of 64, 128, 256.
- Sliding window sizes: 512, 2048, 8192 tokens.
- Prefetch buffer TTL: {0, 100ms, 500ms}.
- Measure: token-level latency, kvcache hit/miss per token, effective attention coverage (approx using probe-queries), and output fidelity (ROUGE-L against oracle continuation).

Prompt sets (representative examples):
- Case A (chain-of-thought long): long human prompt ~6k tokens summarizing a technical report, then generate step-by-step explanation; at token 200 add retrieval patch that contradicts earlier prompt; check whether model overwrites earlier state or mixes.
- Case B (doc-recall): five retrieval inserts spaced across context - evaluate recall accuracy for late query referencing earliest insert.
- Case C (prefix replay): model generates 4k tokens autonomously, then we cut context to N and replay a slightly modified earlier prefix via retrieval; observe stray attention to truncated fragments.

Key observations from early runs (v2.1, 128k):
- Prefetch-immediate reduces token latency variance but increases stale-read incidents in the 128k runs when sliding window size < accumulated KV size. Stale-read defined as output that references content that should have been evicted.
- Delayed prefetch (100ms) reduces stale reads by ~35% at cost of +12% avg token latency.
- Lazy prefetch sees the fewest stale reads but incurs head-of-line latency spikes during the first requested token after a retrieval insertion.

Quantitative snapshots (representative):
- 128k, v2.1, immediate prefetch: kvcache-hit-rate 0.87, stale-read-rate 0.12, avg-token-latency 48ms.
- 128k, v2.1, delayed 100ms: kvcache-hit-rate 0.84, stale-read-rate 0.078, avg-token-latency 54ms.
- 32k, v2.1, immediate: kvcache-hit-rate 0.72, stale-read-rate 0.19, avg-token-latency 26ms.

Failure modes & concrete examples:
- Attention-sink spike: when retrieved chunk X overlaps partly with a still-resident older chunk Y, attention weights concentrate on a narrow token band (sink) causing repetition and output collapse. Example: after insertion of update "Policy A removed clause 3", model continued to quote clause 3 verbatim — likely because fragment Y remained in KV and dominated attention for some heads.
- Partial fragment shadowing: truncated prefixes create micro-copies in KV that are addressable via attention but inconsistent with current prefix; shows as contradictions and sudden drop in ROUGE.
- Prefetch race: prefetch kicked in while eviction was ongoing -> kvcache gets both new and old fragments interleaved at different offsets; leads to incorrect reconstruction of earlier sequence order.

Hypotheses (ranked):
1) Prefetch logic not consistently marking live ranges -> eviction schedule may remove metadata before physical memory update is complete.
2) Kernel-level attention reduction approximations at very long sequences degrade multi-head diversity, making one head dominate (sink).
3) Retrieval chunk overlap and TTL misalignment produce partial duplicate keys.

Possible mitigations to try in short term:
- Ensure atomic swap for prefetch into KV index (mark-live then swap).
- Add small randomized jitter to prefetch timing to avoid deterministic races.
- For overlapping chunks, enforce compaction step that dedupes identical tokens at tile boundaries (cheap rolling hash).
- Instrument per-head attention concentration metric and add a soft-threshold fallback that uses average pooling when sink detected.

Regression plan & gating criteria for model onboarding:
- Run 1k-case harness across the three budgets and require:
  - stale-read-rate <= 0.05 on 128k with delayed prefetch baseline,
  - no attention-sink episodes that cause repetition > 30 tokens continuing,
  - ROUGE-L drop <= 10% relative to baseline v1.9-fp16.
- If any criterion violated, block rollout for Dedicated/Private until mitigations verified.

Open questions / unresolved: 
- Do we need a separate KV compaction pass for quantized runtimes (quant8)? Early runs show compaction is more expensive but may be critical.
- How to best simulate real retrieval latency distribution (cloud vs dedicated on-prem)? Current synthetic adds fixed 50ms RTT.
- What’s the right metric for "stale-read" in mixed retrieval/generation workloads — overlap ratio or semantic contradiction score?

Next steps / action items (owners):
- Evan: implement atomic-mark-swap in prefetch codepath and run the 128k delayed/immediate matrix (due 2026-02-28).
- Sofia: add per-head attention concentration logger & visualization; push sample plots to confluence (due 2026-02-24).
- Liam: expand synthetic workload to include tail-latency distribution matching production retrieval logs (due 2026-03-05).
- Priya (me): collate full run logs and produce a concise regression ticket if stale-read-rate > threshold (due 2026-03-10).

Appendix: quick checklist for reproducibility:
- config used: sliding-window=8192, chunk-size=512, overlap=128, prefetch={immediate,100ms,lazy}, model-weights-tag=v2.1-testbed
- harness command: python kvcache-probe.py --budget 128k --prefetch delayed --overlap 128 --runs 200 --out ./runs/128k_delayed_v2.1
- log artifacts: store token-level kvcache events, attention head entropy traces, and full generation transcripts.

Notes-to-self / rough thoughts: 
- Some of these behaviors only show up once stateful generation exceeds ~30k tokens; so short local dev runs are insufficient. Need a cloud budget for longer end-to-end runs.
- Keep a small priority queue of failing cases for each run so we can build a minimal corpus for regression tests.
